indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: National AI Strategy 2025–2030 (draft) (Albania)
fr1_justif: As mentioned on pp. 21–23 (Education) the strategy addresses AI literacy through systemic integration of AI and digital skills across the education system. The SmartLabs project equips pre-university schools with technological infrastructure and introduces programming from the first grade to build critical thinking and problem-solving skills. The strategy further commits to a national digital skills agenda that supports students in developing competencies to participate in ethical and sustainable AI development while also training teachers and school leaders to effectively use AI tools. This dual focus on student capacity and educator training embeds AI literacy institutionally and long term.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Law No. 124/2024 “On the Protection of Personal Data”
fr1_justif: Law No. 124/2024 safeguards children’s rights in AI contexts by regulating and limiting automated processing and profiling (Art. 20 pp. 12–13) requiring child-specific consent for online services under 16 (Art. 8(6) pg. 6) and mandating clear age-appropriate information.  While the law does not explicitly use the term “Artificial Intelligence” it covers automated and algorithmic processing (Art. 13(1)c) profiling and data protection by design/default which are directly applicable to AI systems used with or by minors.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Commissioner for the Right to Information and Protection of Personal Data

fr1_title: National AI Strategy 2025–2030 (draft) (Albania)
fr1_justif: As mentioned in Section 6 (Cultural Heritage and Tourism) the document addresses cultural and linguistic diversity by promoting AI applications that preserve digitize and enhance access to Albania’s cultural heritage. It highlights the use of image recognition and 3D reconstruction to digitally restore monuments and artifacts as well as machine learning tools to catalog and translate historical documents. The strategy also supports multilingual virtual guides and augmented reality experiences ensuring inclusive access for diverse domestic and international audiences while strengthening cultural identity and global visibility.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National AI Strategy 2025–2030 (draft) (Albania)
fr1_justif: The document addresses environmental impact by recognizing both the strategic opportunity and the sustainability risks associated with AI development. As mentioned on p. 18 (Regional Leadership and Specialized Focus) it encourages Albania to differentiate itself through “Green AI” promoting environmentally sustainable AI solutions as a competitive advantage. At the same time as noted on p. 19 (Resource Constraints and Sustainability) it acknowledges that AI systems are resource-intensive and may strain electricity and digital infrastructure if not carefully managed. The strategy warns that increased computing demands could conflict with environmental objectives unless sustainability considerations are integrated into AI development.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National AI Strategy 2025–2030 (draft) (Albania)
fr1_justif: On p. 39 the document addresses fairness and non-discrimination by emphasizing equality justice and respect for human dignity as foundational principles of trustworthy AI. It recognizes that bias may arise from data algorithms or applications and may result in discriminatory outcomes based on characteristics such as race gender ethnicity or religion. To mitigate these risks it calls for the establishment of guidelines and standards for data collection and analysis algorithm design and testing and evaluation and auditing processes. The framework stresses transparency explainability accountability and alignment with human rights as safeguards against discriminatory impacts.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National AI Strategy 2025–2030 (draft) (Albania)
fr1_justif: On p. 38 the document addresses Human Oversight and Determination by embedding it within “Governance and oversight” and “Responsibility.” It calls for regulatory frameworks that clearly define the roles and obligations of developers providers users and regulators across the AI lifecycle ensuring that AI systems remain subject to institutional control. It further requires oversight mechanisms audits traceability and impact assessments so that AI decisions can be reviewed and attributed. By mandating accountability of public institutions businesses and individuals for AI-related harms it affirms that ultimate decision-making authority and responsibility remain with human actors not autonomous systems.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Digital Agenda of Albania 2022–2026
fr1_justif: The strategy aims to integrate advanced technologies explicitly including AI (p. 11) and frames digital education and training of public employees as an absolute prerequisite for their implementation and adaptation (p. 17). It further emphasises the digitisation of public-sector data and archives as a necessary condition for enabling such technologies (p. 17). In addition it recognises the growing importance of predictive analytics and AI in identifying potential governance challenges early (p. 15) whilst carrying out  actions  aimed  at  improving  the  digital  skills  of  civil  servants  and  employees  in  local  and  regional  government  and  public  administration (p. 36).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National AI Strategy 2025–2030 (draft) (Albania)
fr1_justif: The document addresses reskilling and upskilling in the context of AI mainly through its Education and Workforce Development pillar and the Action Plan. It commits to AI-related upskilling and reskilling programs for individuals already in the workforce including civil servants and professionals to adapt to AI-driven labour changes (pg. 34–36). It foresees continuing education vocational training programs in AI and data science and lifelong learning pathways linked to employability (pg. 35–36). The Action Plan highlights retraining workers for higher-value roles as automation expands and integrates AI skills into national education and training strategies (pg. 42–44).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Law No. 124/2024 “On the Protection of Personal Data”
fr1_justif: The law addresses explainability through Art. 14–15 (pg 11–13) on information duties requiring notice of automated decision-making and “meaningful information about the logic involved'. Art. 22 (pg 18–19) regulates automated decision-making and profiling granting the right to human intervention and to contest decisions.Art. 24–26 (pg 20–23) require records of processing and risk assessments supporting auditability and oversight.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Commissioner for the Right to Information and Protection of Personal Data

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Draft Law on Artificial Intelligence (Angola)
fr1_justif: The Draft Law on AI addresses AI-facilitated misinformation and violence mainly through criminal liability provisions. Article 76 criminalizes the intentional use of AI systems to violate fundamental rights promote discrimination or hatred through algorithms or harm democratic processes and public order. Article 77 increases penalties when crimes are committed using advanced technologies such as “deepfakes or bots” that manipulate public opinion. Article 51 requires AI-generated news content to include visible identifiers to ensure information accuracy. These provisions target malicious uses of AI that spread disinformation or incite harm.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: The Draft Law on Artificial Intelligence explicitly excludes military AI from its scope stating that development and use of AI for military purposes are regulated under a separate legal framework (Article 4). Personal or family AI use and scientific research are also excluded confirming that defence and security are explicitly outside this law’s remit.

fr1_title: Personal Data Protection Law (Draft) (2025) (Angola)
fr1_justif: The Draft Law on the Protection of Personal Data '2025 Version' guarantees Access to Redress and Remedy by granting data subjects the right to request explanations of AI decisions contest outputs and demand human intervention (Article 37 Section II). Article 33 prohibits harmful fully automated decisions and requires disclosure of criteria upon request. Articles 26–32 ensure rights to access rectification deletion and complaints to a supervisory authority reinforcing avenues for remedy.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: The Draft Law on the Protection of Personal Data '2025 Version' explicitly excludes certain data processing activities related to national defense public security internal or external state intelligence and Activities of investigation and repression of criminal offenses from its scope (Article 4 n.º 2 letters a–d). These activities are instead governed by specific legislation which must respect proportionality and necessity principles but are not covered by the general data protection law.

fr1_title: Draft Law on Artificial Intelligence (Angola)
fr1_justif: The Draft Law on Artificial Intelligence requires AI developers to ensure fairness among users preventing discrimination based on gender age ethnicity region belief or economic status (Article 23). Risk assessments must identify potential biases and verify ethical compliance (Article 33). Critical AI requires audited data labeling to improve fairness and accuracy (Article 42). Criminal penalties apply for AI used to discriminate or incite hate (Article 76).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: The Draft Law on Artificial Intelligence explicitly excludes military AI from its scope stating that development and use of AI for military purposes are regulated under a separate legal framework (Article 4). Personal or family AI use and scientific research are also excluded confirming that defence and security are explicitly outside this law’s remit.

fr1_title: Personal Data Protection Law (Draft) (2025) (Angola)
fr1_justif: The draft addresses human oversight indirectly through safeguards on automated processing and AI systems. Chapter III Art. 33 regulates “automated individual decisions” limiting decisions based solely on automated processing and granting data subjects rights when affected by AI-driven processing. Articles 36–38 also defines profiling as automated evaluation of personal aspects and sets obligations and prohibited practices for entities developing or operating AI systems. While these provisions restrict fully automated decisions and establish accountability the draft does not define operational human-in-the-loop procedures or detailed oversight mechanisms for high-risk uses.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: The Draft Law on the Protection of Personal Data '2025 Version' explicitly excludes certain data processing activities related to national defense public security internal or external state intelligence and Activities of investigation and repression of criminal offenses from its scope (Article 4 n.º 2 letters a–d). These activities are instead governed by specific legislation which must respect proportionality and necessity principles but are not covered by the general data protection law.

fr1_title: Personal Data Protection Law (Draft) (2025) (Angola)
fr1_justif: The Draft Law on the Protection of Personal Data (2025 Version) addresses Impact Assessments in the context of AI under Articles 36–38 (Section II) and Articles 43(1–9) (Chapter IV). Article 43(1–9) (Chapter IV) requires controllers to perform a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) before any processing likely to result in high risks such as profiling or automated decision-making using AI systems. The DPIA must evaluate the nature scope context and purposes of processing identify risks to data subjects and define mitigation measures. Supervisory authority consultation is required before deployment if high residual risks remain.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Article 4.2 of the Draft Law on the Protection of Personal Data (2025 Version) explicitly excludes processing of personal data for purposes related to national defence public security internal and external intelligence and criminal investigation or prosecution. And according to 4.3 the processing of personal data referred to in the previous paragraph is governed by specific legislation which must provide for measures that are proportionate and strictly necessary for the pursuit of the public interest as well as the general principles of data protection and the rights of data subjects provided for in this law.

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: White Book on Information and Communication Technologies (2023–2027)
fr1_justif: The White Book on ICT (2023–2027 sec. 5.6.7.1 pp. 99–103) proposes workforce reskilling with AI competencies the National Workforce Training Plan Young Smarts Program training for regulatory authorities scholarships and university partnerships hackathons and ethical AI guidelines promoting both technical and ethical skills for public servants and the judiciary.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Personal Data Protection Law (Draft) (2025) (Angola)
fr1_justif: The draft law (Section II Articles 36–38; Chapter IV Articles 39–43) ensures Safety and Security by prohibiting manipulative AI practices requiring secure data management transparency and human oversight. It mandates data protection by design encryption breach notification and impact assessments to prevent risks to individuals’ rights and data integrity.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Article 4.2 of the Draft Law on the Protection of Personal Data (2025 Version) explicitly excludes processing of personal data for purposes related to national defence public security internal and external intelligence and criminal investigation or prosecution. And according to 4.3 the processing of personal data referred to in the previous paragraph is governed by specific legislation which must provide for measures that are proportionate and strictly necessary for the pursuit of the public interest as well as the general principles of data protection and the rights of data subjects provided for in this law.

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Guide for the Integration of Artificial Intelligences in Education
fr1_justif: The document addresses AI literacy by explaining what AI is how it works and its limitations offering foundational knowledge for teachers and students (Presentation and Introduction pp. 5–7). It promotes critical understanding of algorithms bias and ethical risks (Secondary Education p. 13; Governance and data protection p. 20). It also includes explicit recommendations for developing responsible transparent and informed use of AI in the classroom strengthening digital and AI skills (Recommendations p. 16).
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Guide for Public and Private Entities on Transparency and Protection of Personal Data for Responsible Artificial Intelligence
fr1_justif: Section 5 (pp. 20-21) mandates that AI data processing be 'consistent with international law.' It integrates Law 25326 guaranteeing rights to rectification updating and deletion of inaccurate or irrelevant data plus the right to object to processing for advertising. Critically page 25 establishes that individuals have the right 'not to be subject to a decision based solely on automated processing' that significantly affects them. This includes the right to obtain human intervention express their point of view and dispute the decision. By combining data protection with human oversight the framework ensures individuals maintain control and can challenge automated outcomes throughout the AI lifecycle
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Disposition 2/2023 – 'Recommendations for Reliable Artificial Intelligence'(Undersecretariat of Information Technologies Chief of the Cabinet of Ministers) (Argentina)
fr1_justif: Section 3 Principle 5: “Equidad y no discriminación” (p. 5) states that AI systems should “promote diversity and inclusion safeguard equity and avoid bias or discrimination based on gender ethnicity age or other characteristics.” The document also emphasises the need to identify and mitigate algorithmic bias throughout the AI lifecycle to ensure equitable and human-centred outcomes.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Guide for Public and Private Entities on Transparency and Protection of Personal Data for Responsible Artificial Intelligence
fr1_justif: The Guide addresses gender equality noting AI can amplify discrimination (pp. 6–7). It identifies 'genders and dissidences' as vulnerable groups (p. 7). In 'Main problems and challenges' (pp. 16–18) it highlights gender and ethnic bias specifically in facial recognition (affecting women with darker skin) and language models. Under 'Transparency and Data Protection Principles' (p. 22) gender identity is categorized as sensitive data requiring special protection. Although the guideline acknowledges gender as a critical issue throughout these sections the audit reveals that it is not translated into specific actionable obligations within the final recommended principles
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Guide for Public and Private Entities on Transparency and Protection of Personal Data for Responsible Artificial Intelligence
fr1_justif: Page 25 provides the right to control how people's data is collected. It establishes the right to human intervention allowing them to request a review by a physical person for decisions based on automated processing that significantly affect them such as profiling or credit assessments. Organisations are encouraged to ensure significant human intervention and are required to disclose their forms of human supervision in transparency sheets to avoid purely mechanical determinations (Page 47). The guide recommends appointing specific personnel responsible for manually monitoring system behaviour and providing human communication channels for users who prefer interacting with a person rather than a machine (Pages 37 and 44).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Guide for Public and Private Entities on Transparency and Protection of Personal Data for Responsible Artificial Intelligence
fr1_justif: The document includes impact assessments and risk management across the entire lifecycle of AI systems (pp. 28–32). A subsection titled “Impact assessments” explains that these studies help identify risks such as re-identification of individuals algorithmic bias and misuse of data (p. 29). The document also specifies which companies must develop risk management strategies and provides a risk matrix along with guidance for conducting AI impact assessments (p. 30). It further distinguishes between material and moral impact (p. 32) and emphasises that impact assessments should be conducted continuously during validation implementation operation and maintenance (pp. 31–32).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Draft Law on Regulatory Framework and Development of AI Systems
fr1_justif: Article 5 establishes in i) as one of the core principles of the law the 'Transparency in the algorithmic management of work' which provides the right of workers or their representatives to be informed on whether an automated system is used for monitoring or making decisions that affect them. These systems must be transparent and audited and be constantly supervised by humans. Art. 18 point (c) classifies as high-risk any AI system used for: 'recruitment selection screening candidate evaluation and decision-making regarding promotions or terminations of contractual employment relationships; the distribution of tasks; and the control and evaluation of the performance and behavior of the people affected by these applications
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems
fr1_title: Guide for Public and Private Entities on Transparency and Protection of Personal Data for Responsible Artificial Intelligence
fr1_justif: The Guideline expands on how to achieve algorithmic transparency (promoted on p. 36 as part of the Principle of Transparency and Explainability) including a specific 'AI Systems Transparency Sheet' (pp. 43-45). This section focuses on system characteristics organizational information and technical details that define the technology provided to the user. As stated on page 43 'organizations' explicitly include the public sector. This ensures that transparency obligations apply to state-led implementations fulfilling the requirements for public accountability
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Guide for Public and Private Entities on Transparency and Protection of Personal Data for Responsible Artificial Intelligence
fr1_justif: The guidelines identify security risks such as cyberattacks data breaches and integrity compromises (p. 17). Security is a core principle for protecting personal and sensitive data against unauthorized access or loss (pp. 20 23). In Section 6 the framework mandates security measures for data processing (p. 33) including incident management protocols (p. 34). During the validation stage testing and monitoring are required to ensure data quality and mitigate risks (p. 37). Implementation requires security standards to ensure traceability and human accountability (p. 39). Finally the framework establishes that security monitoring and control are essential during the final stages of the AI lifecycle (p. 41
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Guide for Public and Private Entities on Transparency and Protection of Personal Data for Responsible Artificial Intelligence
fr1_justif: In Section 4 (pp. 16–19) the framework identifies 'lack of transparency' as a key risk noting that 'AI models can be complex and opaque making it difficult to understand how decisions are made.' Consequently if data algorithms and internal processes remain unknown a transparency deficit occurs. To mitigate this Section 5 (pp. 20-26) establishes the 'Principles related to transparency' stating: 'Individuals must have the opportunity to request explanations and information from the AI controller or relevant public sector institutions.' (p.21). This confirms that the right to an explanation regarding decisions made by AI systems is an integral part of the framework's mandate
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Strategy against Disinformation (Armenia)
fr1_justif: According to the strategy the private sector is key in combating disinformation by developing AI- and machine learning-based tools.  Closer cooperation between governments and platforms on algorithms advertising and co-regulation can enhance resilience against large-scale disinformation.(page 22 2.4 Engagement and mobilization of private sector )
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The ministries the National Security Service the Government Staff as well as other governmental bodies and organizations are mentioned

fr1_title: Amendments to the Criminal Code of the Republic of Armenia
fr1_justif: The Draft Law broadens the conceptual scope of child pornography extending its definition to encompass materials generated by means of artificial intelligence
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Australian Framework for Generative Artificial Intelligence in Schools
fr1_justif: The Framework integrates AI literacy by requiring schools to teach students how generative AI works its limitations potential biases and appropriate ethical use. Page 7 (Principle 1.2) states that schools must “engage students in learning about generative AI tools and how they work including their potential limitations and biases.” It also links AI literacy to critical thinking academic integrity safe data use and awareness of risks ensuring students develop informed ethical and practical competencies.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: The Framework states it does not address other forms of AI including predictive AI or broader national security applications (p.3)
fr2_title: Gen AI Strategies for Australian Higher Education: Emerging Practice
fr2_justif: The document embeds AI literacy by requiring institutions to build student and staff capability to “use gen AI tools effectively and ethically” (Foreword p.1). Sections on Student support (pp.26–30) and Staff engagement (pp.32–36) call for training modules guidance workshops and resources to develop skills critical understanding and ethical use. It emphasises literacy in academic integrity modules curriculum review and assessment design (pp.43–51).
fr2_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Criminal Code Amendment (Deepfake Sexual Material) Act 2024
fr1_justif: The Act amends the Criminal Code to create offences for using a carriage service to transmit sexual material of an adult without consent regardless of whether the material is unaltered or “created or altered in any way using technology”. A note clarifies this expressly includes material edited or generated using digital technology “including artificial intelligence” such as realistic but false “deepfakes” (p. 4). Aggravated offences apply where the offender created or altered the material (p. 5).
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: The Australian Consumer Law (ACL)
fr1_justif: Although the ACL is technologically neutral and does not refer to AI it is valid and meaningful for the indicator when read in light of the Government Treasury’s discussion paper Review of AI and the Australian Consumer Law (https://storage.googleapis.com/files-au-treasury/treasury/p/prj3726058ed26a734ddd6e6/public_assets/c2024-584560-dp.pdf). As an official interpretive material it clarifies that consumers of AI-enabled goods and services can rely on existing guarantees and seek compensation (pp. 2 11) while noting challenges due to AI (p. 12). The ACL itself outlines remedies in Sections 259–260 and permits claims for damages under Section 236.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Basic Online Safety Expectations Regulatory Guidance (July 2024)
fr1_justif: Adds an expectation that providers make the “best interests of the child” a primary consideration where services are likely accessed by children (s.6(2A)); requires robust default privacy/safety settings for children’s services (s.6(3)(b)); mandates safety risk assessments incl. child safety (s.6(3)(e)); requires age assurance and improved tech to prevent children’s access to Class 2 material (s.12(2)(a)(c)); enables eSafety to request child/adult user counts (s.20(5)). Specific steps must also be taken when the services use generative AI (s.8A) to mitigate risks and impacts for the child.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Determination is administered by the eSafety Commissioner under section 45 of the Online Safety Act 2021 Several provisions (eg s20(5)) explicitly authorise the Commissioner to request reports and enforce compliance with the Basic Online Safety Expectations

fr1_title: Australia's AI Ethics Principles
fr1_justif: The framework places particular emphasis on cultural diversity as a key safeguard in the design and deployment of AI systems. It encourages organisations to involve individuals from diverse cultural backgrounds alongside different disciplines and perspectives to ensure that AI systems reflect a broader range of social values and lived experiences (p. 4).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National framework for the assurance of artificial intelligence in government (2024) (Australia)
fr1_justif: The framework addresses environmental impact through the principle “Human societal and environmental wellbeing” which states that AI systems should benefit individuals society and the environment throughout their lifecycle (p. 13). It requires governments to define expected outcomes for people society and the environment and to assess likely impacts of AI use cases on people communities societal and environmental wellbeing to determine whether benefits outweigh risks (p. 13). It also refers more broadly to environmental wellbeing in the ministerial statement (p.1). Under contestability it also mentions hat When an AI system significantly impacts the environment there should be a timely process to allow people to contest it (p. 23).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Voluntary AI Safety Standard
fr1_justif: The document addresses fairness and non-discrimination by requiring organisations to ensure AI systems serve all people regardless of attributes such as gender race age or disability and by recognising that bias can lead to unfair disadvantage or unlawful discrimination (pp. 4 7). Under Guardrail 10 organisations must engage stakeholders including marginalised groups identify potential bias and minimise negative effects of unwanted bias in AI systems (pp. 42–44). It also requires organisations to evaluate impacts on diversity inclusion and fairness and to mitigate discriminatory outcomes through governance processes and impact assessments (pp. 26 42–44).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Voluntary AI Safety Standard
fr1_justif: The framework addresses gender equality through its emphasis on fairness inclusion and the mitigation of algorithmic bias. The document notes that AI systems can amplify discrimination against groups such as women and therefore requires organisations to identify and minimise unwanted bias (p.7). The standard also highlights that biased training data can produce discriminatory outcomes such as under-diagnosis or misdiagnosis of women (p.10) requiring risk assessments testing and oversight mechanisms to detect and mitigate such harms (Guardrails 2 pp.19–21).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Voluntary AI Safety Standard
fr1_justif: The framework addresses Human Oversight and Determination through governance and operational guardrails requiring human supervision of AI systems. Guardrail 5 requires organisations to “enable human control or intervention in an AI system to achieve meaningful human oversight across the lifecycle” (pp. 29–30). The standard also requires leadership accountability and governance structures for AI oversight (Guardrail 1 pp. 16–18). Additionally Guardrail 7 requires processes allowing people affected by AI systems to challenge decisions or outcomes (pp. 34-35) ensuring human review and contestability of AI-driven decisions.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National framework for the assurance of artificial intelligence in government (2024) (Australia)
fr1_justif: The framework embeds Impact Assessment processes across the AI lifecycle. It requires governments to assess impacts on people communities society and the environment (p.13 s.1.3) conduct human rights impact assessments (p.14) privacy impact assessments (p.18) and apply a risk-based assessment model throughout design deployment and operation (pp.8–9). It also outlines verification testing monitoring and evaluation obligations (pp.20–23).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr2_title: Checklist: Privacy Considerations when using commercially available AI products
fr2_justif: The checklist requires organisations deploying AI to assess compliance risks across key stages of the AI lifecycle. It directs entities to evaluate collection generation and inference of personal information (p.1) accuracy and oversight of AI outputs (p.2) transparency and accountability measures (p.2) and ongoing monitoring and assurance processes (p.2). These obligations collectively function as a structured privacy-focused impact assessment.
fr2_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems
fr1_title: Standard for AI Transparency Statements: Supporting the policy for responsible use of AI in government
fr1_justif: The document mandates agencies publish a publicly available AI transparency statement outlining their AI use (p.4 “Your responsibilities”) . It requires disclosure of AI intentions classifications public interaction without human intervention governance measures legislative compliance and protections against negative impacts (p.4). Agencies must publish statements on their public website and update them at least annually or when changes occur (p.4). Attachment A (pp.7–8) requires classification of AI usage patterns and domains.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National framework for the assurance of artificial intelligence in government (2024) (Australia)
fr1_justif: The framework includes a dedicated “Procurement” section (p. 10) that requires agencies to embed AI ethics transparency human oversight and risk-based assurance into procurement documentation and contracts. It emphasises due diligence vendor accountability lifecycle performance testing and preventhe prevention of opaque or high-risk systems ensuring the l and safe acquisition of AI across government.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Policy for the responsible use of AI in government (Australia)
fr1_justif: The policy recommends that APS agencies implement AI training for all staff within six months of commencement (p.12). It mandates competencies in AI literacy risk management responsible use and human oversight. Additionally specialized training is required for roles involved in the procurement development and deployment of AI systems. The document also integrates AI considerations into existing frameworks for privacy protective security record keeping and cyber-data management.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: The policy explicitly excludes defence and national intelligence activities from its scope. Under the “National security carveouts” section it states that the policy “does not apply to the use of AI in the defence portfolio” and does not apply to the “national intelligence community (NIC)” including agencies such as ASIO ASD ASIS and others (p.8). It further notes that Defence and NIC entities may only voluntarily adopt elements of the policy where appropriate.

fr1_title: AUSTRALIA’S AI ACTION PLAN
fr1_justif: The framework clearly addresses reskilling and upskilling of the existing workforce combining structured programs with on-the-job training. It frames this as part of a broader system-wide approach to skills development noting that “preparing our existing workforce… requires a system-wide approach… from schools… through to on-the-job training” (p. 14). It also points to programs and incentives aimed at strengthening digital and technological skills with AI identified as a central component (p. 5). More broadly the framework stresses that staying competitive in an AI-driven economy will require the workforce to “constantly retool and reskill” (p. 14).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Voluntary AI Safety Standard
fr1_justif: The framework promotes safe and secure AI deployment through ten guardrails addressing governance risk management testing and monitoring. Guardrail 2 requires organisations to implement risk and impact management processes to identify and mitigate harms from AI systems (pp. 19–21). Guardrail 3 requires data governance cybersecurity and system protection measures to manage risks related to data quality and vulnerabilities (pp. 22–24). Guardrail 4 requires testing AI models before deployment and monitoring system performance after deployment to detect unintended behaviour or harms (pp.25–28). These guardrails collectively support safe reliable and accountable AI systems.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Voluntary AI Safety Standard
fr1_justif: Guardrail 6 (pp. 31–33) requires organisations to ensure transparency and explainability including informing users when AI is used and explaining system purpose and limitations. Guardrail 7 (pp. 34–35) supports contestability of AI-enabled decisions. Guardrail 9 (pp. 39–41) requires documentation and record-keeping to evidence compliance. Together these guardrails enhance transparency accountability and public trust.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Strategy of the Austrian Federal Government: Artificial Intelligence Mission Austria 2030 (AIM AT 2020)
fr1_justif: AI literacy is addressed by building AI-related knowledge and skills through education and training. The strategy first frames the need for such literacy by defining AI and its societal relevance (pg. 11). It then embeds literacy-type goals within the AI ecosystem pillar where Qualification education and training are listed as key fields of action for strengthening capacities to develop and use AI (Section 4.4 pg 37).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: An interministerial working group chaired by the BMK and BMDW

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Bots fake accounts and automated conduct are mechanisms of disinformation campaigns (recital 85) and as such rules are needed to combat them (recital 9). Systemic risk assessments apply to large-scale online platforms and search engines (art. 34) and explicitly include the dissemination or amplification of misleading or fraudulent content and the negative effects on gender-based violence. This includes recommendation systems and other relevant algorithmic systems (art. 34). Mitigation measures include testing and adapting algorithmic and recommendation systems; speeding up the processing of notifications of illegal hate speech and cyber violence; and labeling content generated or manipulated by AI (art.35).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU GDPR
fr1_justif: The law provides a layered redress framework that applies in AI contexts whenever personal data is processed. Data subjects harmed by automated processing based on AI-driven profiling and decision-making hold the right to express their point of view and the right to contest the decision (art. 22); presenting complaints to supervisory authorities (art. 77) resorting to legal measures against gaps in the authorities (art. 78) and bringing proceedings directly against controllers or processors (art. 79). Organizations may be mandated to exercise the rights collectively on behalf of data subjects (art. 80). Supervisory authorities may impose administrative fines because of the violations of core provisions (art. 83).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: This Regulation shall not apply to the processing of personal data by Member States in the context of carrying out activities relating to the Union's common foreign and security policy (recital 16). The processing of personal data by Member States does not apply to activities related to the protection and prevention of threats to public security (Art. 2).
fr1_body_name: The law establishes the European Data Protection Board is comprised of representatives from the supervisory authority of each member states (Arts 68) acting independently (Art 69) providing guidelines recommendations and binding decisions to ensure consistence across Member States (Art 70) The Member State must to establish am independent supervisory authority responsible for monitoring and enforcing the regulation (Art 51)

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Online platform service providers must ensure the privacy security and protection of minors in their services (Art. 28) including prohibiting advertising based on profiling through the automated processing of data about children (Art. 28). Terms and conditions must be understandable to minors (Art. 14) ensuring a high level of privacy and security by default based on good practices (recitals 70 71). Large-scale online platforms and large-scale search engines systematic risk assessment applies periodically evaluating potential negative effects on minors (Art. 34). Mitigation measures are determined: age verification parental control tools abuse reporting tools and support for minors (Art. 35). Voluntary standards apply (Art. 44).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The law focuses on the risk classification system (Art. 6) and the requirements imposed on high-risk AI systems. It requires suppliers of high-risk AI systems to establish implement document and maintain a risk (and damage) management system throughout the system's lifecycle (Art. 9). Training validation and test datasets must conform to data governance in terms of representation error-freeness completeness contextualization and the function the system will be used for handling special personal data for monitoring purposes and detecting and correcting biases. High-risk AI systems must have consistency throughout the lifecycle by requiring appropriate levels of accuracy and robustness and reducing the risk of bias (art.15).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act addresses gender equality through non-discrimination and fundamental rights provisions. Training datasets for high-risk AI must be representative avoinding the discriminatory outcomes and protecting atributes explicitly related gender (Art. 10). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights impact assessments before deploying high-risk AI incluing gender-based discrimination (Art. 27). Equality bodies are granted market surveillance powers enabling them to investigate and sanction AI systems producing discriminatory outcomes (Art. 74).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act establishes requirements to ensure human control over high-risk AI systems requiring providers to design systems that enable natural persons with a minimum level of comprehensibility to monitor outputs detect anomalies and intervene or interrupt operations when necessary (art. 14). Deployers must assign oversight to competent natural persons with authority to act (art. 26). For general-purpose AI with systemic risk adversarial testing and incident reporting reinforce human control (Arts. 55 73). Additional oversight provisions are set in the fundamental rights impact assessment (art. 27) and Annex (IV) 2(e) as part of technical documentation requirements (art. 11).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Law establishes an assessment architecture according to the type and level of risk of the system. High-risk AI suppliers must identify analyze and mitigate risks during the design development and implementation phases within a risk management system (Arti.9) with comprehensive technical documentation (Art.15) and monitoring of operations and anomalies (Art. 26). For AI implementers in the public sector a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment applies before implementing high-risk systems listed in Annex 3 (Art.27). Compliance assessment must include self-assessment or third-party audits depending on the criticality of the system (Art. 43 47). There are ex-post assessments and post-commercialization monitoring (Art.72).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act classifies systems for recruitment promotion termination and worker management as high-risk AI systems (Annex III(4)) (art. 6). Prohibited AI practices include bans on emotion-recognition in the workplace (art. 5). Providers must implement risk-management addressing discrimination (Art. 9) ensure representative datasets and bias-control (art. 10) and maintain technical documentation (Arts. 11). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights assessments before deployment (art. 27). Art. 26 (7-9) establishes safeguards: workers must be informed and may request explanations of AI use. However the Act does not regulate pay conditions or compensation so it mitigates risks without constituting a full labour-rights regime.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Strategy Digital Skills Austria: Together into the Future
fr1_justif: A strategic measure addressing digital competencies and Artificial Intelligence commits to cross-ministerial measures that support Reskilling and Upskilling as AI spreads through the workplace. It foresees mapping ongoing initiatives across education administration and labour markets and developing expert recommendations (pg 61). Moreover this strategic objective is implemented through the government-funded “Digital Everywhere (Digital Überall)” program which provides basic digital competencies including AI with free nationwide workshops (pg 59).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: the Steering Group and its Secretariat (Page 19)

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Safety and security is core to the Act. It establishes a risk-based framework for risk identification and mitigation (Art. 9) oversight measures (Art. 14) and robustness and accuracy requirements (Art. 15). To ensure ongoing safety the Act mandates technical documentation and record-keeping (Arts. 11–12) and in certain high risk situations a conformity assessment procedure prior to market placement (Art. 43). Post-market obligations in Arts. 72–73 require providers and deployers to monitor performance report serious incidents and take corrective action. For GPAI models posing systemic risks Arts. 55–56 impose additional safety and cybersecurity duties including model evaluation and risk mitigation following the Code of Practice.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Articles 11 12 and 13 establish several transparency and documentation obligations for AI developers and deployers. High-risk AI systems must include clear information on their capabilities limitations and intended purpose (Art. 13) and providers must maintain comprehensive technical documentation and automatic logging to ensure traceability (Arts. 11–12). These measures enable deployers to better understand the systems and authorities to audit or investigate when necessary. There are disclosure obligations that require notification when individuals interact with AI systems particularly chatbots and emotion recognition tools (Art. 50). Also for general-purpose AI in terms of model documentation and summary of training data (Art. 53).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Strategy of the Republic of Azerbaijan for 2025–2028
fr1_justif: Article 6.1.3 is exclusively dedicated to improving AI literacy. It is aimed to expand current curriculum (from elementary school till graduation from high school) to cover AI specific skills improvement conducting educational campaigns to improve community awareness on how to work with AI cooperation with top 10 universities with the aim of inviting guest lecturers to teach on AI literacy. Moreover AI Academy has been established by the government. All programs at the new AI Academy are fully funded by scholarships which lowers barriers for talented students from any socio-economic background to pursue advanced AI training including AI literacy.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Education Ministry of Economy State Service for Special Communications and Information Security Ministry of Labor and Social Protection of Population

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Strategy of the Republic of Azerbaijan for 2025–2028
fr1_justif: In the introduction of the document it is indicated that the development of artificial intelligence solutions in Azerbaijani language will pave the way for significant progress in this area. Moreover provision 6.1.2 is set to be the second priority area in the list aiming to improve data management and infrastructure. As part of this priority area developing NLP technologies in the Azerbaijani language and strengthening the position of the language in the global digital space is indicated as a step to be taken. In 8.2.2 it is planned to establish a database in Azerbaijani language development of NLP models with high-precision in Azerbaijani language and execution as a pilot program in 5 government services the results.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

Besides section 2 'Global Trends' (pp.3-4) identifies several challenges currently observed in global practice regarding artificial intelligence systems. fr1_title: Proposed Law on the Regulation of Artificial Intelligence Technologies and their Uses (Bahrain)
fr1_justif: The proposed law does not provide detailed provisions regarding AI literacy and mentions it only in passing. In Article 4 (National Strategy)—found on page 7 of the original document and page 5 of the translated version—the law highlights 8 initiatives through which the Council of Ministers shall establish a comprehensive strategy to support AI  technologies systems and applications.The last initiative listed is: “Including various scientific materials on artificial intelligence within academic curricula.” However the law does not specify the level of education at which this should be implemented or how Artificial Intelligence should be integrated into the education system and does not use the term ' AI Literacy'.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: One page 7 under Article 3  the text reads “ The following are exempted: operations related to national security carried out by the Ministry of Defense the Ministry of Interior the National Guard the National Security Agency or other security agencies and government institutions” indicating the explicit exclusion of defense and security from the scope of this proposed law.

fr1_title: Proposed Law on the Regulation of Artificial Intelligence Technologies and their Uses (Bahrain)
fr1_justif: Under Chapter 2: Responsibilities and Prohibitions Article 14: Prohibited Uses (page 12 of the original document and page 8 of the translated version) the proposed law outlines the circumstances under which the use of AI is prohibited in the country. The text states:1.Causing strife disturbances sabotage or calling for terrorist acts or attempting to overthrow or change the political system.2.Harming others or their data or engaging in deception manipulation or misleading practices.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: One page 7 under Article 3  the text reads “ The following are exempted: operations related to national security carried out by the Ministry of Defense the Ministry of Interior the National Guard the National Security Agency or other security agencies and government institutions” indicating the explicit exclusion of defense and security from the scope of this proposed law.

fr1_title: Proposed Law on the Regulation of Artificial Intelligence Technologies and their Uses (Bahrain)
fr1_justif: Chapter 5 of the proposed law — found on page 15 of the original document and page 9 of the translated version — is dedicated to what the law refers to as “Civil Liberty.” This chapter consists of two articles: Compensation Claims and Robot Owner Liability.The text states:“Anyone who suffers damage arising from programming processing or development activities has the right to demand compensation from the programmer processor or developer as the case may be even if multiple parties are involved for damages.”This article clearly establishes the right to access remedy and redress for harm caused by artificial intelligence in Bahrain.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: The Guiding Manual on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence Use in Member States of the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) (Bahrain)
fr1_justif: On page 5 of the Guidelines the document lists “Environmental Protection and Promotion of Sustainability” as the third of its four core values. The text stresses that ' GCC countries should  consider the environmental risks resulting from the operation and training of AI systems using electricity such as carbon emissions or the use of fresh water for cooling. Measures should be taken to ensure that ecosystems are not degraded and sustainability is maintained. Bahrain has expressed its commitment to this document and listed it on its e-government website as a guiding reference for its AI strategy: https://web.archive.org/web/20251013185716/https://www.bahrain.bh/wps/portal/en/BNP/ExploreBahrain/ArtificialIntelligence?utm_source=chatgpt.com
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: The document does not address issues related to defense or security anywhere in its guidelines. On page 7 under Principle Two: Safety and Harm Prevention it explicitly mentions protection from AI-related attacks but only in the context of safety and harm prevention—not defense or national security.”

fr1_justif: On page 6 the policy lists “Fairness Equity and Non-Discrimination” as the third of the fundamental principles on which it is based. The text reads: ( This principle emphasizes that artificial intelligence technologies must be fair ensuring equity for all . without any discrimination based on race religion gender or any other non-objective factors in order to avoid unjust biases in outcomes). The text explicitly emphasizes Fairness and Non-Discrimination.

fr1_title: General Policy for the Use of Artificial Intelligence (May 2025) (Bahrain)
fr1_justif: The policy outlines 11 fundamental principles for the use of AI the first of which is Human Decision-Making found on page 5. This principle is based on the idea that artificial intelligence technologies serve as tools to assist humans in decision-making. The policy emphasizes the importance of human oversight over AI stating that ‘the final decision in important matters must remain under human control especially in cases related to individual and societal rights.’” Furhtermore the second pillar of the policy ( in page 10) also addresses Human Oversight over AI  it states 'Specific standards must be followed with supervision and accountability assigned to the specialized staff responsible for overseeing these technologies '.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: General Policy for the Use of Artificial Intelligence (May 2025) (Bahrain)
fr1_justif: The fifth principle states that there should also be comprehensive awareness of the impacts of using these technologies (AI) and the associated social responsibility (p. 6). Under the third pillar the document also advocates for education and awareness about artificial intelligence explicitly focusing on improving understanding of its applications and impacts on the workplace and society (p. 10). Finally although more broadly the document notes the importance of  accurate risk assessment to ensure safety (p. 10).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_justif: Public Sector Skills Development in the context of AI were addressed in this policy in 2 different contexts. First it was addressed within the third main pillar found on page 10 titled “Awareness and Education.” The text states: “Government entities aim to raise employee awareness about artificial intelligence focusing on improving understanding of its applications and impacts on the workplace and society”. This text emphasizes the concept of “reskilling” in this policy. Furthermore under the same section on page 11 the policy highlights the objective of enhancing employees’ technical capabilities to ensure the effective and safe use of AI in government operations emphasizing the concept of “upskilling” for government employees
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: General Policy for the Use of Artificial Intelligence (May 2025) (Bahrain)
fr1_justif: The document addresses reskilling and upskilling in the context of AI through capacity-building measures for public sector employees. Under the third pillar on awareness and education government entities are tasked with educating employees about artificial intelligence technologies and their benefits through training programs and workshops related to AI use in government (p. 5). In Section 6.3.2 (Rules and Requirements) entities are required to provide training and continuously develop employees’ technical skills to understand and apply AI (p. 11). Sections 6.4.1 (Objectives) and 6.4.2 (Rules and Requirements) further promote partnerships to develop national AI talent and encourage participation in conferences and workshops (p. 11).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: General Policy for the Use of Artificial Intelligence (May 2025) (Bahrain)
fr1_justif: The Eighth fundamental principle of this policy highlighted on page 6 is Reliability and Safety. The text states:“This principle relates to ensuring the reliability and safety of artificial intelligence systems and software by designing and operating them in a way that guarantees accurate and consistent results with necessary measures taken to protect them from manipulation or breaches. It also aims to build trust in the use of AI and ensure it complies with ethical and security standards creating a positive impact while reducing potential risks.”This clearly indicates that the policy recognizes and understands the importance of taking safety measures when deploying Artificial Intelligence models.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: ICT Governance Committee (ICTGC) and Information & eGovernment Authority (iGA)

fr1_title: General Policy for the Use of Artificial Intelligence (May 2025) (Bahrain)
fr1_justif: The fourth fundamental principle of this policy highlighted on page 6 is Transparency and Explainability. The text states:“This principle emphasizes the importance of ensuring that processes carried out by artificial intelligence systems are clear and transparent such that they are explainable and understandable by users and specialists. This helps ensure informed decisions based on full knowledge of how these systems operate.”This clearly indicates that the policy recognizes and understands the importance of this principle.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: ICT Governance Committee (ICTGC) and Information & eGovernment Authority (iGA)

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy 2024 – DRAFT (Bangladesh)
fr1_justif: The National Artificial Intelligence Policy directly embeds AI literacy within formal education. Section 5.4 (p.12) mandates that AI be integrated into academic curricula and specialized programs and calls for AI research centers in all universities and an AI research hub under the University Grants Commission. Section 4.8 (p.9) promotes AI skills development AI/data clubs and tailored workforce training linking AI literacy to education and employment.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Cyber Security Ordinance 2025 (Bangladesh)
fr1_justif: Article 25 of the Cyber Security Ordinance 2025 (page 16)  criminalizes the intentional or knowing creation storage sending publishing or broadcasting—or threats thereof—of harmful or intimidating content including blackmail materials content for sexual harassment revenge porn child sexual abuse material or sextortion. The provision specifically includes content created or edited by artificial intelligence in formats such as data videos audiovisual content images or graphics holding individuals accountable for AI-enabled digital harm.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Cyber Security Ordinance 2025 (Bangladesh)
fr1_justif: Children have legal protection from sexual abuse (including AI-generated abuse) sextortion and revenge pornography. As per the Section 25 of the ordinance contents involving children (visual audio or written) even if 'simulated' or AI-generated is subject to criminalisation. (Reference: Section 2(q) on pages 03 and 04 and section 25 on page 16)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy 2024 – DRAFT (Bangladesh)
fr1_justif: Although the document doesn't deeply elaborate on sustainability it does touch on environmental impact. On page 5 under AI implementation principles it highlights the promotion of a circular economy and the development of AI technologies that minimize carbon footprints and promote green practices. Additionally in page 7 in the section 4.4 on environment energy and climate change the document notes that AI systems will support sustainable practices and optimize renewable energy production ultimately reducing reliance on fossil fuels.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy 2024 – DRAFT (Bangladesh)
fr1_justif: In the National AI Draft Policy 2024 on page 5 three key principles are highlighted: first Social Equity Equality and Fairness; second Safety Security and Robustness; and third Human-Centered AI. It also emphasizes partnership and collaboration. Then on page 14 the policy discusses challenges and mitigation approaches for AI risks and trustworthiness. It states that AI systems must prevent unintended bias prejudice and discrimination by design. The policy also includes risk management frameworks and privacy considerations.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy 2024 – DRAFT (Bangladesh)
fr1_justif: The national AI policy emphasizes human oversight and decision-making. On page 6 section 3.6 it states that AI must uphold the rule of law human rights and dignity ensuring human intervention and oversight where needed. On page 14 the policy highlights that AI systems should support human decision-making by incorporating fundamental rights and human agency. Furthermore section 6.2 on risk management stresses the importance of diverse inclusive assessment teams and ongoing monitoring to identify and mitigate new risks. Thus the policy ensures that human oversight remains central to AI implementation.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy 2024 – DRAFT (Bangladesh)
fr1_justif: On page 5 under the Key Principles it emphasizes Safety Security and Robustness requiring AI systems to be safe ethical secure from malicious use robust against errors and biases and subject to continuous risk assessment when procuring technologies across providers. Further on page 13 under the Implementation Plan for Private Institutions Businesses and Industries it states that Responsible AI practices shall be adopted for public-sector procurement of AI systems and high-risk AI adoption. On page 14 under the compulsory Risk Management Framework it mandates risk assessment during procurement.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy 2024 – DRAFT (Bangladesh)
fr1_justif: The Draft National AI Policy directly addresses AI reskilling and upskilling through workforce development objectives and implementation measures. Section 2.2.4 (p.5) commits to nurturing a skilled workforce by embedding AI in education and skills development. Section 4.8 (p.9) outlines AI-enhanced education tailored training to address the AI skills gap (4.8.4) and national AI/data clubs (4.8.3). Section 5.3.7 (p.12) mandates capacity-building programs to enhance AI literacy and skills.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy 2024 – DRAFT (Bangladesh)
fr1_justif: In the national AI policy draft safety and security are emphasized in multiple sections. On page five under the key principles it states that AI systems must be safe ethical and secure from malicious use with ongoing risk assessments. On page fourteen in the challenges and mitigation approach it highlights that AI systems should incorporate safety by design ensuring accuracy reliability and reproducibility. Then on page fifteen under data security and cybersecurity the policy highlights the need of measures to strengthen system integrity perform rigorous assessments and establish governance procedures for fallback and recovery.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy 2024 – DRAFT (Bangladesh)
fr1_justif: The National AI Policy draft emphasizes transparency and explainability as core principles. On page 5 it states that transparent data collection storage and usage are crucial for making AI decision-making explainable and accountable. On page 6 (Section- 4.2.3)  the draft highlights that AI platforms will ensure a secure open data ecosystem strengthening public-private data linkages. Additionally on page 14 (Section 6.1.5 and 6.1.6)  it underscores the importance of standardized documentation for datasets and algorithms ensuring traceability and transparency. It also requires clear methods for algorithm auditability including impact reports and redressal mechanisms.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Methodological Recommendations on the Use of Artificial Intelligence Technologies in the Educational Process of General Secondary Education Institutions
fr1_justif: The Methodological Recommendations give teachers detailed guidance on how to use AI technologies responsibly in general secondary education. They describe principles of ethical and safe AI use risks opportunities and procedures for interacting with AI tools (§“AI in Education: Principles…”) and provide subject-specific examples (§“Practice of Application…”). They promote competencies such as critical analysis of AI outputs understanding algorithms prompt design fact-checking.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.
