fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Bots fake accounts and automated conduct are mechanisms of disinformation campaigns (recital 85) and as such rules are needed to combat them (recital 9). Systemic risk assessments apply to large-scale online platforms and search engines (art. 34) and explicitly include the dissemination or amplification of misleading or fraudulent content and the negative effects on gender-based violence. This includes recommendation systems and other relevant algorithmic systems (art. 34). Mitigation measures include testing and adapting algorithmic and recommendation systems; speeding up the processing of notifications of illegal hate speech and cyber violence; and labeling content generated or manipulated by AI (art.35).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU GDPR
fr1_justif: The law provides a layered redress framework that applies in AI contexts whenever personal data is processed. Data subjects harmed by automated processing based on AI-driven profiling and decision-making hold the right to express their point of view and the right to contest the decision (art. 22); presenting complaints to supervisory authorities (art. 77) resorting to legal measures against gaps in the authorities (art. 78) and bringing proceedings directly against controllers or processors (art. 79). Organizations may be mandated to exercise the rights collectively on behalf of data subjects (art. 80). Supervisory authorities may impose administrative fines because of the violations of core provisions (art. 83).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: This Regulation shall not apply to the processing of personal data by Member States in the context of carrying out activities relating to the Union's common foreign and security policy (recital 16). The processing of personal data by Member States does not apply to activities related to the protection and prevention of threats to public security (Art. 2).
fr1_body_name: The law establishes the European Data Protection Board is comprised of representatives from the supervisory authority of each member states (Arts 68) acting independently (Art 69) providing guidelines recommendations and binding decisions to ensure consistence across Member States (Art 70) The Member State must to establish am independent supervisory authority responsible for monitoring and enforcing the regulation (Art 51)

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Online platform service providers must ensure the privacy security and protection of minors in their services (Art. 28) including prohibiting advertising based on profiling through the automated processing of data about children (Art. 28). Terms and conditions must be understandable to minors (Art. 14) ensuring a high level of privacy and security by default based on good practices (recitals 70 71). Large-scale online platforms and large-scale search engines systematic risk assessment applies periodically evaluating potential negative effects on minors (Art. 34). Mitigation measures are determined: age verification parental control tools abuse reporting tools and support for minors (Art. 35). Voluntary standards apply (Art. 44).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: AI Stakeholders in Belgium: Steering Commitee for the National Convergence Plan for the Development of Artificial Intelligence (Belgium)
fr1_justif: Objective 7 – Protect the Environment (pp. 25–27) addresses AI’s environmental impact by acknowledging that AI is energy-intensive and that servers may consume significant electricity (p. 25–26). It notes the need to reduce AI’s intrinsic energy impact while leveraging its benefits for sustainability. The “Lines of Action” include ensuring that AI contributes to a circular economy and in general that AI use does not increase the ecological footprint (p. 27).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The law focuses on the risk classification system (Art. 6) and the requirements imposed on high-risk AI systems. It requires suppliers of high-risk AI systems to establish implement document and maintain a risk (and damage) management system throughout the system's lifecycle (Art. 9). Training validation and test datasets must conform to data governance in terms of representation error-freeness completeness contextualization and the function the system will be used for handling special personal data for monitoring purposes and detecting and correcting biases. High-risk AI systems must have consistency throughout the lifecycle by requiring appropriate levels of accuracy and robustness and reducing the risk of bias (art.15).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act addresses gender equality through non-discrimination and fundamental rights provisions. Training datasets for high-risk AI must be representative avoinding the discriminatory outcomes and protecting atributes explicitly related gender (Art. 10). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights impact assessments before deploying high-risk AI incluing gender-based discrimination (Art. 27). Equality bodies are granted market surveillance powers enabling them to investigate and sanction AI systems producing discriminatory outcomes (Art. 74).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act establishes requirements to ensure human control over high-risk AI systems requiring providers to design systems that enable natural persons with a minimum level of comprehensibility to monitor outputs detect anomalies and intervene or interrupt operations when necessary (art. 14). Deployers must assign oversight to competent natural persons with authority to act (art. 26). For general-purpose AI with systemic risk adversarial testing and incident reporting reinforce human control (Arts. 55 73). Additional oversight provisions are set in the fundamental rights impact assessment (art. 27) and Annex (IV) 2(e) as part of technical documentation requirements (art. 11).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Law establishes an assessment architecture according to the type and level of risk of the system. High-risk AI suppliers must identify analyze and mitigate risks during the design development and implementation phases within a risk management system (Arti.9) with comprehensive technical documentation (Art.15) and monitoring of operations and anomalies (Art. 26). For AI implementers in the public sector a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment applies before implementing high-risk systems listed in Annex 3 (Art.27). Compliance assessment must include self-assessment or third-party audits depending on the criticality of the system (Art. 43 47). There are ex-post assessments and post-commercialization monitoring (Art.72).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act classifies systems for recruitment promotion termination and worker management as high-risk AI systems (Annex III(4)) (art. 6). Prohibited AI practices include bans on emotion-recognition in the workplace (art. 5). Providers must implement risk-management addressing discrimination (Art. 9) ensure representative datasets and bias-control (art. 10) and maintain technical documentation (Arts. 11). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights assessments before deployment (art. 27). Art. 26 (7-9) establishes safeguards: workers must be informed and may request explanations of AI use. However the Act does not regulate pay conditions or compensation so it mitigates risks without constituting a full labour-rights regime.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: AI Stakeholders in Belgium: Steering Commitee for the National Convergence Plan for the Development of Artificial Intelligence (Belgium)
fr1_justif: Objective 9 (pp.33–34) explicitly mandates developing the right AI skills within public services focusing on talent management and competencies adapted to AI’s disruptive impact. The Plan also creates a broader framework for upskilling reskilling and AI basic skills development (p.29). Implementation is supported through governance structures led by the Federal Public Service Policy and Support (FPS BOSA) and the AI4Belgium programme (pp.35–38) which coordinate expertise monitoring and knowledge-sharing across government.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: AI Stakeholders in Belgium: Steering Commitee for the National Convergence Plan for the Development of Artificial Intelligence (Belgium)
fr1_justif: Objective 8 providing better and continuous training (p. 28) focuses on developing new skills fostering the emergence of talent and attracting potential top collaborators. Bringing together all stakeholders in the learning process and the relevant authorities is essential for developing an ambitious long-term plan. Thus training is therefore an essential part of the strategy. It covers both technical and non-technical aspects (p. 29). The actions are: creating a framework for retraining upskilling and developing basic AI skills to change or improve people's general capabilities; a cycle of workshops; stimulating the lifelong learning market; strengthening continuous learning programs creating an AI profile platform (p.30).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Safety and security is core to the Act. It establishes a risk-based framework for risk identification and mitigation (Art. 9) oversight measures (Art. 14) and robustness and accuracy requirements (Art. 15). To ensure ongoing safety the Act mandates technical documentation and record-keeping (Arts. 11–12) and in certain high risk situations a conformity assessment procedure prior to market placement (Art. 43). Post-market obligations in Arts. 72–73 require providers and deployers to monitor performance report serious incidents and take corrective action. For GPAI models posing systemic risks Arts. 55–56 impose additional safety and cybersecurity duties including model evaluation and risk mitigation following the Code of Practice.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Articles 11 12 and 13 establish several transparency and documentation obligations for AI developers and deployers. High-risk AI systems must include clear information on their capabilities limitations and intended purpose (Art. 13) and providers must maintain comprehensive technical documentation and automatic logging to ensure traceability (Arts. 11–12). These measures enable deployers to better understand the systems and authorities to audit or investigate when necessary. There are disclosure obligations that require notification when individuals interact with AI systems particularly chatbots and emotion recognition tools (Art. 50). Also for general-purpose AI in terms of model documentation and summary of training data (Art. 53).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Data Protection Act 2021 (Belize)
fr1_justif: It establishes that decisions with legal or similarly significant effects should not be made without human involvement.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Page 379 (No. 31 -32) state that Parts II III IV VII and section 77 shall not apply where the processing of personal data is required for the purpose of safeguarding national security.

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: National Strategy for Artificial Intelligence and Big Data 2023-2027
fr1_justif: The Strategy treats AI literacy as part of Strategic Objective 2 and explicitly calls for educating all actors (public private civil society and end users) about digital tools and AI (pp.17–18). It operationalises this through Program 2 in the Action Plan (p.23) and two key measures which includes integrating AI learning into the education system (basic knowledge programming and ongoing training). It also states on p. 31 the need to udpate some existing curricula (from basic education to continuing education) so as toadd subjects relating to AI and big data management.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Digital Affairs and Digitalisation (lead coordinating body) supported by the Steering Committee Technical Committee and Program Support Units as set out under the Implementation Mechanism (Chapter 41 pp4243)

fr1_title: Law No. 2017-20 establishing the Digital Code in the Republic of Benin
fr1_justif: The Digital Code’s personal data protections create redress routes relevant where AI is used via automated decision-making (including profiling). Controllers must inform people about automated decision-making and the right to lodge a complaint with the Authority (Arts. 415-416 pp. 130-131). Affected persons can lodge a complaint with the Authority receive updates and then pursue judicial recourse (Arts. 448-450 p. 144). It also provides effective remedy before the competent administrative court if the Authority fails to act within 90 days plus compensation and liability for material/moral damage (Art. 451 p. 144).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Under the Digital Code (Law No. 2017-20) the personal data protection regime explicitly carves out defence national security and public safety processing from its general scope.Book V provides that processing relating to public safety defence the investigation and prosecution of criminal offences and the essential interests of the State is subject to special derogations laid down by other laws rather than the ordinary data protection rules (Book V Scope of application p.114–115).Accordingly defence and security activities are explicitly excluded or treated under special regimes not fully subject to the standard transparency and explainability obligations.

fr1_title: National Strategy for Artificial Intelligence and Big Data 2023-2027
fr1_justif: The Strategy 2023-2027 addresses linguistic diversity under Action 1.2 – Implementation of AI solutions. In the Education sector (p.55) it promotes natural language processing and speech recognition to translate local languages into French and facilitate inclusive digital learning. While not a comprehensive cultural inclusion framework these measures constitute a strategic recognition of linguistic diversity within AI projects emphasizing local language accessibility and the involvement of national actors in solution development
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Digital Affairs and Digitalisation (lead coordinating body) supported by the Steering Committee Technical Committee and Program Support Units as set out under the Implementation Mechanism (Chapter 41 pp4243)

fr1_title: National Strategy for Artificial Intelligence and Big Data 2023-2027
fr1_justif: The Strategy addresses the indicator at a high level by emphasizing the importance of designing and implementing AI systems with due regard to overarching principles explicitly referencing the planet prosperity and sustainability (p. 35).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Digital Affairs and Digitalisation (lead coordinating body) supported by the Steering Committee Technical Committee and Program Support Units as set out under the Implementation Mechanism (Chapter 41 pp4243)

fr1_title: Law No. 2017-20 establishing the Digital Code in the Republic of Benin
fr1_justif: The Digital Code is a comprehensive and binding document that governs all digital-related activities in the Republic of Benin. Although it does not specifically address artificial intelligence it provides a general framework for digital technologies and prohibits any form of discrimination or unfair treatment. Its provisions on fairness transparency (Art. 17) and personal data protection impose non-discrimination obligations in automated processing (Art. 19 - 20). Articles 149 and 150 also enshrine these principles in Benin's digital action within the Digital Code. (See page 53 - Section II)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: According to Article 4 state infrastructure dedicated to public safety or national defense as well as the use of frequencies allocated directly to these administrations do not fall within its scope. These facilities are governed by a specific decree of the Council of Ministers. Furthermore terrestrial broadcasting companies (radio and television) are also excluded with regard to their production and broadcasting activities. Thus the Code establishes a clear framework while recognizing that certain sensitive and strategic functions of the State require separate regulation to guarantee national security and sovereignty.
fr1_body_name: Personal Data Protection Authority (APDP) Regulatory Authority for Electronic Communications and Postal Services (ARCEP-Benin) Beninese Agency for Universal Electronic Communications and Postal Services (ABSU-CEP)

fr1_title: Law No. 2017-20 establishing the Digital Code in the Republic of Benin
fr1_justif: The Digital Code addresses Human Oversight by curbing reliance on solely automated decisions and requiring safeguards that enable human determination. In Book V Art. 401 states that no judicial decision assessing a person’s behaviour may be based on automated processing including profiling so a human decision-maker must remain decisive (p.125). The Code also requires transparency where automated decision-making or profiling is used including “meaningful information” on the logic significance and expected consequences (Arts. 415 - 416 pp.130 - 131). It further defines automated processing as exploitation “without direct human intervention” (p.15).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: According to Article 4 state infrastructure dedicated to public safety or national defense as well as the use of frequencies allocated directly to these administrations do not fall within its scope. These facilities are governed by a specific decree of the Council of Ministers. Furthermore terrestrial broadcasting companies (radio and television) are also excluded with regard to their production and broadcasting activities. Thus the Code establishes a clear framework while recognizing that certain sensitive and strategic functions of the State require separate regulation to guarantee national security and sovereignty.
fr1_body_name: Personal Data Protection Authority (APDP) Regulatory Authority for Electronic Communications and Postal Services (ARCEP-Benin) Beninese Agency for Universal Electronic Communications and Postal Services (ABSU-CEP)

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: National Strategy for Artificial Intelligence and Big Data 2023-2027
fr1_justif: The strategy tackles public-sector AI skills via Strategic Objective 2 (“Strengthen human capabilities in AI and big data management”) which flags insufficient trained resources and calls for training and awareness for stakeholders including public actors (pp.17–19). It creates Programme 2 (“Strengthening the human capacities of stakeholders in AI professions and jobs”) (pp.21–23) and sets actions: integrate programming/continuing education (Action 2.1 p.31) deploy AI capacity-building for public and private stakeholders and users (Action 2.2 pp.31 & 25) and big-data capacity-building (Action 2.3 p.32).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Digital Affairs and Digitalisation (lead coordinating body) supported by the Steering Committee Technical Committee and Program Support Units as set out under the Implementation Mechanism (Chapter 41 pp4243)

fr1_title: National Strategy for Artificial Intelligence and Big Data 2023-2027
fr1_justif: The Strategy treats reskilling/upskilling as a core objective: Strategic Objective 2 is to “strengthen human capabilities” in AI and big data management (Section 1.4; pp.16–18). It creates Program 2: Strengthening human capabilities of actors in AI professions and positions (Section 2.1–2.2; pp.21–23) with actions to integrate AI training into the education system (basic programming and ongoing courses) and roll out capacity-development programmes for AI for public/private actors and end users (Action 2.1–2.2; pp.31; plan of action p.23).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Digital Affairs and Digitalisation (lead coordinating body) supported by the Steering Committee Technical Committee and Program Support Units as set out under the Implementation Mechanism (Chapter 41 pp4243)

fr1_title: Law No. 2017-20 establishing the Digital Code in the Republic of Benin
fr1_justif: While the law primarily regulates general data processing Article 415(13) explicitly imposes an obligation to inform the data subject about the existence of automated decision-making including profiling as referenced in Article 401. In such cases the controller must provide meaningful information about the logic involved as well as the significance and the envisaged consequences of the processing for the data subject.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Under the Digital Code (Law No. 2017-20) the personal data protection regime explicitly carves out defence national security and public safety processing from its general scope.Book V provides that processing relating to public safety defence the investigation and prosecution of criminal offences and the essential interests of the State is subject to special derogations laid down by other laws rather than the ordinary data protection rules (Book V Scope of application p.114–115).Accordingly defence and security activities are explicitly excluded or treated under special regimes not fully subject to the standard transparency and explainability obligations.
fr1_body_name: Personal Data Protection Authority (APDP) This is the most applicable body for the Transparency and Explainability indicator Under the Digital Code (Book V) the APDP is mandated to supervise and enforce compliance with personal data protection obligations including transparency duties information to data subjects and safeguards relating to automated processing and profiling These functions directly underpin explainability and transparency in AI-enabled data processing- Electronic Communications and Postal Regulatory Authority (ARCEP Benin)- Beninese Universal Service Agency for Electronic Communications and Postal Services (ABSU-CEP)

fr1_title: Child Online Protection (COP) Guidelines for Policy-Makers (Localized for Bhutan 2023)
fr1_justif: The Guidelines address children’s rights by recognizing AI-driven risks to their development privacy and wellbeing. Algorithmically curated content and inappropriate material are discussed (Quick References pp. 9–11) highlighting threats to autonomy and participation. Children’s right to privacy and data protection is emphasized across age groups (pp. 9–11). Crucially the General Guidelines for Industry (Quick References p. 14) explicitly mandate identifying and mitigating adverse impacts on children’s rights requiring safety- and privacy-by-design approaches including privacy-preserving AI.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: National Data Governance Framework (NDGF) (2025)(Bhutan)
fr1_justif: PILLAR THREE (Data Security & Protection page 14): Mandates minimum data security requirements including encryption of data 'in use' by AI systems access control mechanisms multi-factor authentication security protocols aligned with international standards and data breach detection/notification within 72 hours—directly applicable to AI system operations. Section 2.3.2(k) explicitly requires: 'Ensure AI systems align with legal and user safety standards in their data usage'—binding safety requirement for AI. PILLAR FIVE (Critical Information Infrastructure Protection page 16): Establishes cybersecurity framework protecting government data infrastructure
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: (Draft Law) General Law on Artifiical Intelligence (PL 558/2024-2025) (Chamber of Deputies) (Bolivia)
fr1_justif: Art. 8.1 mandates responsible AI development for social well-being while Art. 10(c) requires developers to establish internal oversight and error-correction mechanisms. Arts. 14–15 enforce risk assessment and mitigation especially for high-risk systems. Crucially Arts. 22–24 establish the Supervisory and Enforcement Authority responsible for monitoring inspection and compliance. These provisions address human oversight through institutional supervision and accountability mechanisms. However the framework does not explicitly mandate human-in-the-loop systems or a specific right to human review of automated decisions.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Bill for the Promotion Management and Use of Artificial Intelligence (Chamber of Deputies) (Bolivia)
fr1_justif: The document directly addresses Impact Assessments through mandatory obligations to prevent mitigate and address risks associated with AI systems. Articles 10 11 12 13 and 14 establish risk classification and mitigation measures. Article 15 establishes mandatory impact assessments for AI systems used by both public and private entities
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems
fr1_title: (Draft Law) General Law on Artifiical Intelligence (PL 558/2024-2025) (Chamber of Deputies) (Bolivia)
fr1_justif: Article 26 establishes a public registry for AI systems requiring providers to disclose their objectives algorithmic logic and personal data utilized. Critically it mandates that developers and users as responsible parties keep this information updated whenever changes occur in technical characteristics or data processing. Furthermore Article 27 empowers the Artificial Intelligence Supervision and Oversight Authority (ASFIA) to monitor these systems. ASFIA is authorized to demand information from both public and private sectors to ensure a robust mechanism for risk mitigation and ongoing compliance.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: (Draft Law) General Law on Artifiical Intelligence (PL 558/2024-2025) (Chamber of Deputies) (Bolivia)
fr1_justif: Art. 5.1 mandates an ethical framework for AI based on dignity transparency and safety. Art. 15.2 promotes inter-institutional cooperation for ethical and operational testing. Art. 18 establishes the right to access and rectify personal data within AI systems. Art. 21 promotes digital literacy and AI human rights awareness to ensure informed decision-making. Finally Art. 26 creates a Mandatory Public Registry for high-risk AI. Developers must disclose system objectives algorithmic characteristics and data classifications with a strict obligation to keep this information updated whenever technical or data processing changes occur (Art. 26.3).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Data Protection Act 2024 (Act No. 18 of 2024) (Botswana)
fr1_link: n/a (although the document has been gazetted i found only the previous 2018 version online)
fr1_justif: The Botswana Data Protection Act 2024 addresses redress in AI-related contexts though it does not explicitly mention AI. Its provisions apply to automated and algorithmic processing. Section 49 regulates automated decision-making granting data subjects the right to human intervention and to contest automated decisions. Section 38(7)(b) requires controllers to inform data subjects of their right to complain and seek judicial remedy. Section 80 enables complaints for contraventions and Section 72 requires redress mechanisms in binding corporate rules.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Data Protection Act 2024 (Act No. 18 of 2024) (Botswana)
fr1_link: n/a (not found/documented on the internet yet)
fr1_justif: The Botswana Data Protection Act (2024) addresses fairness and non-discrimination in the context of AI through provisions governing automated decision-making and personal data processing.Section 19 establishes a core principle that personal data must be processed “lawfully fairly and transparently” which applies to AI systems that process personal data. Section 49 provides a key safeguard by granting data subjects the right not to be subject to decisions based solely on automated processing or profiling that produce legal or similarly significant effects and requires measures such as human intervention and the right to contest decisions. Section 37 complements this by imposing transparency obligations requiring information
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Data Protection Act 2024 (Act No. 18 of 2024) (Botswana)
fr1_justif: The Botswana Data Protection Bill/Act 2024 explicitly tackles human oversight and determination in the context of automated decision-making (often relevant for AI systems) in Section 49. It gives data subjects the right not to be subject to decisions based solely on automated processing including profiling. Where such processing is permitted (e.g. with explicit consent or for contracts) the controller must implement suitable safeguards which include the right to obtain human intervention to express one’s point of view and to contest the decision.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: AI for the Good of All - Brazilian Artificial Intelligence Plan
fr1_justif: Axis 2 Dissemination Training and Qualification (p. 54-58) provides for AI training at all levels developing talent and meeting the demand for qualified professionals. This includes comprehensive digital literacy and AI outreach initiatives aiming to popularize technological fundamentals transparency everyday applications risks and citizens' rights. This includes partnerships with universities and technology companies for workshops mentoring and information campaigns. The creation of Educator Training Laboratories in digital literacy and the pedagogical use of AI will also be included. Undergraduate courses in AI and related fields will be created. Competitive scholarships for doctoral programs will be offered.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Ten Principles for Regulating Social Networks (Brazil)
fr1_justif: The Brazilian Internet Steering Committee established principles for regulating social networks which include (4) protecting the right to information and promoting the accuracy consistency and reliability of content processes and information systems and (2) combating incitement to violence hate speech and all forms of discrimination on social networks. The principles include defining social media platforms as digital technological infrastructure that intensively uses data Artificial Intelligence technologies and network effects. Social networks are a type of digital platform service qualified by the action of automated mechanisms (machine learning algorithms and advanced Artificial Intelligence models).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Bill No. 2338 (Brazil)
fr1_justif: The LGPD addresses access to redress and remedy in the context of AI particularly regarding automated decision-making. Article 20 grants individuals the right to request a review of decisions based solely on automated processing ensuring a pathway to contest such outcomes. Article 18 provides complementary rights (e.g. access correction deletion objection) enabling individuals to challenge unlawful data use. Additionally Article 18(§1) allows complaints to the national authority (ANPD) and Article 22 ensures the possibility of judicial remedies. Together these provisions establish mechanisms for redress including administrative and judicial avenues.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Article 4(III) of the LGPD explicitly excludes data processing carried out for public security national defense state security and criminal investigations from its scope. It further clarifies that such activities must be governed by separate specific legislation (§1) confirming that defense and security are outside the framework’s direct applicability.
fr1_body_name: National Authority (ANPD - Autoridade Nacional de Proteo de Dados)

fr1_title: Law No.15 211 of September 17 2025: Digital Statute of Children and Adolescents
fr1_justif: The law is an extension of the Child and Adolescent Statute geared towards the digital environment aiming to protect minors (children and adolescents under 18) from inappropriate content on the internet such as abuse pornography drug use bullying and commercial exploitation. According to Article 17 Article 17 providers of information technology products or services aimed at children and adolescents or likely to have access to them must regularly review artificial intelligence tools with the participation of experts and competent agencies in order to disable the system and ensure security. Art.22 Art. 22. prohibits the use of profiling techniques including emotional analysis augmented and virtual reality for this purpose.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Autonomous administrative authority for the protection of the rights of children and adolescents in the digital environment (not yet established) The National Data Protection Authority regulates reports and violations in the digital environment (National Data Protection Agency status changed by measure No 1317/2025)

fr1_title: AI for the Good of All - Brazilian Artificial Intelligence Plan
fr1_justif: The central premise of the plan is that AI should be for the good of all: human-centered and accessible to all based on respect for dignity social rights cultural regional and human diversity and the appreciation of work and workers preventing inequality and discriminatory prejudices (p. 25). It establishes actions to enrich national data and create linguistic models based on this data prioritizing diversity and culture in the Portuguese language (p. 70). Actions to enrich national data and create linguistic models based on this data prioritizing diversity and culture in the Portuguese language (p. 31). (google drive)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: AI for the Good of All - Brazilian Artificial Intelligence Plan
fr1_justif: Environmental sustainability (Ecological Transition) is one of the premises that underpins the structuring and implementation of the plan. Several short-term initiatives 31 actions were established due to their potential for expansion replication and sustainability such as a digital technical guidance service with AI for agriculture and fishing. Other initiatives: Axis 1 - Sustainability and Renewable Energy Program for AI; Sustainable AI infrastructure based on renewable energy; Creation of the Center for Monitoring and Promotion of Sustainable AI; Axis 4 - Establishment of high-capacity 'green' data centers powered by renewable energy and optimized for the sustainable use of water resources.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Brazilian Strategy of Artificial Intelligence
fr1_justif: Regarding legislation regulation and ethical use: the strategy reinforces that AI should not create or reinforce biases capable of unfairly or disproportionately impacting certain individuals especially those related to sensitive characteristics. The data protection agency can establish audits to verify discriminatory aspects. In the AI ??research and development axis an ethical design approach should be adopted to make the system as fair as possible and reduce potential discrimination and bias. Stimulate the production of ethical AI by funding research projects in the fields of equity/non-discrimination. Governance axis: disclosure of open source code capable of verifying discriminatory tendencies and IA acquisition criteria.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Science Technology and Innovation

fr1_title: Brazilian Strategy of Artificial Intelligence
fr1_justif: The strategy is a strategic document with long-term guidelines to guide actions research principles and training. About gender the strategy has a general principle that AI should not create or reinforce biases that could unfairly or disproportionately impact certain individuals based on their gender. In this regard developers and users should periodically review whether AI is responsible for biases. Regarding actions the workforce should encourage the composition of AI development teams with respect to gender race sexual orientation and other sociocultural aspects. Regarding educational initiatives it should create mechanisms to increase interest in STEM disciplines with a special focus on gender and racial inclusion programs.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Science Technology and Innovation

fr1_title: Bill No. 2338 (Brazil)
fr1_justif: Art. 3. The development implementation and use of AI systems shall observe effective and appropriate human oversight and determination throughout the AI ??life cycle considering the degree of risk involved; and self-determination and freedom of decision and choice.  Article 6 the person or group affected by a high-risk AI system has control over the decision's explanation review and challenge including. Art. 8. Human oversight of high-risk AI systems shall seek to prevent or minimize risks to the rights and freedoms of affected individuals or groups. Art. 40. Appropriate internal oversight mechanisms shall be established to manage the risks arising from the application of AI systems in their respective domains of activity.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: According to Article 1 paragraph 1 the law does not apply to the AI ??system developed and used solely and exclusively for national defense purposes.

fr1_title: Brazilian Strategy of Artificial Intelligence

And particularly data protection impact reports. Also the creation of allowlists and blocklists for certain rules and algorithmic analyses; the adoption of transparent and auditable methodologies for the development of AI systems data sources procedures and project documentation. In the public sector application axis (p. 46) conduct impact analyses on AI use cases that directly affect citizens or public servants. In the public security axis (p. 50) monitoring and submitting a data protection impact report before implementation.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Brazilian Strategy of Artificial Intelligence
fr1_justif: Workforce and training (p. 32) is one of the strategic pillars recognizing that access to appropriate knowledge and skills will remain crucial at all stages of the AI ??lifecycle. There is a diversity gap in IT professions and pointing out that certain occupations are at greater or lesser risk in the labor market (p. 33). Strategic actions include partnerships with the private sector and academia for training and development ongoing training programs in companies and the public sector awareness campaigns for ethical AI development retention of AI talent in the country public policies for the reality of the labor market and a diverse composition of AI development teams (p. 34-35).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Bill No. 2338 (Brazil)
fr1_justif: Art. 23. All public entities that develop or use high-risk AI systems must adopt measures to disclose with easy access on their websites the high-risk assessments developed implemented or used by the public sector. Art. 24. The Federal Executive Branch must establish minimum transparency standards for the AI ??systems used and monitor compliance with these obligations. Art. 69. AI systems of public entities must guarantee transparency regarding the use of AI systems. General application: Art. 28. Conclusions of algorithmic impact assessments will be public. Art. 30. Developers of general-purpose and generative AI systems must publish a summary of the training dataset. Art. 44. The authority must create and maintain a public database.

fr1_defence_and_security_justif: According to Article 1 paragraph 1 the law does not apply to theAI system developed and used solely and exclusively fornational defense purposes.

fr1_title: Brazilian Strategy of Artificial Intelligence
fr1_justif: In the area of ??public sector application the following strategic action was established: to consider in bidding processes and administrative contracts aimed at acquiring Artificial Intelligence products and services criteria focused not only on technical efficiency but also on the incorporation of ethical principles related to transparency equity and non-discrimination. (Annex to the ordinance establishing the strategy no pagination.) There is a general recommendation within the framework of legislation regulation and ethical use that legal compliance regarding data protection should be considered both in the acquisition and in the development and use of these technologies involving AI systems.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Science Technology and Innovation

fr1_title: AI for the Good of All - Brazilian Artificial Intelligence Plan
fr1_justif: Axis 3 AI for Improving Public Services. Training federal public servants to leverage the potential of AI in optimizing processes and data-driven decision-making. Estimated capacity to train 115000 federal public servants by 2026 (20% of the total)  and resources (2024-28): R$7.5 million according to Action 26 for training public servants in AI.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: AI for the Good of All - Brazilian Artificial Intelligence Plan
fr1_justif: The plan recognizes that it is essential to invest in the training and development of AI professionals from the technical level to postgraduate studies (p. 22) through the AI ??Training Program (pp. 73-74). The AI ??Training Qualification and Requalification Program includes the national platform for online AI qualification courses as well as in-person qualification courses via internships in companies (p. 75); a multi-institutional initiative for AI training and development with intensive use of experimental environments (p. 75); and technological training and development in AI in partnership with the private sector (internships in companies) (p. 76).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Brazilian Strategy of Artificial Intelligence
fr1_justif: The legislation regulation and ethical use axis (p. 17-23) suggests AI risk analyses through the preparation of environmental safety and human rights impact reports; the creation of whitelists and blacklists for certain rules and algorithmic analyses; the adoption of transparent and auditable methodologies for the development of AI systems data sources procedures and project documentation; and the development of voluntary and consensual standards to manage the risks associated with AI applications. Furthermore the adoption of regulatory sandboxes for research development (p. 37) and public safety (p. 50). Productive sector axis (p.40) establishes specific objectives accuracy indicators of technology developed.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Science Technology and Innovation

fr1_title: Brazilian Strategy of Artificial Intelligence
fr1_justif: The strategic axis legislation regulation and ethical use (p.17-23) includes: encouraging the production of ethical AI by funding research projects aimed at applying ethical solutions particularly in the areas of responsibility/accountability and transparency known as the FAT matrix; encouraging transparency and responsible disclosure regarding the use of AI systems (p.23); Encourage transparency and responsible disclosure regarding the use of AI systems and promote compliance by such systems with human rights (p.23). There are no specific actions for explainability a general recommendation for specific funding for research or pilot programs to create explainable and transparent AI (p.16).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Science Technology and Innovation

fr1_title: Concept for the Development of Artificial Intelligence in Bulgaria until 2030
fr1_justif: The Concept treats AI literacy as a central pillar emphasizing education reform lifelong learning and awareness-raising across society. It frames AI literacy not only as technical skills but also as ethical legal and social understanding of AI. Knowledge and Skills Base: The document highlights the need to create knowledge and skills for the development and use of AI to achieve competitiveness and trust (s. 2.3 4 Vision and Goals; 4.2) Education System Reform: calls for reforms across all levels of education—from schools to universities—to integrate AI-related content and digital skills (s.5.3); Lifelong Learning (s.5.3 - Vocational training and continuing education).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Bots fake accounts and automated conduct are mechanisms of disinformation campaigns (recital 85) and as such rules are needed to combat them (recital 9). Systemic risk assessments apply to large-scale online platforms and search engines (art. 34) and explicitly include the dissemination or amplification of misleading or fraudulent content and the negative effects on gender-based violence. This includes recommendation systems and other relevant algorithmic systems (art. 34). Mitigation measures include testing and adapting algorithmic and recommendation systems; speeding up the processing of notifications of illegal hate speech and cyber violence; and labeling content generated or manipulated by AI (art.35).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU GDPR
fr1_justif: The law provides a layered redress framework that applies in AI contexts whenever personal data is processed. Data subjects harmed by automated processing based on AI-driven profiling and decision-making hold the right to express their point of view and the right to contest the decision (art. 22); presenting complaints to supervisory authorities (art. 77) resorting to legal measures against gaps in the authorities (art. 78) and bringing proceedings directly against controllers or processors (art. 79). Organizations may be mandated to exercise the rights collectively on behalf of data subjects (art. 80). Supervisory authorities may impose administrative fines because of the violations of core provisions (art. 83).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: This Regulation shall not apply to the processing of personal data by Member States in the context of carrying out activities relating to the Union's common foreign and security policy (recital 16). The processing of personal data by Member States does not apply to activities related to the protection and prevention of threats to public security (Art. 2).
fr1_body_name: The law establishes the European Data Protection Board is comprised of representatives from the supervisory authority of each member states (Arts 68) acting independently (Art 69) providing guidelines recommendations and binding decisions to ensure consistence across Member States (Art 70) The Member State must to establish am independent supervisory authority responsible for monitoring and enforcing the regulation (Art 51)

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Online platform service providers must ensure the privacy security and protection of minors in their services (Art. 28) including prohibiting advertising based on profiling through the automated processing of data about children (Art. 28). Terms and conditions must be understandable to minors (Art. 14) ensuring a high level of privacy and security by default based on good practices (recitals 70 71). Large-scale online platforms and large-scale search engines systematic risk assessment applies periodically evaluating potential negative effects on minors (Art. 34). Mitigation measures are determined: age verification parental control tools abuse reporting tools and support for minors (Art. 35). Voluntary standards apply (Art. 44).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Concept for the Development of Artificial Intelligence in Bulgaria until 2030
fr1_justif: For this indicator emphasis is on equal access fairness and inclusivity in AI systems. Introduction: refers to EU’s vision of “trustworthy AI” with requirements such as diversity non-discrimination and fairness ensuring universal design and equal access. Section 2.2. AI as a Driver of Digital Transformation (pp. 10-11) – Notes the role of AI in machine translation and language localization (e.g. e-commerce platforms digital assistants) directly addressing Bulgaria’s multilingual context and cultural diversity. Section 5.5 Raising awareness and building trust in society – Stresses the need for public trust and inclusivity. Section 6 paragraph Software industry and Creating AI applications for educational purposes.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The law focuses on the risk classification system (Art. 6) and the requirements imposed on high-risk AI systems. It requires suppliers of high-risk AI systems to establish implement document and maintain a risk (and damage) management system throughout the system's lifecycle (Art. 9). Training validation and test datasets must conform to data governance in terms of representation error-freeness completeness contextualization and the function the system will be used for handling special personal data for monitoring purposes and detecting and correcting biases. High-risk AI systems must have consistency throughout the lifecycle by requiring appropriate levels of accuracy and robustness and reducing the risk of bias (art.15).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.
fr1_body_name: AI office coordinates EU implementation (art64) European AI Board composed of one representative per Member State aims to issue guidance support consistent application and coordinate national authorities (art65 66) the national competent authorities (art70) once each country must designate at least one market surveillance authority and a notifying authority The Advisory Forum (Art 67) provides technical expertise advising the commission and council The Scientific Panel of Independent Experts (Art 68) provides support with specialized scientific and technical knowledge However neither directly participates in implementation

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act addresses gender equality through non-discrimination and fundamental rights provisions. Training datasets for high-risk AI must be representative avoinding the discriminatory outcomes and protecting atributes explicitly related gender (Art. 10). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights impact assessments before deploying high-risk AI incluing gender-based discrimination (Art. 27). Equality bodies are granted market surveillance powers enabling them to investigate and sanction AI systems producing discriminatory outcomes (Art. 74).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act establishes requirements to ensure human control over high-risk AI systems requiring providers to design systems that enable natural persons with a minimum level of comprehensibility to monitor outputs detect anomalies and intervene or interrupt operations when necessary (art. 14). Deployers must assign oversight to competent natural persons with authority to act (art. 26). For general-purpose AI with systemic risk adversarial testing and incident reporting reinforce human control (Arts. 55 73). Additional oversight provisions are set in the fundamental rights impact assessment (art. 27) and Annex (IV) 2(e) as part of technical documentation requirements (art. 11).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Law establishes an assessment architecture according to the type and level of risk of the system. High-risk AI suppliers must identify analyze and mitigate risks during the design development and implementation phases within a risk management system (Arti.9) with comprehensive technical documentation (Art.15) and monitoring of operations and anomalies (Art. 26). For AI implementers in the public sector a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment applies before implementing high-risk systems listed in Annex 3 (Art.27). Compliance assessment must include self-assessment or third-party audits depending on the criticality of the system (Art. 43 47). There are ex-post assessments and post-commercialization monitoring (Art.72).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act classifies systems for recruitment promotion termination and worker management as high-risk AI systems (Annex III(4)) (art. 6). Prohibited AI practices include bans on emotion-recognition in the workplace (art. 5). Providers must implement risk-management addressing discrimination (Art. 9) ensure representative datasets and bias-control (art. 10) and maintain technical documentation (Arts. 11). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights assessments before deployment (art. 27). Art. 26 (7-9) establishes safeguards: workers must be informed and may request explanations of AI use. However the Act does not regulate pay conditions or compensation so it mitigates risks without constituting a full labour-rights regime.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: (Draft) Law on the Use and Development of Artificial Intelligence (Bulgaria)
fr1_justif: The bill addresses public sector skills development by establishing a national framework for training and certifying civil servants in the ethical and effective use of AI led by the Institute of Public Administration in coordination with the Minister of e-Government. It mandates sector-specific upskilling programs across education healthcare social policy interior and defense with annual reporting to ensure accountability. In addition it integrates AI training into employment promotion policies requiring the Council of Ministers to allocate funding including EU resources for advanced AI training and workforce qualification (see Articles 16 - 18).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Concept for the Development of Artificial Intelligence in Bulgaria until 2030
fr1_justif: An emphasis is placed on teh overall low digital skills across the Bulgarian population and the need for up-skilling and re-skilling for people whose profession may be threatened by AI and for better adaptation to the AI technologies and tools (see section 3.2 paragrapgh Digital Skills on pp.24-25). One of the main goals of the AI Concept is in the area of education and skills (section 4 'Goals). It is argued that Education and training at all levels have a leading role in overcoming the challenges posed by the development and implementation of AI (section 4.2). In section 5.3 there is a list ofmeasure to be undertaken for Vocational training and continuing education in the AI area (see p.38).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Safety and security is core to the Act. It establishes a risk-based framework for risk identification and mitigation (Art. 9) oversight measures (Art. 14) and robustness and accuracy requirements (Art. 15). To ensure ongoing safety the Act mandates technical documentation and record-keeping (Arts. 11–12) and in certain high risk situations a conformity assessment procedure prior to market placement (Art. 43). Post-market obligations in Arts. 72–73 require providers and deployers to monitor performance report serious incidents and take corrective action. For GPAI models posing systemic risks Arts. 55–56 impose additional safety and cybersecurity duties including model evaluation and risk mitigation following the Code of Practice.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Articles 11 12 and 13 establish several transparency and documentation obligations for AI developers and deployers. High-risk AI systems must include clear information on their capabilities limitations and intended purpose (Art. 13) and providers must maintain comprehensive technical documentation and automatic logging to ensure traceability (Arts. 11–12). These measures enable deployers to better understand the systems and authorities to audit or investigate when necessary. There are disclosure obligations that require notification when individuals interact with AI systems particularly chatbots and emotion recognition tools (Art. 50). Also for general-purpose AI in terms of model documentation and summary of training data (Art. 53).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_justif: Article 19 (page 12) indicates that people have the right to challenge AI decisions. This implies an access to redress
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: In the end of article 16 (page 11) it is mentioned that 'This obligation to provide information does not apply to the collection of personal data necessary for the detection of an offense relating to state security defense and public safety'

fr1_title: Digital Media and Information Literacy Competency Framework (DMIL) (Cambodia)
fr1_justif: The document addresses AI literacy through the promotion of Digital Media and Information Literacy (DMIL) competencies across education training and public sector capacity building. DMIL is integrated into primary secondary and tertiary education to help students develop knowledge and responsible use of communication and information technologies in academic professional and everyday contexts (p. 6). The framework also supports training programs for civil servants and sector-specific initiatives through government training centers and other institutions (p. 21). In addition it enables the development of assessment tools to measure and monitor DMIL competency levels and evaluate the effectiveness of training/literacy programs (22).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Cambodian Child Online Protection Guidelines for the Digital Technology Industry
fr1_justif: The Guidelines integrate children’s rights into AI-related practices. Sec. 2.1 (p.11) requires Child Rights Impact Assessments across all products and systems including those using AI. Section 2.3 (p.12-13) mandates safety- and privacy-by-design ensuring age-appropriate design and strict safeguards for children’s data. The Guidelines also directly address AI use: Sec. 2.6 (p.16) encourages companies to adopt automated or AI-driven Child sexual abuse materials detection tools while prioritising children’s privacy and safety.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Draft National Artificial Intelligence Strategy (2025-2030) (Cambodia)
fr1_justif: The document addresses the indicator of human oversight by requiring that processes involving artificial intelligence operate under human supervision (p. 15). This provision ensures that AI systems remain subject to human monitoring and control rather than operating autonomously. It also requires clear indicators when AI is the creator of content which promotes transparency and allows users to distinguish between human- and AI-generated outputs (p. 15). These measures support human determination over AI-driven processes and aim to maintain accountability reduce risks associated with AI use and strengthen trust in the deployment of AI technologies.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Cambodia Digital Economy and Society Policy Framework 2021–2035
fr1_justif: Under the considerations for the future development the policy explicitly lists (3). Raising digital awareness and digitalskills for civil servants at all levels including local administration. (p. 174).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: National Council for Digital Economy and Society (NC-DES)

fr1_title: Digital Media and Information Literacy Competency Framework (DMIL) (Cambodia)
fr1_justif: P.1 emphasises that DMIL is 'increasingly necessary to equip individuals with pertinent knowledge skills attitudes and value' to protect themselves from risks (like disinformation and privacy violations) and critically to 'empower them to benefit from the opportunities that artificial intelligence brings.' (p. 11). P.3 mandates comprehensive policy measures to promote DMIL 'at all levels through professional trainings in formal education informal trainings and sectoral support programmes.' P.15 focuses on upgrading the skills of frontier technologies. Section 4 mandates the document to serve as the foundation for organising various upskilling and training initiatives.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Draft National Artificial Intelligence Strategy (2025-2030) (Cambodia)
fr1_justif: Strategy 5 Promoting Ethical and Responsible AI - aiming to build trust by establishing an appropriate governance framework to manage risks and address the negative impacts of AI. P.9 - Aim 5 is to Manage AI Risks by implementing social technical and regulatory measures to ensure ethical and responsible AI use. P.816 - acknowledges well-known anecdotal cases of AI being used in frauds and notes that the Draft Law on Personal Data Protection and the Draft Law on Cybersecurity are undergoing review which will apply to AI systems.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Voluntary Code of Conduct on the Responsible Development and Management of Advanced Generative AI Systems (Canada)
fr1_justif: This document is a set of guidelines that informs the development and management of generative AI systems. It explicitly mentions 'the capability to generate realistic images and video or to impersonate the voices of real people' and AI systems that can enable deception (p. 1). The Code includes the resource of 'Implementation guide for managers of artificial intelligence systems' (https://shorturl.at/jfF2O) and encourages the creation of a risk management framework proportionate to the nature and risk profile of activities (p. 6).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Directive on Automated Decision-Making (Canada)
fr1_justif: Section 6.4.1 of the Directive on Automated Decision-Making states that the assistant deputy minister responsible fror the program using the automated decision system or any other senior official named by the deputy head is responsible for '[i]nforming clients of recourse options to challenge the administrative decision.' 6.4.1.1 states that the said entities are responsible for '[e]nsuring that recourse options are timely effective and easy to access.'
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: The Directive states that 'This directive applies to all institutions subject to the Policy on Service and Digital (s. 8.1 p. 8). Section 6.3 of the Policy on Service and Digital states that 'This policy does not apply to national security systems except where the Chief Information Officer of Canada is identified as the system business owner.'
fr1_body_name: Treasury Board Secretariat heads of agents of Parliament

fr1_title: Bill C-216: An Act to enact the Protection of Minors in the Digital Age Act and to amend two Acts
fr1_justif: This proposed law creates a duty of care on digital service providers ('operators') to prevent or mitigate the effects of physical harm or incitement of harm; online sexual violence against minors; the creation of imagery of a minor that is sexually exploitative; the promotion and marketing of unlawful products or services to minors; mental health disorders; addictive behaviours; the use of accounts that have not been age verified; predatory/deceptive marketing practices (s. 4 p. 7). It also broadens the definition of intimate image under s. 162.1 of the Criminal Code to encompass deepfakes/images or videos created from scratch online (p. 16).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Guiding principles on diversity of content online (Canada)
fr1_justif: The Guiding Principles on Diversity of Content Online promote cultural and linguistic diversity by emphasizing access to and discoverability of content reflecting different cultures and perspectives including in local and Indigenous languages. They call for inclusive creation equitable access and transparency in algorithms that recommend diverse sources and minimize systemic bias. The principles and Theme 4 especially seek to recognize linguistic minorities within broader goals of digital inclusion and equity aiming to address barriers that limit participation in the digital space.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: AI Strategy for the Federal Public Service 2025-2027 (Canada)
fr1_justif: The document states that: 'The Government of Canada should minimize the environmental impact of its AI use by deploying AI appropriately and choosing solutions that demonstrate responsible environmental practices.' pp. 36-37 'ensuring that the benefits of AI do not come at theexpense of our environment' p. 10.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Directive on Automated Decision-Making (Canada)
fr1_justif: The Directive provides structural tools (algorithmic impact assessments (s. 6.1) bias testing (s. 6.3) transparency (s. 6.2) recourse (s. 6.4) peer review (s. 6.3.7)) that can be leveraged to protect against discrimination in automated systems. Its integration of GBA-plus — a tool used to examine how policies programs and initiatives may differently impact various genders — is relevant to ensuring gender equality in particular is considered proactively in the design deployment and oversight of automated decision systems by the federal government.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: The Directive states that 'This directive applies to all institutions subject to the Policy on Service and Digital (s. 8.1 p. 8). Section 6.3 of the Policy on Service and Digital states that 'This policy does not apply to national security systems except where the Chief Information Officer of Canada is identified as the system business owner.'
fr2_title: Pre-market guidance for machine learning-enabled medical devices (Canada)
fr2_justif: The guidance document provides guidance to manufacturers who are submitting new or amended applications for Class II-IV medical devices under Canada's medical device regulations. It provides guidelines for considerations that ought to inform the lifecycle of AI-based medical products. It states that bias should be part of the risk analysis as part of risk management and that Sex and Gender-based Analysis Plus ought to be used to identify how a product/initiative may impact diverse groups of people. The document refers to the need to consider various identity factors (sex gender race age etc.) when making decisions around the diversity of training and input data.
