fr2_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Directive on Automated Decision-Making (Canada)
fr1_justif: The Directive provides structural tools (algorithmic impact assessments (s. 6.1) bias testing (s. 6.3) transparency (s. 6.2) recourse (s. 6.4) peer review (s. 6.3.7)) that can be leveraged to protect against gender discrimination in automated systems. Its integration of GBA-plus — a tool used to examine how policies programs and initiatives may differently impact various genders — is particularly relevant to ensuring gender equality is considered proactively in the design deployment and oversight of automated decision systems by the federal government.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: The Directive states that 'This directive applies to all institutions subject to the Policy on Service and Digital (s. 8.1 p. 8). Section 6.3 of the Policy on Service and Digital states that 'This policy does not apply to national security systems except where the Chief Information Officer of Canada is identified as the system business owner.'
fr2_title: Playbook for Gender Equality in the Digital Age
fr2_justif: Developed by the Digital Inclusion Lab at Global Affairs Canada the purpose of the Playbook for Gender Equality in the Digital Age is to put forward a set of best practices to support gender equality in digital contexts. They note under Culture Point 9 that everyone needs to assure that algorithms and machine learning systems do not perpetuate historical patterns of gender-based discrimination and that the use of Artificial Intelligence to perpetrate sexual and gender based violence is adequately addressed.
fr2_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Directive on Automated Decision-Making (Canada)
fr1_justif: The document addresses human oversight and determination in Appendix C (p. 13). Level I systems can make decisions and assessments without direct human involvement though humans must be involved in quality assurance and can intervene in decision-making where appropriate (p. 20). Level III and above require humans to make the final decisions (p. 20). Decisions must review the decision or recommendations for accuracy and appropriateness (p. 20).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: The Directive states that 'This directive applies to all institutions subject to the Policy on Service and Digital (s. 8.1 p. 8). Section 6.3 of the Policy on Service and Digital states that 'This policy does not apply to national security systems except where the Chief Information Officer of Canada is identified as the system business owner.'
fr2_title: Pre-market guidance for machine learning-enabled medical devices (Canada)
fr2_justif: This document addresses human oversight and determination by requiring manufacturers to clearly define the intended user and how the device fits into clinical workflows including the degree of autonomy (i.e. the level of user intervention needed to perform a clinical function) (p. 9-10). It also emphasizes transparency and explainability (p. 12 and 23) so users can interpret outputs and includes human-centred information in labeling and instructions (p. 23-24). These elements help ensure that healthcare professionals understand oversee and appropriately use ML-enabled systems in practice.
fr2_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Directive on Automated Decision-Making (Canada)
fr1_justif: The core requirement of this document is the completion of an algorithmic impact assessment (s. 6.1 p. 4). The algorithmic impact assessment is a risk identification and mitigation tool that requires the completion of such an assessment when any automated decision-making system is used by the Canadian federal government. The AIA assesses risk areas (decision type algorithm data system design) and mitigation measures generating impact levels I-IV (p. 11-20). Components include: transparency requirements quality assurance human oversight training peer review recourse mechanisms and compliance with administrative law principles of fairness accountability and legality (p. 3 5-7).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: This directive applies to all institutions subject to the Policy on Service and Digital which in section 6.3 states that 'This policy does not apply to national security systems except where the Chief Information Officer of Canada is identified as the system business owner.'

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems
fr1_title: Directive on Automated Decision-Making (Canada)
fr1_justif: The Directive requires that the results of algorithmic impact assessments be published in an accessible format on the Open Government Portal prior to the production of any automated decision system (s. 6.1.1). Reporting requirements include publishing information on the effectiveness and efficiency of the automated decision system in meeting program objectives on the Open Government Portal (s. 6.5.1) as well as publishing information on this portal on how the use of the automated decision system is fair and transparent and does not violate human rights and freedoms (s. 6.5.2). Notably there is a list of Agents of Parliaments at s. 8.3.1 which are not required to publish algorithmic impact assessments or reporting information (s. 8.3.4).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: No defence or security entity is listed as falling under the scope of the Directive so defence and security is explicitly excluded from the scope of the framework.
fr1_body_name: Treasury Board Secretariat Heads of Agents of Parliament

fr1_title: Directive on Automated Decision-Making (Canada)
fr1_justif: The Directive on Automated Decision-Making applies to automated decision systems both developed internally and procured from vendors (s.1.2). Section 6.2.4–6.2.6 establishes procurement-specific obligations: departments must determine appropriate software licences (including open source) obtain all released versions of software components and if using proprietary systems retain rights to access test audit and monitor the system (s.6.2.6.1–6.2.6.2). Departments remain responsible for completing the AIA regardless of whether the system is built or procured (s.6.1.1).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: No defence or security entity is listed as falling under the scope of the Directive so defence and security is explicitly excluded from the scope of the framework.
fr1_body_name: Treasury Board Secretariat Heads of Agents of Parliament

fr1_title: AI Strategy for the Federal Public Service 2025-2027 (Canada)
fr1_justif: The strategy addresses public sector skills development by proposing and developing an evergreen training plan for public servants including general training to build AI understanding and tailored training for specific roles with both technical and leadership skills (17). It benchmarks talent needs and existing skills to create learner personas and targeted training (17-18). It also includes a talent plan for recruitment and retention flexible career pathways and partnerships with educational institutions; co-op programs apprenticeships and internships support talent pipelines (18). These initiatives prepare the workforce to responsibly adopt AI and adapt to evolving roles promoting a culture of continuous learning and AI competence.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: The document states that it applies to departments and agencies covered by the Policy on Service and Digital (p. 7). Section 6.3 of the Policy on Service and Digital states that 'This policy does not apply to national security systems except where the Chief Information Officer of Canada is identified as the system business owner.' Instead organizations that are partially or wholly exempt from the Policy on Service and Digital including those that fall under the national security exemption are encouraged to comply with the AI Strategy as much as possible as a matter of good practice (p. 7).

fr1_title: Pan-Canadian Artificial Intelligence Strategy
fr1_justif: The Pan-Canadian Artificial Intelligence Strategy led by the Government of Canada addresses reskilling and upskilling through its “Talent and Research” pillar. It supports institutions such as CIFAR Amii Mila and the Vector Institute to train and develop AI talent strengthening the national skills base. At the same time initiatives like the Global Innovation Clusters promote industry adoption enabling workers to build practical AI competencies. Together these measures support continuous workforce skill development across research and industry.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: CIFAR (coordination of research & talent programs) AI institutes (Amii Mila Vector Institute) Global Innovation Clusters (industry adoption)Standards Council of Canada (standards pillar) Digital Research Alliance of Canada (compute infrastructure)

fr1_title: Voluntary Code of Conduct on the Responsible Development and Management of Advanced Generative AI Systems (Canada)
fr1_justif: This document is a short code of conduct and set of guidelines that informs the development and management of generative AI systems. It encourages developers and managers of generative AI systems to achieve the outcomes of: accountability safety fairness and equity transparency human oversight and monitoring and validity and robustness. As of page 6 these principles are laid out in a table with the measures required for each principle. Page 8 states that comprehensive testing must be done to achieve system safety to test how the system could be used inappropriately or maliciously. Validity and robustness testing set out in pages 13-14 are essential parts of security and adversarial testing is a key requirement for this principle.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Directive on Automated Decision-Making (Canada)
fr1_justif: The Directive addresses transparency by requiring those using AI systems to complete approve and publish the algorithmic impact assessments regarding the deployment of AI (section 6.1 Appendix C). It also requires notice that decisions will be made or assisted by AI systems (section 6.2) and that meaningful explanations to clients be provided as to how and why the decision was made (s. 6.2.3).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: This directive applies to all institutions subject to the Policy on Service and Digital which in section 6.3 states that ' This policy does not apply to national security systems except where the Chief Information Officer of Canada is identified as the system business owner.
fr2_title: Pre-market guidance for machine learning-enabled medical devices (Canada)
fr2_justif: Transparency is a key requirement in this document which serves as a guideline for new or amended Class II-IV medical device licence applications in Canada. This document provides guidelines for measures to undertake throughout the lifecycle of such a medical device. Transparency is a throughline in the product lifecycle. This document states that transparency and explainability involve robust and detailed device labelling (including instructions for use) and providing detailed device design change performance and limitation information. Labelling requirements for transparency and explainability can be found at pages 23-25.
fr2_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems
Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Chilean National Policy on Artificial Intelligence (UPDATE 2024)
fr1_justif: The Chilean AI policy addresses AI literacy mainly in Axis Nº1 – Enabling Factors Section 1.1 Talent Development (pp. 14-15). It promotes skills for understanding using and critically analyzing AI across schools higher education and the workforce and explicitly includes a new measure to strengthen citizen education and training on the ethical use of AI its risks and benefits promoting critical thinking and digital literacy for public officials and the general public. AI literacy is further addressed in Section 3.7 Children and Adolescents (p. 23) which highlights digital literacy in AI and critical thinking from an early age.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Science Technology Knowledge and Innovation

fr1_title: Chilean National Policy on Artificial Intelligence (UPDATE 2024)
fr1_justif: The Chilean AI Policy addresses AI-facilitated misinformation and digital violence in Section 3.10 “Safe Digital Ecosystem”. It proposes studying their impact and implementing measures to prevent and counter them including their effects on mental health (p. 25). It calls for interdisciplinary research with emphasis on vulnerable groups suchas children adolescents and women and for digital education and literacy to promote the responsible use of AI and reduce harm in online environments. Section 3.7 addresses AI systems involving children and adolescents mandating the protection of their data and privacy. It requires preventing discrimination bias and abuse while guaranteeing their physical emotional and mental well-being (p.23)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Science Technology Knowledge and Innovation

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Bill (Chile)
fr1_justif: While the bill does not explicitly use the terms redress or remedy it addresses Access to Redress and Remedy in different sections. Title II grants individuals the right to pursue legal action to defend their consumer rights when harmed by an AI system (pp. 18 and 32). Title V requires operators to act when an AI system is linked to a serious incident including notification and corrective measures (pp. 37–38 Art. 13). Article 28 allows affected individuals to file a joint civil lawsuit to stop harmful acts and seek compensation (pp. 46–47).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: The bill clearly excludes defence and security AI systems from its scope. Article 2 paragraph (a) states that the law “shall not apply to AI systems developed and used for national defence purposes.” It further establishes that the Ministry of National Defence will issue a confidential resolution listing the exempted systems (p. 27 Art. 2).

fr1_title: Chilean National Policy on Artificial Intelligence (UPDATE 2024)
fr1_justif: The Chilean National AI Policy (2024) addresses Children's Rights under its Governance and Ethics axis section 3.7. Children and Adolescents (NNA) (pp. 23-24). It recognises AI's profound impact on children and adolescents (NNA) in education entertainment and social media. The policy commits to promoting the responsible use of AI oriented towards exercising their rights. This includes developing regulations to protect their data and privacy prevent discrimination and abuse and guarantee their physical emotional and mental well-being. It also advocates for digital literacy and critical thinking from an early age ensuring their participation in shaping a safe AI environment.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Science Technology Knowledge and Innovation

fr1_title: Chilean National Policy on Artificial Intelligence (UPDATE 2024)
fr1_justif: The Chilean AI policy addresses cultural and linguistic diversity by embedding it in its ethical foundations and governance measures. Principle 1(e) explicitly requires AI systems to promote cultural diversity and avoid discriminatory outcomes while Section 3.5 advances inclusion by encouraging participation of diverse social groups and the use of bias-mitigation tools. Additionally Section 3.9 promotes AI capacities for preserving Chile’s material and immaterial cultural heritage fostering open databases and interdisciplinary research (p. 24). Together these elements ensure that AI development supports Chile’s cultural and linguistic plurality.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Science Technology Knowledge and Innovation

fr1_title: Chilean National Policy on Artificial Intelligence (UPDATE 2024)
fr1_justif: The update of the AI policy addresses Environmental Impact of AI in the Axis N°3: Governance and Ethics specifically under the new Section 3.3 “Environment and Climate Crisis” (p. 20 in translated version). It highlights AI’s dual role—both as a tool for environmental protection and as an energy-intensive technology. The policy promotes AI applications for monitoring and reducing environmental harm supports AI research for sustainability and urges the use of non-conventional renewable energies in the development of infrastructure for AI to ensure a socio-ecological development of AI.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Science Technology Knowledge and Innovation

fr1_title: Chilean National Policy on Artificial Intelligence (UPDATE 2024)
fr1_justif: Chile's National AI Policy includes non-discrimination as one of the fundamental ethical guidelines within the first principle ‘Ethical and responsible use of AI centered on people’ which highlights the guideline ‘Diversity Non-Discrimination and Equality’ (pp. 11-12 in translated version). In its ethics and governance section it explicitly addresses these issues in several of its policy objectives including references to non-discrimination and bias reduction in ‘3.5. Inclusion and Non-Discrimination’ (pp. 21-22) and ‘3.7. Children and Adolescents (NNA)’ (p. 23). These include promoting good practices in IA development and integrating bias assessment tools throughout the AI lifecycle.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Science Technology Knowledge and Innovation

fr1_title: Chilean National Policy on Artificial Intelligence (UPDATE 2024)
fr1_justif: In the Chilean National Policy on Artificial Intelligence (2024 Update) Gender Equality is addressed primarily in the Section 3.4 “Gender Equality”  (pp. 20-21 in translated version) located under Axis N°3: Governance and Ethics. The policy proposes promoting women’s participation and equity in AI research development and industry areas to reach OCDE level supported by gender-focused indicators and monitoring systems inclusive labor policies and measures to prevent automation-related gender disparities. It is also addressed in PRINCIPLE 1: Ethical and responsible use of AI centred on people specifically in point e on Diversity Non-Discrimination and Equality (p. 12) and Section 3.5 “Inclusion and Non-Discrimination” (p. 22).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Science Technology Knowledge and Innovation

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Bill (Chile)
fr1_justif: Chile´s AI bill addresses Human Oversight primarily as a general principle (Art. 4(a) pp. 31) and a mandatory rule for high-risk AI systems (Art. 8(f) pp. 35-36). It mandates that high-risk systems be designed for supervision by technically qualified persons to prevent/minimise risks to health safety and fundamental rights. This is embedded within a risk-based regulatory framework where oversight is a key compliance requirement linked to accountability.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Yes. The bill clearly excludes defence and security AI systems from its scope. Article 2 paragraph (a) states that the law “shall not apply to AI systems developed and used for national defence purposes.” It further establishes that the Ministry of National Defence will issue a confidential resolution listing the exempted systems (p. 27 Art. 2).

fr1_title: Chilean National Policy on Artificial Intelligence (UPDATE 2024)
fr1_justif: The policy does not directly address impact assessments but it does touch on related issues in section 3: Governance and ethics specifically in section 3.1 “Regulation and institutional framework” (p. 18-19) and section 3.5 (pp. 21-22) as it establishes the promotion of regulatory and ethical tools to ensure that AI systems respect fundamental rights transparency and accountability. Although it does not specify “AI impact assessments” as such it emphasises the assessment of social environmental and ethical effects as part of responsible AI governance. Where impact assessments are explicitly mentioned is in the policy implementation plan where “bias and non-discrimination tools” are translated into algorithmic impact assessments.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Science Technology Knowledge and Innovation

fr1_title: Law No 21431 - Amends the labor code regulating the contract of workers of digital platforms (Chile)
fr1_justif: The document addresses AI labour protections primarily through transparency and non-discrimination rules. On pages 6-7 Article 152 (quinquies B C D E F G and H) mandates platform transparency employees´ right to information  prohibits discrimination via automated decision-making mechanisms establishes requirements for worker training and protective equipment legal compensation and collective rights of digital platform workers. The Labour Directorate shall oversee compliance and define fines (p. 7-8 Art. 152 quinquies I).
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.
fr2_title: Chilean National Policy on Artificial Intelligence (UPDATE 2024)
fr2_justif: The document addresses Labour Protections mainly under Axis N°3: Governance and Ethics specifically Section 3.6 “Impacts on Work” (p. 36). This section explains how AI and automation are transforming jobs and tasks creating risks of job displacement. It proposes labour foresight retraining and reskilling pathways and the updating of labour legislation to protect workers’ rights in the context of AI-driven technological change. It also emphasises regulating human–machine interaction merely creating good practice guidelines for the ethical use of AI and preventing rights violations due to automation with potential updates to labour legislation. More specific measures are included in the action plan of the same policy.
fr2_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr2_body_name: Ministry of Science Technology Knowledge and Innovation

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems
fr1_title: Guide for the adoption of the recommendations from the Council for Transparency on transparency algorithms (Chile)
fr1_justif: The document addresses public disclosure through proactive algorithmic transparency obligations for government Automated or Semi-Automated Decision Systems (SDAs). Section V mandates that SDAs affecting fundamental rights or access to public services must be publicly disclosed on agencies’ active transparency websites under a new item titled “Algorithmic Transparency” (pp. 21–23). It specifies what must be published: (a) the SDAs in use (b) the services or procedures where they apply and (c) their specifications including purpose and data categories (Sections V.2–V.4 pp. 22–27).Earlier Section II.1 clarifies that SDA-related information qualifies as public information under transparency law subject to disclosure unless lawful secrecy
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Public Procurement Directive No. 44 on Recommendations for the Acquisition of Projects That Include Data Science and Artificial Intelligence (AI) (Chile)
fr1_justif: The guideline regulates the public procurement of AI by establishing a structured process ensuring ethical transparent and secure acquisitions. It details procurement phases—from problem identification solution proposals to contract management—and includes measures for transparency data protection bias mitigation and algorithmic auditing. Sections 4–5 (pp. 4–19) outline project design market research ethical evaluation and contract management for AI systems. It mandates clauses on data protection impact assessments transparency explainable AI algorithmic auditing and bias analysis. These measures ensure accountability prevent discrimination and promote responsible AI use across public bodies.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Chilean National Policy on Artificial Intelligence (UPDATE 2024)
fr1_justif: The National AI Policy addresses Public Sector Skills Development mainly under Axis 1: Enabling Factors – Talent Development and Axis 3: Governance and Ethics. It introduces a new objective to promote education and training programs on the ethical use of AI for public officials strengthening permanent capacity-building within public administration (Axis 1.1 Talent Development p.14-15 of translated to English document). Additionally Axis 2 highlights training and incentives to accelerate State modernization through AI (Axis 2.2 Adoption p.18). Axis 3 further reinforces skills development by promoting training for public officials on ethical AI use and the development of tools and guidelines for AI use in the State (Axis 3.1 p.19).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Science Technology Knowledge and Innovation

fr1_title: Chilean National Policy on Artificial Intelligence (UPDATE 2024)
fr1_justif: The Chilean National AI Policy (2024 Update) integrates reskilling and upskilling as central to its talent development strategy under Axis 1 Talent Development (1.1). It commits to 'Promote the formation of skills for the use development understanding and critical analysis of AI for workers in Chile' (p.6) specifically with the goal of 'Enhance programs and training instruments to update and retrain (upskilling and reskilling) the country's workers' (p. 15). Within Axis 3 point 3.6 'Impacts on Work' the framework proposes to prospect and anticipate changes in the labor market. The goal is to reduce gaps in specific sectors and address the need for retraining and training (upskilling/reskilling)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Science Technology Knowledge and Innovation

fr1_title: Law 21719 on protection and processing of personal data (Chile)
fr1_justif: The law addresses Safety and Security in the AI context through data-protection duties applicable to automated and AI-driven processing. Article 14 and its sub-articles (pp.12–17) set out obligations of data controllers including duties of secrecy or confidentiality information and transparency protection by design and by default (Art. 14 quater) the duty to adopt security measures ensuring confidentiality integrity availability and resilience (Art. 14 quinquies) and the obligation to report security breaches (Art. 14 sexies). AI-related risks are further covered through rules on automated decisions and profiling and required data protection impact assessments for high-risk automated processing (Art. 15 ter p. 18)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Chilean National Policy on Artificial Intelligence (UPDATE 2024)
fr1_justif: The document addresses Transparency and Explainability in Principle 1: Ethical and responsible use of AI centred on people (pp. 11-12 in translated version) requiring AI systems to ensure traceability and explainability so users understand their interactions results and limitations. Axis 3: Governance and Ethics (p. 18-19) further promotes ethical and transparency requirements in regulation and public procurement as well as the development and agreement on minimum requirements on transparency accountability personal data protection among others to safeguard the responsible development and use of AI.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Science Technology Knowledge and Innovation

fr1_title: Opinions of the State Council on Deepening the Implementation of the “Artificial Intelligence+” Initiative (China)
fr1_justif: The document mandates comprehensive integration of AI into formal education under the subsection 'AI+ People's Livelihoods and Well-Being': 'Introduce more productive learning approaches' (Subsection (4) point 2 page 5). It calls for: Integrating AI into 'all elements and processes of education and teaching'; Innovating 'new human-computer coordination teaching models' (e.g. smart learning partners); Promoting 'more flexible and resource-rich self-directed learning'.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: AI Safety Governance Framework 2.0 (China)
fr1_justif: The document addresses AI-facilitated misinformation and violence primarily under Information Content Risks and through comprehensive countermeasures. Specifically it notes the risk of Al-generated content being used to fabricate and spread disinformation to mislead the public and threaten social stability and public security (3.2.2 (a); p.44). It also notes the risk of AI in faciilitating traditional illegal or criminal activities including those related to violence (3.2.2 (b) p.44).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Personal Information Protection Law of the People’s Republic of China
fr1_justif: The PIPL creates comprehensive legal pathways for redress against harms especially those resulting from Automated Decision-Making (AI systems). It grants individuals the right to require handlers to explain the matter and the right to refuse decisions made solely through automation that have a 'major influence' on their rights (article 24 p.7). Critically it provides for judicial redress (the right to sue) (e.g. article 50 pp.12-13 and 70 p.19) and introduces strict liability requiring handlers to bear compensation unless they can prove they are not at fault (article 69 p.19).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Basic Safety Requirements for Generative Artificial Intelligence Services (China)
fr1_justif: The document addresses children's rights in AI through multiple provisions. It requires services suitable for minors to enable parental controls for anti-addiction measures restrict paid services beyond minors' legal capacity and prioritize content beneficial to children's well-being (Section 7a)3) page 7). Services unsuitable for minors must implement preventive technical or management measures (Section 7a)4) page 7).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Special Action Plan for Green and Low-Carbon Development of Data Centers (China)
fr1_justif: The document addresses the environmental impact indicator by setting specific targets for data centers such as increasing renewable energy use and lowering Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE) (section I p.1). It outlines key tasks to achieve these goals including optimizing layouts (section II (3) p.2) improving energy and water efficiency (section II (2) p.2) promoting the use of renewable energy (section II (4) p.3) and encouraging resource recycling and reuse (section II (5) p.3). It also mentions the application of 'artificial intelligence energy-saving technologies' to optimize operations (Section II (6) p.4).
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.
fr1_body_name: The National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC) the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT) the National Energy Administration (NEA) and the National Data Administration (NDA)

fr1_title: Internet Information Service Algorithmic Recommendation Management Provisions (China)
fr1_justif: The document addresses fairness and non-discrimination by requiring service providers to follow the principles of 'fairness and justice'. It specifically prohibits algorithmic recommendation service providers from using algorithms to engage in 'unreasonably differentiated treatment in trading conditions such as trading prices' and other such unlawful activities based on consumer characteristics (article 21 p.4). It mandates algorithms to uphold the principle of fairness and justice (article 4 p.2). The framework also mandates that providers protect the lawful rights and interests of various groups (article 10 p.3).
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.
fr2_title: AI Safety Governance Framework 2.0 (China)
fr2_justif: The framework addresses fairness and non-discrimination by stressing bias and discrimination as the inherent safety risks of AI technology. It emphasizes that the risk may lead to discriminatory outcomes including content regarding ethnic nationality religion and gender (3.1.1 b). It advises developers to avoid improper annotation of training data enhance model architectures expand diversity of training data etc. to mitigate bias (4.1.1 b).
fr2_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: AI Safety Governance Framework 2.0 (China)
fr1_justif: The 'AI Safety Governance Framework 2.0' addresses gender equality by identifying gender discrimination as a risk to be prevented. The document states that during the research development design and training of models and algorithms biases may be unintentionally introduced which could lead to discriminatory outcomes. The framework specifically lists 'gender' as a factor for which AI could produce discriminatory content or outputs (3.1.1.(b) p.40).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: The Opinions on Regulating and Strengthening the Applications of Artificial Intelligence in the Judicial Fields
fr1_justif: The framework emphasizes human oversight and determination by clearly defining AI as a strictly supportive tool in adjudication. It affirms that AI must not replace judges under any circumstances with all judicial decisions authority and accountability remaining solely with human adjudicators. AI outputs are limited to supplementary references to assist decision-making rather than determine outcomes (p. 3). At the same time the framework reinforces user autonomy by granting individuals the right to choose whether to use AI-assisted tools and to terminate their interaction with such systems at any point thereby ensuring that both institutional and individual control over decision-making is preserved.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Technology Ethics Management Service Measures (Trial) (Draft for public comments) (China)
fr1_justif: A Next Generation Artificial Intelligence Development Plan impact assessment through provisions on risk assessment evaluation of AI’s economic social and security impacts and the establishment of monitoring testing and certification mechanisms. It includes dynamic evaluation systems early warning mechanisms and lifecycle supervision of AI systems. (p. 26 (4) Establish an AI security supervision and evaluation system).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: National Science and Technology Structural Reform and Innovation System Construction Leading Small Group

fr1_title: Internet Information Service Algorithmic Recommendation Management Provisions (China)
fr1_justif: This document addresses the indicator by explicitly requiring algorithm-recommended service providers who provide workers with job scheduling services to protect workers' lawful rights and interests in obtaining labour remuneration and to improve algorithms on platform order distribution. It also includes provisions aimed at protecting the rights of workers subject to algorithmic scheduling (article 20 p.4).
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Notice on Widely Carrying out General Education for Professional Technical Personnel on Artificial Intelligence Continued Education (China)
fr1_justif: The document mandates promoting AI education across a professional cohort including civil servants. It focuses on advancing the AI literacy and innovation ability of professional technical personnel (section 1 page 1) by emphasizing the basic principles algorithms ethics and law of AI (see section 2 (3) p.2 learning content). The goal is to cultivate a professional workforce adapted to the digital transformation and to prevent risks posed by AI (section 1 page 1).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Human Resources and Social Security departments
fr2_title: Opinions on Regulating and Strengthening the Judicial Application of Artificial Intelligence (China)
fr2_justif: The document mandates strengthening the skills of judicial personnel to handle AI. The final section VI. Comprehensive Supports stresses the need to enhance 'publicity and training' and encourage judicial personnel (cadres) to 'fully participate' in AI applications (Section 18 p.4).
fr2_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Opinions of the State Council on Deepening the Implementation of the “Artificial Intelligence+” Initiative (China)
fr1_justif: The document directly addresses the indicator under Section (4) 'AI+ People's Livelihoods and Well-Being'. Point 1 (p.5) states the government will 'Vigorously support AI skills training to stimulate the vitality of AI-based innovation entrepreneurship and reemployment'.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: AI Safety Governance Framework 2.0 (China)
fr1_justif: The document addresses AI safety and security by: 1. Classifying risks into Inherent (e.g. poor robustness unreliable output) application (e.g. cyber system risks abuse for cyberattacks) and derivative (e.g. loss of control disrupting social norms) (chapter 3 starting p.39); 2. Mandating technical measures to ensure robustness (e.g. adversarial training 4.1.1.(c) p.49) and controllability (e.g. explicit labeling 4.2.2.(c) p.52); 3. Establishing fault-tolerance measures such as decision verification circuit breakers and one-click control to mitigate harm from system failure (4.2.3 p.52).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Internet Information Service Algorithmic Recommendation Management Provisions (China)
fr1_justif: The document addresses transparency in algorithmic recommendation systems through several provisions. Article 4 establishes “openness and transparency” as guiding principles for algorithmic services. Article 7 requires providers to formulate and disclose norms related to algorithmic recommendation services and implement management and review mechanisms. Article 12 encourages providers to improve the transparency and understandability of recommendation processes such as search ranking and push notifications. Additionally Article 25 requires authorities to publish filing information for registered algorithmic services enhancing public transparency.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.
fr2_title: AI Safety Governance Framework 2.0
fr2_justif: The framework addresses transparency by stressing the need for robust risk management practices and requiring organizations to conduct thorough impact assessments of AI systems. The document seeks to mitigate 'unexpected untruthful and inaccurate outputs' (p.53) and calls for establishing traceability and explainability systems to identify the origins of AI-generated information (p.67-68). It also advocates for the disclosure of AI technologies' principles capabilities and safety risks (p.51) regular disclosure of information on the auditing and anomaly (p.65) and disclosure of functional objectives logic usage and data sources (p.77).
fr2_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

country: China Hong Kong Special Administrative Region
fr1_title: The Chief Executive's 2024 Policy Address: Reform for enhancing development and building our future together (Hong Kong)
fr1_justif: Policy Address 2024 summarises the policy directive on education for 2024/2025. Under the policy address the Education Bureau is tasked to establish the Steering Committee on Strategic Development of Digital Education to promote digital education in primary and secondary schools launch pilot scheme on AI assisted teaching host an international summit and workshop series on the use of AI and launch online learning platform for knowledge and skills related to AI and computational thinking within 2025 (p.81).
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.
fr1_body_name: Education Bureau Steering Committee on Strategic Development of Digital Education

country: China Hong Kong Special Administrative Region
fr1_title: Hong Kong Generative Artificial Intelligence Technical and Application Guideline
fr1_justif: To promote the effective and beneficial use of generative AI this Guideline introduces a governance framework based on five dimensions including crime prevention. With reference to P.19 it is elaborated that generative AI is increasingly used to create deceptive material to spread misinformation manipulate public opinion and impersonate individuals in communications and electronic transactions for fraudulent purpose. As such when developing generative AI the guideline calls for the use of a holistic approach which incorporates ethical considerations social implications public acceptance and community response (p.19).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

country: China Hong Kong Special Administrative Region
fr1_title: Ethical Artificial Intelligence Framework (Hong Kong)
fr1_justif: The framework provides various recommendations on addressing redress and remedy.With reference to 3-32  during the AI system operation and monitoring phase customer redress processes should be established to capture issues/incidents related to AI applications.  This is further elaborated on 4-46 where project managers and data scientists involved in AI applications are recommended to introduce feedback mechanisms for end users and the public to share information about failed predictions or cases.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

country: China Hong Kong Special Administrative Region
fr1_title: Ethical Artificial Intelligence Framework (Hong Kong)
fr1_justif: The Ethical Artificial Intelligence Framework establishes a common approach and structure to govern the development and deployment of AI applications. It consists of an assessment template to assess the implications of AI applications. Under the assessment template organisations are recommended to consider whether AI applications are being used in new domains which may have impact on vulnerable groups including children(3-25 and appendix 6-7).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

country: China Hong Kong Special Administrative Region
fr1_title: Ethical Artificial Intelligence Framework (Hong Kong)
fr1_justif: The Framework sets out 12 ethical AI principles which includes Fairness. Under the fairness principle on 3-8recommendations and results from AI applications should treat individuals within similar groups in a fair manner without favouritism or discrimination and without causing or resulting in harm. This entails maintaining respect for the individuals behind the data and refraining from using datasets that contain discriminatory biases. The principle forms the basis of practices that organisations can follow throughout he various stages of AI Lifecycle when developing or deploying AI applications.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

country: China Hong Kong Special Administrative Region
China Hong Kong Special Administrative Region
fr1_title: Ethical Artificial Intelligence Framework (Hong Kong)
fr1_justif: The framework listed fairness as one of the 12 ethical AI principles. Under the fairness principle recommendation/result from the AI applications should treat individuals within similar groups in a fair manner without favouritism or discrimination and without causing or resulting in harm. This entails maintaining respect for the individuals behind the data and refraining from using datasets that contain discriminatory biases (p.1-4)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

country: China Hong Kong Special Administrative Region
fr1_title: Hong Kong Generative Artificial Intelligence Technical and Application Guideline
fr1_justif: With reference to P.23 Section 2.3.4 the document highlighted 5 key principles of AI Governance including fairness and objectivity. Under fairness and objectivity it is recommended that during model training and content generation scientific algorithms and rigorous review mechanisms should be employed to prevent the generation of biased or discriminatory content targeting gender.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

country: China Hong Kong Special Administrative Region
fr1_title: Ethical Artificial Intelligence Framework (Hong Kong)
fr1_justif: Human oversight is one of the 12 ethical AI principles under the framework. It refers to the degree of human intervention required as part of AI application’s decision-making or operations dictated by the level of the perceived severity of ethical issues. It is determined based on different factors such as the benefits and risks of the AI application impacts of the AI decisionoperational cost and evolving societal norms and values. Human oversight should also be used as a form of control to intervene/ auto-shut AI applications or systems in case of system failure (3-17). The guideline recommends three different approaches for oversight which include humans in the loop humans out of the loop and humans in command (4-21).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

country: China Hong Kong Special Administrative Region
fr1_title: Ethical Artificial Intelligence Framework (Hong Kong)
fr1_justif: The framework includes a section on AI Assessment (5-2 to 6-2). Specifically it includes an assessment template for organisations to assess thebenefits impact and risks of AI applications to ensure that the impact across the AI Lifecycle is managed and that related Ethical AI Principles have been considered.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

country: China Hong Kong Special Administrative Region
fr1_title: Hong Kong Generative Artificial Intelligence Technical and Application Guideline
fr1_justif: The guideline classifies service risks into 4 types: unacceptable risk high risk limited risk and low risk. According to the risk category recruitment tools are categorised as AI applications with limited risk. The corresponding regulatory approach thus is to require organisations to fulfil transparency obligations establish user opt out mechanisms and conduct annual compliance audits (p.11).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

country: China Hong Kong Special Administrative Region
indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems
fr1_title: Ethical Artificial Intelligence Framework (Hong Kong)
fr1_justif: The framework provides a list of 12 ethical AI principles and best practices for government bureau/ ministries to follow when designing and implementing AI projects. Specifically the framework provides guidance on accountability practices for high-risk AI systems (3-25 4-94-42) and outlining the mechanism for disseminating clear and accessible explanation of AI systems to the public (4-444-46). The framework is originally intended only for government bodies but the revision framework is now designed for both government bodies and organizations in general (1-3)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

country: China Hong Kong Special Administrative Region
fr1_title: Ethical Artificial Intelligence Framework (Hong Kong)
fr1_justif: The framework applies to bureau/departments when procuring AI systems and applications. It provides guidance on evaluating third-party vendor tools data and techniques (4-14) emphasising the need for intrepretability and explainability for AI models (4-16)devising a plan for procurement (4-14 4-15) setting clear responsibilities and service level agreements for all AI applications (3-32)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

country: China Hong Kong Special Administrative Region
China Hong Kong Special Administrative Region
China Hong Kong Special Administrative Region
fr1_title: Ethical Artificial Intelligence Framework (Hong Kong)
fr1_justif: The framework includes 'reliability robustness and security' as one of the 12 ethical principles. This is also introduced as 'performance principles' which are fundamental principles that need to be achieved to create a foundation for the execution of other ethical principles(P.1-4). The principle states that AI applications should be developed such that they will operate reliably over long periods of time using the right models and datasets while ensuring they are both robust (i.e. providing consistent results and capable to handle errors) and remain secure against cyber-attacks as required by the relevant legal and industry frameworks(P.3-8).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

country: China Hong Kong Special Administrative Region
fr1_title: Ethical Artificial Intelligence Framework (Hong Kong)
fr1_justif: The framework includes transparency and interpretability as one of the 12 ethical principles. Under the principle organisations should be able to explain decision-making processes of the AI applications to humans in a comprehensible manner (3-8). The framework provides further guidance on how Explainability Transparency and Provability can be materialised (3-10 to 3-12). Regarding explainability the guideline recommends built-in and post-hoc intrepretations. Regarding transparency the guideline outlines Transparency as Traceability Transparency as Verifiability Transparency as Non-deception and Honest Design and transparency and personal privacy. Provability refers to mathematical certainty behind AI models' decisions.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy (CONPES 4144) (2025)(Colombia)
fr1_justif: The document addresses AI Literacy under the Section 5.3.4 Digital Talent and Capacity Development (pp.93–96). It emphasizes developing competencies in the education system for ethical and responsible AI use (p.94) the training of teacher and student and the integration of AI in curricula (p.94) AI literacy and digital skills programmes for the population (p.95) knowledge appropriation and public awareness strategies (pp.95–96).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Information Technology and Communications Ministry of National Education Ministry of Science Technology and Innovation Ministry of Labour National Learning Service Colombian Institute for the Evaluation of Education and the National Planning Department

fr1_title: Law 2502 of 2025 - (through which an aggravating circumstance is modified and established in article 296 of Law 599 of 2000 Colombian Penal Code and other provisions are issued) (The Congress of Colombia)
fr1_justif: Law 2502 of 2025 addresses AI-facilitated violence and disinformation by classifying AI impersonation as an aggravating factor in the crime of personal misrepresentation (Article 3). It defines DeepFake and establishes a public policy to prevent the use of AI for deception or harm including guidelines on ethics algorithmic transparency deepfake detection and rapid response protocols (Article 4). It also mandates judicial traceability of AI-related cases (Article 5).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The National government the Attorney Generals Office the National Police and the Ministry of Information and Communications Technology

fr1_title: Bill 043 of 2025 'Through which artificial intelligence is regulated in Colombia'
fr1_justif: Bill 043 of 2025S addresses access to redress and remedy through Articles 14 and 30–36 establishing civil administrative and criminal liability for AI-related harm. It mandates human oversight impact assessments and sanctions for violations. It also outlines procedures for complaints corrective measures and guarantees due process and protection of fundamental rights.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Consensus on Digital Care - Colombian Communications Regulatory Commission (2025)
fr1_justif: The document (CRC 2025) highlights that AI shapes children's emotional cognitive and social development (p.9). It calls for ethically designed AI systems prioritizing inclusion privacy and security with age-appropriate adaptations (pp.6 15 17). Human oversight is essential for automated decisions affecting minors (p.18). The consensus recommends impact assessments before deploying new technologies to identify biases (p.18). It also emphasizes restrictions on the commercial use of sensitive data from minors requiring age verification and parental consent (pp.4 7 15 19). Educational campaigns on privacy and technology use are advocated (p.17).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: National Committee for Safe Digital Environments

fr1_title: Bill 043 of 2025 'Through which artificial intelligence is regulated in Colombia'
fr1_justif: Bill 043 of 2025S addresses cultural and linguistic diversity in the development of artificial intelligence through guiding principles such as diversity inclusion and a differential approach that urges respect for ethnic cultural and territorial plurality (Article 3.2). It promotes the participation of historically excluded communities and the use of ancestral and community knowledge (Article 3.15). In the field of education it establishes the incorporation of intercultural and territorial content in AI training programs (Articles 21 and 23) ensuring a critical digital citizenship that respects the country's cultural diversity.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy (CONPES 4144) (2025)(Colombia)
fr1_justif: Document CONPES 4144 (National Planning Department 2025) initially addresses the environmental impact of AI highlighting its intensive energy and water consumption in data centers the generation of electronic waste and the carbon footprint of the technology's life cycle (pp.71-72). It points out the lack of measurement tools in Colombia and proposes continuously evaluating the social economic and environmental effects of AI to ensure its sustainability.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development in coordination with the Ministry of Information and Communications Technology the National Environmental Licensing Authority DANE the Ministry of Science Technology and Innovation and DNP

fr1_title: Standards on Algorithmic Transparency for the Algorithmic Systems used by the state (Office of the Attorney General of Colombia and Office of the Ombudsman of Colombia)
fr1_justif: Joint Directive 007 of 2025 addresses fairness and non-discrimination by establishing the principle of 'primacy of fundamental rights' (Article 5a) requiring prior analysis of algorithmic systems' impacts on equality and non-discrimination. It mandates justification for affirmative actions (Article 13 Par.2) and demands algorithmic impact analysis to identify and mitigate risks of differentiated biases or affectations (Article 13c). These measures aim to ensure equitable treatment especially for vulnerable populations.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Among the considerations it is established that Article 24 of Law 1755 of 2015 indicates that confidential information and documents include those related to national defense or security among others (Joint Directive 007 2025 p.3). This implies that algorithmic systems related to national defense and security are explicitly excluded from the scope of the Directive by virtue of legal exceptions to access to public information.

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy (CONPES 4144) (2025)(Colombia)
fr1_justif: Document CONPES 4144 (National Planning Department of Colombia -NDP- 2025) addresses gender equality in AI by proposing strategies against gender-based violence in digital environments such as deepfakes and warning about algorithmic biases (p. 99). It promotes a differential and intersectional approach to the data life cycle and is aligned with the Public Policy on Gender Equality (CONPES 4080 of 2022 p.30). It indicates that these actions seek to ensure ethical inclusive and equitable AI with the active participation of women in its development and application.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Communications Regulation Commission the National Planning Department and the Ministry of Equality and Equity

fr1_title: Roadmap for the Development and Application of Artificial Intelligence in Colombia (Ministry of Science of Colombia)
fr1_justif: The Roadmap (Ministry of Science 2024) addresses human oversight and determination in AI mainly in the sections on Ethics in Artificial Intelligence (p.21) and Colombia and its Framework for AI (pp.49-52). The document defines Human-in-the-loop models as total human control over decision-making and Human-over-the-loop as a transition towards greater autonomy of the algorithmic system (p.51) promotes human intervention in automated decisions (p.51) and establishes principles such as transparency explainability accountability (pp.50-54) and citizen participation (p.76) as mechanisms for inclusive oversight and guaranteeing human agency.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy (CONPES 4144) (2025)(Colombia)
fr1_justif: Document CONPES 4144 (National Planning Department 2025) addresses AI impact assessments mainly in the strategic area of Risk Mitigation. On page 96 line of action 5.1 is proposed which seeks to promote knowledge and reduce vulnerabilities related to AI. In addition line of action 5.2 (p.98) proposes monitoring the impact on employment and 5.3 (p.98) addresses privacy protection. These actions reflect an ex-ante and ex-post approach to identifying and mitigating social ethical and environmental risks.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Law 2466 of 2025 - (through which labor regulations are partially modified and a Labor Reform for decent and dignified work in Colombia is adopted) (The Congress of Colombia)
fr1_justif: Law 2466 of 2025 addresses labour protections in the context of AI specifically for digital delivery platforms. Articles 29 and 30 require transparency regarding automated monitoring and decision-making systems that affect access to orders income security and contractual status. They also guarantee the right to human review of automated decisions and prohibit unjustified suspensions (pp.9–10). These provisions aim to prevent discrimination and strengthen labour rights within algorithmic management ensuring fair treatment of workers in the digital economy.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems
fr1_title: Standards on Algorithmic Transparency for the Algorithmic Systems used by the state (Office of the Attorney General of Colombia and Office of the Ombudsman of Colombia)
fr1_justif: Joint Directive 007 of 2025 establishes active disclosure obligations regarding algorithmic systems (Art. 8-9) requiring minimum public content such as purpose data used and developer. It also regulates enhanced transparency for high-impact systems (Art. 13) algorithmic impact analysis (Art. 14) and access to source code or meaningful explanation (Art. 17) which consolidates public disclosure mechanisms.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Among the considerations it is established that Article 24 of Law 1755 of 2015 indicates that confidential information and documents include those related to national defense or security among others (Joint Directive 007 2025 p.3). This implies that algorithmic systems related to national defense and security are explicitly excluded from the scope of the Directive by virtue of legal exceptions to access to public information.

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy (CONPES 4144) (2025)(Colombia)
fr1_justif: CONPES 4144 Document (DNP 2025) addresses public procurement of AI in the Action Plan specifically in Objective 5.3.6 (pp.101-103). It establishes guidelines for incorporating AI systems as an attribute in technological solutions acquired through public procurement mechanisms for reducing digital security risks and technical support strategies for public entities.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: National Public Procurement Agency (Colombia Compra Eficiente) MinTIC and DNP

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy (CONPES 4144) (2025)(Colombia)
fr1_justif: CONPES 4144 (DNP 2025) addresses the indicator in the context of AI mainly under Objective 4 and its actions (pp.93-95). It proposes strategies to overcome technological barriers in education (Line of action 4.1 p.93) develop competencies for ethical and responsible AI use (line of action 4.2 pp.93-94) strengthen human capital for AI-related roles (Line of action 4.3 pp.94-95) promote social appropriation of AI knowledge (Line of action 4.4 p.95) and addresses labor reconversion to mitigate job displacement (Line of action 5.2 p.98).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Information Technology and Communications Ministry of National Education Ministry of Science Technology and Innovation Ministry of Labour National Learning Service Colombian Institute for the Evaluation of Education and the National Planning Department

fr1_title: Guidelines on the Processing of Personal Data in Artificial Intelligence Systems (Superintendency of Industry and Commerce of Colombia) (2024)
fr1_justif: External Circular 002 of 2024 mentions the principle of security as part of the design of AI projects (p.3). A privacy impact assessment is required prior to the development of AI including security measures and risk assessment (p.6). Technological human and administrative measures are detailed to prevent unauthorized access manipulation or destruction of data (p.7). These provisions seek to ensure the protection of personal data in AI systems.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Roadmap for the Development and Application of Artificial Intelligence in Colombia (Ministry of Science of Colombia)
fr1_justif: The Roadmap (Ministry of Science 2024) defines algorithmic transparency as the availability of clear and understandable information about AI systems including active and passive practices to inform citizens (p.48). It highlights the importance of explainability so that people understand how systems work and how decisions are made even in complex or ‘black box’ systems (pp. 48-49). In addition it promotes mechanisms for ethical auditing citizen participation education in AI ethics and continuous evaluation to strengthen trust and accountability (pp. 81-82).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2024-2027 (Costa Rica)
fr1_justif: The ENIA 2024-2027 establishes education & training as a cross-cutting principle explicitly committing to the integration of AI education into school technical & university curricula to ensure that all individuals have the necessary skills to use and benefit from AI (p.35). The Strategy further specifies that AI education should begin in the early stages of the educational system (p.55) a commitment aligned with the National Science Technology and Innovation Plan (NPSTI) (p.61). These provisions address curriculum reform across primary secondary vocational & tertiary levels of formal education.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Science Innovation Technology and Telecommunications

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2024-2027 (Costa Rica)
fr1_justif: The ENIA 2024-2027 addresses this indicator narrowly through its respective section (p.26). The strategy recognises that children & adolescents are particularly vulnerable to data manipulation exposure to inappropriate content & the exploitation of sensitive data for commercial purposes. To mitigate these risks the strategy states that it is essential to establish robust ethical safeguards including data anonymisation limitations on the collection & use of personal information & the implementation of effective parental controls. These provisions address the protection of children's personal data and the prevention of exploitative uses of AI directed at children.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Science Innovation Technology and Telecommunications

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2024-2027 (Costa Rica)
fr1_justif: Through two guiding principles which constitute the operative basis it addresses this indicator. The Equity & Non-discrimination principle (p.32) which says AI systems be accessible to all sectors of society respecting differences in age ethnicity gender religion economic capacity & educational level & that systems be adapted to be culturally & linguistically appropriate. The Inclusion & Accessibility principle (p.34) which requires that AI be culturally & linguistically appropriate. The strategy also notes in p.20 that Costa Rica can advocate internationally for an inclusive approach respecting cultural & linguistic diversity & identifies cultural bias and contextual misinterpretation as key risks associated with AI (pp.39 41).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Science Innovation Technology and Telecommunications

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2024-2027 (Costa Rica)
fr1_justif: The Strategy mentions that non-discrimination is a central concern in AI applications if not carefully designed and monitored AI systems can replicate and amplify biases (p.23). To ensure equity and non-discrimination it is essential to create opportunities for training and skills development in emerging technologies for everyone (p.32). That is necessary to analyze the training data and algorithms to identify and mitigate potential biases that could result in discrimination (p.42). And that technical regulations and ethical guidelines will ensure that AI does not perpetuate discrimination (p.55).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Science Innovation Technology and Telecommunications

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2024-2027 (Costa Rica)
fr1_justif: The ENIA 2024-2027 addresses gender equality in AI across multiple provisions. At p. 24 the strategy defines a gender approach establishing gender equality as a substantive consideration in AI development and deployment. At p.34 gender is designated as a cross-cutting principle governing the entire strategy meaning all axes and action lines are expected to reflect gender equality considerations. At p.55 the strategy recommends that AI training programmes be accessible and inclusive explicitly ensuring that people of all genders acquire the necessary AI skills directly addressing gender representation in the AI workforce. The ENIA is a non-binding strategy; its provisions constitute recommendations rather than enforceable obligations.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Science Innovation Technology and Telecommunications

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2024-2027 (Costa Rica)
fr1_justif: The Strategy defines this principle as maintaining ultimate human control over systems that use AI ensuring that ethical and legal responsibility can always be clearly attributed to individuals or legal entities at any stage of these systems’ lifecycle (p.31). It mentions examples for the healthcare and judicial sectors (p.31). And it also says that hight risk IA applications will have mandatory human oversight mechanisms (p.42).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Science Innovation Technology and Telecommunications

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2024-2027 (Costa Rica)
fr1_justif: The Accountability guiding principle of the Strategy states that actors involved in the lifecycle of AI systems must implement robust oversight mechanisms including impact assessments particularly for decisions with significant consequences for human rights and environmental well-being (p.33). The Costa Rica Reference Framework further specifies that an impact assessment must be conducted as a mitigation measure before implementing any AI solution identified as high-risk (p.42).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Science Innovation Technology and Telecommunications

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems
fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2024-2027 (Costa Rica)
fr1_justif: The ENIA 2024-2027 addresses this indicator narrowly. At p.32 the strategy requires that any automated decision affecting citizens in the governmental sphere be accompanied by a clear and accessible explanation of how the decision was made including the data and algorithms used. This provision applies exclusively to the public sector. The ENIA does not establish a public registry or inventory of government AI systems nor does it require proactive disclosure of high-risk system information or impact assessment results.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Science Innovation Technology and Telecommunications

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2024-2027 (Costa Rica)
fr1_justif: Costa Rica's National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2024-2027 (ENIA) launched by MICITT in October 2024 explicitly prioritises AI capacity building for public officials. The Training and Education Enabling Mechanism (p.55) establishes that continuous training programmes are essential to keep public officials updated on AI advancements. Axis 5 (p.68) further sets equipping public officials with tools to understand and apply AI in their fields as a strategic objective. Both provisions directly address public sector skills development.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Science Innovation Technology and Telecommunications
