fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2024-2027 (Costa Rica)
fr1_justif: The Strategy establishes continuous training for workers across all productive sectors as a cross-cutting principle recognising the need to equip the workforce with AI-related competencies (p.35). It further states that educational efforts must be directed toward AI training to ensure the workforce is prepared for future challenges (p.57). The new productive and labour model identified in the Strategy calls for policies facilitating the acquisition of skills and competencies related to AI across the labour market (p.61). As a non-binding strategy these provisions constitute recommendations rather than legally enforceable obligations.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Science Innovation Technology and Telecommunications

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2024-2027 (Costa Rica)
fr1_justif: To guarantee safety and cybersecurity mechanisms must be implemented to allow for supervision intervention and if necessary the safe deactivation of systems. AI security and cybersecurity also entail the creation of resilient systems this includes adopting security-by-design practices implementing robust access controls encrypting data and others (p.33-34). The Cybersecurity Directorate of the Ministry must focus on mitigating cyber risks and act as a comprehensive response center (p.43). This office will be strengthened to: Development of Forensic Capabilities Incident Response in Various Contexts Efficient Response to AI Incidents Training and Multidisciplinary Collaboration and others (p.44-46).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Science Innovation Technology and Telecommunications (and The Cybersecurity Directorate which is part of the Ministry)

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2024-2027 (Costa Rica)
fr1_justif: The framework requests entities developing and implementing AI to provide comprehensible context-appropriate information that explains the capabilities and limitations of AI crucial to enable users to understand how and when IA impacts them (p.31). It further states that any automated decision in the public sector should be accompanied by a clear and accessible explanation of how the decision was made. And for the private sector they must inform customers when AI is used and provide options for users to opt out of being affected by these systems (p.32). And it also contains mitigation measures (p.42) such as periodic reviews by ethical and technical committees and mechanisms for correction and continuous improvement.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Science Innovation Technology and Telecommunications

fr1_title: Côte d'Ivoire's National Artificial Intelligence Strategy (SNIA) for 2030
fr1_justif: SNIA 2030 takes a concrete and targeted approach to AI literacy within the formal education system promoting measures to develop learners' understanding use evaluation and creation of AI while implicitly integrating ethical dimensions. These measures focus on the gradual integration of AI into curricula early awareness-raising among young people and training in technical and critical skills: Page 13 (section on “Training and skills development”); Pages 17-19 (sections on “Establishing specialised training programmes” and “Raising awareness and inspiring younger generations”): There are plans to introduce “basic AI concepts into school curricula with practical activities such as coding or robotics” (p. 18).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr2_title: Circular No. 0320: Maintaining the teaching of ICT in the National Education system in Côte d'Ivoire
fr2_justif: The gradual introduction of Information and Communication Technologies for Education (ICTE) from pre-primary and primary school onwards is a programme launched by the Ministry of National Education and Literacy (MENA) for the 2025-2026 school year. It began at the start of the 2025-2026 school year (early September 2025) with digital technology being integrated as a subject or teaching tool from pre-primary (nursery) and primary school onwards. This includes the introduction of ICT (basic computer skills digital tools digital literacy) as recommended by the National Education and Literacy Council (EGENA). The Ministry's circular and the associated programme set the foundations for a digital culture (simple coding use of digital tools).
fr2_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr2_body_name: National AI Agency/Agence National de lIA (ANIA)

fr1_title: Côte d'Ivoire's National Artificial Intelligence Strategy (SNIA) for 2030
fr1_justif: The SNIA 2030 addresses cultural and linguistic diversity in the context of AI in an integrated and positive way considering AI as a tool for preserving enhancing and promoting multicultural heritage. This approach is grounded in the principles of inclusion equity and ethics in order to avoid bias and adapt AI to local realities. Pages 3 and 4 (Section I. Introduction and Context): AI is seen as a lever for inclusive transformation. Page 11 (Section II. Vision Mission and Objectives): The vision explicitly includes promoting AI to enhance cultural and linguistic diversity. Page 37 includes inclusion as a core Developing multilingual tools is also one of the core strategic recommendations on page 40.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: National AI Agency/Agence National de lIA (ANIA)

fr1_title: Côte d'Ivoire's National Artificial Intelligence Strategy (SNIA) for 2030
fr1_justif: SNIA 2030 considers fairness and non-discrimination to be cross-cutting and mandatory principles in the development and use of AI. Equity and non-discrimination are integrated as fundamental principles (p. 13) concrete sectoral objectives (pp. 36–49) and operational governance commitments (pp. 49–54 58) with tools such as audits SafeAI certification and independent collaboration to prevent bias and discrimination. This version retains your original structure while incorporating the additions requested by the reviewer word for word (pp. 49 51 54 collaboration with independent organizations) with clear references and logical progression.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: National AI Agency/Agence National de lIA (ANIA)

fr1_title: Côte d'Ivoire's National Artificial Intelligence Strategy (SNIA) for 2030
fr1_justif: SNIA 2030 addresses the issue of gender equality in the context of AI in a cross-cutting and integrated manner mainly through the ‘Inclusion’ pillar and social equity objectives. Analysis of SNIA (keywords: SNIA and gender equality) shows that it does not devote a chapter exclusively to gender but integrates gender equality as a fundamental principle to avoid bias and promote inclusive AI specifically targeting women (and young people) as priority groups for access to AI-related skills innovation and economic opportunities (page 42 section on the Inclusion pillar). It explicitly plans to ‘actively include women and young people in technology initiatives and AI training programmes’.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: National AI Agency/Agence National de lIA (ANIA)

fr1_title: Côte d'Ivoire's National Artificial Intelligence Strategy (SNIA) for 2030
fr1_justif: SNIA 2030 addresses the issue of human oversight and human in the loop/meaningful human control in a relatively clear and explicit manner but remains at the level of principles and future commitments rather than detailed operational implementation. The approach focuses on protecting fundamental rights preventing fully automated decisions in sensitive areas and ensuring meaningful human control when AI systems have a significant impact on individuals. These can be found in the Guiding Principles (Section II – Vision Mission and Principles – around p. 13): Principle No. 1: Priority to humans) and Pillar 1 – Investment (Section IV around p. 32).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: National AI Agency/Agence National de lIA (ANIA)

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Côte d'Ivoire's National Artificial Intelligence Strategy (SNIA) for 2030
fr1_justif: SNIA 2030 addresses the development of AI skills in the public sector in a prioritised and structured manner mainly through its ‘Investment’ pillar (including infrastructure and human talent) and phase 1 of implementation (6 months after launch). The aim is to modernise public administration through AI (simplification of procedures e-services) filling the skills gaps among public servants to ensure an effective and inclusive digital transformation. ‘Investment’ pillar (talent and skills). To this end there are plans to map AI skills in order to identify the needs of the public sector (administrative staff) and develop appropriate training programmes.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: National AI Agency/Agence National de lIA (ANIA)

fr1_title: Côte d'Ivoire's National Artificial Intelligence Strategy (SNIA) for 2030
fr1_justif: SNIA2030 takes a cross-cutting and proactive approach to safety and security. It does so through four guiding principles: Page 13 (Section II. Guiding Principles) to guide the overall strategy emphasising risk prevention and the reliability of AI systems. Concrete safety and security measures:  Page 33 (Investment pillar section on innovation and infrastructure): Implementation of the SafeAI certification process.  Page 37 (Inclusion pillar section on sectoral applications): Use of AI for risk management and public safety. Page 52 (Governance pillar section on ethical safeguards): Definition of safeguards accompanying the adoption of AI including algorithmic transparency regular audits and the protection of citizens' rights.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: National AI Agency/Agence National de lIA (ANIA)

fr1_title: Côte d'Ivoire's National Artificial Intelligence Strategy (SNIA) for 2030
fr1_justif: SNIA 2030 addresses transparency and explainability as essential pillars of ethical and responsible AI governance. These concepts are addressed in a cross-cutting manner primarily in the guiding principles Pillar 1 (Investment in the ecosystem and regulatory/ethical framework (pages 13-14) and Pillar 3 (Ethical and effective governance (pages 49-54)) with an emphasis on the need to build public trust and reduce the risks associated with opaque (‘black box’) systems. The approach is forward-looking declarative and sets out principles future obligations (audits national standards) and commitments for high-risk systems without going into the technical details of immediate implementation.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: National AI Agency/Agence National de lIA (ANIA)

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Bots fake accounts and automated conduct are mechanisms of disinformation campaigns (recital 85) and as such rules are needed to combat them (recital 9). Systemic risk assessments apply to large-scale online platforms and search engines (art. 34) and explicitly include the dissemination or amplification of misleading or fraudulent content and the negative effects on gender-based violence. This includes recommendation systems and other relevant algorithmic systems (art. 34). Mitigation measures include testing and adapting algorithmic and recommendation systems; speeding up the processing of notifications of illegal hate speech and cyber violence; and labeling content generated or manipulated by AI (art.35).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU GDPR
fr1_justif: The law provides a layered redress framework that applies in AI contexts whenever personal data is processed. Data subjects harmed by automated processing based on AI-driven profiling and decision-making hold the right to express their point of view and the right to contest the decision (art. 22); presenting complaints to supervisory authorities (art. 77) resorting to legal measures against gaps in the authorities (art. 78) and bringing proceedings directly against controllers or processors (art. 79). Organizations may be mandated to exercise the rights collectively on behalf of data subjects (art. 80). Supervisory authorities may impose administrative fines because of the violations of core provisions (art. 83).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: This Regulation shall not apply to the processing of personal data by Member States in the context of carrying out activities relating to the Union's common foreign and security policy (recital 16). The processing of personal data by Member States does not apply to activities related to the protection and prevention of threats to public security (Art. 2).
fr1_body_name: The law establishes the European Data Protection Board is comprised of representatives from the supervisory authority of each member states (Arts 68) acting independently (Art 69) providing guidelines recommendations and binding decisions to ensure consistence across Member States (Art 70) The Member State must to establish am independent supervisory authority responsible for monitoring and enforcing the regulation (Art 51)

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Online platform service providers must ensure the privacy security and protection of minors in their services (Art. 28) including prohibiting advertising based on profiling through the automated processing of data about children (Art. 28). Terms and conditions must be understandable to minors (Art. 14) ensuring a high level of privacy and security by default based on good practices (recitals 70 71). Large-scale online platforms and large-scale search engines systematic risk assessment applies periodically evaluating potential negative effects on minors (Art. 34). Mitigation measures are determined: age verification parental control tools abuse reporting tools and support for minors (Art. 35). Voluntary standards apply (Art. 44).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The law focuses on the risk classification system (Art. 6) and the requirements imposed on high-risk AI systems. It requires suppliers of high-risk AI systems to establish implement document and maintain a risk (and damage) management system throughout the system's lifecycle (Art. 9). Training validation and test datasets must conform to data governance in terms of representation error-freeness completeness contextualization and the function the system will be used for handling special personal data for monitoring purposes and detecting and correcting biases. High-risk AI systems must have consistency throughout the lifecycle by requiring appropriate levels of accuracy and robustness and reducing the risk of bias (art.15).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act addresses gender equality through non-discrimination and fundamental rights provisions. Training datasets for high-risk AI must be representative avoinding the discriminatory outcomes and protecting atributes explicitly related gender (Art. 10). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights impact assessments before deploying high-risk AI incluing gender-based discrimination (Art. 27). Equality bodies are granted market surveillance powers enabling them to investigate and sanction AI systems producing discriminatory outcomes (Art. 74).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act establishes requirements to ensure human control over high-risk AI systems requiring providers to design systems that enable natural persons with a minimum level of comprehensibility to monitor outputs detect anomalies and intervene or interrupt operations when necessary (art. 14). Deployers must assign oversight to competent natural persons with authority to act (art. 26). For general-purpose AI with systemic risk adversarial testing and incident reporting reinforce human control (Arts. 55 73). Additional oversight provisions are set in the fundamental rights impact assessment (art. 27) and Annex (IV) 2(e) as part of technical documentation requirements (art. 11).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Law establishes an assessment architecture according to the type and level of risk of the system. High-risk AI suppliers must identify analyze and mitigate risks during the design development and implementation phases within a risk management system (Arti.9) with comprehensive technical documentation (Art.15) and monitoring of operations and anomalies (Art. 26). For AI implementers in the public sector a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment applies before implementing high-risk systems listed in Annex 3 (Art.27). Compliance assessment must include self-assessment or third-party audits depending on the criticality of the system (Art. 43 47). There are ex-post assessments and post-commercialization monitoring (Art.72).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act classifies systems for recruitment promotion termination and worker management as high-risk AI systems (Annex III(4)) (art. 6). Prohibited AI practices include bans on emotion-recognition in the workplace (art. 5). Providers must implement risk-management addressing discrimination (Art. 9) ensure representative datasets and bias-control (art. 10) and maintain technical documentation (Arts. 11). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights assessments before deployment (art. 27). Art. 26 (7-9) establishes safeguards: workers must be informed and may request explanations of AI use. However the Act does not regulate pay conditions or compensation so it mitigates risks without constituting a full labour-rights regime.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Recovery and Resilience Plan (2021-2026) (Croatia)
fr1_justif: Although the framework does not explicitly refer to AI it has been in practice applied to AI-related upskilling. The framework establishes the voucher system for adult education as a financial support instrument of the Croatian Employment Service enabling (un)employed persons to acquire green digital and other in-demand skills aligned with the Croatian Qualifications Framework (measure C4.1.R3.). Participants choose the training program and the voucher value is paid directly to the provider (p. 935). The system supports AI upskilling as several AI-specific courses are available in the voucher course database (https://web.archive.org/web/20260108203555/https://vauceri.hzz.hr/katalog-vjestina/popis-vjestina-i-programa/).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Safety and security is core to the Act. It establishes a risk-based framework for risk identification and mitigation (Art. 9) oversight measures (Art. 14) and robustness and accuracy requirements (Art. 15). To ensure ongoing safety the Act mandates technical documentation and record-keeping (Arts. 11–12) and in certain high risk situations a conformity assessment procedure prior to market placement (Art. 43). Post-market obligations in Arts. 72–73 require providers and deployers to monitor performance report serious incidents and take corrective action. For GPAI models posing systemic risks Arts. 55–56 impose additional safety and cybersecurity duties including model evaluation and risk mitigation following the Code of Practice.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Articles 11 12 and 13 establish several transparency and documentation obligations for AI developers and deployers. High-risk AI systems must include clear information on their capabilities limitations and intended purpose (Art. 13) and providers must maintain comprehensive technical documentation and automatic logging to ensure traceability (Arts. 11–12). These measures enable deployers to better understand the systems and authorities to audit or investigate when necessary. There are disclosure obligations that require notification when individuals interact with AI systems particularly chatbots and emotion recognition tools (Art. 50). Also for general-purpose AI in terms of model documentation and summary of training data (Art. 53).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy of the Dominican Republic
fr1_justif: The Dominican Republic's National AI Strategy has a dedicated pillar on talent and education with concrete AI-literacy actions: integrating age-appropriate AI modules in schools (p 49) training public-school teachers and university faculty (p 49-50) and creating secondary/vocational AI courses (p 50) to build critical thinking and STEM skills. It adds certification programs targeting 10000 students new degrees scholarships and a women-in-AI training track (p 50-52).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: General Directorate of Innovation and Digital Development

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy of the Dominican Republic
fr1_justif: The Dominican Republic's National AI Strategy (ENIA) acknowledges AI-driven threats to democracy specifically citing fake news and election hacking (p. 18). To counter these the strategy aims to implement policies that enhance online content integrity focusing on hate speech disinformation false information and identity falsification while clearly defining intermediary responsibilities (p. 39). Furthermore the strategy addresses AI-enabled violence by proposing both a domestic ban and a regional treaty to prohibit autonomous weapons (p. 40).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: General Directorate of Innovation and Digital Development

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy of the Dominican Republic
fr1_justif: The Dominican Republic's National Artificial Intelligence Strategy addresses user redress by updating Law 172-13 to enhance user control over personal data including rights to access modify limit or erase data along with clear notice and consent requirements (pp. 36-37). The strategy commits to an Ethical AI Code that will inform when AI is used for profiling decision-making or during interactions. This code will establish mechanisms of supervision compensation and avenues of recourse allowing regulators and courts to request system information (p. 38). It plans to implement quality monitoring incorporating transparency criteria and citizen feedback to improve complaint handling and facilitate corrective actions in AI (p. 42).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Dominican Republics National AI Strategy establishes a General Directorate of Innovation and Digital Development through Objective 111 to coordinate implement and monitor the National AI Strategy and proper AI use nationwide It outlines roles for inter-agency coherence and follow-up on responsible AI use but authority timelines and enforcement tools depend on the forthcoming General Law on Innovation and Digital Development and related decrees (page 37)

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy of the Dominican Republic
fr1_justif: The Dominican Republic’s National AI Strategy partially addresses cultural and linguistic diversity by adopting inclusion and diversity as guiding principles (page 52) promoting multilingualism in regional interoperability standards (page 65) referencing Dominican diaspora communities (page 53) and signaling participation in regional AI ethics forums (page 64). However it sets no concrete measures such as targets budgets localization requirements or minority-language datasets so recognition remains high-level and non-operational.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: General Directorate of Innovation and Digital Development

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy of the Dominican Republic
fr1_justif: The Dominican Republic's National Artificial Intelligence Strategy prioritizes the environment as a key mission. It proposes initiatives to promote sustainability such as creating ecological open environmental datasets for research through HUB #YoSoyFuturoRD. This initiative will allocate essential financial resources to projects tackling high-impact environmental challenges (p54). Furthermore the strategy advocates for the development of regional data repositories (p56) to house climate and environmental data which will be used to inform policies on adaptation and resilience (p65).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: General Directorate of Innovation and Digital Development

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy of the Dominican Republic
fr1_justif: The Dominican Republic’s National AI Strategy partially addresses fairness and non-discrimination by adopting human-rights and inclusion principles (pages 37and 39) committing to update personal-data rules (page 37) creating an ethical AI code with transparency and redress (page 39) and setting pre-deployment review via a regulatory sandbox (page38). However it sets no concrete measures such as legally defined fairness metrics mandatory bias/impact assessments or protected-group monitoring and reporting so recognition remains high-level and non-operational.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: General Directorate of Innovation and Digital Development

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy of the Dominican Republic
fr1_justif: The Dominican Republic’s National Artificial Intelligence Strategy addresses gender equality by creating a women’s AI talent training program under objective 4.2.5 and the action plan led by the Ministry of Women; committing to detect and mitigate bias and algorithmic discrimination; aligning AI ethics with UNESCO guidance; requiring transparency and remedies when AI profiles or makes decisions about people; and expanding inclusive education and workforce upskilling to increase women’s participation in the AI ecosystem.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: General Directorate of Innovation and Digital Development

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy of the Dominican Republic
fr1_justif: The National Artificial Intelligence Strategy of the Dominican Republic addresses AI public procurement in several sections. On p.42 (Objective 2.1.4) it promotes using AI and e-procurement in procurement processes to prevent fraud and enhance transparency. On p.43 (Objective 2.1.7) it proposes transforming procurement into a catalyst for the AI ecosystem by including AI criteria in contracting establishing certified vendor lists and requiring special approval processes. On p.72 it provides for a resolution to include AI systems in procurement and contracting regulations.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: General Directorate of Innovation and Digital Development

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy of the Dominican Republic
fr1_justif: The Dominican Republic’s National AI Strategy directly tackles public-sector skills gaps. Under the Smart Government pillar it commits the State to revitalize the development of capacities competencies and technical knowledge within government so it can become an anticipatory innovative administration (p 35). It establishes a training track to prepare public servants to plan design and use AI in public-service delivery through a programme of master’s degrees courses and diplomas that form functional specialists in applying AI to public services (p 44).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: General Directorate of Innovation and Digital Development

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy of the Dominican Republic
fr1_justif: The Dominican Republic’s National AI Strategy links this indicator directly to Objective 4.3 which aims to prepare the Dominican workforce for the future of work. Under this objective it proposes a national certification programme targeting 10000 learners (p. 50) new tertiary programmes such as a Data Science BSc and an AI MSc to expand the talent pipeline (p. 51) and a national Upskilling and Reskilling programme aligned with labor market needs (p. 52). It also includes mandatory training for public servants and a Center of Excellence in Public Administration to develop functional AI specialists and support implementation across government (p. 44) alongside fiscal incentives to encourage private-sector investment in worker retraining
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: General Directorate of Innovation and Digital Development

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy of the Dominican Republic
fr1_justif: The Dominican Republic’s National Artificial Intelligence Strategy addresses safety and security through several key concepts and actions. It highlights the role of cybersecurity as essential for national protection and assigns the National Cybersecurity Center to deploy AI systems to detect and respond to cyber threats (page 61). It prohibits the development and use of autonomous weapons promoting a regional treaty against them (page 40). The Strategy also emphasizes protecting sensitive data and ensuring integrity of online content to combat disinformation and hate speech (pages 38–40).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: General Directorate of Innovation and Digital Development

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy of the Dominican Republic
fr1_justif: The Dominican Republic’s National Artificial Intelligence Strategy addresses transparency and explainability by committing to update Personal Data Protection Law 172-13 (page 37); creating an ethical AI code with user notice disclosure of AI use and redress/supervision (pages 39-40); strengthening open data policy with APIs data-quality monitoring and a National Interoperability Strategy (pages 40-41); defining access/retention/exclusion rules in a federated data architecture through the HUB de Datos (pages 61); and mandating OGTIC’s quarterly reviews and impact evaluations (pages 87–89).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: General Directorate of Innovation and Digital Development

fr1_title: Proposal for the Promotion of Development and Ethical and Responsible Use of Artificial Intelligence in Ecuador
fr1_justif: The document addresses AI literacy through structured education and talent development measures. On p.5 (1.1 Development of Human Talent) it mandates integrating AI into curricula from basic to higher education creating AI university programs with ethics components and establishing certification programs on ethical AI use. Under AXIS 1 (p.9) it sets measurable targets including a 30% increase in STEM graduates with AI training. Page 4 (G. Awareness and Education) also promotes digital skills for informed AI use.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Proposal for the Promotion of Development and Ethical and Responsible Use of Artificial Intelligence in Ecuador
fr1_justif: The strategy seeks to establish an ethical regulatory and operational framework for AI that protects human rights promotes environmental sustainability and fosters social inclusion. Based on principles such as D-Proportionality and Harmlessness which ensures AI systems are appropriate to legitimate objectives and do not cause avoidable harm to people society or the environment; also E-Responsibility and Accountability requiring developers and users of AI systems to act responsibly and with respect for the environment. And Principle H promotes the development and use of AI systems that support the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and encourages ongoing evaluation of their impacts.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Proposal for the Promotion of Development and Ethical and Responsible Use of Artificial Intelligence in Ecuador
fr1_justif: The strategy mentions in Principle B Equity and Non-Discrimination that developers and implementers must safeguard equity and implement measures such as sufficient training processes to mitigate and prevent discriminatory or biased results ensuring that the technology avoids social racial and gender-based economic exclusion.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Proposal for the Promotion of Development and Ethical and Responsible Use of Artificial Intelligence in Ecuador
fr1_justif: This strategy currently under development highlights in page 3 section B the importance of placing equity and the fight against all types of discrimination at the center of how artificial intelligence systems are designed and implemented. It states that developers and implementers must safeguard equity and adopt measures to mitigate and prevent the generation of discriminatory or biased outcomes. The  strategy makes a specific reference to the need to avoid gender-based exclusion. Also one of its Lines of action number 20 promotes the inclusion of women in the AI ecosystem.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Proposal for the Promotion of Development and Ethical and Responsible Use of Artificial Intelligence in Ecuador
fr1_justif: This strategy includes among its principles I. Human supervision and decisions indicating that widespread human supervision is promoted in the development and implementation of systems based on artificial intelligence as necessary according to their level of risk to correct errors or minimize the violation of the principles stated here ensuring that ethical and legal responsibility can be clearly attributed to natural or legal persons at any stage of the life cycle of these systems as appropriate.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Proposal for the Promotion of Development and Ethical and Responsible Use of Artificial Intelligence in Ecuador
fr1_justif: This strategy includes Axis 2. Adoption of AI which promotes research development and innovation with local impact prioritizing the impact assessment of the use of AI based on the development of technical standards and certifications. According to the public consultation link the draft was published on August 1 2025 https://aportecivico.gobiernoelectronico.gob.ec/legislation/processes/78/draft_versions/99
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Organic Law Project for the Regulation and Promotion of Artificial Intelligence in Ecuador
fr1_justif: The proposed law includes a chapter on education in the context of AI and in its article 51 page 60 indicates that continuing education professional development and retraining programs in AI will be promoted with an emphasis on applied learning innovation and entrepreneurship to facilitate the adaptation of workers to technological changes and take advantage of the opportunities of the digital economy. Article 57 (p.63) mandates initial and continuing teacher training programs to develop AI competencies including fundamental AI knowledge learning analytics interpretation critical risk assessment pedagogical integration strategies and ethical/data protection practices.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Proposal for the Promotion of Development and Ethical and Responsible Use of Artificial Intelligence in Ecuador
fr1_justif: This strategy includes among its principles J. Transparency stating that transparency is promoted in the development and implementation of AI-based systems with the purpose of empowering users and those affected to make informed decisions about their interaction with said systems. In addition Axis 3. Governance and Ethics of this strategy includes line of action 3.4 Supervision Regulation and Control of AI Systems which establishes the development of audit protocols that allow for the verification of transparency traceability explainability and mitigation of biases in algorithms used by institutions and companies.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Egypt National Artificial Intelligence Strategy (Second Edition) (2025-2030)
fr1_justif: The doc. promotes AI literacy under the third pillar (p.19) AI for Human Capacity Building (AI4H) to address the educational and professional gaps. Various talent development capacity building initiatives have been adopted across the country. the 'Ecosystem' and 'Talents' pillars. Initiative 17 'Public Awareness of AI Development' (P.79) is central outlining actions like 'E-117-1 Develop Positive AI News Publication Initiative' and 'E-117-2 Develop AI Awareness Programs in Schools' to dispel misconceptions and integrate AI education. Additionally Initiative 18 'Senior AI Expert Cultivation and Attraction' (P.81) aims to 'Improve the basic knowledge of citizens of AI-based tools' thereby enhancing the public's overall AI literacy.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Legislative Framework for Artificial Intelligence (Egypt)
fr1_justif: The study addresses (Access to Redress and Remedy) through provisions on civil and criminal liability (pp. 31–32). It assigns responsibility to AI developers owners and users for harms resulting from AI systems and affirms victims’ rights to compensation p.32. The framework emphasizes compliance transparency and risk assessment requiring companies to document AI operations and report incidents. It also provides for administrative and criminal sanctions for violations ensuring accountability. However while it establishes foundational principles for redress it lacks detailed procedural mechanisms for individuals to file complaints or seek remedies directly for AI-related harms.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Egyptian Charter for Responsible AI (2023 v1.0)
fr1_justif: The Egyptian Charter for Responsible AI addresses children's rights by emphasizing the protection of vulnerable groups from the potential harms of AI systems. According to S 'General Guidelines' on page 3 Guideline 3 states 'Special considerations must be taken to protect vulnerable and marginalized groups such as children PWDs and those with lower economic or educational level.' These considerations include 'checking potential data bias tuning system parameters periodically and preferring development team diversity.' Additionally Guideline 1 on the same page and section highlights that the 'primary goal of using Al in Government is the well-being of citizens' implicitly including children.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Egypt National Artificial Intelligence Strategy (Second Edition) (2025-2030)
fr1_justif: The document emphasizes Egypt's commitment to cultural and linguistic diversity by prioritizing the development of tailored AI solutions. This includes significant investment in Arabic Natural Language Processing NLP p. 22 capabilities with focus on achieving high accuracy in Egyptian accent recognition p.19. The strategy outlines the development of a National Foundation Model p.30 that is Arabic-language specific ensuring that AI systems can effectively process and understand vast amounts of Arabic language data. The document highlights practical applications such as multi-lingual customer support chatbot p.31. the strategy acknowledges broader diversity by including Representation of Different Ethnicities and Social Groups P.89
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Consultation on Draft GSR-23 Best Practice Guidelines (Egypt)
fr1_justif: The document highlights the need for new dimensions in ex-ante impact assessments and ex-post evaluations to include economic environmental social and health impacts especially with the proliferation of new and emerging technologies like AI. [p.2.New dimensions for ex-ante impact assessments and ex-post evaluations are needed. The economic environmental social and health impacts of major new proposed policy and regulatory initiatives especially with the proliferation of new and emerging technologies are needed...such technologies could pose risks beyond the tradition economic and environmental impact...e.g. attention-grabbing strategies impacts and the use of artificial intelligence (AI)]
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Egyptian Charter for Responsible AI (2023 v1.0)
fr1_justif: The doc. addresses Fairness by mandating that AI systems avoid harming individuals especially vulnerable groups through measures like checking for data bias and promoting diverse development teams (P3 3). It also ensures individuals can challenge adverse AI outcomes (P3 4) and provides policies for unauthorized AI use (P3 5). Implementation guidelines instruct developers to use representative beneficiary samples (P5 6) consider cultural impacts in NLP applications (P6 9) and ensure data inputs are comprehensive disaggregated and corrected for distortions (P6 11) with regular monitoring for data drift (P6 12). Government AI projects require impact assessments for data diversity to minimize bias (P7 14g)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Egyptian Charter for Responsible AI (2023 v1.0)
fr1_justif: The document strongly emphasizes Human Oversight and Determination. It explicitly states that 'Ultimate responsibility and accountability for the behavior and outcomes of an Al system must always lie with natural or legal persons' clarifying that AI systems should not have legal personality (Page 4 Section 10). Furthermore it guarantees 'Final human determination is always in place' meaning humans are always in charge of decisions able to modify stop or retire AI systems (Page 4 Section 11). This ensures AI remains a tool under human control particularly in critical areas.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Egyptian Charter for Responsible AI (2023 v1.0)
fr1_justif: The document emphasizes thorough impact assessments for government AI projects to ensure maximum benefit and adherence to responsible AI development. This is detailed in 'Implementation Guidelines' (page 7 section 14). These assessments should evaluate problem suitability for AI data readiness process engineering financial social and environmental impacts and data diversity to minimize bias. Projects are approved only if benefits outweigh costs.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Egyptian Charter for Responsible AI (2023 v1.0)
fr1_justif: The document directly addresses protection against job displacement from automation. P.3. Sec. 6. explicitly states that if job losses are an unavoidable consequence of beneficial AI system owners (government private sector) must ensure a fair transition for workers. This includes providing training programs throughout their working life offering support for those affected by displacement and 0facilitating access to new labor market opportunities page 4p;ji. The objective is to mitigate the negative impact on the workforce by enabling adaptation and re-entry into an evolving job market.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Egypt National Artificial Intelligence Strategy (Second Edition) (2025-2030)
fr1_justif: Egypt’s AI strategy emphasizes broad capacity building across sectors. It calls for developing responsible AI training for government industry and the public and supporting AI education and research (P.48). The strategy recommends to establish pilot programs to train official data managers in technology use and data-quality control (P.58). Specialized AI training is also recommended for energy-sector workers (P.71) alongside workshops and programs for government employees and youth at Creativa Innovation Hubs (P.20).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Egypt National Artificial Intelligence Strategy (Second Edition) (2025-2030)
fr1_justif: The Egypt AI Strategy strongly focuses on upskilling and reskilling the workforce to meet AI industry needs. ITI and NTI (P.19) run specialized programs with international partners to upgrade digital and AI skills while Creativa Hubs train youth and government employees to adapt to new technologies. A national talent drive (P.51) provides targeted training for advanced AI development. Data governance programs (P.57) reskill managers and professionals to use data effectively. Sectoral initiatives (P.71) upskill energy workers and teacher training (P.81) modernizes AI education delivery. The AI Certification System (P.85) formalizes continuous upskilling and professional growth.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Egyptian Charter for Responsible AI (2023 v1.0)
fr1_justif: The Charter prioritizes safety and security by mandating 'robust secure and safe' AI systems throughout their lifecycle (P5p1) ensuring proper function even under misuse. It introduces certification for 'safety transparency robustness and reliability' (P4s8). Additional measures are required for 'sensitive or mission-critical AI applications' to ensure data protection and harm avoidance (P5s3). 'final human determination is always in place' for 'security and safety' empowering humans to modify or stop AI systems (P4s11). A systematic risk management approach is advocated covering 'digital security safety' (P5s7). Data use necessitates pre-authorization anonymization encryption and adherence to security standards(p6s11).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Egyptian Charter for Responsible AI (2023 v1.0)
fr1_justif: The doc. addresses a fundamental right for end-users is knowing when they are interacting with an AI system not a human as seen with automated call centers (P3S2). Furthermore individuals adversely affected by AI systems have the right to challenge outcomes with access to 'plain and easy-to-understand information on the factors and the logic that served as the basis for the prediction recommendation or decision' (P3S4). Developers are obligated to 'always strive to provide transparent and explainable Al solutions' acknowledging potential tradeoffs between accuracy and explainability (P6S8). In cases of doubt simpler more explainable models are preferred without  compromising minimum quality (p7s15)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Promotion and Technologies Act of El Salvador
fr1_justif: Art. 2(c) establishes the integration of AI education within the national system and encourages private sector investment in training. To operationalize this Art. 8(e) empowers the National Agency for AI to collaborate with the Ministry of Education. This partnership aims to incorporate AI and related technologies at all educational levels ensuring a comprehensive approach that balances technical competence with ethical considerations. The focus is on fostering digital literacy transparency and innovation preparing both students and professionals for a changing technological landscape through the responsible use and deployment of AI systems
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Promotion and Technologies Act of El Salvador
fr1_justif: The Artificial Intelligence and Technology Promotion Law establishes in Article 21 that the State will facilitate the use of renewable energy sources as part of actions to promote environmental sustainability in the development of AI.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Promotion and Technologies Act of El Salvador
fr1_justif: Article 20 of the Law requires that those who conduct AI activities in El Salvador ensure the ethical and responsible use of AI systems throughout the system's lifecycle. In Article 4 the Law defines bias as any systematic error in the results of an AI system that disproportionately favors or disadvantages certain groups or individuals on the basis of characteristics such as gender race or socioeconomic status.  In general Article 5 of the Law establishes non-discrimination inclusion and equity as its guiding ethical principles.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Promotion and Technologies Act of El Salvador
fr1_justif: The Artificial Intelligence and Technology Promotion Law establishes in Article 4 that the definition of bias within the framework of the law refers to: 'Any systematic error in the results of an AI system that disproportionately favors or disadvantages certain groups or individuals based on characteristics such as gender race or socioeconomic status.' Article 2 - Purpose -  states that the purpose of the Law is to address the risks of bias in the use of artificial intelligence by citizens.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Provisions relating to the implementation of Artificial Intelligence and Technologies (Salvador)
fr1_justif: Art. 10 & 11 mandate human oversight and registration for AI in sensitive sectors. Art. 24 grants a Right to Notification for automated decisions affecting rights or finances requiring an explanation of the AI's role and a challenge mechanism. However Art. 26 limits the impact of 'meaningful human review' by stating that ANIA will only issue non-binding illustrative guidance on these processes. This creates a regulatory gap: while users have the right to challenge a decision (Art. 24) the standards for how a human should actually review and potentially override that AI decision (Art. 26) are not mandatory leaving the quality of human intervention to the entity's discretion.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Promotion and Technologies Act of El Salvador
fr1_justif: Art. 8 empowers ANIA to supervise compliance and report violations while Art. 17 mandates a flexible Risk Assessment Framework for systems handling sensitive data. Art. 19 provides liability safeguards for registered entities (sandboxes research and third-party misuse) and Art. 20 establishes shared responsibility across the AI lifecycle. Crucially Art. 27 sets a strict 90-day deadline from the law's enactment for ANIA to issue technical safety criteria and registration procedures.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Agenda Digital El Salvador 2020-2030
fr1_justif: The framework addresses the indicator by explicitly referencing emerging technologies including AI (p. 9) and linking them to national development priorities. It highlights the need for public servants to develop the skills required to deliver high-quality user-centred digital services (p. 21 Section 3 “Public Servant Capabilities”) supported by measures such as a coordinated public service career plan (p. 26) and training partnerships with organisations and institutions (p. 27). From the outset it frames “robotics and artificial intelligence” as key opportunities thereby grounding its capacity-building efforts in the broader integration of AI and related technologies across sectors.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Promotion and Technologies Act of El Salvador
fr1_justif: Art. 5 mandates that all AI activities respect principles like equity transparency and non-discrimination. Under Art. 17 ANIA will establish a Comprehensive Risk Assessment Framework mandatory for systems handling personal or confidential data. This framework is designed to be flexible integrating future international safety standards. Furthermore Art. 20 defines shared responsibility across the AI lifecycle: developers must meet ethical/technical standards implementers ensure correct deployment and service providers maintain secure infrastructure. This ensures that every stakeholder from creator to end-user is accountable for the ethical safety and responsible use of AI systems
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Promotion and Technologies Act of El Salvador
fr1_justif: Art. 5 establishes core ethical principles including fairness transparency and non-discrimination. Art. 4(h) defines 'Explainability' as the system's ability to provide understandable process explanations. Under Art. 20 responsibility is shared across the lifecycle: developers must meet ethical standards while implementers and providers ensure security and correct use. Crucially Art. 18 mandates that commercial or service-related AI decisions be notified to users with transparent explanations.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Bots fake accounts and automated conduct are mechanisms of disinformation campaigns (recital 85) and as such rules are needed to combat them (recital 9). Systemic risk assessments apply to large-scale online platforms and search engines (art. 34) and explicitly include the dissemination or amplification of misleading or fraudulent content and the negative effects on gender-based violence. This includes recommendation systems and other relevant algorithmic systems (art. 34). Mitigation measures include testing and adapting algorithmic and recommendation systems; speeding up the processing of notifications of illegal hate speech and cyber violence; and labeling content generated or manipulated by AI (art.35).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU GDPR
fr1_justif: The law provides a layered redress framework that applies in AI contexts whenever personal data is processed. Data subjects harmed by automated processing based on AI-driven profiling and decision-making hold the right to express their point of view and the right to contest the decision (art. 22); presenting complaints to supervisory authorities (art. 77) resorting to legal measures against gaps in the authorities (art. 78) and bringing proceedings directly against controllers or processors (art. 79). Organizations may be mandated to exercise the rights collectively on behalf of data subjects (art. 80). Supervisory authorities may impose administrative fines because of the violations of core provisions (art. 83).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: This Regulation shall not apply to the processing of personal data by Member States in the context of carrying out activities relating to the Union's common foreign and security policy (recital 16). The processing of personal data by Member States does not apply to activities related to the protection and prevention of threats to public security (Art. 2).
fr1_body_name: The law establishes the European Data Protection Board is comprised of representatives from the supervisory authority of each member states (Arts 68) acting independently (Art 69) providing guidelines recommendations and binding decisions to ensure consistence across Member States (Art 70) The Member State must to establish am independent supervisory authority responsible for monitoring and enforcing the regulation (Art 51)

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Online platform service providers must ensure the privacy security and protection of minors in their services (Art. 28) including prohibiting advertising based on profiling through the automated processing of data about children (Art. 28). Terms and conditions must be understandable to minors (Art. 14) ensuring a high level of privacy and security by default based on good practices (recitals 70 71). Large-scale online platforms and large-scale search engines systematic risk assessment applies periodically evaluating potential negative effects on minors (Art. 34). Mitigation measures are determined: age verification parental control tools abuse reporting tools and support for minors (Art. 35). Voluntary standards apply (Art. 44).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Action Plan 2024–2026 (Estonia)
fr1_justif: The plan explicitly identifies the limited number of Estonian language users as a weakness that 'hinders the initiation of larger KT projects'. The strategic objective is to 'ensure the future-proofing of Estonian language technology' positioning the development of the language as a core function of AI strategy. page 31 32
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Economic Affairs and Communications

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The law focuses on the risk classification system (Art. 6) and the requirements imposed on high-risk AI systems. It requires suppliers of high-risk AI systems to establish implement document and maintain a risk (and damage) management system throughout the system's lifecycle (Art. 9). Training validation and test datasets must conform to data governance in terms of representation error-freeness completeness contextualization and the function the system will be used for handling special personal data for monitoring purposes and detecting and correcting biases. High-risk AI systems must have consistency throughout the lifecycle by requiring appropriate levels of accuracy and robustness and reducing the risk of bias (art.15).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act addresses gender equality through non-discrimination and fundamental rights provisions. Training datasets for high-risk AI must be representative avoinding the discriminatory outcomes and protecting atributes explicitly related gender (Art. 10). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights impact assessments before deploying high-risk AI incluing gender-based discrimination (Art. 27). Equality bodies are granted market surveillance powers enabling them to investigate and sanction AI systems producing discriminatory outcomes (Art. 74).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act establishes requirements to ensure human control over high-risk AI systems requiring providers to design systems that enable natural persons with a minimum level of comprehensibility to monitor outputs detect anomalies and intervene or interrupt operations when necessary (art. 14). Deployers must assign oversight to competent natural persons with authority to act (art. 26). For general-purpose AI with systemic risk adversarial testing and incident reporting reinforce human control (Arts. 55 73). Additional oversight provisions are set in the fundamental rights impact assessment (art. 27) and Annex (IV) 2(e) as part of technical documentation requirements (art. 11).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Law establishes an assessment architecture according to the type and level of risk of the system. High-risk AI suppliers must identify analyze and mitigate risks during the design development and implementation phases within a risk management system (Arti.9) with comprehensive technical documentation (Art.15) and monitoring of operations and anomalies (Art. 26). For AI implementers in the public sector a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment applies before implementing high-risk systems listed in Annex 3 (Art.27). Compliance assessment must include self-assessment or third-party audits depending on the criticality of the system (Art. 43 47). There are ex-post assessments and post-commercialization monitoring (Art.72).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act classifies systems for recruitment promotion termination and worker management as high-risk AI systems (Annex III(4)) (art. 6). Prohibited AI practices include bans on emotion-recognition in the workplace (art. 5). Providers must implement risk-management addressing discrimination (Art. 9) ensure representative datasets and bias-control (art. 10) and maintain technical documentation (Arts. 11). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights assessments before deployment (art. 27). Art. 26 (7-9) establishes safeguards: workers must be informed and may request explanations of AI use. However the Act does not regulate pay conditions or compensation so it mitigates risks without constituting a full labour-rights regime.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Action Plan 2024–2026 (Estonia)
fr1_justif: Section 5.4 “Strategic objectives” sets the goal of “creating transparency about the use of algorithms in the public sector” and an indicator “public sector algorithms for which the algorithm transparency standard has been implemented and made available:  (p.38). Section 5.5 “Activities towards a reliable IT and legal space” includes the activity “Public sector algorithms” which foresees developing the algorithm transparency standard and guidelines a user-friendly publication solution on each algorithm’s purpose operating principles and other key facts and a legal analysis on when compliance with the standard should be mandatory in legislation (pp.40–41).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Economic Affairs and Communications

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Action Plan 2024–2026 (Estonia)
fr1_justif: Within Section 1.4 “Activities aimed at the public sector” there is a dedicated activity titled (in the English translation) “Developing competencies in the public sector”. This is the core provision on public-sector AI skills. It sets out that:• at least 500 public sector managers and employees are to be trained annually;• training is directed to AI subscribers specialists and developers to ensure sustainable implementation of projects with an artificial intelligence component including better procurement project management implementation and interpretation of results; and• at least 10 institutions per year are to be assessed for their data science competence capacity and structure with recommendations for improvement.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Economic Affairs and Communications
fr2_title: Judges’ Training Strategy 2025–2027 (Estonia)
fr2_justif: The strategy makes digital/AI skills a specific objective. Under 7.5 Developing digital competences it states that judges’ digital skills and knowledge of technological developments must be kept up to date and that participation in artificial intelligence training including abroad is supported; it also foresees training on managing virtual sessions and on cybercrime (p.6 sec. 7.5).
fr2_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Action Plan 2024–2026 (Estonia)
fr1_justif: The Action Plan frames AI skills as central to labour-market adaptation. In the R&D education and competences chapter it lists continuing education and retraining programmes on AI-related topics including “training programs for AI and data management and analytics specialists” adult education and ICT refresher modules and training for SMEs and business leaders to support digital transformation (sec. 3.1–3.2 pp. 23–24). Annexed activity tables in sec. 3.9 specify concrete AI trainings with budgets and deadlines.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Economic Affairs and Communications

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Safety and security is core to the Act. It establishes a risk-based framework for risk identification and mitigation (Art. 9) oversight measures (Art. 14) and robustness and accuracy requirements (Art. 15). To ensure ongoing safety the Act mandates technical documentation and record-keeping (Arts. 11–12) and in certain high risk situations a conformity assessment procedure prior to market placement (Art. 43). Post-market obligations in Arts. 72–73 require providers and deployers to monitor performance report serious incidents and take corrective action. For GPAI models posing systemic risks Arts. 55–56 impose additional safety and cybersecurity duties including model evaluation and risk mitigation following the Code of Practice.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Articles 11 12 and 13 establish several transparency and documentation obligations for AI developers and deployers. High-risk AI systems must include clear information on their capabilities limitations and intended purpose (Art. 13) and providers must maintain comprehensive technical documentation and automatic logging to ensure traceability (Arts. 11–12). These measures enable deployers to better understand the systems and authorities to audit or investigate when necessary. There are disclosure obligations that require notification when individuals interact with AI systems particularly chatbots and emotion recognition tools (Art. 50). Also for general-purpose AI in terms of model documentation and summary of training data (Art. 53).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.
