fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy (Ethiopia)
fr1_justif: The Policy frames AI literacy as closing an “awareness gap” and building citizens’ understanding and capacity to use AI. Under Section 4.1 (Human Resource Development) it commits to AI awareness activities capacity-building tasks and a practice-oriented education system (Section 4.1.1). It also requires lifelong learning and short/medium-term AI trainings (Section 4.1.2) leadership capability (Section 4.1.3) and special training for those with unique AI talents/skills (Section 4.1.4). It operationalises this via a Human Resource Development Program (Section 7.3).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy (Ethiopia)
fr1_justif: The Policy addresses cultural and linguistic diversity mainly at an objective and implementation-principle level. It requires AI systems to respect Ethiopia’s cultural values ethics customs and traditions (Guiding Principles Section 3.2.2). More explicitly it states that AI implementation must ensure that all languages and cultures in Ethiopia obtain proportional recognition and receive equal benefit (Inclusive AI Implementation Section 4.7.2). While this recognises linguistic diversity the Policy does not define concrete measures technical requirements or enforcement mechanisms such as local-language datasets or multilingual system standards leaving implementation largely aspirational.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy (Ethiopia)
fr1_justif: The Policy addresses fairness and non-discrimination in AI mainly at a principles and policy-direction level. It recognises risks of bias and discrimination in AI systems and promotes inclusiveness and fairness as guiding values for AI development and deployment (Section 3.1; Guiding Principles Section 3.2.2). It further commits to ethical accountable and human-centred AI including the development of ethical guidelines to reduce discriminatory outcomes (Ethics and Governance Section 3.3). More concretely Section 4.7.2 requires AI implementation to ensure inclusive and fair outcomes and equal societal benefit. However these provisions remain non-binding and lack technical or enforcement mechanisms.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Proclamation No.1321/2024: Proclamation to provide for personal data (Ethiopia)
fr1_justif: The law embeds human oversight mainly via Art. 31 (Automated Individual Decision Making): individuals may not be subject to decisions based solely on automated processing (incl. profiling) and can request human intervention express their views and contest outcomes (pp. 38 - 39). It also requires “meaningful information” on the logic significance and consequences of such processing and appropriate safeguards (Art. 31(4)- (5). Transparency support comes from Art. 24(1)(m) (notice of automated decision-making/profiling) and review pathways via Art. 58 (complaints/appeal).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Proclamation No.1321/2024: Proclamation to provide for personal data (Ethiopia)
fr1_justif: The law creates an AI-relevant impact-assessment duty where AI uses personal data. Section 47 requires controllers/processors to carry out a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) before processing likely to risk data-subjects’ rights expressly covering automated processing/profiling that can significantly affect individuals (s.47(1) - (2)(a). The DPIA must describe the processing/purpose assess necessity & proportionality identify risks and set safeguards/security measures (s.47(3) p.50). High-risk DPIAs trigger prior consultation/authorisation and the Authority may prohibit non-compliant processing (s.48(2)–(3); s.48(5).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Proclamation No.1321/2024: Proclamation to provide for personal data (Ethiopia)
fr1_justif: The law protects workers where employers use AI-like automated processing/profiling in HR (e.g. performance scoring/decisions). It defines “profiling” to include analysing/predicting “performance at work” (Art.2(8). Employers must inform staff about automated decision-making/profiling (Art.24(1)(m). Employees have the right not to be subject to solely automated decisions that significantly affect them and to obtain human intervention (Art.31) High-risk profiling requires a DPIA first (Art.47(2)(a).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy (Ethiopia)
fr1_justif: The Policy embeds reskilling/upskilling under “4.1 Human Resource Development”: it requires society-wide AI awareness building citizens’ capacity to use AI a practice-oriented education system lifelong learning and short-/medium-term training in identified AI target areas (Secs. 4.1.1–4.1.2). It also tackles job displacement by requiring AI-related education and training programmes (with education–industry linkages) and work-transition programmes for displaced workers (Sec. 4.8.3). The Ministry of Labor and Skills is tasked with retraining displaced workers (p.60).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy (Ethiopia)
fr1_justif: The Policy identifies AI “security threats” and “data privacy breaches” as key risks to mitigate (s.3.1) It sets safeguards in s.4.8.1 “Data Privacy and Security”: data-protection rules/consent public awareness cybersecurity infrastructure trained experts and security audits. It also backs AI surveillance/monitoring infrastructure for security institutions to protect national infrastructure/security (s.4.2.1)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Proclamation No.1321/2024: Proclamation to provide for personal data (Ethiopia)
fr1_justif: The law addresses AI-related transparency/explainability indirectly through rules on automated processing automated decision-making and profiling (rather than AI-specific technical duties). It treats AI “explainability” mainly as process transparency or contestability not technical model interpretability. Art 12 requires privacy information to be concise transparent intelligible and in plain language so people can understand AI-enabled processing. Art 24 mandates informing data subjects when automated decision-making/profiling is used. Art 31 adds “explainability” via rights to human intervention and information on the envisaged effects of automated decisions.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Decree No. 2019-919 of 30 August 2019 relating to the development of digital skills in school education higher education and continuing education and to the digital skills reference framework (France)
fr1_justif: The Decree addresses Artificial Intelligence literacy by mandating the development of 'digital skills' that constitute the foundational competencies for understanding Artificial Intelligence. Article 1 defines these skills across five domains most notably '1. Information and data' (aligning with Data Literacy) and '3. Creation of content' (which covers programming and computational thinking). Furthermore Article 2 mandates that this training must enable a 'safe critical and creative use of digital technology' ensuring students can critically evaluate technologies a key component of the Artificial Intelligence literacy definition.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.
fr1_body_name: public interest group national deliberation board (jury national de dlibration)

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Bots fake accounts and automated conduct are mechanisms of disinformation campaigns (recital 85) and as such rules are needed to combat them (recital 9). Systemic risk assessments apply to large-scale online platforms and search engines (art. 34) and explicitly include the dissemination or amplification of misleading or fraudulent content and the negative effects on gender-based violence. This includes recommendation systems and other relevant algorithmic systems (art. 34). Mitigation measures include testing and adapting algorithmic and recommendation systems; speeding up the processing of notifications of illegal hate speech and cyber violence; and labeling content generated or manipulated by AI (art.35).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU GDPR
fr1_justif: The law provides a layered redress framework that applies in AI contexts whenever personal data is processed. Data subjects harmed by automated processing based on AI-driven profiling and decision-making hold the right to express their point of view and the right to contest the decision (art. 22); presenting complaints to supervisory authorities (art. 77) resorting to legal measures against gaps in the authorities (art. 78) and bringing proceedings directly against controllers or processors (art. 79). Organizations may be mandated to exercise the rights collectively on behalf of data subjects (art. 80). Supervisory authorities may impose administrative fines because of the violations of core provisions (art. 83).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: This Regulation shall not apply to the processing of personal data by Member States in the context of carrying out activities relating to the Union's common foreign and security policy (recital 16). The processing of personal data by Member States does not apply to activities related to the protection and prevention of threats to public security (Art. 2).
fr1_body_name: The law establishes the European Data Protection Board is comprised of representatives from the supervisory authority of each member states (Arts 68) acting independently (Art 69) providing guidelines recommendations and binding decisions to ensure consistence across Member States (Art 70) The Member State must to establish am independent supervisory authority responsible for monitoring and enforcing the regulation (Art 51)

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Online platform service providers must ensure the privacy security and protection of minors in their services (Art. 28) including prohibiting advertising based on profiling through the automated processing of data about children (Art. 28). Terms and conditions must be understandable to minors (Art. 14) ensuring a high level of privacy and security by default based on good practices (recitals 70 71). Large-scale online platforms and large-scale search engines systematic risk assessment applies periodically evaluating potential negative effects on minors (Art. 34). Mitigation measures are determined: age verification parental control tools abuse reporting tools and support for minors (Art. 35). Voluntary standards apply (Art. 44).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: AI Action Strategy (Ministry of Culture) (France)
fr1_justif: The strategy aims for an AI ecosystem representing France's linguistic and cultural richness. It seeks to counter bias and content uniformity stemming from dominant models by supporting the training of AI respectful of cultural artistic and linguistic diversity (Axis A p.11). This involves using diverse sources including regional languages preserving plurilingualism (p.11) leveraging national collections (p.11) and specific projects like ALT-EDIC/LANGU:IA (p.27). It also promotes AI for cultural heritage knowledge and valorisation (Axis D p.21).
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Law No. 2025-391 of April 30 2025 containing various provisions for adaptation to European Union law in economic financial environmental energy transport health and movement of people matters (1) (France)
fr1_justif: Article 25 inserts Chapter VI (Arts L. 236-1 to L. 236-3) into the Code de l'énergie. Art L. 236-1 mandates data centers >500 kW publicly disclose environmental and energy data. Art L. 236-2 requires data centers >1 MW to recover waste heat. This directly addresses energy efficiency and mandatory reporting for the infrastructure underpinning AI tackling the environmental impact ('sustainability of AI') by promoting transparency and reducing energy waste from data centers. Pages 41-42.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Yes the framework explicitly excludes defence and security. Paragraph I of the inserted Article L. 236-1 states that the environmental obligations for data centers (paragraph II) do not apply to centers belonging to operators mentioned in articles L. 1332-1 or L. 1332-2 of the defense code nor to those used by the armed forces or civil protection or providing services exclusively for defense or civil protection purposes. (Source: Page 41)

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The law focuses on the risk classification system (Art. 6) and the requirements imposed on high-risk AI systems. It requires suppliers of high-risk AI systems to establish implement document and maintain a risk (and damage) management system throughout the system's lifecycle (Art. 9). Training validation and test datasets must conform to data governance in terms of representation error-freeness completeness contextualization and the function the system will be used for handling special personal data for monitoring purposes and detecting and correcting biases. High-risk AI systems must have consistency throughout the lifecycle by requiring appropriate levels of accuracy and robustness and reducing the risk of bias (art.15).

Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act addresses gender equality through non-discrimination and fundamental rights provisions. Training datasets for high-risk AI must be representative avoinding the discriminatory outcomes and protecting atributes explicitly related gender (Art. 10). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights impact assessments before deploying high-risk AI incluing gender-based discrimination (Art. 27). Equality bodies are granted market surveillance powers enabling them to investigate and sanction AI systems producing discriminatory outcomes (Art. 74).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act establishes requirements to ensure human control over high-risk AI systems requiring providers to design systems that enable natural persons with a minimum level of comprehensibility to monitor outputs detect anomalies and intervene or interrupt operations when necessary (art. 14). Deployers must assign oversight to competent natural persons with authority to act (art. 26). For general-purpose AI with systemic risk adversarial testing and incident reporting reinforce human control (Arts. 55 73). Additional oversight provisions are set in the fundamental rights impact assessment (art. 27) and Annex (IV) 2(e) as part of technical documentation requirements (art. 11).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Law establishes an assessment architecture according to the type and level of risk of the system. High-risk AI suppliers must identify analyze and mitigate risks during the design development and implementation phases within a risk management system (Arti.9) with comprehensive technical documentation (Art.15) and monitoring of operations and anomalies (Art. 26). For AI implementers in the public sector a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment applies before implementing high-risk systems listed in Annex 3 (Art.27). Compliance assessment must include self-assessment or third-party audits depending on the criticality of the system (Art. 43 47). There are ex-post assessments and post-commercialization monitoring (Art.72).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act classifies systems for recruitment promotion termination and worker management as high-risk AI systems (Annex III(4)) (art. 6). Prohibited AI practices include bans on emotion-recognition in the workplace (art. 5). Providers must implement risk-management addressing discrimination (Art. 9) ensure representative datasets and bias-control (art. 10) and maintain technical documentation (Arts. 11). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights assessments before deployment (art. 27). Art. 26 (7-9) establishes safeguards: workers must be informed and may request explanations of AI use. However the Act does not regulate pay conditions or compensation so it mitigates risks without constituting a full labour-rights regime.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems
fr1_title: Code of Relations between the Public and the Administration (France)
fr1_justif: The document addresses this in Book III Title I Chapter II (Dissemination of administrative documents). Specifically Article L. 312-1-3 mandates that administrations must publish online the 'rules defining the main algorithmic processing used in the performance of their missions when they form the basis of individual decisions.' This article establishes an affirmative obligation for public bodies (with more than 50 employees as per Article D. 312-1-4) to disclose the logic underlying their algorithmic systems subject only to protected secrets (like national defense).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Article L. 312-1-3 states that the obligation to publish algorithmic rules is 'Subject to the secrets protected pursuant to point 2 of Article L. 311-5'. Article L. 311-5 explicitly lists information not communicable due to protected interests including 'The secrecy of national defence' and 'National security public safety'. Therefore algorithmic systems used for defense and security purposes are explicitly excluded from the transparency requirements of this framework.
fr1_body_name: Commission for Access to Administrative Documents

fr1_title: Public procurement of innovative solutions: Practical guide (France)
fr1_justif: 'Practical Guide' is designed to assist public buyers in acquiring 'innovative solutions' a category that encompasses Artificial Intelligence (AI). It addresses the procurement of such technologies by outlining specific procedures for sourcing in Fact Sheet 4 (pages 30-36) defining functional specifications rather than technical ones to allow for AI solutions in Fact Sheet 4 (pages 24-29) and managing Intellectual Property (IP) rights (critical for AI software) in Fact Sheet 10 (pages 52-55). It provides operational 'Fact Sheets' on how to use legal levers within the Public Procurement Code specifically detailing the innovation partnership in Fact Sheet 13 (pages 65-71) to de-risk the purchase of emerging technologies like AI.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Master plan for lifelong professional development for civil servants for the period 2024-2027 (France)
fr1_justif: The document addresses public-sector skills development through its 2024-2027 Master Plan which is structured into six strategic axes. It mandates the professionalisation of civil servants through interministerial training pathways. Key areas include training 100% of staff in Republican values and secularism (Axis I) the ecological transition (Axis II) and digital sovereignty specifically focusing on security and AI (Axis III). Pages 7 and 8 detail the induction of first-time managers and the modernisation of Human Resources practices. Axis VI (page 9) focuses on cross-cutting skills like public procurement finance and European issues.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Directorate General for Public Administration and the Civil Service (DGAFP) Mentor interministerial training platform Interministerial Digital Directorate (DINUM)
fr2_title: AI in the service of justice: Strategy and operational solutions (France)
fr2_justif: The strategy prioritizes 'supporting professionals' to ensure they master new tools (p. 48). It mandates the creation of a 'Digital Justice Campus' (Campus du numérique Justice) to pool resources across the four justice schools and foster a 'shared digital culture' (p. 5 48).Key measures include:Awareness: Launching MOOCs on AI fundamentals and 'AI Justice Café' webinars to discuss ethical and technical challenges (p. 48 85).Continuous Training: Integrating AI into initial curricula and offering lifelong learning modules including 'serious games' and VR simulations for realistic professional scenarios (p. 48 86).Timeline: A 'gradual skills development' phase is specifically scheduled for 2026 (p. 8) following initial awareness
fr2_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.
fr2_body_name: AI Observatory (Observatoire de lIA) AI Program Management (Direction de programme IA)Strategic Committee (Comit stratgique)

fr1_title: Labour Code: Article L6321-1 (France)
fr1_justif: Article L6321-1 explicitly mandates that the employer 'ensures the adaptation of employees to their workstation' and 'watches over the maintenance of their ability to hold a job' specifically regarding the 'evolution of... technologies' ('l'évolution... des technologies'). This statutory text creates a direct legal obligation for companies to invest in training to adapt their workforce to technological changes. Since AI is a major technological evolution this provision inherently compels employers to provide upskilling for AI adoption. This interpretation is formally supported by the CCI Paris Ile-de-France (August 2025) which issued guidance stating this article imposes a direct duty to integrate AI training actions into skills plans.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr2_title: National Strategy for Artificial Intelligence (Third Phase) (France)
fr2_justif: The strategy addresses upskilling through a €360M investment in 9 'IA Clusters' to train 100000 people annually by 2030 including 20000 in continuing education [p. 17]. It leverages the 'Skills and Future Professions' (CMA) plan to generalise AI training across all qualification levels [p. 17]. Sector-specific measures include a €119M initiative to train 500000 healthcare professionals over five years [p. 32] and mandatory AI training for civil servants to support the deployment of sovereign tools like 'Albert' [p. 24 28]. Additionally 'AI Cafés' aim to raise awareness for 2 million citizens by 2027 [p. 26].
fr2_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr2_body_name: Interministerial Committee on AI National Institute for AI Assessment and Security (INESIA) Ministerial Agency for Defence AI (AMIAD) Interministerial Directorate for Digital Affairs (DINUM)

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Articles 11 12 and 13 establish several transparency and documentation obligations for AI developers and deployers. High-risk AI systems must include clear information on their capabilities limitations and intended purpose (Art. 13) and providers must maintain comprehensive technical documentation and automatic logging to ensure traceability (Arts. 11–12). These measures enable deployers to better understand the systems and authorities to audit or investigate when necessary. There are disclosure obligations that require notification when individuals interact with AI systems particularly chatbots and emotion recognition tools (Art. 50). Also for general-purpose AI in terms of model documentation and summary of training data (Art. 53).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Digital Economy Master Plan 2024 - 2034 (Gambia)
fr1_justif: The document includes AI as part of the digital economy's foundational framing (5). Plan explicitly calls on the government to introduce ICT education in primary and secondary schools (50). The 'Digital Literacy Curriculum in Schools' initiative proposes 'introducing digital literacy as part of the school curriculum to ensure that students acquire essential digital skills from an early age' covering 'coding application development internet safety basic computer skills.' (54).  Investment in 'the Gambian higher education system to further increase the number and quality of software development courses nationwide' (51). It also mentions investment into universities for digital connectivity (19).

fr1_title: The Gambia's National Child and Vulnerable Groups Online Protection Policy
fr1_link: The date for the CVGOP policy can be found in this article: https://web.archive.org/web/20260112212724/https://thepoint.gm/africa/gambia/headlines/govt-validates-policy-to-protect-child-and-vulnerable-groups-online. 2. The problem is that the CVGOP PDF can be found when you do search engine query but when you click on the link it immediately downloads the file (that is why it has been added as a google doc). While I can ascertain the url from the meta data it will not save on web.archive as it does not take you to an url but a direct download. The link is https://mocde.gov.gm>uploads>2024/11>Gambia-CVGOP-Policy.docx
fr1_justif: The policy aims to comprehensively address the wide spectrum of online risks and harms experienced by children women persons with disabilities and older persons in The Gambia (11)' including risks from the use of artificial intelligence (AI) technologies  (2). These risks or harms include AI tools being used to create  spread and amplify  sexual abuse material cyberbullying pornography disinformation and misinformation hate speech and content that promotes self-harm and violence (2). The policy also acknoweldges the spread of technology-facilitated violence as a new form of gender-based violence (9).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: The Gambia's National Child and Vulnerable Groups Online Protection Policy
fr1_justif: The purpose of the policy is to 'implement a comprehensive online protection framework in The Gambia that upholds the rights and well-being of all children' (pg. 12). It recognizes that there are a wide spectrum of online risks and harms for children which are exasberated by AI (pg. 2). The policy specifically recognizes the following AI risks for children: child sexual abuse material (pg. 2-3); disinformation and misinformation (pg. 2-4); AI enabling sextortion through deepfakes (pg. 2 4); radicalization through targeted content (p.24);  cyberbullying (pg. 25); targeted advertising exploiting vulnerabilities (pg. 3) ; perpetuating biases in access to services (pg. 3); chatbots encouraging harmful behaviors (pg. 26).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Law of Georgia on Personal Data Protection
fr1_justif: The document addresses access to redress and remedy in the AI context mainly through rules on automated decision-making and profiling. Article 19 provides procedural safeguards by allowing individuals to contest decisions based solely on automated processing express their views and request human involvement. While Article 19 does not establish a full right to remedy or compensation Articles 52 61 and 62 complement it by addressing remedies and liability outlining mechanisms for redress accountability and enforcement in cases of violations arising from automated data processing.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Article 2 clause 2 states: This Law shall not apply to the processing of data for the purposes of national security (including economic security) defence intelligence and counter-intelligence activities;

fr1_title: Decree No. 151/04 of the Governor of the National Bank of Georgia On Approval of the Regulation about Data-driven Statistical Al and Machine Learning Model Risk Management
fr1_justif: The document lists detailed steps to mitigate the bias factors (see article 9 clause 4)
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Decree No. 151/04 of the Governor of the National Bank of Georgia On Approval of the Regulation about Data-driven Statistical Al and Machine Learning Model Risk Management
fr1_justif: While the document doesn't use terms like 'human-in-the-loop' it mandates human control across the model lifecycle: Article 6 allows replacing model parameters based on expert judgment if validation is unsatisfactory. It requires monitoring 'overrides' made by experts to identify model limitations; (Articles 5-8 11) A qualified person independent of development must approve a model's launch. For regulatory models NBG's human assessment determines approval; The second line of defense must consist of staff with the expertise to critically analyze mathematical logic and assumptions. Transparency: Transparency requirements ensure humans can interpret 'black box' outcomes and explain the logic to customers.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Decree No. 151/04 of the Governor of the National Bank of Georgia On Approval of the Regulation about Data-driven Statistical Al and Machine Learning Model Risk Management
fr1_justif: In accordance with the Regulation on Data-driven Statistical AI and Machine Learning Model Risk Management issued by the National Bank of Georgia financial institutions must follow specific standards for model integrity: Impact Assessment: Under Article 5 Clause 10 development tests must evaluate how specific assumptions influence results and identify conditions where a model's effectiveness may decrease. Risk Management: Per Article 1 Clause 4 users must maintain a risk management system proportionate to the model's complexity and its potential impact on the institution's material risks. This framework ensures that AI and statistical tools are both transparent and aligned with a bank's/firm's overall risk profile.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Decree No. 151/04 of the Governor of the National Bank of Georgia On Approval of the Regulation about Data-driven Statistical Al and Machine Learning Model Risk Management
fr1_justif: the document addresses safety and security mainly through its model-risk management framework. It mandates that all AI ML and statistical models used by supervised entities must undergo rigorous internal validation testing and monitoring to prevent losses from incorrect model design data use or implementation. The regulation requires data quality controls (completeness accuracy timeliness audit trace) and establishes a “three lines of defense” structure—development independent validation and internal audit—to ensure transparency accountability and reliability. These provisions collectively safeguard the integrity stability and secure operation of models within financial institutions. (Article 5. Model building and testing)
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Decree No. 151/04 of the Governor of the National Bank of Georgia On Approval of the Regulation about Data-driven Statistical Al and Machine Learning Model Risk Management
fr1_justif: Article 5 clause 10 (page 4) states that the model should be transparent and allow for explanation and interpretation. To ensure transparency the model’s logic technical content and potential biases must be examined during the validation process. The requirement for transparency becomes even more significant when the model’s decisions directly affect consumers and the decision-making
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Strategy of the German Federal Government (2020 Update)
fr1_justif: AI literacy is framed as a societal competence: under “AI skills for all” the update aims that “every citizen” is well informed about AI’s opportunities and challenges to “demystify” AI and strengthen digital skills for everyday dealings with AI (Society “AI skills for all” p.25). It operationalises this via the free online course “Elements of AI” for the full breadth of the population including those without prior knowledge (p.25). It also commits to adapting education and training curricula across school vocational and higher education plus continuing training at companies (p.11)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Bots fake accounts and automated conduct are mechanisms of disinformation campaigns (recital 85) and as such rules are needed to combat them (recital 9). Systemic risk assessments apply to large-scale online platforms and search engines (art. 34) and explicitly include the dissemination or amplification of misleading or fraudulent content and the negative effects on gender-based violence. This includes recommendation systems and other relevant algorithmic systems (art. 34). Mitigation measures include testing and adapting algorithmic and recommendation systems; speeding up the processing of notifications of illegal hate speech and cyber violence; and labeling content generated or manipulated by AI (art.35).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU GDPR
fr1_justif: The law provides a layered redress framework that applies in AI contexts whenever personal data is processed. Data subjects harmed by automated processing based on AI-driven profiling and decision-making hold the right to express their point of view and the right to contest the decision (art. 22); presenting complaints to supervisory authorities (art. 77) resorting to legal measures against gaps in the authorities (art. 78) and bringing proceedings directly against controllers or processors (art. 79). Organizations may be mandated to exercise the rights collectively on behalf of data subjects (art. 80). Supervisory authorities may impose administrative fines because of the violations of core provisions (art. 83).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: This Regulation shall not apply to the processing of personal data by Member States in the context of carrying out activities relating to the Union's common foreign and security policy (recital 16). The processing of personal data by Member States does not apply to activities related to the protection and prevention of threats to public security (Art. 2).
fr1_body_name: The law establishes the European Data Protection Board is comprised of representatives from the supervisory authority of each member states (Arts 68) acting independently (Art 69) providing guidelines recommendations and binding decisions to ensure consistence across Member States (Art 70) The Member State must to establish am independent supervisory authority responsible for monitoring and enforcing the regulation (Art 51)

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Online platform service providers must ensure the privacy security and protection of minors in their services (Art. 28) including prohibiting advertising based on profiling through the automated processing of data about children (Art. 28). Terms and conditions must be understandable to minors (Art. 14) ensuring a high level of privacy and security by default based on good practices (recitals 70 71). Large-scale online platforms and large-scale search engines systematic risk assessment applies periodically evaluating potential negative effects on minors (Art. 34). Mitigation measures are determined: age verification parental control tools abuse reporting tools and support for minors (Art. 35). Voluntary standards apply (Art. 44).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Strategy of the German Federal Government (2020 Update)
fr1_justif: The National Strategy explicitly links preventing discrimination and bias to the inclusion of 'social diversity' within development teams going beyond just gender (p. 24: Security robustness and sustainability of AI). In the cultural sector the Strategy funds AI projects for the 'preservation development accessibility networking and communication of cultural programmes' (p.30). It also emphasises developing AI skills to verify media content specifically to ensure diversity of opinion (p.30). The strategy also aims to establish 'AI made in Europe' as a trademark rooted in 'common European rules and values (p. 2: Summary p.13).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Law on increasing energy efficiency in Germany (Energy Efficiency Act - EnEfG)
fr1_justif: The EnEfG law implements the EU Energy Efficiency Directive (EED) at a national level and regulates the environmental footprint of data centres where AI workloads run. It mandates that data centers with a load of =300 kW meet strict performance metrics such as a Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE) of =1.2 (Section 11) for new facilities and an Energy Reuse Factor (ERF) of 20% by 2028 (Section 11(2)). By requiring 100% renewable energy use by 2027 and annual reporting to the Energy Efficiency Register (Section 11(5)) the EnEfG ensures that AI growth aligns with national climate goals. For reporting data center operators must submit annual energy and water usage data to the Energy Efficiency Register (RZReg) (Section 13).
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Defense and security are explicitly excluded from the scope of the EnEfG through Section 21. This section stipulates that the mandatory energy reduction targets for public bodies and the requirement to implement management systems do not apply to the field of National Defense. These exemptions ensure that the Act's stringent technical standards such as waste heat reuse or renewable energy quotas  do not jeopardise the operational readiness or security-critical infrastructure of the Bundeswehr. While the defense sector is encouraged to contribute to sustainability through separate strategies it remains legally exempt from the EnEfG.
fr1_body_name: The Federal Office for Economic Affairs and Export Control (BAFA)

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The law focuses on the risk classification system (Art. 6) and the requirements imposed on high-risk AI systems. It requires suppliers of high-risk AI systems to establish implement document and maintain a risk (and damage) management system throughout the system's lifecycle (Art. 9). Training validation and test datasets must conform to data governance in terms of representation error-freeness completeness contextualization and the function the system will be used for handling special personal data for monitoring purposes and detecting and correcting biases. High-risk AI systems must have consistency throughout the lifecycle by requiring appropriate levels of accuracy and robustness and reducing the risk of bias (art.15).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act addresses gender equality through non-discrimination and fundamental rights provisions. Training datasets for high-risk AI must be representative avoinding the discriminatory outcomes and protecting atributes explicitly related gender (Art. 10). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights impact assessments before deploying high-risk AI incluing gender-based discrimination (Art. 27). Equality bodies are granted market surveillance powers enabling them to investigate and sanction AI systems producing discriminatory outcomes (Art. 74).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act establishes requirements to ensure human control over high-risk AI systems requiring providers to design systems that enable natural persons with a minimum level of comprehensibility to monitor outputs detect anomalies and intervene or interrupt operations when necessary (art. 14). Deployers must assign oversight to competent natural persons with authority to act (art. 26). For general-purpose AI with systemic risk adversarial testing and incident reporting reinforce human control (Arts. 55 73). Additional oversight provisions are set in the fundamental rights impact assessment (art. 27) and Annex (IV) 2(e) as part of technical documentation requirements (art. 11).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Law establishes an assessment architecture according to the type and level of risk of the system. High-risk AI suppliers must identify analyze and mitigate risks during the design development and implementation phases within a risk management system (Arti.9) with comprehensive technical documentation (Art.15) and monitoring of operations and anomalies (Art. 26). For AI implementers in the public sector a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment applies before implementing high-risk systems listed in Annex 3 (Art.27). Compliance assessment must include self-assessment or third-party audits depending on the criticality of the system (Art. 43 47). There are ex-post assessments and post-commercialization monitoring (Art.72).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act classifies systems for recruitment promotion termination and worker management as high-risk AI systems (Annex III(4)) (art. 6). Prohibited AI practices include bans on emotion-recognition in the workplace (art. 5). Providers must implement risk-management addressing discrimination (Art. 9) ensure representative datasets and bias-control (art. 10) and maintain technical documentation (Arts. 11). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights assessments before deployment (art. 27). Art. 26 (7-9) establishes safeguards: workers must be informed and may request explanations of AI use. However the Act does not regulate pay conditions or compensation so it mitigates risks without constituting a full labour-rights regime.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' fr1_title: Guidelines for the Use of Artificial Intelligence in the Federal Administration (Germany)
fr1_justif: The Guidelines address public disclosure by prioritizing transparency to ensure administrative action remains traceable. Guideline B6 (p. 22) mandates that federal authorities disclose their AI use as transparently as possible to foster public trust. This involves implementing internal and external labeling and documentation obligations. Guideline A5 (p. 16) specifically requires marking AI-generated content when shared with the public. Furthermore the framework promotes 'Explainable AI' (p. 7) requiring that the logic and data basis of AI results be reproducible and understandable for affected citizens.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: In the footnote of Section 3 (p. 10) it states that deviations from the guidelines are permissible in justified cases such as for military or national security purposes.

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Strategy of the German Federal Government (2020 Update)
fr1_justif: The document addresses Public Procurement of AI by expanding the KOINNO center to provide public buyers with specialised training and guidelines (p. 22). It prioritises 'AI made in Germany/Europe' by revising guidelines to facilitate access for start-ups through innovation partnerships (p. 16 22). The strategy mandates that procurement processes define standard requirements for the traceability and verifiability of AI systems to ensure transparency (p. 14 24). Furthermore it utilises 'learning and experimentation spaces' for administrations to test AI applications ensuring they meet ethical and technical standards before being awarded contracts (p. 21).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Strategy of the German Federal Government (2020 Update)
fr1_justif: The AI Strategy for the Justice System highlights public-sector skills. Under Action area 5.7 “Strengthen AI competence of justice personnel” it states that sufficient AI competence among justice staff is a key prerequisite for introducing procuring and using AI systems (p. 34). It then sets measures: G1 “Roles and competence profiles” (defining competence needs and potential specialist roles) G2 “Knowledge-transfer formats and training/support materials” and G3 “Training” (cross-Länder training concepts and continuing training and awareness offers) (pp. 34–35).
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.
fr1_body_name: Federal-State Commission for Information Technology in the Judiciary (BLK) AI working group of the Federal-State Commission for Information Technology in the Judiciary (BLK-AG AI)

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Strategy of the German Federal Government (2020 Update)
fr1_justif: The National AI Strategy establishes comprehensive government-funded reskilling programs. It prioritises “skills development and continuing training at companies” and links this to the Federal Government’s National Skills Strategy (p. 11). It also states that information and skills-development schemes are being expanded including the “AI trainer programme” at the Mittelstand 4.0 Centres of Excellence and related train-the-trainer support (p. 17). The annex further lists creating an AI-supported online portal for continuing vocational training and launching INVITE projects for digital continuing education (p. 26).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Safety and security is core to the Act. It establishes a risk-based framework for risk identification and mitigation (Art. 9) oversight measures (Art. 14) and robustness and accuracy requirements (Art. 15). To ensure ongoing safety the Act mandates technical documentation and record-keeping (Arts. 11–12) and in certain high risk situations a conformity assessment procedure prior to market placement (Art. 43). Post-market obligations in Arts. 72–73 require providers and deployers to monitor performance report serious incidents and take corrective action. For GPAI models posing systemic risks Arts. 55–56 impose additional safety and cybersecurity duties including model evaluation and risk mitigation following the Code of Practice.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Articles 11 12 and 13 establish several transparency and documentation obligations for AI developers and deployers. High-risk AI systems must include clear information on their capabilities limitations and intended purpose (Art. 13) and providers must maintain comprehensive technical documentation and automatic logging to ensure traceability (Arts. 11–12). These measures enable deployers to better understand the systems and authorities to audit or investigate when necessary. There are disclosure obligations that require notification when individuals interact with AI systems particularly chatbots and emotion recognition tools (Art. 50). Also for general-purpose AI in terms of model documentation and summary of training data (Art. 53).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Republic of Ghana National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2023-2033
fr1_justif: The Ghana National Artificial Intelligence Strategy (2023-2033) addresses AI literacy under Pillar 1: Expand AI Education & Training (p. 26–27) which calls for the “AI Ready Ghana” programme expansion of AI courses teacher training and public awareness to close digital literacy gaps. Additionally Pillar 5 (p. 34) promotes community engagement and knowledge sharing to strengthen AI understanding and collaboration across sectors
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Data Protection Act 2012 (Ghana)
fr1_justif: The Data Protection Act provides redress mechanisms relevant to AI through Section 41 which gives individuals the right to challenge decisions made solely by automated processing. It ensures they can request human review and reconsideration. Additionally Sections 43-44 grant rights to compensation and correction or erasure of inaccurate data while Sections 75-80 empower the Data Protection Commission to investigate complaints and issue enforcement notices. Together these enable redress for harms from AI-driven processing.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: This is stated in Section 60 which exempts data processing for purposes of “public order public safety public morality national security or public interest” from the Act’s provisions.

fr1_title: Republic of Ghana National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2023-2033
fr1_justif: The Strategy addresses Fairness and Non-discrimination by highlighting risks of model bias algorithmic bias and unfair discrimination arising from foreign-trained models not suited to Ghana’s context. On page 15 it notes that gaps in digital inclusion underrepresentation and poor data quality can widen inequalities and exclusion. On page 18 the SWOT analysis lists as a threat the risk of failures errors and discrimination due to models unfit for local data contexts stressing the need for inclusive contextually relevant AI.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Republic of Ghana National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2023-2033
fr1_justif: On page 77-78 there is a section for 'AI Impact on Gender Diversity and Bias'. In this section the strategy briefly addresses potential impacts of AI on gender and minority groups and how AI can be used to solve some problems faced by such groups. Specifically it explains that systems that are not trained on representative data can perpetuate existing biases and widen the gap between genders. It also explains that AI can be a tool to bridge this same gap. Additionally on page 72 the strategy highlights some training programs on Gender & AI organised by Huawei Technologies Ghana i.e. Seeds for the Future-Women in Tech Programme
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Data Protection Act 2012 (Ghana)
fr1_justif: The Ghana Data Protection Act 2012 (Act 843) Section 41 ensures Human Oversight and Determination by granting individuals the right not to be subject to decisions based solely on automated processing including profiling that produce legal effects or significantly affect them. Decisions require human intervention or explicit consent enabling review and accountability. This embeds human control and responsibility into AI-driven processes supporting transparency and explainability.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Under the heading 'exemptions'  section 60 explicitly states that the provisions of the act are exempt for the purposes of national security

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Republic of Ghana National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2023-2033
fr1_justif: The Ghana National AI Strategy (2023–2033) addresses public procurement of AI by promoting start-up participation in government projects for AI adoption enabling bidding consortia and partnerships with larger companies and supporting trial programs or sandboxes to test AI solutions in public-sector contexts. It also encourages co-creation of AI intellectual property (IP) and prioritizes local AI development to enhance innovation and reduce dependence on foreign vendors. This is under Pillar 8: Promote AI Adoption in the Public Sector page 39
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Republic of Ghana National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2023-2033
fr1_justif: The document addresses public sector skills development primarily in Pillar 8 of the Action Plan (outlined in pages 7 - 8 and expanded on page 39 ). It proposes establishing a structured programme to train civil and public servants to design lead and implement AI projects in public services building on existing examples such as UNESCO’s AI Competency Framework for Civil Servants and the Tony Blair Institute’s Harnessing Responsible AI Toolkit. It further recommends incentivising AI adoption through rewards promotions and recognition to embed a sustainable AI-ready culture across the public sector.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Republic of Ghana National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2023-2033
fr1_justif: The document emphasizes reskilling and upskilling through initiatives like annual skills gap assessments to identify workforce needs (Page 26) and launching the “AI Ready Ghana” program to train over 1 million youth by 2033 (Page 26). It advocates expanding AI courses promoting teacher training and ensuring inclusive education for rural populations and women (Page 26). The strategy also aims to prepare youth for AI roles such as data labeling and research fostering local AI talent (Page 26).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Republic of Ghana National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2023-2033
fr1_justif: The Ghana National AI Strategy addresses transparency and explainability as core principles of responsible AI. In the “Risks of AI” section (pp. 15-16) it warns that the lack of transparency and explainability in algorithmic outcomes particularly in deep learning systems hinders bias detection and accountability. Under Pillar 4: Facilitate Data Access & Governance (pp. 32-33) it promotes trust and transparency through national data-sharing agreements and ethical AI guidelines. Additionally the proposed Responsible AI Office (p. 25) enhances transparency via a public-facing portal for oversight and engagement.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: A Blueprint for Greece's AI Transformation
fr1_justif: The Blueprint addresses AI literacy as a core educational goal combining “hard” skills (programming data science ML) with “soft” ones (ethical understanding societal impact) (p. 59). It promotes AI learning at all education levels and in vocational Education (p.61-69; 77). The AI Center of Excellence (p. 17) aims to provide national expertise training programs and capacity building in AI while the Global AI Forum (p. 23) serves a public educational role through lectures courses publications and podcasts to promote nationwide AI literacy.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Bots fake accounts and automated conduct are mechanisms of disinformation campaigns (recital 85) and as such rules are needed to combat them (recital 9). Systemic risk assessments apply to large-scale online platforms and search engines (art. 34) and explicitly include the dissemination or amplification of misleading or fraudulent content and the negative effects on gender-based violence. This includes recommendation systems and other relevant algorithmic systems (art. 34). Mitigation measures include testing and adapting algorithmic and recommendation systems; speeding up the processing of notifications of illegal hate speech and cyber violence; and labeling content generated or manipulated by AI (art.35).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU GDPR
fr1_justif: The law provides a layered redress framework that applies in AI contexts whenever personal data is processed. Data subjects harmed by automated processing based on AI-driven profiling and decision-making hold the right to express their point of view and the right to contest the decision (art. 22); presenting complaints to supervisory authorities (art. 77) resorting to legal measures against gaps in the authorities (art. 78) and bringing proceedings directly against controllers or processors (art. 79). Organizations may be mandated to exercise the rights collectively on behalf of data subjects (art. 80). Supervisory authorities may impose administrative fines because of the violations of core provisions (art. 83).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: This Regulation shall not apply to the processing of personal data by Member States in the context of carrying out activities relating to the Union's common foreign and security policy (recital 16). The processing of personal data by Member States does not apply to activities related to the protection and prevention of threats to public security (Art. 2).
fr1_body_name: The law establishes the European Data Protection Board is comprised of representatives from the supervisory authority of each member states (Arts 68) acting independently (Art 69) providing guidelines recommendations and binding decisions to ensure consistence across Member States (Art 70) The Member State must to establish am independent supervisory authority responsible for monitoring and enforcing the regulation (Art 51)

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Online platform service providers must ensure the privacy security and protection of minors in their services (Art. 28) including prohibiting advertising based on profiling through the automated processing of data about children (Art. 28). Terms and conditions must be understandable to minors (Art. 14) ensuring a high level of privacy and security by default based on good practices (recitals 70 71). Large-scale online platforms and large-scale search engines systematic risk assessment applies periodically evaluating potential negative effects on minors (Art. 34). Mitigation measures are determined: age verification parental control tools abuse reporting tools and support for minors (Art. 35). Voluntary standards apply (Art. 44).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: A Blueprint for Greece's AI Transformation
fr1_justif: The Blueprint for Greece’s AI Transformation acknowledges cultural and linguistic diversity as a key factor in AI deployment. It highlights the goal of developing platforms tailored to the Greek language and culturally relevant data (p. 20). Furthermore it proposes establishing a dedicated Data Space for Greek cultural content and multilingual AI applications as outlined in Proposal 6.3: Develop the Greek Language and Culture Data Space (p. 102).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: A Blueprint for Greece's AI Transformation
fr1_justif: The Blueprint for Greece’s AI Transformation integrates environmental impact as a cross cutting concern. It promotes “harmonizing technological progress with environmental stewardship” to reduce the ecological footprint of AI (p. 11) and lists environmental protection among key normative principles shaping AI policy (p. 13). Additionally Proposal 3.8 recommends leveraging the Daedalus supercomputer to provide more environmentally friendly AI computing resources and lower-energy infrastructure for local companies (p. 43). DAEDALUS is a EuroHPC supercomputer being built in Greece with a performance of 89 Petaflops set to become the most powerful in Greece and one of the leading systems in Europe.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The law focuses on the risk classification system (Art. 6) and the requirements imposed on high-risk AI systems. It requires suppliers of high-risk AI systems to establish implement document and maintain a risk (and damage) management system throughout the system's lifecycle (Art. 9). Training validation and test datasets must conform to data governance in terms of representation error-freeness completeness contextualization and the function the system will be used for handling special personal data for monitoring purposes and detecting and correcting biases. High-risk AI systems must have consistency throughout the lifecycle by requiring appropriate levels of accuracy and robustness and reducing the risk of bias (art.15).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act addresses gender equality through non-discrimination and fundamental rights provisions. Training datasets for high-risk AI must be representative avoinding the discriminatory outcomes and protecting atributes explicitly related gender (Art. 10). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights impact assessments before deploying high-risk AI incluing gender-based discrimination (Art. 27). Equality bodies are granted market surveillance powers enabling them to investigate and sanction AI systems producing discriminatory outcomes (Art. 74).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act establishes requirements to ensure human control over high-risk AI systems requiring providers to design systems that enable natural persons with a minimum level of comprehensibility to monitor outputs detect anomalies and intervene or interrupt operations when necessary (art. 14). Deployers must assign oversight to competent natural persons with authority to act (art. 26). For general-purpose AI with systemic risk adversarial testing and incident reporting reinforce human control (Arts. 55 73). Additional oversight provisions are set in the fundamental rights impact assessment (art. 27) and Annex (IV) 2(e) as part of technical documentation requirements (art. 11).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Law establishes an assessment architecture according to the type and level of risk of the system. High-risk AI suppliers must identify analyze and mitigate risks during the design development and implementation phases within a risk management system (Arti.9) with comprehensive technical documentation (Art.15) and monitoring of operations and anomalies (Art. 26). For AI implementers in the public sector a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment applies before implementing high-risk systems listed in Annex 3 (Art.27). Compliance assessment must include self-assessment or third-party audits depending on the criticality of the system (Art. 43 47). There are ex-post assessments and post-commercialization monitoring (Art.72).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act classifies systems for recruitment promotion termination and worker management as high-risk AI systems (Annex III(4)) (art. 6). Prohibited AI practices include bans on emotion-recognition in the workplace (art. 5). Providers must implement risk-management addressing discrimination (Art. 9) ensure representative datasets and bias-control (art. 10) and maintain technical documentation (Arts. 11). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights assessments before deployment (art. 27). Art. 26 (7-9) establishes safeguards: workers must be informed and may request explanations of AI use. However the Act does not regulate pay conditions or compensation so it mitigates risks without constituting a full labour-rights regime.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Law No. 4961/2022: Emerging information and communication technologies strengthening digital governance and other provisions (Greece)
fr1_justif: The law requires public disclosure of government AI systems through mandatory transparency rules. Article 6 obliges public bodies to provide accessible information on system purpose parameters data categories and decision logic. Article 8 requires each body to maintain and update a public register of AI systems. Article 5 mandates an algorithmic impact assessment before deployment. These provisions ensure public disclosure of algorithmic systems.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Article 4(2) of the Law 4961/2022 explicitly excludes the Ministries of National Defense and Citizen Protection their supervised bodies and the National Intelligence Service (NIS) from the scope of application of this framework (p. 4). This provision clearly indicates that defence and security sectors are not covered by the obligations and safeguards outlined in the document.
fr1_body_name: Coordinating Committee on AI National Strategy Supervisory Committee for the development of AI AI Observatory

fr1_title: Law No. 4961/2022: Emerging information and communication technologies strengthening digital governance and other provisions (Greece)
fr1_justif: The law regulates AI related public procurement in Article 7 requiring every tender for designing or developing an AI system to include conditions ensuring: contractor obligations to provide system parameters (Art. 6(b-c)) allow study of the system’s operating mode ensure legal framework compatibility and deliver the system so the public body can improve or audit it (p. 4-5).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Article 4(2) of the Law 4961/2022 explicitly excludes the Ministries of National Defense and Citizen Protection their supervised bodies and the National Intelligence Service (NIS) from the scope of application of this framework (p. 4). This provision clearly indicates that defence and security sectors are not covered by the obligations and safeguards outlined in the document.

fr1_title: The Greek National Digital Decade Strategic Roadmap
fr1_justif: The document addresses Public Sector Skills Development through targeted training measures. Measure 1A06M provides reskilling and upskilling programmes for General Government employees local authorities and healthcare staff (p.110-111). Measure 1A03M supports capacity building through the training of 120.000 teachers in digital skills (p.108). ?he Cybersecurity Skills Academy delivers specialised training for public officials including in the curricula AI cyber forensics and malware analysis (p.98). As for the measures that do not explicitly reference AI they operate national Law 4961/2022 that establishes the framework for AI and emerging ICTs in the public sector upon which the Roadmap’s skills-related measures build (p.6-7).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Digital Transformation Executive Network ELSTAT EKT SEPE National Coalition for Digital Skills and Jobs

fr1_justif: The Blueprint for Greece’s AI Transformation emphasizes rapid reskilling and upskilling to prepare the workforce for AI adoption. It calls for training programs targeting technical non-technical employees and technology leaders promoting continuous learning and AI literacy. Universities should reduce bureaucratic barriers to offer accessible reskilling opportunities (p. 76). Generative AI is proposed to personalize training delivery while vocational AI programs and certification schemes aim to build a skilled adaptable workforce (p. 77).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Hungary Artificial Intelligence Strategy (2025–2030)
fr1_justif: The Strategy addresses AI literacy within the formal education sector in Section 5.3 “Oktatás kompetenciafejlesztés” (pp. 51 101–104). It includes curriculum development and teacher training in public education (p.104) data literacy programs in schools (p.101) the introduction of sector-specific AI modules in higher education (p.55)  Launching a high school 'AI lab' program where students can develop simple AI applications (p. 56) and the expansion of AI capacities in universities (p.84). These measures focus exclusively on strengthening AI-related knowledge and competences in secondary (including vocational) and tertiary education.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
