fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Bots fake accounts and automated conduct are mechanisms of disinformation campaigns (recital 85) and as such rules are needed to combat them (recital 9). Systemic risk assessments apply to large-scale online platforms and search engines (art. 34) and explicitly include the dissemination or amplification of misleading or fraudulent content and the negative effects on gender-based violence. This includes recommendation systems and other relevant algorithmic systems (art. 34). Mitigation measures include testing and adapting algorithmic and recommendation systems; speeding up the processing of notifications of illegal hate speech and cyber violence; and labeling content generated or manipulated by AI (art.35).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU GDPR
fr1_justif: The law provides a layered redress framework that applies in AI contexts whenever personal data is processed. Data subjects harmed by automated processing based on AI-driven profiling and decision-making hold the right to express their point of view and the right to contest the decision (art. 22); presenting complaints to supervisory authorities (art. 77) resorting to legal measures against gaps in the authorities (art. 78) and bringing proceedings directly against controllers or processors (art. 79). Organizations may be mandated to exercise the rights collectively on behalf of data subjects (art. 80). Supervisory authorities may impose administrative fines because of the violations of core provisions (art. 83).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: This Regulation shall not apply to the processing of personal data by Member States in the context of carrying out activities relating to the Union's common foreign and security policy (recital 16). The processing of personal data by Member States does not apply to activities related to the protection and prevention of threats to public security (Art. 2).
fr1_body_name: The law establishes the European Data Protection Board is comprised of representatives from the supervisory authority of each member states (Arts 68) acting independently (Art 69) providing guidelines recommendations and binding decisions to ensure consistence across Member States (Art 70) The Member State must to establish am independent supervisory authority responsible for monitoring and enforcing the regulation (Art 51)

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Online platform service providers must ensure the privacy security and protection of minors in their services (Art. 28) including prohibiting advertising based on profiling through the automated processing of data about children (Art. 28). Terms and conditions must be understandable to minors (Art. 14) ensuring a high level of privacy and security by default based on good practices (recitals 70 71). Large-scale online platforms and large-scale search engines systematic risk assessment applies periodically evaluating potential negative effects on minors (Art. 34). Mitigation measures are determined: age verification parental control tools abuse reporting tools and support for minors (Art. 35). Voluntary standards apply (Art. 44).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Hungary’s Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2020-2030
fr1_justif: The framework addresses linguistic and cultural diversity by explicitly prioritising the development of language technologies tailored to the Hungarian language (pp. 26 44). It recognises that without such efforts smaller or less globally dominant languages risk marginalisation in the digital and AI-driven landscape. By supporting the creation of Hungarian-language corpora open-access tools and AI-based language processing systems the framework aims to ensure that Hungarian remains functional and competitive in digital environments. It further links this to cultural preservation framing language technology as essential to maintaining national identity.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: National Innovation Office (NKFIH) Ministry for Innovation and Technology Hungarian Chamber of Commerce and Industry

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The law focuses on the risk classification system (Art. 6) and the requirements imposed on high-risk AI systems. It requires suppliers of high-risk AI systems to establish implement document and maintain a risk (and damage) management system throughout the system's lifecycle (Art. 9). Training validation and test datasets must conform to data governance in terms of representation error-freeness completeness contextualization and the function the system will be used for handling special personal data for monitoring purposes and detecting and correcting biases. High-risk AI systems must have consistency throughout the lifecycle by requiring appropriate levels of accuracy and robustness and reducing the risk of bias (art.15).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act addresses gender equality through non-discrimination and fundamental rights provisions. Training datasets for high-risk AI must be representative avoinding the discriminatory outcomes and protecting atributes explicitly related gender (Art. 10). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights impact assessments before deploying high-risk AI incluing gender-based discrimination (Art. 27). Equality bodies are granted market surveillance powers enabling them to investigate and sanction AI systems producing discriminatory outcomes (Art. 74).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act establishes requirements to ensure human control over high-risk AI systems requiring providers to design systems that enable natural persons with a minimum level of comprehensibility to monitor outputs detect anomalies and intervene or interrupt operations when necessary (art. 14). Deployers must assign oversight to competent natural persons with authority to act (art. 26). For general-purpose AI with systemic risk adversarial testing and incident reporting reinforce human control (Arts. 55 73). Additional oversight provisions are set in the fundamental rights impact assessment (art. 27) and Annex (IV) 2(e) as part of technical documentation requirements (art. 11).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Law establishes an assessment architecture according to the type and level of risk of the system. High-risk AI suppliers must identify analyze and mitigate risks during the design development and implementation phases within a risk management system (Arti.9) with comprehensive technical documentation (Art.15) and monitoring of operations and anomalies (Art. 26). For AI implementers in the public sector a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment applies before implementing high-risk systems listed in Annex 3 (Art.27). Compliance assessment must include self-assessment or third-party audits depending on the criticality of the system (Art. 43 47). There are ex-post assessments and post-commercialization monitoring (Art.72).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act classifies systems for recruitment promotion termination and worker management as high-risk AI systems (Annex III(4)) (art. 6). Prohibited AI practices include bans on emotion-recognition in the workplace (art. 5). Providers must implement risk-management addressing discrimination (Art. 9) ensure representative datasets and bias-control (art. 10) and maintain technical documentation (Arts. 11). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights assessments before deployment (art. 27). Art. 26 (7-9) establishes safeguards: workers must be informed and may request explanations of AI use. However the Act does not regulate pay conditions or compensation so it mitigates risks without constituting a full labour-rights regime.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Safety and security is core to the Act. It establishes a risk-based framework for risk identification and mitigation (Art. 9) oversight measures (Art. 14) and robustness and accuracy requirements (Art. 15). To ensure ongoing safety the Act mandates technical documentation and record-keeping (Arts. 11–12) and in certain high risk situations a conformity assessment procedure prior to market placement (Art. 43). Post-market obligations in Arts. 72–73 require providers and deployers to monitor performance report serious incidents and take corrective action. For GPAI models posing systemic risks Arts. 55–56 impose additional safety and cybersecurity duties including model evaluation and risk mitigation following the Code of Practice.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Articles 11 12 and 13 establish several transparency and documentation obligations for AI developers and deployers. High-risk AI systems must include clear information on their capabilities limitations and intended purpose (Art. 13) and providers must maintain comprehensive technical documentation and automatic logging to ensure traceability (Arts. 11–12). These measures enable deployers to better understand the systems and authorities to audit or investigate when necessary. There are disclosure obligations that require notification when individuals interact with AI systems particularly chatbots and emotion recognition tools (Art. 50). Also for general-purpose AI in terms of model documentation and summary of training data (Art. 53).

fr1_title: National Education Policy 2020
fr1_justif: The National Education Policy 2020 promotes AI literacy by integrating AI into formal education across all levels. It mandates the introduction of contemporary subjects such as Artificial Intelligence within curricula to develop relevant skills early on (p. 15). It further emphasises expanding research capacity in AI and machine learning to build national expertise (p. 45). Additionally it recognises that emerging technologies will transform both what and how students learn calling for continued educational and technological research (p. 56).
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Information Technology (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules 2021
fr1_justif: Rule 3(1)(b) page 4-5 mentions due diligence requirements for intermediaries directing them to inform users that they do not upload or share content that is unlawful including misinformation impersonation obscene content and content that is harmful to children.Taking down unlawful content within 36 hours upon receiving actual knowledge either through a court order or notification from the appropriate government. In light of the advisory (https://www.pib.gov.in/PressReleaseIframePage.aspx?PRID=1975445&reg=3&lang=2#:~:text=Given%20the%20significant%20challenges%20posedmeasures%20t) issued by the government of India for AI companies serving as official interpretative materials this applies to AI specifically deepfakes and misinformation
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: The Information Technology (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules 2021 (India)
fr1_justif: The IT rules establish rights to redress through grievance mechanisms and oversight bodies including intermediary-level grievance systems (Rule 3(2)(a)) and Grievance Appellate Committees (Rule 3A) with user rights to challenge decisions (Rule 4(8)). These apply to platforms using AI and algorithmic tools (Rule 4(4)). Importantly an official government advisory (https://www.pib.gov.in/PressReleaseIframePage.aspx?PRID=1975445&reg=3&lang=2#:~:text=Given%20the%20significant%20challenges%20posedmeasures%20t) serving as interpretative material explicitly links AI harms to redress stating that individuals affected by deepfakes should file FIRs and avail remedies under the IT rules reinforcing access to redress for AI-related harms.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 (India)
fr1_justif: page 8 Section 9 directs companies to take verifiable parental consent before processing the data of anyone under the age of 18 prevents companies from processing the data of under 18 users in ways that will have detrimental effects on their wellbeing and restricts tracking and behavioral monitoring.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 allows the Central Government to exempt State agencies from the Act’s provisions in the interests of sovereignty integrity of India security of the State friendly relations with foreign States public order or preventing incitement to offences (Section 17(2)(a) page 11).

fr1_title: National Language Translation Mission (NLTM) White Paper
fr1_justif: The framework addresses the indicator of linguistic and cultural diversity by explicitly recognising India’s extensive linguistic landscape noting that “several hundred languages and dialects are spoken across the country” and highlighting the lack of digital content in local languages (Executive Summary). It positions AI-driven language technologies as a key mechanism to ensure inclusion and accessibility for speakers of these languages. This is operationalised through the development of machine translation systems multilingual datasets and speech technologies across diverse Indian language groups alongside an emphasis on large-scale multilingual data for training AI models (Section 3).
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Report on AI Governance Guidelines Development (India)
fr1_justif: Page 4: mentions fairness and non-discrimination as a proposed core principle for AI governance. Page 11: mentions key concerns surrounding AI led bias and discrimination with a specific point about how only legally and socially prohibited biases need to be protected against. Page 14: mentions that  to enable appropriate measurement of fairness accountability and transparency in the Indian context it is an essential prerequisite to have access to the right datasets relevant to the Indian context which allows users to assess the fairness and bias of their models across standard datasets.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Information. Technology (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules 2021
fr1_justif: In November 2023 the Indian government issued an advisory under these rules directed at AI related harms (including harms against women) as official interpretative materials. The advisory asked intermediary platforms to take reasonable efforts to identify deepfakes and take expeditious action against them. The advisory also mandated that platforms have to remove content that spreads misinformation within the first 36 hours. Failure to comply with this will allow aggrieved users to take platforms to court under the provisions of the Indian Penal Code. Public link: https://www.pib.gov.in/PressReleaseIframePage.aspx?PRID=1975445#:~:text=Given%20the%20significant%20challenges%20posedmeasures%20t
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Report on AI Governance Guidelines Development (India)
fr1_justif: Ethical Guidelines for Application of AI in Biomedical Research and Healthcare provide concrete and operational provisions on human oversight and it is sector-specific - applies to healthcare contexts (pp.11 12: Autonomy Human in The Loop Safety and Risk Minimization).
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: National strategy for Artificial Intelligence #AIFORALL
fr1_justif: The framework addresses the indicator by explicitly promoting the use of impact assessments across multiple dimensions including fairness privacy harm and labour market effects. It calls on developers to “identify the in-built biases and assess their impact” to mitigate discriminatory outcomes (p. 85) recommends the use of “Data Privacy Impact Assessment Tools” to manage privacy risks (p. 87) and proposes “damage impact assessment at every stage of development of an AI model” within a negligence-based framework (p. 88). It also extends impact assessment to broader societal consequences advocating continuous monitoring of AI’s effects on employment and suggesting the creation of taskforce to track labour market changes (p. 92).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: The Artificial Intelligence (Protection of Rights of Employees) Bill 2023 (India)
fr1_justif: Right to Transparency (Section 3(a) Page 2-3): provide comprehensive documentation to employees about the purpose requirement functionality and ramifications of AI systems on their jobs Right to Refuse and Challenge decisions based solely on AI (Section 3(c) Page 3) Right to Training and Adaptation for employees affected by AI implementation (Section 3(d) Page 3) Data Privacy and Personal Information Protection during AI implementation (Section 3(e)) Explicit Informed Consent from employees for AI implementation (Section 4(a) Page 3) Bias Mitigation (Section 3(b) Page 3 Equality Impact Assessment (Section 5 Page 3)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Technical Guidelines on SBOM QBOM & CBOM AIBOM HBOM Version 2.0 (Indian Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT-In))
fr1_justif: (Section 9.4.1 Pages 56-57) All government public sector essential services organizations and organizations involved in AI-driven development and services should include AIBOM requirements in all AI-related procurements and solutions. All AI solutions supplied to government public sector and essential services organizations must be accompanied by AIBOM detailing the components and dependencies within the AI system.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Empowering Public Sector Leadership: A Commentary Framework for AI Integration in India
fr1_justif: The framework directly addresses public sector AI skills development by outlining structured competency requirements and training pathways. Pages 6–8 emphasize upskilling and reskilling government officials for AI integration. Sections 4.2.3 (pp.38–39) and 4.3.2 (pp.42–43) promote continuous learning and AI leadership. Sections 5.1.1 (p.46) and 5.2 (pp.57–58) define AI literacy and functional competencies listing training programs. Pages 45–59 detail procurement data governance and AI architecture skills.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Indian Standard: Information Technology = Artificial Intelligence - Management System Standard (IS/ISO/IEC 42001:2023)
fr1_justif: The framework explicitly addresses AI security and safety by recognising that “safety and robustness” are essential for building trust particularly in high-risk applications (p. 8). It identifies concrete security risks including adversarial attacks data manipulation and model vulnerabilities across the AI lifecycle (pp. 23–24). The document further establishes a Principle of Safety and Reliability requiring safeguards risk minimisation and continuous monitoring to ensure systems perform as intended and protect stakeholders from harm (p. 41).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Report on AI Governance Guidelines Development (India)
fr1_justif: The Strategy directly addresses Transparency and Explainabilty (p.85 86-88): by highlighting the “black box phenomenon” in AI systems and the risks of limited understanding of how decisions are made. It emphasizes the need for “explainability” rather than full technical disclosure ensuring that AI decisions are understandable to users especially in high-impact contexts. The document also promotes research in Explainable AI (XAI) to develop systems that allow users to understand trust and effectively manage AI outputs thereby strengthening transparency and accountability.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: National Institution for Transforming India Aayog Centres of Research Excellence (COREs) and International Centres for Transformational AI (ICTAIs)

fr1_title: AI National Strategy 2020-2045 (Stranas KA) (Indonesia)
fr1_justif: Chapter 4 (p.49-59) focuses on building human resources and strengthening AI literacy across the country. It highlights several strategic issues such as the shortage of skilled professionals in AI and data science the limited alignment between education and industry needs and the lack of public awareness about AI technologies. It stresses that Indonesia’s education system-both formal and non-formal—must be reoriented to produce competent AI practitioners and a workforce that is ready to adapt to AI-driven transformation. To address these challenges the strategy proposes initiatives including integrating AI data science and machine learning into school and university curricula; offering training workshops and vocational programmes.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: KORIKA (Indonesian AI Research and Innovation Committee/ Komite Riset dan Inovasi Kecerdasan Artifisial Indonesia)

fr1_title: The Concept for Artificial Intelligence Ethical Guideline (Indonesia)
fr1_justif: The document (Page. 9) mentioned the benefit of ethical AI one of them is to identify risk and mitigation as well as safety measures including for disinformation risk (the ability of AI systems to generate false content such as deepfakes can open up opportunities for abuse. This phenomenon increases the potential for information manipulation which can undermine integrity). It also includes steps of safeguard measures depending on the risk (page 12-13 table 1).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Law of the Republic of Indonesia No. 27 of 2022 on Personal Data Protection
fr1_justif: Yes data subjects have the right to file complaints with the Personal Data Protection Authority (PDPA) if they believe their personal data has been processed unlawfully or their rights have been violated (Art 56-57 p. 15-16). These articles establish a formal administrative mechanism for redress requiring the authority to follow up and resolve complaints. Art. 58 guarantees individuals the right to compensation for material or immaterial losses resulting from violations of their data protection rights allowing them to pursue remedies through civil proceedings. The law also provides for administrative sanctions (Art. 57): warnings temporary suspension and deletion of personal data and criminal sanctions (Art. 67-73) for severe breach.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Personal Data Protection Authority (PDPA or Otoritas Perlindungan Data Pribadi)

fr1_title: Regulation of the Government of the Republic of Indonesia Number 17 of 2025 on Governance of the Organization of Electronic Systems in Child Protection
fr1_justif: Art. 7 (p.4) protecting children’s personal data by obtaining parental or guardian consent and conducting a Personal Data Protection Impact Assessment. They must design child-accessible products services and features with high privacy settings by default and provide complete accurate and non-misleading information to users.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Circular Letter of the Minister of Communication and Informatics Number 9 of 2023 concerning Artificial Intelligence Ethics (Indonesia)
fr1_justif: Yes it does include a bottom-line mention of environmental impact and sustainability in the context of AI ethics. For example one of the listed ethical values is Sustainable Development and Environment (Art 6B.8 page 5) mentioned that the implementation of Artificial Intelligence carefully considers the impacts on humans the environment and other living things to achieve sustainability and social welfare. This means AI deployment must consider impacts on humans the environment and other living beings in order to achieve sustainability and social welfare.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: A Guideline for Responsible and Trusted Artificial Intelligence (AI) Code Of Ethics In The Financial Technology Industry (Indonesia)
fr1_justif: It is explicitly mentioned in part B.3 (Page 3-4) that the use of AI-based applications relates to validity and accuracy taking into account the principles of fairness and non-discrimination taking into account Fintech providers must ensure their AI-based business models and applications do not harm consumers violate privacy or create discrimination due to the “black box” nature of AI. They must implement risk mitigation frameworks to ensure algorithms code and data are accurate unbiased accountable and aligned with data protection regulations. The AI systems must be tested to meet high accuracy standards and be developed in line with ethical values and legal requirements.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: The Concept for Artificial Intelligence Ethical Guideline (Indonesia)
fr1_justif: The document addresses gender equality in the context of AI by recognizing that AI systems can unintentionally reinforce existing gender-based inequalities through algorithmic bias (p.9) Under the principle of justice and adequacy the document explicitly states that AI should not reinforce social economic or gender inequalities (p.12) demonstrating a clear commitment to gender equality. Additionally the document identifies women as a vulnerable group highlighting their increased exposure to risks such as unauthorized data collection lack of transparency and biased AI outputs which can further entrench discrimination if left unaddressed (p.9).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Circular Letter of the Minister of Communication and Informatics Number 9 of 2023 concerning Artificial Intelligence Ethics (Indonesia)
fr1_justif: The “humanity” principle (Art.6B.2) aligns with the definition of human oversight because it places clear emphasis on keeping humans in control of AI systems especially in contexts that affect human rights dignity and social values. The circular explicitly instructs that AI must not act as the final decision-maker in matters concerning humanity thereby ensuring that human actors retain the authority to monitor supervise control review and intervene in AI operations. By embedding the humanity principle in its ethical framework the circular mandates that human decision-making must stay central preventing fully autonomous or unchecked AI operations and thereby fulfilling the fundamental objectives of human oversight.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: AI National Strategy 2020-2045 (Stranas KA) (Indonesia)
fr1_justif: It addresses reskilling and upskilling initiatives in the context of AI primarily under its Talent / Human Resources development pillar. In Chapter 4.1 (p.49-50) the document emphasises the need to upgrade existing workforce capabilities and develop new talent so that Indonesia can keep pace with AI-driven change in industries and society. It proposes initiatives such as expanding non-formal and vocational education (e.g. training workshops certification programmes) alongside formal education thereby enabling workers already in the labour market to reskill (learn new AI-related skills) or upskill (enhance their current skills) so they remain relevant (p.51-52)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: KORIKA (Indonesian AI Research and Innovation Committee/ Komite Riset dan Inovasi Kecerdasan Artifisial Indonesia)

fr1_title: Circular Letter of the Minister of Communication and Informatics Number 9 of 2023 concerning Artificial Intelligence Ethics (Indonesia)
fr1_justif: Yes the document places safety and security as one of its nine core ethical values emphasizing that AI development and use must protect users data and systems from harm or misuse. It requires AI providers and electronic system operators to establish internal ethical policies covering data protection risk management and crisis response to ensure system reliability and prevent misuse (Art. 6B.3 page 4).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Circular Letter of the Minister of Communication and Informatics Number 9 of 2023 concerning Artificial Intelligence Ethics (Indonesia)

Businesses and Electronic Sytem Provider can provide authorized users with access to information about data management in the development of AI-based technologies.

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Bots fake accounts and automated conduct are mechanisms of disinformation campaigns (recital 85) and as such rules are needed to combat them (recital 9). Systemic risk assessments apply to large-scale online platforms and search engines (art. 34) and explicitly include the dissemination or amplification of misleading or fraudulent content and the negative effects on gender-based violence. This includes recommendation systems and other relevant algorithmic systems (art. 34). Mitigation measures include testing and adapting algorithmic and recommendation systems; speeding up the processing of notifications of illegal hate speech and cyber violence; and labeling content generated or manipulated by AI (art.35).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Online platform service providers must ensure the privacy security and protection of minors in their services (Art. 28) including prohibiting advertising based on profiling through the automated processing of data about children (Art. 28). Terms and conditions must be understandable to minors (Art. 14) ensuring a high level of privacy and security by default based on good practices (recitals 70 71). Large-scale online platforms and large-scale search engines systematic risk assessment applies periodically evaluating potential negative effects on minors (Art. 34). Mitigation measures are determined: age verification parental control tools abuse reporting tools and support for minors (Art. 35). Voluntary standards apply (Art. 44).

fr1_title: Guidelines for the Responsible Use of Artificial Intelligence in the Public Service (Ireland)
fr1_justif: 4.6.1 Overview We are committed to ensuring that all AI systems in the Irish Public Service have a positive impact on Irish society. According to the European Commission “AI systems should benefit all human beingsincluding future generations. It must hence be ensured that they are sustainable and environmentally friendly. Moreover they should take into account the environment including other living beings and their social and societal impact should be carefully considered
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The law focuses on the risk classification system (Art. 6) and the requirements imposed on high-risk AI systems. It requires suppliers of high-risk AI systems to establish implement document and maintain a risk (and damage) management system throughout the system's lifecycle (Art. 9). Training validation and test datasets must conform to data governance in terms of representation error-freeness completeness contextualization and the function the system will be used for handling special personal data for monitoring purposes and detecting and correcting biases. High-risk AI systems must have consistency throughout the lifecycle by requiring appropriate levels of accuracy and robustness and reducing the risk of bias (art.15).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act addresses gender equality through non-discrimination and fundamental rights provisions. Training datasets for high-risk AI must be representative avoinding the discriminatory outcomes and protecting atributes explicitly related gender (Art. 10). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights impact assessments before deploying high-risk AI incluing gender-based discrimination (Art. 27). Equality bodies are granted market surveillance powers enabling them to investigate and sanction AI systems producing discriminatory outcomes (Art. 74).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act establishes requirements to ensure human control over high-risk AI systems requiring providers to design systems that enable natural persons with a minimum level of comprehensibility to monitor outputs detect anomalies and intervene or interrupt operations when necessary (art. 14). Deployers must assign oversight to competent natural persons with authority to act (art. 26). For general-purpose AI with systemic risk adversarial testing and incident reporting reinforce human control (Arts. 55 73). Additional oversight provisions are set in the fundamental rights impact assessment (art. 27) and Annex (IV) 2(e) as part of technical documentation requirements (art. 11).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Law establishes an assessment architecture according to the type and level of risk of the system. High-risk AI suppliers must identify analyze and mitigate risks during the design development and implementation phases within a risk management system (Arti.9) with comprehensive technical documentation (Art.15) and monitoring of operations and anomalies (Art. 26). For AI implementers in the public sector a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment applies before implementing high-risk systems listed in Annex 3 (Art.27). Compliance assessment must include self-assessment or third-party audits depending on the criticality of the system (Art. 43 47). There are ex-post assessments and post-commercialization monitoring (Art.72).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act classifies systems for recruitment promotion termination and worker management as high-risk AI systems (Annex III(4)) (art. 6). Prohibited AI practices include bans on emotion-recognition in the workplace (art. 5). Providers must implement risk-management addressing discrimination (Art. 9) ensure representative datasets and bias-control (art. 10) and maintain technical documentation (Arts. 11). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights assessments before deployment (art. 27). Art. 26 (7-9) establishes safeguards: workers must be informed and may request explanations of AI use. However the Act does not regulate pay conditions or compensation so it mitigates risks without constituting a full labour-rights regime.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_justif: Pg 30- section 4.4.1 Overview: Mandate for Algorithmic Transparency and Public Accountability: To uphold the principles of administrative justice and public trust Ireland is committed to the proactive disclosure of all algorithmic systems through a standardized documentation approach. This Guideline ensures that citizens are clearly notified at the point of contact whenever they interact with an AI system or receive a decision-driven output. The Government pledges to provide accessible non-technical explanations of each system’s logic intended purpose and impact ensuring that the technology remains understandable to the diverse public it serves.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Safety and security is core to the Act. It establishes a risk-based framework for risk identification and mitigation (Art. 9) oversight measures (Art. 14) and robustness and accuracy requirements (Art. 15). To ensure ongoing safety the Act mandates technical documentation and record-keeping (Arts. 11–12) and in certain high risk situations a conformity assessment procedure prior to market placement (Art. 43). Post-market obligations in Arts. 72–73 require providers and deployers to monitor performance report serious incidents and take corrective action. For GPAI models posing systemic risks Arts. 55–56 impose additional safety and cybersecurity duties including model evaluation and risk mitigation following the Code of Practice.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Articles 11 12 and 13 establish several transparency and documentation obligations for AI developers and deployers. High-risk AI systems must include clear information on their capabilities limitations and intended purpose (Art. 13) and providers must maintain comprehensive technical documentation and automatic logging to ensure traceability (Arts. 11–12). These measures enable deployers to better understand the systems and authorities to audit or investigate when necessary. There are disclosure obligations that require notification when individuals interact with AI systems particularly chatbots and emotion recognition tools (Art. 50). Also for general-purpose AI in terms of model documentation and summary of training data (Art. 53).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: 2023 Artificial Intelligence Regulation and Ethics Policy Principles Document (Israel)
fr1_justif: The document addresses Human Oversight in Part Four under the section 'Human Involvement.' It explores the need for human participation in AI decision-making outlining both its advantages and challenges. ?????The policy details different levels of oversight such as human-in-the-loop (where a person is part of the decision) and human-on-the-loop (where a person supervises or can veto a decision) . It highlights benefits like improved accountability and legitimacy but also notes challenges like 'automation bias'. Ultimately Part Five recommends that the required level of human involvement be determined based on the context and the specific risks of the AI application

For risk management it recommends integrating human involvement throughout the AI lifecycle (ex ante real-time or ex post). For high-risk systems the guide explicitly recommends implementing human control and an 'off switch'.These concepts are primarily discussed in the principles of 'Responsible Use' (Chapter 2) and the risk mitigation strategies (Appendix D).

fr1_title: 2023 Artificial Intelligence Regulation and Ethics Policy Principles Document (Israel)
fr1_justif: The document discusses impact assessments primarily as tools for transparency and risk management. In Section 5.3 (Explainability) it suggests requiring AI users to publish an Algorithm Impact Statement (AIS) drawing a parallel to Regulatory Impact Assessments (RIA) used in other fields. This statement would disclose the system's development operation and expected impact.Furthermore Section 5.1 (Discrimination) recommends conducting risk assessments to evaluate concerns about discrimination and potential harm. The text also notes international precedents such as the EU's 'prior assessment' for high-risk systems and the Council of Europe's draft convention regarding risk assessment duties.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Government Knowledge and Coordination Center for AI Regulation
fr2_title: Guide for Risk Management and Responsible Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Tools in the Public Sector (Israel)
fr2_justif: The document addresses Impact Assessments through its Risk Management Methodology detailed in Appendix D. This iterative process functions as an impact assessment via:Analysis & Assessment (Stage 1): Users must map benefits and risks explicitly evaluating the 'magnitude of impact' and scope of affected parties (e.g. vulnerable populations).Triage (Stage 3): A decision-making phase to determine if the system should proceed based on the assessment.Mitigation: Section 3.1 mandates an 'impact mitigation plan' for high-risk systems.Rights Assessment: Appendix D Part 3 requires examining systems for significant harm to fundamental rights.
fr2_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems
fr1_title: Guide for Risk Management and Responsible Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Tools in the Public Sector (Israel)
fr1_justif: The document addresses Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems mainly through its transparency obligations. Chapter 3 requires organizations to “create public engagement processes” and publish periodic reports (p.6 sec. 3.1). The AI Governance Officer must publish information about AI systems on the organization’s website (p.9 sec. 3.4). In Appendix D transparency is detailed as part of system implementation including disclosure of system use data types capabilities limitations and incident reporting (pp.18–19 “Transparency to the Public”).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Guide for Risk Management and Responsible Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Tools in the Public Sector (Israel)
fr1_justif: The document addresses Public Procurement of AI mainly through guidance on acquiring and approving AI tools. Appendix A §1.3 sets a dedicated policy for procuring off-the-shelf AI tools requiring coordination with cyber privacy and accounting units and compliance with IT Directive 16.12.1.2 and cloud-procurement rules. It also discusses examining vendor information as part of risk assessment (Appendix D Stage 1).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Guide for Risk Management and Responsible Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Tools in the Public Sector (Israel)
fr1_justif: The guide positions skills development as essential for responsible AI. The National Digital Agency offers training via 'HaDigitalit' and an expert center (Sec. 1.4). The AI Governance Officer is responsible for 'Leading Organizational Learning Processes' (Appendix A) involving learning plans workshops and training to deepen risk understanding. End Users must maintain 'Literacy' by taking courses to understand tool limitations (Appendix C). Finally 'Awareness Raising' and 'knowledge transfer' are key risk mitigation strategies (Appendix D).
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: National Program for Artificial Intelligence (Israel)
fr1_justif: The document addresses reskilling and upskilling in Section 10.3 “Preparation for the Changes Expected in the Labor Market” (pp. 78–79). It outlines measures such as training schemes to develop AI skills a dedicated career-change track for “professions at risk” and programs for retraining vulnerable workers. The section explains that AI will redefine jobs and require new professional capabilities necessitating updated training and workforce policies to equip workers for emerging AI-driven roles.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Funding for security and defense is stated in page 5 as an issue to be treated separately. Yet AI in general is treated as a national security issue.
fr1_body_name: central directorate (These later was defined as National AI Headquarters) and the Telem Forum for R&D and different governmental offices according to topic - Labor Education and Ministry of Innovation The Ministry of Innovation is generally in charge of implementing and overseeing the AI plans in Israel but as specific tasks are under the purview of specific offices - they are each assigned the responsibility for implementation in their area Telem Forum is a bit unique in that aspect as it is an expert forum with expertise and national R&D

fr1_title: 2023 Artificial Intelligence Regulation and Ethics Policy Principles Document (Israel)
fr1_justif: The document addresses Safety and Security by requiring AI systems to be reliable robust secure and safe across their entire lifecycle. It highlights risks from technical failures vulnerabilities manipulation misuse by malicious actors and the added safety risks created when physical products integrate AI that can learn or change after deployment. It also stresses risk-management duties documentation and information-sharing between actors.Key locations: Section “Reliability Robustness Security and Safety”
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Government Knowledge and Coordination Center for AI Regulation
fr2_title: Guide for Risk Management and Responsible Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Tools in the Public Sector (Israel)
fr2_justif: The document treats Safety and Security as core principles for responsible AI use. In Chapter 2 “System security and safety” (p. 5–6) it requires AI systems to remain reliable secure and non-hazardous in both planned and unplanned scenarios and to include mechanisms for handling risk situations.Appendix D (Risk Classification p. 17–18) details information-security risks model-manipulation attacks data leakage and safety harms. Mitigation tools appear in Appendix D pp. 19–22.
fr2_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: 2023 Artificial Intelligence Regulation and Ethics Policy Principles Document (Israel)
fr1_justif: As detailed in the Executive Summary and Part Six the policy addresses the 'black box' problem where an AI's decision-making process is opaque. It proposes that people should be informed when interacting with or being affected by AI systems. Furthermore it recommends providing explanations for an AI's decisions or actions especially considering the impact on individuals and technological limitations. This principle aims to protect human dignity enable accountability and build public trust in AI technologies.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr2_title: Guide for Risk Management and Responsible Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Tools in the Public Sector (Israel)
fr2_justif: Section 2 (p.5) - Lists it as Principle #3 of OECD responsible AI use requiring organizations to provide substantial information about system operation data sources underlying logic capabilities/limitations and ways to address negative outcomes.Appendix A (p.9) - Requires AI Governance Officers to publish information about AI systems on organizational websites and report AI incidents.Appendix C (p.11-13) - Mandates end users to note when AI generates content and avoid presenting it as human creation; includes Documentation requirements.Appendix D (p.16 p.22) - Lists technical solutions like RAG/CAG and Reasoning to enhance explainability; addresses the 'black box' challenge (p.17). Recommends Disclosure components in products (p.21
fr2_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Italian Law on Artificial Intelligence Law No. 132 of 23 September 2025 — Provisions and delegations to the Government in matters of Artificial Intelligence
fr1_justif: The law mandates AI literacy and training across education and professional sectors. Art. 24(e)-p.15 provides “pathways for literacy and training in the use of artificial intelligence systems”. Art. 24(g)-p.15 strengthens STEM skills “within school curricula” to guide students toward AI disciplines. Art. 24(i)-p.15 requires universities AFAM institutions and ITS Academies to deliver technical understanding and informed use of AI including interpreting AI outputs.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: While still requiring adherence to constitutional rights Article 6 expressly excludes the framework of this law from developments in intelligence defense and national security fields so the framework is only applicable to civil applications but it is not applicable to the defense or security domains.
fr1_body_name: Agency for Digital Italy (AgID) and National Cybersecurity Agency (ACN)

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Bots fake accounts and automated conduct are mechanisms of disinformation campaigns (recital 85) and as such rules are needed to combat them (recital 9). Systemic risk assessments apply to large-scale online platforms and search engines (art. 34) and explicitly include the dissemination or amplification of misleading or fraudulent content and the negative effects on gender-based violence. This includes recommendation systems and other relevant algorithmic systems (art. 34). Mitigation measures include testing and adapting algorithmic and recommendation systems; speeding up the processing of notifications of illegal hate speech and cyber violence; and labeling content generated or manipulated by AI (art.35).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU GDPR
fr1_justif: The law provides a layered redress framework that applies in AI contexts whenever personal data is processed. Data subjects harmed by automated processing based on AI-driven profiling and decision-making hold the right to express their point of view and the right to contest the decision (art. 22); presenting complaints to supervisory authorities (art. 77) resorting to legal measures against gaps in the authorities (art. 78) and bringing proceedings directly against controllers or processors (art. 79). Organizations may be mandated to exercise the rights collectively on behalf of data subjects (art. 80). Supervisory authorities may impose administrative fines because of the violations of core provisions (art. 83).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: This Regulation shall not apply to the processing of personal data by Member States in the context of carrying out activities relating to the Union's common foreign and security policy (recital 16). The processing of personal data by Member States does not apply to activities related to the protection and prevention of threats to public security (Art. 2).
fr1_body_name: The law establishes the European Data Protection Board is comprised of representatives from the supervisory authority of each member states (Arts 68) acting independently (Art 69) providing guidelines recommendations and binding decisions to ensure consistence across Member States (Art 70) The Member State must to establish am independent supervisory authority responsible for monitoring and enforcing the regulation (Art 51)

fr1_title: Italian Law on Artificial Intelligence Law No. 132 of 23 September 2025 — Provisions and delegations to the Government in matters of Artificial Intelligence
fr1_justif: The law addresses children’s rights mainly through data privacy and age-based access rules. Article 4 (p.2-3) requires parental consent for minors under 14 to use AI systems and process related personal data; minors aged 14–18 may consent directly if information on risks is accessible and understandable.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: While still requiring adherence to constitutional rights Article 6 expressly exclude the framework of this law from developments in intelligence defense and national security field so the framework is only applicable to civil applications but it is not applicable to the defense or security domains.
fr1_body_name: Agency for Digital Italy (AgID) and National Cybersecurity Agency (ACN)

fr1_title: Italian Strategy for Artificial Intelligence 2024–2026
fr1_justif: The Italian Strategy for Artificial Intelligence 2024–2026 warns about stereotyping and cultural homogenization due to reliance on generative AI systems built elsewhere highlighting threats to linguistic and cultural diversity (pp. 11–12 Risks of the strategy). In order to maintain Italy's unique cultural character and democratic ideals it emphasizes the necessity of developing AI in accordance with national and constitutional norms. According to the plan generative technologies with an Italian context should be promoted cultural diversity should be respected and reliance on outside models should be avoided.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Italian Strategy for Artificial Intelligence 2024–2026
fr1_justif: The Strategy explicitly frames environmental sustainability as a cross-cutting priority for AI. It links competitiveness to socially responsible AI highlighting risks of energy consumption privacy and ecological impacts (pp. 4–6 8–10). Strategy for Research Action R.3 calls for 'environmental sustainability' by seeking innovative techniques to 'reduce the size of models' and their energy footprint (p. 19) also Action R.4 suggests interdisciplinary research projects focused on 'landscape protection sea or environmental protection' and the 'optimization of environmental and energy resources (p. 19).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Department for Digital Transformation - Agency for Digital Italy (AgID) - FAIR Foundation

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The law focuses on the risk classification system (Art. 6) and the requirements imposed on high-risk AI systems. It requires suppliers of high-risk AI systems to establish implement document and maintain a risk (and damage) management system throughout the system's lifecycle (Art. 9). Training validation and test datasets must conform to data governance in terms of representation error-freeness completeness contextualization and the function the system will be used for handling special personal data for monitoring purposes and detecting and correcting biases. High-risk AI systems must have consistency throughout the lifecycle by requiring appropriate levels of accuracy and robustness and reducing the risk of bias (art.15).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.
fr1_body_name: AI office coordinates EU implementation (art64) European AI Board composed of one representative per Member State aims to issue guidance support consistent application and coordinate national authorities (art65 66) the national competent authorities (art70) once each country must designate at least one market surveillance authority and a notifying authority The Advisory Forum (Art 67) provides technical expertise advising the commission and council The Scientific Panel of Independent Experts (Art 68) provides support with specialized scientific and technical knowledge However neither directly participates in implementation

fr1_title: Italian Law on Artificial Intelligence Law No. 132 of 23 September 2025 — Provisions and delegations to the Government in matters of Artificial Intelligence
fr1_justif: Article 3 establishes “non-discrimination” and “gender equality” as guiding principles for the development and use of AI alongside transparency explainability and human oversight. Article 7(2) prohibits AI in healthcare from conditioning access to services on discriminatory criteria. Article 11(3) guarantees that AI in the workplace respects workers’ inviolable rights “without discrimination based on sex age ethnic origin religion sexual orientation.” Articles 14–15 confirm AI is supportive not substitutive safeguarding rights in public administration and justice.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: While still requiring adherence to constitutional rights Article 6 expressly removes AI-related actions pertaining to intelligence defense and national security from the purview of the law. The framework is only applicable to civil applications but it is not applicable to the defense or security domains.
fr1_body_name: Agency for Digital Italy (AgID) and National Cybersecurity Agency (ACN)

fr1_title: Italian Law on Artificial Intelligence Law No. 132 of 23 September 2025 — Provisions and delegations to the Government in matters of Artificial Intelligence
fr1_justif: The law requires human oversight and intervention in AI systems particularly in sensitive areas (Arts. 5-7 pp. 12-16) necessitates the designation of accountable personnel in charge of AI use and consequences (Art. 7 p. 15). Citizens have the right to human review and appeal of AI-based decisions (Article 9 pp. 18-19). Besides it restricts complete automation in vital areas such as justice healthcare and law enforcement (Arts. 6-7 pages. 13-15).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Law establishes an assessment architecture according to the type and level of risk of the system. High-risk AI suppliers must identify analyze and mitigate risks during the design development and implementation phases within a risk management system (Arti.9) with comprehensive technical documentation (Art.15) and monitoring of operations and anomalies (Art. 26). For AI implementers in the public sector a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment applies before implementing high-risk systems listed in Annex 3 (Art.27). Compliance assessment must include self-assessment or third-party audits depending on the criticality of the system (Art. 43 47). There are ex-post assessments and post-commercialization monitoring (Art.72).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act classifies systems for recruitment promotion termination and worker management as high-risk AI systems (Annex III(4)) (art. 6). Prohibited AI practices include bans on emotion-recognition in the workplace (art. 5). Providers must implement risk-management addressing discrimination (Art. 9) ensure representative datasets and bias-control (art. 10) and maintain technical documentation (Arts. 11). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights assessments before deployment (art. 27). Art. 26 (7-9) establishes safeguards: workers must be informed and may request explanations of AI use. However the Act does not regulate pay conditions or compensation so it mitigates risks without constituting a full labour-rights regime.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

It also mandates technical organisational and training measures to guarantee responsible use. These provisions establish transparency duties regarding how government AI operates ensuring that individuals are informed when administrative processes rely on AI tools.

fr1_title: Italian Strategy for Artificial Intelligence 2024–2026
fr1_justif: The Strategy sets multiple actions to develop AI skills in the public sector. PA.1 (p.22) promotes awareness methodological guidance and understanding of AI risks. PA.3 (p.23) requires training staff in advanced AI-related technologies. PA.6 (pp.24–25) creates AI-focused upskilling programmes and postgraduate training within the National School of Administration and other PA training schools. The overview (p.21) highlights the need to build AI-specific competencies across the public administration.

fr2_justif: The only indirect reference is in Article 15 which requires judicial authorities to understand AI systems used in justice but it does not mandate or outline training programs. There are no sections establishing AI-related training for public administration personnel

fr1_title: Italian Strategy for Artificial Intelligence 2024–2026
fr1_justif: The Strategy explicitly prioritises AI-related reskilling and upskilling. It states “no worker can be left behind” and “roll out a robust programme of incentives for reskilling and upskilling initiatives” (pp. 29). Action F.5 provides “training and professional development courses on AI to retrain and update workers’ skills” (pp. 33-34). Supply-chain academies offer structured reskilling  (p. 33). PA.6 mandates postgraduate AI training for public administration employees  (p. 24).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Safety and security is core to the Act. It establishes a risk-based framework for risk identification and mitigation (Art. 9) oversight measures (Art. 14) and robustness and accuracy requirements (Art. 15). To ensure ongoing safety the Act mandates technical documentation and record-keeping (Arts. 11–12) and in certain high risk situations a conformity assessment procedure prior to market placement (Art. 43). Post-market obligations in Arts. 72–73 require providers and deployers to monitor performance report serious incidents and take corrective action. For GPAI models posing systemic risks Arts. 55–56 impose additional safety and cybersecurity duties including model evaluation and risk mitigation following the Code of Practice.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Articles 11 12 and 13 establish several transparency and documentation obligations for AI developers and deployers. High-risk AI systems must include clear information on their capabilities limitations and intended purpose (Art. 13) and providers must maintain comprehensive technical documentation and automatic logging to ensure traceability (Arts. 11–12). These measures enable deployers to better understand the systems and authorities to audit or investigate when necessary. There are disclosure obligations that require notification when individuals interact with AI systems particularly chatbots and emotion recognition tools (Art. 50). Also for general-purpose AI in terms of model documentation and summary of training data (Art. 53).

fr1_title: Data Protection Act 2020 (Jamaica)
fr1_justif: Section 12 (“Rights in relation to automated decision-taking”) of Jamaica’s Data Protection Act (p. 22 approved in 2020 according https://oic.gov.jm/page/data-protection-act) establishes that individuals have the right not to be subject to decisions that significantly affect them when these are based solely on automated processing and it also entitles data subjects to request that such decisions be reconsidered or remade with human involvement
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Part V Exemption to Data Protection Standards or to Disclosure to Data Subject Requirements (p. 48) covers this. Section 33 (p. 48) deals with National Security. Section 33 (1) talks to exemptions for the purpose of national security.
