fr1_title: Data Protection Act 2020 (Jamaica)
fr1_justif: Section 6(2)(d) of the Data Protection Act 2020 (pg. 14) requires data controllers to inform data subjects of the logic involved in automated decision-making processes directly addressing explainability requirements. Section 12 (pg. 22) further reinforces transparency by entitling individuals to request reconsideration of automated decisions that significantly affect them ensuring data controllers must be accountable for the reasoning behind such decisions. Together these provisions establish a transparency and explainability framework applicable to automated processing in Jamaica.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Part V Exemption to Data Protection Standards  or to Disclosure to Data Subject Requirements (pg. 48) covers this. Section 33 (pg.48) deals with National Security. Section 33 (1) talks to exemptions for the purpose of national security.

fr1_title: Guidelines for the Use of Generative AI at the primary and secondary education level (Japan)
fr1_justif: The Guidelines from the Ministry of Education Culture Sports Science and Technology (MEXT) stress that children should learn to use tools like generative AI [p.3]. They also define teachers’ roles: to use generative AI effectively in schools teachers must attain core AI literacy understanding how it works and its characteristics to build a foundation for appropriate classroom use [p.7]. In addition students should gain a scientific understanding of generative AI its operation societal effects and related laws systems and etiquette and learn to apply it to identify and solve problems [p.9].
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: AI White Paper 2024 New Strategies in Stage II Toward the world's most AI-friendly country (Japan)
fr1_justif: The White Paper treats AI-facilitated misinformation as a critical safety and governance concern. It highlights how generative AI can produce fake images videos and audio that enable fraud and undermine trust [p.16] and how such AI generated misinformation (especially during disasters and accidents) can become life-threatening [p.19]. It also warns of AI’s role in election interference and democratic disruption [p.19]. To counter this it urges legal measures for faster deletion of illegal content platform transparency literacy campaigns and public-private development of detection technologies [pp.20–21] linking misinformation control to preventing harm and maintaining public safety.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Guidelines for the Use of Generative AI at the primary and secondary education level (Japan)
fr1_justif: The guidelines adopt a human-centered rule: AI use must not violate fundamental human rights; humans make final decisions and bear responsibility [§2 Basic Concept – Human-centered principle p.7]. Student use must follow age restrictions and as needed parents’ understanding/consent [Key points for utilization p.19; Checklist p.25]. Students are instructed not to input personal information [p.19] and teachers check outputs for bias [p.19]. Transparency is required: cite AI use and inform parents of its purpose/nature [p.20]. Educators consider students’ developmental stage [Checklist p.25].
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Governance Guidelines for Implementation of AI Principles Ver. 1.1 (Japan)
fr1_justif: The Guidelines recommend to AI system developers to take cultural differences into account and increase the diversity of the team when developing AI systems to ensure that the diversities found in Japan are also well recognized in the discussion [p.26; 69-71].
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: The Guideline for Japanese Governments’ Procurements and Utilizations of Generative AI for the sake of Evolution and Innovation of Public Administration
fr1_justif: The Guidelines emphasizes that the development and expansion of more advanced AI is a significant impact on the environment due to the large amounts of electricity required [p.27]. Evaluation point 11 of the Procurement Check Sheet (Appendix 3) [p.62 of the PDF] explicitly states providing or using a generative AI system that considers environmental impact; for example by using models trained on hardware featuring energy-efficient semiconductors. It lists Documentation that clarifies superior environmental performance in terms of power consumption etc. as examples for supporting this goal.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: AI Guidelines for Business Ver. 1.1 (Japan)
fr1_justif: The Guidelines promote fairness by promoting the elimination of harmful bias/discrimination tied to protected traits and respecting human rights and cultural diversity [pp. 16 23].They require bias controls across the lifecycle of AI systems (data models/algorithms prompts and external services) to prevent unfair outcomes [p.23]. They also call for timely human intervention to mitigate automation bias and correct unfair results [p.17].
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: New Female Digital Talent Development Plan (Japan)
fr1_justif: The plan links gender equality to AI by noting how AI is reshaping daily life and work making AI skills essential for workers [pp. 2 4] and emphasizing women’s acquisition of AI/cloud skills to raise productivity and deliver services [p.4]. It also notes AI tools’ rapid spread and the need for women to keep skills updated [pp. 2 4]; and pairing this with measures that promote women’s economic independence and career advancement via digital upskilling pathways and targets for women’s leadership (30% executives by 2030) [p.3].
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Economy Trade and Industry (METI) Gender Equality Bureau Cabinet Office (translated as Headquarters for Promoting Gender Equality)

fr1_title: AI Guidelines for Business Ver. 1.1 (Japan)
fr1_justif: The guidelines emphasize the necessity of having timely human intervention rather than letting AI make decisions alone and also advocate for implementing measures to protect human judgement from AI influence [pp.1517].
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: AI White Paper 2024 New Strategies in Stage II Toward the world's most AI-friendly country (Japan)
fr1_justif: The White Paper states that “the basis of Japan’s strategy is to continuously assess and reduce risks” [p.18]. It calls for ongoing updates in response to technological and business changes and close cooperation between government and industry to promote risk reduction [p.18]. To address serious risks the government should consider reviewing existing laws and regulations [p.19] and promote preventive measures [p.20]. It also emphasizes Sharing advanced use-cases of AI and quantitative and qualitative impacts in public and private sectors [p.9] and developing internationally consistent safety evaluation and red-teaming methods including checking tools and test implementation methods [pp.20–21].
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems
fr1_title: The Guideline for Japanese Governments’ Procurements and Utilizations of Generative AI for the sake of Evolution and Innovation of Public Administration
fr1_justif: These Guidelines are intended for gov. staff involved in procurement and use of GenAI [p. 6] and are organized around evaluation viewpoints (EV) that function as a practical checklist. Within this framework the they suggest optional requirements such as enabling reasonable disclosure or information-sharing by planners or providers to ensure that relevant prompts or system parameters are not concealed (EV8 having technical measures or documentation in place that allow end users to distinguish AI-generated outputs from human-generated information (EV13) and emphasize the need for management and oversight mechanisms that allow the system’s overview origins and operations to be verified through logs and technical specifications (EV21).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: The Guideline for Japanese Governments’ Procurements and Utilizations of Generative AI for the sake of Evolution and Innovation of Public Administration
fr1_justif: The Guidelines emphasize the need for all individuals involved in the procurement of generative AI within each ministry or agency  understanding both the benefits and risks of generative AI and measures for mitigating the risks associated with generative AI [p.10]. It also notes the importance of Identifying and disseminating best practices for the procurement [p.17] checks on procurements [p. 19] and comply with laws regulations and guidelines related to government information systems when procuring generative AI [p.29]. They also recommend utilizing a 'Procurement Check Sheet' to organize the requirements for procuring generative AI systems [pp.36 39].
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: The guideline does not apply to government information systems handling confidential information related to national security the maintenance of public safety and order as well as information that may potentially become confidential [p. 6].

fr1_title: The Guideline for Japanese Governments’ Procurements and Utilizations of Generative AI for the sake of Evolution and Innovation of Public Administration
fr1_justif: The Guideline addresses Public Sector Skills Development by requiring systematic training and literacy-building for government staff. It mandates education on responding to information-security incidents and risk cases specific to generative AI systems (Evaluation Viewpoint 4) and requires role-tailored training to ensure appropriate literacy technical knowledge risk awareness and ethics for personnel involved in development and operation of generative AI (Evaluation Viewpoint 5). Guiding Principle 8 further highlights education reskilling and stakeholder support as central components of ensuring AI literacy within the public sector [p.31].
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: The guideline does not apply to government information systems handling confidential information related to national security the maintenance of public safety and order as well as information that may potentially become confidential [p.6].

fr1_title: AI Guidelines for Business Ver. 1.1 (Japan)
fr1_justif: The Guidelines note that AI use can be viewed as a solution to some social challenges such as decreasing labor caused by a declining birthrate and aging population [p. 2]. Consequently the Guidelines prognose that work tasks will vastly change due to AI and advocates to take the necessary steps to ensure that the persons engaged in AI in each AI business actor acquire AI literacy [pp. 21 23] to discuss actively about education and reskilling to promote new ways of working [p. 21 23] and provide educational opportunities so that various people can acquire a deeper understanding of benefits of AI [p. 21].
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: AI White Paper 2024 New Strategies in Stage II Toward the world's most AI-friendly country (Japan)
fr1_justif: The white paper highlights AI safety and security as central to Japan’s balanced governance approach. It promotes voluntary risk assessment and reduction complemented by minimal regulatory measures for high-risk systems [p.18]. To strengthen safety infrastructure it proposes enhancing the role of the AI Safety Institute (AISI) in evaluation red-teaming and international collaboration [pp.20-21]. Further emphasis is placed on research into AI vulnerabilities and the creation of a secure data environment that fosters responsible innovation [p.21].
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: AI Guidelines for Business Ver. 1.1 (Japan)
fr1_justif: The guidelines outline the importance that each AI business actor provides stakeholders with information to the reasonable extent necessary In order to ensure verifiability relating to decisions made by AI [pp.18 23]. They also recommend to establish a situation that allows the origin of data and decisions made during the development provision or use of the AI system or service to be traced forward and backward to the extent that is reasonable and technically possible [p.19] and improving explainability and interpretability for relevant stakeholders [pp.19 23].
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Jordan's Artificial Intelligence Strategy and Implementation Plan 2023 - 2027
fr1_justif: The Strategy addresses AI Literacy in pages 15 to 17 (First Objective: Building Capacities and Developing Jordanian Skills). Mainly in subsections on Raising Awareness Education and Digital Literacy. and the Integration of AI topics into school university and vocational curricula (p. 17).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Digital Economy and Entrepreneurship (MoDEE) National Council for Entrepreneurship and a Steering Committee

fr1_title: The National Artificial Intelligence Code of Ethics (Jordan)
fr1_justif: The Code of Ethics explicitly integrates access to redress and remedy under Section 5 (Responsibility and Accountability p. 19). It requires all parties developing or operating AI systems to provide affected individuals with simple accessible and prompt complaint mechanisms (point 2) and to address and correct any negative effects or harms caused by AI systems including serious material or moral damage (point 4). These provisions align with non-judicial redress and remedial accountability principles.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: The National Artificial Intelligence Code of Ethics (Jordan)
fr1_justif: In the Inclusiveness and Justice section on page 14 the Code emphasizes on 'ensuring the fairness of artificial intelligence techniques and not to be biased discriminated or targeted against any group or component of society''.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: The National Artificial Intelligence Code of Ethics (Jordan)
fr1_justif: The National AI Code of Ethics addresses Gender Equality only in a limited and indirect manner. On page 9 the document states that AI systems should ensure inclusiveness and impartiality by avoiding bias linked to specific data categories explicitly including gender alongside race and religion. However the framework does not contain gender-specific objectives measures or implementation mechanisms. As such Gender Equality is acknowledged at the level of bias prevention rather than addressed as a standalone or substantive policy area.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: The National Artificial Intelligence Code of Ethics (Jordan)
fr1_justif: The Jordan National AI Code of Ethics (2022) addresses it in the Humanity and Society section on page 13 emphasizing that AI must respect human dignity role and status in society by “not forcing humans to submit to artificial intelligence decisions without granting AI the right of human intervention.” This principle underscores that humans retain ultimate control and decision-making authority over AI systems ensuring accountability and preventing full automation of judgment.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: AI Regulatory Framework for the banking sector (2025) (Jordan)
fr1_justif: The AI Regulatory Framework for the Jordanian Banking Sector (CBJ 2024) mandates banks to conduct AI-specific impact assessments prior to system deployment (Sec. 2.4 & 6.1–6.2). Institutions must evaluate ethical operational and cybersecurity risks document decision-making logic and maintain a centralised AI-incident log. These assessments feed into the CBJ’s supervisory reviews through FinCERT and compliance audits ensuring that AI applications undergo structured risk evaluation human oversight and accountability throughout their lifecycle to prevent harm and ensure transparency.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Jordan's Artificial Intelligence Strategy and Implementation Plan
fr1_justif: The framework addresses the indicator by explicitly recognising the risk that AI adoption may “lead to the loss of traditional jobs” and proposing mitigation measures such as workforce retraining and rehabilitation (p. 31). It further establishes the principle that AI systems should act as “a complement to the existing workforce not a replacement for it” (p. 7) thereby directly framing AI deployment in relation to labour protection. By acknowledging displacement risks and outlining concrete measures to address them the strategy goes beyond a purely economic focus and aligns with the indicator’s inclusion of protection against job loss due to automation.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Digital Economy and Entrepreneurship (MoDEE) and National Council for Entrepreneurship

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Jordan Artificial Intelligence Policy 2020
fr1_justif: The framework addresses the indicator by explicitly identifying public procurement as a key mechanism for AI adoption stating that “government’s procurement mechanisms are the main drivers to adopt smart solutions in the public sector” and calling for a review of procurement legislation to facilitate this (p. 6). It further outlines concrete priorities for shaping this process including the promotion of open-source solutions and the empowerment of Jordanian entrepreneurs and start-ups as suppliers of AI systems (p. 6).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: National Ministerial Committee for AI (the Committee)

fr1_title: Jordan's Artificial Intelligence Strategy and Implementation Plan 2023 - 2027
fr1_justif: The Jordan AI Strategy (2023–2027) prioritizes capacity building under its First Objective (pp. 15–18) outlining initiatives to train civil servants through awareness programs technical and professional AI training and the establishment of professional standards. It also highlights education and digital literacy development across levels. Additionally the Third Axis: Data and Skills (p. 29) emphasizes training public sector cadres to enhance performance and innovation.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Digital Economy and Entrepreneurship (MoDEE) National Council for Entrepreneurship and a Steering Committee

fr1_title: Jordan's Artificial Intelligence Strategy and Implementation Plan 2023 - 2027
fr1_justif: In the Strategy page 18 (Capacity Building and Upgrading Skills and Competencies) and page 30 (Risk Management). The first explicitly highlights a focus on specialized training programs for graduates and workers to build AI-relevant technical skills (p. 18). While the Risk management requires “modernization and rehabilitation projects to pave the way for employees to change their work area” (p. 30).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Digital Economy and Entrepreneurship (MoDEE) and the National Council for Entrepreneurship

fr1_title: Jordan's Artificial Intelligence Strategy and Implementation Plan 2023 - 2027
fr1_justif: The Jordan Artificial Intelligence Strategy and Implementation Plan (2023–2027) addresses AI safety and security mainly under Objective Four: Ensuring a legislative and regulatory environment that supports the safe deployment of AI (page 21) and Risk Management (page 30 & 31). It mandates continuous review of laws to manage risks protect privacy and strengthen public trust. It promotes adherence to the National AI Code of Ethics includes cybersecurity measures and requires safeguards to prevent misuse and ensure reliable explainable and safe AI applications.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Digital Economy and Entrepreneurship (MoDEE) National Council for Entrepreneurship and a Steering Committee

fr1_title: The National Artificial Intelligence Code of Ethics (Jordan)
fr1_justif: The Jordan National AI Code of Ethics (2022) explicitly addresses Transparency and Explainability across several sections. Page 9 lists Transparency among the key legal references guiding the Code. A full section on Transparency appears on page 18 detailing requirements for clarity explainability and disclosure in AI systems. On page 24 the principles for virtual environments emphasize responsibility transparency and integrity. On page 27 under Ethics of Scientific Research it mandates structural transparency in data flow and results.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Conceptual Framework for the Implementation of Artificial Intelligence in the System of Secondary Technical and Vocational and Post-Secondary Education for 2025–2029
fr1_justif: The Concept treats AI literacy as a foundational competence for both learners and teachers. For learners AI literacy is embedded in formal curricula through Digital Literacy and Informatics covering AI concepts ethics data protection and algorithmic thinking (Chapter 1.2) and conceptualised as a basic skill comparable to reading and writing under Priority 1: Educational Content (Chapter 3.1) including extracurricular programmes and partnerships. Teacher AI literacy is addressed through the development of AI competencies aligned with UNESCO frameworks (Chapter 1.4) and a structured professional development model (Chapter 3.3). Learners’ AI literacy proficiency is formally monitored within the evaluation framework (Chapter 4.3).
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.
fr1_body_name: JSC Taldau designated as the body responsible for coordinating monitoring and evaluation of the Concept (Chapter 42) Ybyray Altynsarin National Academy of Education responsible for methodological support development of the Open Methodological Complex AI ethics guidance and implementation of Priority 5 (Chapters 32 and 35) JSC National Centre for Professional Development rleu (NCPC rleu) responsible for teacher professional development and AI competency training (Chapters 14 and 33)

fr1_title: Dossier on the Draft Law of the Republic of Kazakhstan “On Artificial Intelligence” (September 2025)
fr1_justif: The Draft Law explicitly addresses the risks of AI-facilitated misinformation and manipulation through synthetic content. Article 1(4) defines “synthetic results of AI systems” as images videos audio text or combinations that imitate people or events that did not occur. Article 17(3) prohibits the creation and use of AI systems that:- Use manipulative methods to distort individual behavior to cause or threaten harm.  - Classify individuals based on behavior or traits for discriminatory purposes. - Generate or distribute content prohibited by Kazakh law. These clauses directly regulate the creation and spread of deceptive AI-generated content and behaviors linked to misinformation and social manipulation.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Dossier on the Draft Law of the Republic of Kazakhstan “On Artificial Intelligence” (September 2025)
fr1_justif: The Draft Law addresses redress and remedy indirectly. Article 24 provides that harm caused by AI systems shall be compensated in accordance with the Civil Code of the Republic of Kazakhstan establishing a general pathway for redress.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Conceptual Framework for the Implementation of Artificial Intelligence in the System of Secondary Technical and Vocational and Post-Secondary Education for 2025–2029
fr1_justif: The framework embeds children’s rights as a core condition for the use of AI in education. The Mission statement requires AI to be used in the best interests of the learner prioritising the rights of the child and the role of the teacher (Introduction). Chapter 2.1 (Ethical and Legal Principles) establishes the principle of protecting the rights of the child and pedagogical responsibility requiring human oversight in significant decisions safeguarding dignity and safety and guaranteeing the right to appeal. The same chapter mandates privacy and personal data protection and prohibits discrimination ensuring inclusiveness for learners with special educational needs. Chapter 2.2 reinforces children’s autonomy via academic integrity.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.
fr1_body_name: JSC Taldau designated as the body responsible for coordinating monitoring and evaluation of the Concept (Chapter 42) Ybyray Altynsarin National Academy of Education responsible for methodological support development of the Open Methodological Complex AI ethics guidance and implementation of Priority 5 (Chapters 32 and 35) JSC National Centre for Professional Development rleu (NCPC rleu) responsible for teacher professional development and AI competency training (Chapters 14 and 33)

fr1_title: Concept for the Development of Artificial Intelligence for 2024-2029 (Resolution of the Government of the Republic of Kazakhstan No. 592 of 24 July 2024)
fr1_justif: The Concept actively promotes cultural and linguistic diversity by prioritizing AI tools that strengthen the Kazakh language. In Section 5 Chapter 2 Direction 4 (Scientific Research and AI Technologies) it mandates the development of the Kazakh Large Language Model (KazLLM) and expansion of Kazakh-language tokens and semantic units. It also supports the multilingual AI translator Tilmas covering Kazakh Russian English Turkish Tatar and Uzbek and the creation of Kazakh text-to-speech technologies ensuring AI systems function across languages and cultural contexts.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: the Ministry of Digital Development Innovation and Aerospace Industry (MDDIAI) Sectoral Council on AI Development and the Committee for AI and Innovation Development within the Ministry of Digital Development Innovation and Aerospace Industry

fr1_title: Dossier on the Draft Law of the Republic of Kazakhstan “On Artificial Intelligence” (September 2025)
fr1_justif: The Draft Law on Artificial Intelligence (September 2025) includes limited but explicit references to environmental impact. Article 8.3 states that the creation and operation of AI systems must account for energy efficiency and aim to reduce negative environmental impact. Article 18.1–2 outlines AI risk management obligations (that could include environmental risks based on Article 8.3) requiring system owners to regularly assess and update risk mitigation measures across the AI lifecycle. While these provisions are general they reflect an emerging legal acknowledgment that AI development must consider sustainability and ecological safety.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Dossier on the Draft Law of the Republic of Kazakhstan “On Artificial Intelligence” (September 2025)
fr1_justif: The draft AI law embeds fairness and non-discrimination as core legal principles. Article 6 (Fairness and Equality) requires that AI systems respect human dignity and rights and prohibits discrimination based on race nationality language religion beliefs or other status. Article 17(3) bans the creation or use of AI systems that classify individuals based on biometric data (e.g. to infer race religion political views) for discriminatory purposes.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Dossier on the Draft Law of the Republic of Kazakhstan “On Artificial Intelligence” (September 2025)
fr1_justif: Article 6 requires AI systems to respect citizens' rights ensure fairness and equality and prohibit discrimination based on sex and other characteristics. Article 4 lists “fairness and equality” as a core principle of state AI regulation. These provisions establish explicit protections against gender-based bias in AI design and deployment.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Dossier on the Draft Law of the Republic of Kazakhstan “On Artificial Intelligence” (September 2025)
fr1_justif: The Draft Law “On Artificial Intelligence” (September 2025) strongly emphasizes human oversight. Article 9 states that AI must preserve human autonomy and free will placing human life and rights as the highest value. Article 17 introduces restrictions on fully autonomous AI systems especially those that may manipulate or exploit users without human awareness. Article 21 requires labeling of AI-generated content and prohibits automated decision-making that significantly affects individual rights without compliance with data protection rules. Collectively these provisions ensure that human actors remain in control and informed of AI decisions
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Dossier on the Draft Law of the Republic of Kazakhstan “On Artificial Intelligence” (September 2025)
fr1_justif: The Draft Law addresses impact assessments through a risk-based approach. Article 17 defines categories of AI systems by degree of risk (low medium high) and autonomy. Article 18 establishes a formal risk management process requiring owners/operators to identify analyze and mitigate both intended and unintended risks throughout the AI system’s lifecycle. This includes annual risk updates and mandatory suspension if prohibited risks are detected. Article 15(2)(3) requires documentation of the system depending on its level of impact on safety rights and public order. Article 20 mandates audits including evaluating data quality and illegal functionalities. These provisions functionally amount to a mandatory impact assessment.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Concept for the Development of Artificial Intelligence for 2024-2029 (Resolution of the Government of the Republic of Kazakhstan No. 592 of 24 July 2024)
fr1_justif: The Concept addresses AI-related reskilling and upskilling in Section 5 Chapter 2 (Approaches) Direction 3: Human Capital. It mandates implementing reskilling and upskilling programs mentorship for AI instructors and allocating 15% of Tech Orda vouchers for AI training (Direction 3 item 1). It also provides for integration of AI modules into higher and vocational education and creation of regional competence centers to train 25000 specialists (Direction 3 items 1-4). Workforce forecasting is revised to align training with AI-driven labour needs (Direction 3 item 3).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: the Ministry of Digital Development Innovation and Aerospace Industry (MDDIAI) Sectoral Council on AI Development and the Committee for AI and Innovation Development within the Ministry of Digital Development Innovation and Aerospace Industry

fr1_title: Dossier on the Draft Law of the Republic of Kazakhstan “On Artificial Intelligence” (September 2025)
fr1_justif: The Draft Law “On Artificial Intelligence” includes detailed provisions to ensure the safety and security of AI systems.  Article 10 requires AI operators to implement protective measures against unauthorized access system misuse and data breaches. Article 17(3) prohibits the use of AI systems that can lead to mass violation of human rights such as covert surveillance emotion detection or social profiling without consent.  Article 18 requires AI system owners to conduct risk assessments document their systems and ensure the reliability of high-risk AI applications. The law mandates immediate suspension of AI systems if they pose foreseeable harm and outlines criteria for classifying systems as critical infrastructure.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Dossier on the Draft Law of the Republic of Kazakhstan “On Artificial Intelligence” (September 2025)
fr1_justif: The Draft Law embeds “transparency and explainability”  among the foundational principles of state regulation in the AI domain (Article 4). Article 7 mandates that users receive full information on the operational characteristics and limitations of AI systems and grants individuals subject to automated decisions the right to be informed to object and to seek protection. Article 21 requires that  -Users be informed when goods or services are produced using AI systems. Synthetic results (e.g. AI-generated content) must be labeled in machine-readable form. Article 22 adds that legal information must be encoded to support automated recognition.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Kenya National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2025-2030
fr1_justif: The Strategy addresses AI literacy through its pillar on Talent Development [p.76] where it sets key objectives such as; Integrating AI and data science into school curicular at all levels [4.1 P.76] where it lists flagship projects for the implementation of the objective. These include; Development of an AI awareness and foundational skills awareness for schools  Roll out of AI awareness and foundational skills curricula in schools and  Development and implemention of a  Training program for AI trainers program across different [schooling] levels.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Data Protection Act No.24 of 2019 (Kenya)
fr1_justif: Sections 57–66 of Kenya’s Data Protection Act establish enforcement and redress mechanisms. They empower the Data Commissioner to investigate complaints issue enforcement and penalty notices and compel compliance with the Act. Individuals may lodge complaints regarding unlawful or unfair processing including automated processing by AI systems. The Commissioner may order cessation correction or compensation for violations. Offences and sanctions ensure accountability providing individuals with effective remedies for data misuse arising from automated or AI-driven processing.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Section 51(2) of the Act states that the processing of personal data is exempt from the provisions of the Act if it is necessary for national security or public interest.
fr1_body_name: Office of the Data Protection Commissioner (ODPC)

fr1_title: Kenya National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2025-2030
fr1_justif: The Strategy explicitly highlights the use of Natural Language Processing (NLP) as a key tool in promoting Kenya's rich linguistic diversity with research aimed at developing chatbots that can interact with various local languages [Pg.18]. The Strategy identifies development of local language models as an opportunity which can democratise access to AI making it more inclusive and relevant to the Kenyan population while preserving linguistic diversity [Pg.8: Investments in the Local AI Ecosystem]. The Strategy identifies cultural preservation as one of its guiding principles [Pg.63] and states that AI systems will be developed that are enriched with Kenyan cultural values and that preserve and promote the nation’s cultural heritage.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Kenya National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2025-2030
fr1_justif: Kenya's AI Strategy addresses environmental impact in the context of AI. It integrates this through one of its objective (Pillar on AI Digital Infrastructure Objective 1.3 Pg.70) where the Strategy has one of its main objectives as; Increasing the supply and use of green energysources to power AI infrastructure ensuring sustainability and reducing environmental impact. The flagship project under this is;  Review of power supply to digital infrastructure and enhance the ratio of green energy.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Kenya National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2025-2030
fr1_justif: The Strategy (p.63) mandates 'Inclusivity and non-discrimination' as core principles promoting fairness and autonomy in AI design. Under 'Enablers' (p.82) Goal 7 aims to foster ethical AI through mandatory Ethical Impact Assessments for the public sector (Art. 7.1.1) and sector-specific standards that include vulnerable groups (Art. 7.1.3). Crucially Art. 7.1.4 establishes a complaints and redress mechanism via the Ombudsman for AI-related concerns. Additionally the policy requires data labeling and classification standards specifically designed to address bias and ensure inclusivity in the data value chain
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Data Protection Act No.24 of 2019 (Kenya)
fr1_justif: The Data Protection (General) Regulations 2021 adds procedural obligations to the Data Protection Act 2019. This regulation provides clear provisions that enables human review under 14 (3C) 22(1i)  and 36(e). The Regulations 2021 a. The Regulations is an addendum legislation that enable procedural enforcement of the Data Protection Act. Section 35 (1) of the DPA already prohibits subjecting individuals to decisions based solely on automated processing which produces legal effects or significantly affects individuals.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: The regulations notes exceptions in section: 54. (1) For the purposes of section 51(2) (b) of the Act the processing of personal data by a national security organ referred to inArticle 239 (1) of the Constitution in furtherance of their mandateconstitutes a processing for national security.
fr1_body_name: Office of the Data Protection Commissioner (ODPC)

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Kenya National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2025-2030
fr1_justif: The framework speaks on the government updating and developing guidelines for accountable public sector AI procurement and deployment  [7.1.2 pg.81]. Under Pillar 3 on AI R&D and Innovation [3.3.3 p.75] the framework recommends a review of public procurement regulations to ensure that the government prioritises the procurement of locally developed AI products.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Kenya AI Strategy 2025-2030
fr1_justif: The strategy explicitly identifies that “AI skills gaps of government officials could also keep the government from harnessing the full potential of AI and maintaining procured systems” (p. 45). Based on this it is reasonable to assume that the strategy’s aim of developing AI expertise in Kenya (as explicitly noted on p. 55) also extends to government officials since they are explicitly identified as an area requiring improvement within the framework.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Kenya National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2025-2030
fr1_justif: The Strategy addresses reskilling and upskilling intiatives under the Talent Development theme/pillar [Pg.76 section 4.2]. The Section 4.1 has an overarching objective  to develop AI talent to meet emerging demands of the AI ecosystem. These consists of 7 key elements of which 6 are relevant to re-skilling and upskilling:  Design and implement ecosystem-driven quality specialised training programs that nurture local AI/data talent to meet industry needs; Develop and implement an AI training of trainers (AI TOT) program; Incentivize the development of AI talent; Create partnerships for AI talent development and placement; Create a policy for knowledge and skills transfer in project implementation; Map AI talent for relevant needs
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Kenya AI Strategy 2025-2030
fr1_justif: It explicitly addresses the safety and security of AI systems as a core governance objective by including the development of “AI risk and safety frameworks” to ensure that AI systems “operate securely and ethically minimising potential harms” (p. 67). In addition the strategy places strong emphasis on cybersecurity infrastructure as part of AI system protection noting the need for “a strong cybersecurity infrastructure to safeguard digital systems AI models and sensitive data from malicious threats” (p. 66) and further operationalises this through measures such as establishing AI-specific threat detection and response systems and a national cybersecurity operations framework (pp. 70–71).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Concept: Digital Transformation of the Kyrgyz Republic for 2024-2028
fr1_justif: The Concept promotes AI literacy primarily through education and capacity-building initiatives. Chapter 2 §5 “Artificial Intelligence” (p.7) outlines measures to introduce AI training programs in higher education institutions covering AI machine learning neural networks and big data. In particular training of the following specialties: Data Scientist Data Engineer AI Architect DataArchitect AI Project Manager. The document also establishes a National Council for AI Development tasked with popularising AI and involving schoolchildren and students in the process of AI learning and teaching AI technologies (p.7). The Action Plan (Task No.12) operationalises these commitments through concrete measures related to AI education.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Digital Code of the Kyrgyz Republic 2025
fr1_justif: The Digital Code of the Kyrgyz Republic (2024) ensures Access to Redress and Remedy in AI through several provisions. Article 84 grants individuals the right to object to automated decision-making and to appeal such processing to a regulator or court. Articles 67 119 162 and 170 establish complaint and dispute mechanisms for digital services while Article 90 designates the State Agency for Personal Data Protection as the complaint-handling authority. Articles 194–196 outline developer and operator liability for AI-related harm.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Ministry of Digital Development and Innovative Technologies

fr1_title: Digital Code of the Kyrgyz Republic 2025
fr1_justif: The Digital Code addresses children’s rights in the digital environment including AI systems through Article 81 “Peculiarities of processing children’s personal data.” (p.56). It establishes safeguards for lawful processing of children’s data requiring that it be in the child’s interest and subject to protection of fundamental rights and freedoms. Processing is only permitted under specific legal grounds or with consent of a legal representative while children aged 14+ may give consent themselves. It also mandates child-accessible language for consent requests and obliges controllers to prevent unlawful processing.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Ministry of Digital Development and Innovative Technologies

fr1_title: Program Development of the state language and improvement of language policy in the Kyrgyz Republic for 2021–2025
fr1_justif: The Programme addresses cultural and linguistic diversity in connection with AI by promoting the digitalization of the Kyrgyz language through AI-based tools. On p.4 it links language dissemination to the “Digital Kyrgyzstan 2019–2023” strategy calling for automated systems and AI-powered applications. Chapter 5. Digitalization of the State Language (p.29 sections 8.3–8.4) further details the development of AI translation products to and from Kyrgyz and the creation of infrastructure for their dissemination supporting the preservation and wider use of the state language in digital spaces.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.
fr1_body_name: The National Commission on the State Language under the President of the Kyrgyz Republic

fr1_title: Digital Code of the Kyrgyz Republic 2025
fr1_justif: The Digital Code addresses Environmental Impact in the context of AI by identifying the environment as a protected good (“?????????? ?????”) potentially affected by AI systems in Article 1(41). Then Article 192 explicitly includes the environment among protected goods requiring AI developers and users to minimize risks of environmental harm and ensure accountability. Article 193 mandates risk assessment for all AI systems including impacts on the environment. Article 171 also ties digital systems regulation to preventing harm to the environment.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Ministry of Digital Development and Innovative Technologies

fr1_title: Digital Code of the Kyrgyz Republic 2025
fr1_justif: The Digital Code establish foundational fairness and non-discrimination principles that apply across all digital technologies including AI systems. Article 3 establishes fairness and proportionality principles and prioritizes individual interests. Article 41 guarantees non-discrimination principle in digital identification ensuring equal rights regardless of identification status. Article 113 prohibits discrimination in digital services based on gender race language disability ethnicity religion or other status. Article 176 promotes equal competition among telecom operators.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Ministry of Digital Development and Innovative Technologies

fr1_title: National Strategy of the Kyrgyz Republic to achieve Gender Equality by 2030
fr1_justif: The National Gender Equality Strategy 2030 of Kyrgyzstan explicitly links gender equality with AI (section 5.7. pp. 40-41) which mandates training-of-trainers on new academic standards and subjects including artificial intelligence cybersecurity and neural networks alongside the development of training modules and video lessons. This aims to strengthen women’s participation in STEM and reduce gender gaps in emerging technologies.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Ministry of Labor Social Security and Migration of the Kyrgyz Republic

fr1_title: Digital Code of the Kyrgyz Republic 2025
fr1_justif: The Digital Code addresses Human Oversight and Determination through explicit provisions: Article 57(2) grants the right to human oversight of automated decisions; Article 122(1) mandates that health-related decisions be made only by humans. And Chapter 23: Artificial Intelligence Systems: Article 191(4) introduces the principle of effective human control requiring human oversight in AI use; Article 192(3) assigns responsibility to AI owners for preventing harm; Article 196(1)(3) obliges users of high-risk AI to ensure effective control of system operation.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Ministry of Digital Development and Innovative Technologies

fr1_title: Digital Code of the Kyrgyz Republic 2025
fr1_justif: The Digital Code addresses AI-related Impact Assessments through two mechanisms. Article 82 establishes requirements for impact assessments on personal data protection prior to automated decision-making or large-scale data processing including risk management and stakeholder consultation. Article 193 introduces a hazard assessment for AI systems mandating evaluation of potential risks to protected interests at design deployment and modification stages aligned with global practices.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Ministry of Digital Development and Innovative Technologies

fr1_title: Concept: Digital Transformation of the Kyrgyz Republic for 2024-2028
fr1_justif: The Concept addresses Public Sector Skills Development in: §1 Reform of public administration §5 Artificial Intelligence and §10 Digitalization of the judiciary. It mandates digital and AI-related training including AI competence centers specialist education (AI Architect Data Scientist etc.) and public servant upskilling. The text also mentions training programs for civil servants in digital skills data management analytics and cybersecurity; introduces digital competence certification (p.4) and integrates digital skills into daily workflows. §10 adds continuous courses for judges and court staff on digital tools and AI-based systems. §5 emphasises AI research education and training programs for officials and institutions.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Concept: Digital Transformation of the Kyrgyz Republic for 2024-2028
fr1_justif: The Concept addresses reskilling and upskilling in the context of AI through multiple sectoral initiatives. It emphasizes training medical staff in digital skills including the use of AI in healthcare and continuing professional development (§ 1. Digital health). It also calls for training and professional development in digital and AI tools for the labor market and AI competence centers and AI research support under the National AI Platform (§ 5. Artificial Intelligence). It further promotes digital education and AI training for specialists in defense and construction (§ 12. Digital development of the armed forces) and train civil servants in digital skills data management cybersecurity (p.4).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Digital Code of the Kyrgyz Republic 2025
fr1_justif: The Digital Code’s Chapter 23 “Artificial Intelligence Systems” (Articles 191–197) establishes AI Safety and Security principles. Article 191 defines key principles: risk reduction openness explainability human control accuracy reliability and safety. Articles 192–196 introduce mandatory risk management danger assessment conformity declaration reliability and data-quality standards and obligations for monitoring control and suspension of unsafe systems. Article 194 adds stricter requirements for high-risk AI systems mandating characteristics that ensure openness explainability controllability accuracy reliability digital resilience and high-quality data.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Ministry of Digital Development and Innovative Technologies

fr1_title: Digital Code of the Kyrgyz Republic 2025
fr1_justif: The Digital Code of the Kyrgyz Republic explicitly embeds Transparency and Explainability principles for AI systems. Article 191(2–3) establishes clarity predictability and openness as core principles and requires availability of technical documentation (“principle of openness”) and clarity of the basis for AI decisions and their re-verification (“principle of explainability”). Article 194(4) mandates that high-risk AI systems ensure openness explainability controllability and reliability. Article 197 requires disclosure when AI generates deepfakes or biometric classification reinforcing transparency to affected individuals.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Ministry of Digital Development and Innovative Technologies

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Bots fake accounts and automated conduct are mechanisms of disinformation campaigns (recital 85) and as such rules are needed to combat them (recital 9). Systemic risk assessments apply to large-scale online platforms and search engines (art. 34) and explicitly include the dissemination or amplification of misleading or fraudulent content and the negative effects on gender-based violence. This includes recommendation systems and other relevant algorithmic systems (art. 34). Mitigation measures include testing and adapting algorithmic and recommendation systems; speeding up the processing of notifications of illegal hate speech and cyber violence; and labeling content generated or manipulated by AI (art.35).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU GDPR
fr1_justif: The law provides a layered redress framework that applies in AI contexts whenever personal data is processed. Data subjects harmed by automated processing based on AI-driven profiling and decision-making hold the right to express their point of view and the right to contest the decision (art. 22); presenting complaints to supervisory authorities (art. 77) resorting to legal measures against gaps in the authorities (art. 78) and bringing proceedings directly against controllers or processors (art. 79). Organizations may be mandated to exercise the rights collectively on behalf of data subjects (art. 80). Supervisory authorities may impose administrative fines because of the violations of core provisions (art. 83).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: This Regulation shall not apply to the processing of personal data by Member States in the context of carrying out activities relating to the Union's common foreign and security policy (recital 16). The processing of personal data by Member States does not apply to activities related to the protection and prevention of threats to public security (Art. 2).
fr1_body_name: The law establishes the European Data Protection Board is comprised of representatives from the supervisory authority of each member states (Arts 68) acting independently (Art 69) providing guidelines recommendations and binding decisions to ensure consistence across Member States (Art 70) The Member State must to establish am independent supervisory authority responsible for monitoring and enforcing the regulation (Art 51)

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Online platform service providers must ensure the privacy security and protection of minors in their services (Art. 28) including prohibiting advertising based on profiling through the automated processing of data about children (Art. 28). Terms and conditions must be understandable to minors (Art. 14) ensuring a high level of privacy and security by default based on good practices (recitals 70 71). Large-scale online platforms and large-scale search engines systematic risk assessment applies periodically evaluating potential negative effects on minors (Art. 34). Mitigation measures are determined: age verification parental control tools abuse reporting tools and support for minors (Art. 35). Voluntary standards apply (Art. 44).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Centre Law (Latvia)
fr1_justif: The Law aims to promote the sustainability of the Latvian language the inclusion of cultural data in artificial intelligence solutions and adaptation to them.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The law focuses on the risk classification system (Art. 6) and the requirements imposed on high-risk AI systems. It requires suppliers of high-risk AI systems to establish implement document and maintain a risk (and damage) management system throughout the system's lifecycle (Art. 9). Training validation and test datasets must conform to data governance in terms of representation error-freeness completeness contextualization and the function the system will be used for handling special personal data for monitoring purposes and detecting and correcting biases. High-risk AI systems must have consistency throughout the lifecycle by requiring appropriate levels of accuracy and robustness and reducing the risk of bias (art.15).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act addresses gender equality through non-discrimination and fundamental rights provisions. Training datasets for high-risk AI must be representative avoinding the discriminatory outcomes and protecting atributes explicitly related gender (Art. 10). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights impact assessments before deploying high-risk AI incluing gender-based discrimination (Art. 27). Equality bodies are granted market surveillance powers enabling them to investigate and sanction AI systems producing discriminatory outcomes (Art. 74).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act establishes requirements to ensure human control over high-risk AI systems requiring providers to design systems that enable natural persons with a minimum level of comprehensibility to monitor outputs detect anomalies and intervene or interrupt operations when necessary (art. 14). Deployers must assign oversight to competent natural persons with authority to act (art. 26). For general-purpose AI with systemic risk adversarial testing and incident reporting reinforce human control (Arts. 55 73). Additional oversight provisions are set in the fundamental rights impact assessment (art. 27) and Annex (IV) 2(e) as part of technical documentation requirements (art. 11).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Law establishes an assessment architecture according to the type and level of risk of the system. High-risk AI suppliers must identify analyze and mitigate risks during the design development and implementation phases within a risk management system (Arti.9) with comprehensive technical documentation (Art.15) and monitoring of operations and anomalies (Art. 26). For AI implementers in the public sector a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment applies before implementing high-risk systems listed in Annex 3 (Art.27). Compliance assessment must include self-assessment or third-party audits depending on the criticality of the system (Art. 43 47). There are ex-post assessments and post-commercialization monitoring (Art.72).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act classifies systems for recruitment promotion termination and worker management as high-risk AI systems (Annex III(4)) (art. 6). Prohibited AI practices include bans on emotion-recognition in the workplace (art. 5). Providers must implement risk-management addressing discrimination (Art. 9) ensure representative datasets and bias-control (art. 10) and maintain technical documentation (Arts. 11). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights assessments before deployment (art. 27). Art. 26 (7-9) establishes safeguards: workers must be informed and may request explanations of AI use. However the Act does not regulate pay conditions or compensation so it mitigates risks without constituting a full labour-rights regime.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Safety and security is core to the Act. It establishes a risk-based framework for risk identification and mitigation (Art. 9) oversight measures (Art. 14) and robustness and accuracy requirements (Art. 15). To ensure ongoing safety the Act mandates technical documentation and record-keeping (Arts. 11–12) and in certain high risk situations a conformity assessment procedure prior to market placement (Art. 43). Post-market obligations in Arts. 72–73 require providers and deployers to monitor performance report serious incidents and take corrective action. For GPAI models posing systemic risks Arts. 55–56 impose additional safety and cybersecurity duties including model evaluation and risk mitigation following the Code of Practice.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Articles 11 12 and 13 establish several transparency and documentation obligations for AI developers and deployers. High-risk AI systems must include clear information on their capabilities limitations and intended purpose (Art. 13) and providers must maintain comprehensive technical documentation and automatic logging to ensure traceability (Arts. 11–12). These measures enable deployers to better understand the systems and authorities to audit or investigate when necessary. There are disclosure obligations that require notification when individuals interact with AI systems particularly chatbots and emotion recognition tools (Art. 50). Also for general-purpose AI in terms of model documentation and summary of training data (Art. 53).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.
