fr1_title: Lebanon's National Education AI Framework: A Guide to Policy and Competencies for Teachers and Students (Version 1.0)
fr1_justif: The framework defines AI literacy as the ability to understand evaluate and use AI responsibly. It introduces AI competencies for students and teachers across cognitive technical and ethical dimensions (Parts 2 and 3 pp. 14-53). It promotes data and algorithm literacy critical thinking and responsible AI use in curricula from primary to tertiary levels with a focus on teacher training and inclusive access to AI education.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Policy and Implementation Plan (Second Draft) (Lesotho)
fr1_justif: The draft creates an AI-skills pipeline via curriculum reform teacher training and public awareness. It proposes embedding AI/digital literacy in basic and higher education upskilling the workforce and running awareness campaigns for citizens and SMEs. It links ethics and safe AI use to learning outcomes and leverages innovation hubs/scholarships to widen access. (Education & Skills and Public Awareness sections pp. 24–27; Ethics & Governance pp. 12–14; Implementation pp. 30–33.)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Policy and Implementation Plan (Second Draft) (Lesotho)
fr1_justif: It introduces a User Rights Framework allowing individuals to request explanations of AI-driven decisions and dispute them if necessary and proposes product liability standards clarifying responsibility when AI systems cause harm (Sec.5.1 5.2 p. 23–26). Additionally public input mechanisms and feedback systems allow stakeholders to raise concerns about AI deployment (pp. 23 5.6.5. Feedback Mechanisms).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Child Online Protection Guidelines (Lesotho)
fr1_justif: The framework addresses children rights in the digital environment which explicitly includes AI automated systems and algorithms (p.3). The guidelines describe children online rights (p. 4) child online obligations (p.5)obligations of mobile and internet service providers (p. 6) obligations of content service providers (p. 7) what children need to know to be safe online (p. 7) and duties and tips for parents (p.8-9).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Policy and Implementation Plan (Second Draft) (Lesotho)
fr1_justif: The plan supports cultural/linguistic diversity mainly through inclusion and accessibility measures: it proposes “Localized Training” to “develop content in local languages” (Implementation Plan—Training 7.2 p.36–37) and targets “underrepresented communities” in “Public Awareness Campaigns” (2.3 p.38). It also sets core ethics values (fairness non-discrimination inclusivity) that help counter exclusion in AI governance (Guidelines p.54; Guiding Principles p.13–14).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Policy and Implementation Plan (Second Draft) (Lesotho)
fr1_justif: The plan ties AI growth to sustainability via infrastructure measures. Under Objective 4 (“Building and providing AI infrastructure”) it requires local data centres to “meet international energy efficiency standards to minimize environmental impact” and references AI in sustainability as an emerging focus area. These are cross-cutting prescriptions rather than sector-specific rules.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Policy and Implementation Plan (Second Draft) (Lesotho)
fr1_justif: The draft embeds fairness non-discrimination and inclusion as core principles (Guiding Principles p.14). It operationalizes these via Ethical Oversight Committees to monitor deployments (Objective 6 p.16) Regular audits and reporting on safety/ethics (p.17) and Bias-mitigation programs to identify and reduce discriminatory outcomes (p.17). It also creates Public Input Mechanisms so affected groups can raise concerns about unfair impacts (p.17). Together these provisions target fairness throughout design deployment and oversight.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Policy and Implementation Plan (Second Draft) (Lesotho)
fr1_justif: The document addresses human oversight through safety and risk-management provisions. Under AI Safety Protocols (Sec.2.1-2.2 pp. 56–57) it mandates risk assessment and mitigation protocols for high-risk AI systems including scenario testing stress testing and fail-safe mechanisms before deployment. It also requires human-in-the-loop safeguards explainability features and real-time monitoring allowing human intervention when systems fail or produce harmful outcomes (Sec. 3.1-3.2 p. 57). Additionally the Compendium of International Best Practices (p. 69) emphasizes transparency and explainability in AI decision-making to ensure accountability.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Policy and Implementation Plan (Second Draft) (Lesotho)
fr1_justif: Impact/risk assessment is operationalised through mandatory risk assessment + documentation before deployment and ongoing audits. In 6.2 “AI Safety Protocols” (p.57) the plan sets risk assessment standards (bias data security reliability) and requires scenario/stress testing + fail-safes for high-risk AI. It also mandates human-in-the-loop explainability and real-time monitoring (p.57). 6.2 “Regular Audits and Reporting” (pp.58–59) adds periodic audits transparent reporting and regulator oversight.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Policy and Implementation Plan (Second Draft) (Lesotho)
fr1_justif: The Plan names “Building AI capacity … across the public sector and society” as a key focus with training programs education support and awareness campaigns (p.7). It further outlines workforce upskilling/reskilling via technical and vocational programs modular/role-specific courses and core topics including ethics data interpretation and working with AI systems (pp.33–35) plus feedback and assessment to monitor impact.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Policy and Implementation Plan (Second Draft) (Lesotho)
fr1_justif: The document promotes AI-related reskilling and upskilling initiatives to help the workforce adapt to AI-driven economic changes. It proposes technical training and vocational programs that teach workers how to use AI tools develop data analysis and machine learning skills and transition into new AI-related roles (pp. 33–34 “Technical Training Programs”). The policy also includes skill gap assessments and role-specific training modules to address industry needs. Additionally it sets targets for participants in reskilling and upskilling programs (see table point 7 p. 64) and recommends strategies to mitigate automation impacts through workforce training and social protections (p. 70 Sec. 8).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Policy and Implementation Plan (Second Draft) (Lesotho)
fr1_justif: Under Guiding Principles – Safety and Reliability it states that AI systems should be safe reliable and operate as intended and that risks and unintended consequences should be prevented (p. 13 Sec. 5.4 1.1–1.2). It proposes security and misuse prevention measures including preventing AI use in terrorism cyberattacks phishing and harmful surveillance and establishing mechanisms to identify and mitigate AI-related threats (p. 14 Sec. 6.1–6.2). The policy also mandates safety protocols human-in-the-loop safeguards monitoring audits and regulatory oversight to ensure safe deployment and compliance (pp. 57–59).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Policy and Implementation Plan (Second Draft) (Lesotho)
fr1_justif: The draft embeds transparency/explainability as core principles (e.g. clear purpose traceability) in the Guiding Principles (pp. 11–12). It requires organizations to document system purpose data sources and decision logic keep audit trails undergo regular/independent audits and publish monitoring reports (Audit/Monitoring & Reporting Standards pp. 18–20). It also creates Feedback & Public Input Mechanisms so people can query/contest AI decisions and receive meaningful explanations (pp. 33–36). High-risk uses face enhanced disclosure and oversight (pp. 19–20).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence National Policy (Libya)
fr1_justif: The National AI Policy mentions the need to adjust curricula to adapt to the pace of new technology development 'adapt education in Libya to ensure that young students acquire modern science technology engineering and mathematics skills by developing a long-term action plan to modernise and develop primary and secondary education' and 'building the capacities of teachers in the context of AI' (p.12).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Charter for the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence (Libya)
fr1_justif: The charter assigns accountability to developers and deployers for ensuring transparency oversight and responsiveness to user harm. It states that 'Responsibility for the ethical implications of the use of artificial intelligence technologies lies with all those involved in the development and dissemination of these technologies and the legislative bodies must establish laws and mechanisms that guarantee the right of individuals and society to sue to hold accountable and to challenge the decisions of artificial intelligence.Compensation laws must be clear when damage is caused by AI decisions including liability for bias violation of privacy or negativeimpacts on employment' (p.7).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Charter for the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence (Libya)
fr1_justif: Equity and Fairness Section in the National Charter on page 7 (Arabic Version) of the document highlights the connection between AI and Child protection non-discrimination and accountability. 'Applications must limit discrimination based on race gender age disability wealth or political opinion.' (p. 6 in the translated document). In addition: 'The use of artificial intelligence systems by children minors with special needs or limited technical capabilities must It also states that the use of artificial intelligence systems by children minors persons with special needs or those with limited technical abilities must be carefully considered with the necessary measures put in place to protect them and ensure that they are not harmed.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Charter for the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence (Libya)
fr1_justif: Principle 11 of the national charter specifically focuses on environmental sustainability. The principle aims to 'Ensure that artificial intelligence technologies do not unnecessarily drain energy resources while increasing their effectiveness and efficiency to reduce their carbon impact on the environment thus contributing to meeting the challenges of climate change' (p.9) Principle 8 on page 14  translated document) also highlights more broadly its objective to ensure that AI systems achieve tangible and sustainable benefits for individuals society and the environment throughout their entire lifecycle.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Charter for the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence (Libya)
fr1_justif: The Libyan National Charter for the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence (2025) explicitly identifies fairness and non-discrimination as one of its twelve ethical benchmarks set out in the section titled “Ethical Principles of Artificial Intelligence” on page 6 of the document. The detailed articulation of this benchmark then follows on page 7 under the subsection Fairness and Equity which states that AI systems must be inclusive avoid sustaining or deepening social injustice and must “limit discrimination or bias based on race color sex language age disability political or non-political opinion wealth or any other status'.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence National Policy (Libya)
fr1_justif: In the section on principles underpinning the national AI policy (p.6) one of the key principles highlighted is Equality and Justice: Full respect for the rule of law and alignment with human values in ensuring fairness equality and justice in how artificial intelligence systems treat all individuals regardless of race gender class or culture. Unjustified bias and preferential treatment must be avoided. In section 5 of the policy on the design and use of AI systems one of the objectives cited is ensuring that there is no discrimination or bias in favor of a particular group within the algorithms of artificial intelligence systems (p.13)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: National Council/Authority (exact name to be determined) in accordance with decision issued by the Council of Ministers

fr1_title: National Charter for the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence (Libya)
fr1_justif: The principle aims to determine the extent of human intervention required within decision-making processes or operations in AI applications Regulations should be applied that allow for human intervention or automatic shutdown of the system in case of malfunction especially when such failure affects human safety. The Charter encourages the creation of controlled testing environments to enhance responsible oversight in the field of research and innovation. 'Those responsible for the different stages of the life cycle of an AI system must be identified and accountable for the results of these systems while enabling human oversight' (p.7)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence National Policy (Libya)
fr1_justif: On page 12 the policy calls for “training and qualifying local expertise” and “providing mid-term training programs” to build institutional capacity within administrative bodies explicitly linking AI adoption to skills development in government institutions. Further on page 12 the policy recommends “periodic awareness programs for employees of the public sector ” and “partnerships between public and private institutions to disseminate AI knowledge and skills” confirming a structured objective to equip public servants with foundational AI competencies rather than imposing binding training requirements.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_justif: On page 12 the policy also commits to “preventing brain drain ensuring appropriate employment opportunities and providing mid-term training for local experts while attracting foreign expertise”. Finally page 14 expands these aims by requiring “periodic awareness programs for public sector employees and partnerships between public and private institutions to spread AI knowledge and skills” and instructs “the Ministry of Education to adapt schooling so young students acquire modern science technology engineering and mathematics skills.”

fr1_title: National Charter for the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence (Libya)
fr1_justif: The principle aims to 'prevent potential risks and ensure safety during the development and use of artificial intelligence technologies.”  (page 8) The principle also stipulates that the collection use management and any handling of data used in AI technologies must comply with the applicable laws and regulations in Libya. It is necessary to ensure the quality of the data that feeds AI technologies in terms of accuracy and soundness and such data should be subject to technical auditing and review when needed. Data governance and the protection of personal data must be guaranteed and any individual or entity that violates data privacy shall be held accountable.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Charter for the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence (Libya)
fr1_justif: The principle defines transparency as ensuring that AI decision-making processes are clear and understandable to users and stakeholders. It requires responsible and timely disclosure clear justifications for AI system results and providing information that helps individuals understand outcomes including key factors used in decisions.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Lithuanian Action Plan for the Development of Artificial Intelligence Technologies 2023-2026
fr1_justif: Measures relating to financial instruments focusing on funding participation in AI summer camps/courses/internships for university professors researchers and AI specialists working in companies (p. 22). Specifically identify high-quality summer camps/courses/internships/conferences for professional development in Lithuania or abroad and invite university professors researchers and business specialists to submit funding applications. Participation is funded up to 100% for universities and researchers (p. 22).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Bots fake accounts and automated conduct are mechanisms of disinformation campaigns (recital 85) and as such rules are needed to combat them (recital 9). Systemic risk assessments apply to large-scale online platforms and search engines (art. 34) and explicitly include the dissemination or amplification of misleading or fraudulent content and the negative effects on gender-based violence. This includes recommendation systems and other relevant algorithmic systems (art. 34). Mitigation measures include testing and adapting algorithmic and recommendation systems; speeding up the processing of notifications of illegal hate speech and cyber violence; and labeling content generated or manipulated by AI (art.35).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU GDPR
fr1_justif: The law provides a layered redress framework that applies in AI contexts whenever personal data is processed. Data subjects harmed by automated processing based on AI-driven profiling and decision-making hold the right to express their point of view and the right to contest the decision (art. 22); presenting complaints to supervisory authorities (art. 77) resorting to legal measures against gaps in the authorities (art. 78) and bringing proceedings directly against controllers or processors (art. 79). Organizations may be mandated to exercise the rights collectively on behalf of data subjects (art. 80). Supervisory authorities may impose administrative fines because of the violations of core provisions (art. 83).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: This Regulation shall not apply to the processing of personal data by Member States in the context of carrying out activities relating to the Union's common foreign and security policy (recital 16). The processing of personal data by Member States does not apply to activities related to the protection and prevention of threats to public security (Art. 2).
fr1_body_name: The law establishes the European Data Protection Board is comprised of representatives from the supervisory authority of each member states (Arts 68) acting independently (Art 69) providing guidelines recommendations and binding decisions to ensure consistence across Member States (Art 70) The Member State must to establish am independent supervisory authority responsible for monitoring and enforcing the regulation (Art 51)

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Online platform service providers must ensure the privacy security and protection of minors in their services (Art. 28) including prohibiting advertising based on profiling through the automated processing of data about children (Art. 28). Terms and conditions must be understandable to minors (Art. 14) ensuring a high level of privacy and security by default based on good practices (recitals 70 71). Large-scale online platforms and large-scale search engines systematic risk assessment applies periodically evaluating potential negative effects on minors (Art. 34). Mitigation measures are determined: age verification parental control tools abuse reporting tools and support for minors (Art. 35). Voluntary standards apply (Art. 44).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Resolution on the Approval of the Guidelines for the Development of the Lithuanian Language in the Digital Media and Advancement of Language Technologies for the Years 2021-2027
fr1_justif: Guidelines for the development of the Lithuanian language in the digital media and for the advancement of language technologies in 2021-2027 aimed at ensuring the full use of the Lithuanian language in the digital media consolidating and maintaining the status of the Lithuanian language in the information society promoting the development of technologies adapted to the Lithuanian language and improving the quality of services based thereon (art.2 1). The purpose is to ensure the full functioning of the Lithuanian language in the digital environment to promote the development of technologies adapted to the Lithuanian language and to improve the quality of services to the public by them (p.24).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The law focuses on the risk classification system (Art. 6) and the requirements imposed on high-risk AI systems. It requires suppliers of high-risk AI systems to establish implement document and maintain a risk (and damage) management system throughout the system's lifecycle (Art. 9). Training validation and test datasets must conform to data governance in terms of representation error-freeness completeness contextualization and the function the system will be used for handling special personal data for monitoring purposes and detecting and correcting biases. High-risk AI systems must have consistency throughout the lifecycle by requiring appropriate levels of accuracy and robustness and reducing the risk of bias (art.15).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act addresses gender equality through non-discrimination and fundamental rights provisions. Training datasets for high-risk AI must be representative avoinding the discriminatory outcomes and protecting atributes explicitly related gender (Art. 10). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights impact assessments before deploying high-risk AI incluing gender-based discrimination (Art. 27). Equality bodies are granted market surveillance powers enabling them to investigate and sanction AI systems producing discriminatory outcomes (Art. 74).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act establishes requirements to ensure human control over high-risk AI systems requiring providers to design systems that enable natural persons with a minimum level of comprehensibility to monitor outputs detect anomalies and intervene or interrupt operations when necessary (art. 14). Deployers must assign oversight to competent natural persons with authority to act (art. 26). For general-purpose AI with systemic risk adversarial testing and incident reporting reinforce human control (Arts. 55 73). Additional oversight provisions are set in the fundamental rights impact assessment (art. 27) and Annex (IV) 2(e) as part of technical documentation requirements (art. 11).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Law establishes an assessment architecture according to the type and level of risk of the system. High-risk AI suppliers must identify analyze and mitigate risks during the design development and implementation phases within a risk management system (Arti.9) with comprehensive technical documentation (Art.15) and monitoring of operations and anomalies (Art. 26). For AI implementers in the public sector a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment applies before implementing high-risk systems listed in Annex 3 (Art.27). Compliance assessment must include self-assessment or third-party audits depending on the criticality of the system (Art. 43 47). There are ex-post assessments and post-commercialization monitoring (Art.72).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act classifies systems for recruitment promotion termination and worker management as high-risk AI systems (Annex III(4)) (art. 6). Prohibited AI practices include bans on emotion-recognition in the workplace (art. 5). Providers must implement risk-management addressing discrimination (Art. 9) ensure representative datasets and bias-control (art. 10) and maintain technical documentation (Arts. 11). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights assessments before deployment (art. 27). Art. 26 (7-9) establishes safeguards: workers must be informed and may request explanations of AI use. However the Act does not regulate pay conditions or compensation so it mitigates risks without constituting a full labour-rights regime.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: Lithuanian Action Plan for the Development of Artificial Intelligence Technologies 2023-2026
fr1_justif: Companies and institutions face a shortage of AI specialists training or the necessary expertise (p. 20). Concrete activities and specific targets are planned. Learning program for young professionals to acquire the knowledge and practical skills needed to work with AI including participation in distance learning courses intensive courses to prepare for the real implementation of AI projects and project implementation in companies/institutions (p. 19- 20). Funding for AI summer camps/courses/internships is also aimed at AI specialists working in companies (p. 12). Program participants will also benefit from mentoring networking and practical experience. Participants remain working in AI or R&D (p. 2).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Safety and security is core to the Act. It establishes a risk-based framework for risk identification and mitigation (Art. 9) oversight measures (Art. 14) and robustness and accuracy requirements (Art. 15). To ensure ongoing safety the Act mandates technical documentation and record-keeping (Arts. 11–12) and in certain high risk situations a conformity assessment procedure prior to market placement (Art. 43). Post-market obligations in Arts. 72–73 require providers and deployers to monitor performance report serious incidents and take corrective action. For GPAI models posing systemic risks Arts. 55–56 impose additional safety and cybersecurity duties including model evaluation and risk mitigation following the Code of Practice.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Articles 11 12 and 13 establish several transparency and documentation obligations for AI developers and deployers. High-risk AI systems must include clear information on their capabilities limitations and intended purpose (Art. 13) and providers must maintain comprehensive technical documentation and automatic logging to ensure traceability (Arts. 11–12). These measures enable deployers to better understand the systems and authorities to audit or investigate when necessary. There are disclosure obligations that require notification when individuals interact with AI systems particularly chatbots and emotion recognition tools (Art. 50). Also for general-purpose AI in terms of model documentation and summary of training data (Art. 53).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: Data Protection Act No.3 of 2024 (Malawi)
fr1_justif: Section 25 (p.15): Grants data subjects the 'right not to be subject to a decision based solely on automated processing of personal data including profiling which produces a legal or similarly significant effect.' Exceptions exist for contractual necessity legal authorization with safeguards or explicit consent - but controllers must 'implement appropriate measures to safeguard rights and interests.'Section 30(2)(a) (p.17): Mandates Data Protection Impact Assessments when 'personal data will be processed using an automated processing system including profiling' ensuring prior human review of high-risk AI deployments.Section 15(1)(e) (p.11): Requires controllers to inform subjects about 'the existence of automated decision-making
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Data Protection Act No.3 of 2024 (Malawi)
fr1_justif: The Data Protection Act 2024 requires impact assessments for personal data processing likely to pose high risk to rights and freedoms including automated processing and profiling (Section 30 pp. 44–46). Assessments are mandatory for AI-related large-scale or sensitive processing with reports submitted to MACRA before processing. Section 25 also addresses automated decision-making (pp. 41–43).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Guidelines for the Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence (GAI) Technology in Teaching and Learning (T&L) in Higher Education
fr1_justif: The document addresses AI Literacy by formally guiding students and educators on the understanding responsible use and critical application of Generative Artificial Intelligence in teaching and learning including expectations on ethical use academic integrity and appropriate integration into curriculum and assessment practices. It embeds AI literacy within formal higher education processes covering governance instructional design assessment and professional development for educators thereby ensuring learners acquire competencies to use AI tools knowledgeably and responsibly. (Introduction and Scope p 4–5 7–9; Governance and Academic Integrity p20–24; Professional Development p24–26)
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: National Guidelines on AI Governance and Ethics (AIGE) (Malaysia)
fr1_justif: The guidelines establish Environmental Impact as one of the three pillars of sustainability that AI policy-making must balance requiring that policies assess the energy consumption and carbon footprint associated with AI hardware infrastructure data centers and computing resources (p70). To mitigate these risks the framework encourages Sustainable AI policies to promote the development and adoption of energy-efficient algorithms hardware and practices (p70).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Guidelines on AI Governance and Ethics (AIGE) (Malaysia)
fr1_justif: As a foundational principle fairness in the AIGE mandates that AI systems must be designed to avoid discrimination and bias against target users across diverse demographics requiring the use of unbiased and representative data and the implementation of regular algorithmic auditing procedures to ensure equitable treatment and outcomes (page 107).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Malaysia National Artificial Intelligence Roadmap 2021 - 2025 (AI-RMAP)
fr1_justif: Based on the document Malaysia National AI Roadmap 2021-2025 on the page 59 at the Section S4.3 - Attracting & Retaining AI Talents this document addresses Gender Equality in AI through two main areas: 1. Promotion of women's participation in AI programmes; where it establishes Women-in-AI (WAI) initiatives such as WAICamp WAI-2-Go and WAIDatathon (Horizon 1 2021-2022) directly targeting female engagement and leadership in AI development. 2. Institutional support and recognition: under Horizon 2 (2023-2024) the Women-in-AI Awards are introduced to strengthen visibility mentorship and recognition for women AI professionals reflecting a commitment to equitable representation.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Public Consultation Paper No. 3/2025: Automated Decision Making and Profiling Guideline (ADMP Guideline) (Malaysia)
fr1_justif: The document addresses the issue by proposing mandatory ADM Restrictions when AI is used to make decisions that legally or significantly affect a data subject (p. 17 Para 2.24; p. 7 Para 2.9). The proposed restrictions formalize human determination rights including the Right to Refuse a solely automated decision and the Right to Request a Human Review (p. 9 Para 2.13). To ensure meaningful oversight the Right to Human Review requires the reviewer to have the ‘authority and competence’ to go against the automated recommendation (p. 18 Para 2.25(f); p. 11 Para 2.13(c)(i)).  The Right to Information mandates transparency by requiring data controllers to provide meaningful information about the logic involved (p. 10 Para 2.13(b) 34).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Defense and security are explicitly excluded from the application of the core regulatory measures of the framework as the ADM Restrictions (Right to Refuse Right to Information and Right to Human Review) will not apply to personal data processed for the prevention or detection of crime investigations or the apprehension or prosecution of offenders (p. 14 Para 2.19(b)(i) (ii)).

fr1_title: National Guidelines on AI Governance and Ethics (AIGE) (Malaysia)
fr1_justif: 1. The document encourages developers and end-users to carry out risk and impact assessments to identify evaluate and address potential issues associated with AI use analyzing the potential impact on individuals society and the environment (Page 87). 2. For policymakers Impact Assessment and Risk Analysis are crucial steps within the 'Performance Measures & Evaluation' block of the AI Governance and Ethics (AIGE) Framework used to determine the risk level for each AI system (Page 64 Page 62). 3. The Responsible AI Impact Assessment (“RAIIA”) Tool is provided as a specific assessment aid to help the industry evaluate risks and develop mitigation strategies for implementing AI systems responsibly (Page 86 Page 123).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Guidelines on AI Governance and Ethics (AIGE) (Malaysia)
fr1_justif: The document addresses labour protections in the context of AI through the principles of Fairness and Accountability requiring AI systems used in employment contexts (e.g. recruitment promotion monitoring) to prevent discrimination ensure transparency to employees respect privacy and remain auditable (pp. 25–28 67–69 133).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Guidelines for the Adoption of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in the Public Sector (Malaysia)
fr1_justif: The document address public procurement of AI by requiring responsible AI principles to be applied across the AI value chain. These principles are Data privacy and security transparency accountability fairness inclusiveness reliability and robustness and sustainability (p.44). Developers are required to conduct comprehensive testing at various stages of the development phase before launching the AI system to ensure it delivers robust and consistent results in a variety of situations and environments (p. 59).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Roadmap 2021-2025
fr1_justif: The document explicitly provides AI-specific upskilling initiatives for public sector actors including “AI awareness programs for government officials” targeting Federal and State officials as well as senior middle and junior public servants with a concrete target of training 3600 officials (p. 61) and further expanding these into “Accelerated AI programs” designed to enhance practical understanding and drive AI adoption across ministries through mechanisms such as AI performance ratings (p. 64) thereby clearly going beyond general digital literacy and constituting a structured public sector AI skills development effort aligned with the indicator.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: It notes that 'While other countries strategically focus on specific areas of AI such as national security defence cybersecurity energy and healthcare to drive their national agenda at least in the public arena it is therefore proposed that Malaysia places a strategic focus on AI in Supply Chain Management (SCM).' Based on this it can reasonably be said that the framework does indeed not try to regulate defence related issues.

fr1_title: Malaysia National Artificial Intelligence Roadmap 2021 - 2025 (AI-RMAP)
fr1_justif: Strategic Initiative 4.2 highlights the necessity of reskilling and upskilling the existing workforce to mitigate job disruption or displacement caused by AI stressing that this effort must be strategic and include both workers with no prior AI exposure and those who already possess AI-related skills. To operationalise this the document proposes the development of a dedicated AI Reskilling and Upskilling System (AI-RUS) online platform which will initially focus on sectors with the highest likelihood of AI disruption and subsequently expand to all sectors supported by features such as asynchronous reskilling and upskilling modules and job-matching mechanisms for reskilled AI talents (p55).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Guidelines on AI Governance and Ethics (AIGE) (Malaysia)
fr1_justif: The document addresses Safety and Security by requiring AI systems to be reliable robust and secure with risks identified and mitigated throughout the AI lifecycle including design development testing and deployment (p. 21 25). It emphasises risk management safeguards against misuse system reliability and protection against security vulnerabilities supported by monitoring and human oversight to prevent harm from AI system failures or malicious use. (p.39)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Guidelines for the Adoption of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in the Public Sector (Malaysia)
fr1_drive: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1Kgy4qs_xAItgpMsdt1tub_08IgDWDSe7/view?usp=sharing    The launch document link to check the date: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1ooSQVm6MunwT7XzLQ792U0kFm7lqZ5YZ/view?usp=drive_link
fr1_justif: The Guidelines for the Adoption of Artificial Intelligence in the Public Sector explicitly addressed in the dedicated Transparency section (pp. 49-51) which requires agencies to ensure AI systems are and the data is understandable traceable and properly documented including disclosure of system purpose data usage and decision logic with human oversight to ensure AI-assisted decisions in the public sector remain reviewable and accountable.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Defense and security are explicitly excluded from the application of the core regulatory measures of the framework as the ADM Restrictions (Right to Refuse Right to Information and Right to Human Review) will not apply to personal data processed for the prevention or detection of crime investigations or the apprehension or prosecution of offenders (p. 14 Para 2.19(b)(i) (ii)).
fr2_title: Public Consultation Paper No. 3/2025: Automated Decision Making and Profiling Guideline (ADMP Guideline) (Malaysia)
fr2_justif: The Automated Decision Making and Profiling Guideline (ADMP Guideline) which is proposed to be enforced under the Personal Data Protection Act 2010 (Act 709) mandates Transparency and Explainability (T&E) by establishing specific rights for individuals when a decision based solely on automated means legally or significantly affects them. This approach requires data controllers to provide the Right to Information (meaningful disclosure of the logic involved data used and likely impact) and the Right to Human Review which must be conducted by someone with the authority and capability to change the decision to ensure meaningful scrutiny of algorithmic outcomes (page 10 11).
fr2_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Guidelines on the use of AI in Higher Education (Mauritius)
fr1_justif: This is a formal framework for building AI literacy within tertiary institutions. On page 19 there is a recommendation to develop critical AI Literacy and competency where the main aim is to implement continuous and regular AI literacy training for stakeholders involved in higher education. On page 16 there is also a recommendation for Mauritian institutions to promote diversity and inclusion in AI literacy research and faculty development to bridge digital and knowledge divides.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Responsible use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in financial services (Fintech Series Guidance Notes 4)
fr1_justif: The guideline recognizes that bias in data not properly taken care of might result into AI decision making which might discriminate certain groups. It states that AI systems that treat consumers unfairly or violate fundamental rights including the right to non-discrimination should not be brought to market. To prevent this it requires a “fairness by design” approach embedded within AI governance and risk management systems supported by organisational culture staff training continuous monitoring auditing and transparency to detect and mitigate bias. Section 6.0;Page 10
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Responsible use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in financial services (Fintech Series Guidance Notes 4)
fr1_justif: The guideline specifically calls for governance through human oversight in section 5.0 in pages 8 and 9. It entails that existing human supervisory expectations should still be maintained though the development and use of AI systems. It also states that insurers that rely heavily on AI in areas that significantly influence consumer outcomes it is crucial to have sufficient expertise at the Board level to ensure that AI solutions are consistently effective and protective against consumer harm.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Mauritius Artificial Intelligence Strategy (November 2018)
fr1_justif: The strategy explicitly promotes workforce reskilling and upskilling in response to AI driven transformation including recognizing the need for workers to “adjust” to new AI tools and emphasizing education training and talent development to prepare the workforce for AI adoption (pp. 16–17); the framework further identifies “skill attraction/capacity building” as a core strategic focus area and highlights that “training of domestic talent is of paramount importance” (Executive Summary p. 3; p. 17).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Responsible use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in financial services (Fintech Series Guidance Notes 4)
fr1_justif: This regulator guidance explicitly defines and discusses “Explainable AI” and links it to trust transparency and accountability in high-stakes financial systems. It places expectations on licensees for documentation explainability and human oversight(Page 22). Furthermore on page 11 Transparency and explainability are being precisely referenced in terms of being able to meaningfully explain how decisions are made to be able to identify any potential biases in the process
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Guidelines and Principles for the Strategic Development and Regulated Use of Artificial Intelligence at the National Electoral Institute (Mexico)
fr1_justif: These guidelines establish a reference framework for the development adoption validation and responsible use of AI systems at the National Electoral Institute ensuring technical feasibility and alignment with institutional principles. They include specific provisions on authorization mechanisms risk supervision data protection transparency non-discrimination equality and freedom of expression. AI use within the Institute is strictly auxiliary subject to human oversight and may not replace institutional decision-making particularly in electoral functions or the protection of political-electoral rights (Acuerdo INE/JGE175/2025). fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.
fr2_title: Initiative with Draft Decree by Which the Law is Issued (Federal for the Ethical Sovereign and Inclusive Development of the Artificial Intelligence) (Mexico)
fr2_justif: The law addresses AI-facilitated misinformation and violence through explicit prohibitions and duties. It bans AI systems that generate simulated audiovisual or textual content for fraud disinformation or digital violence unless they include authentication and visible labeling mechanisms (Art. 38.V). General duties prohibit AI from inducing harmful violent or manipulative outcomes (Art. 35.V). For children specifically AI systems that promote violence hatred discrimination or disseminate false distorted or decontextualized information are prohibited including synthetic sexually exploitative material (Art. 63 Ch. XIV)
fr2_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Initiative with Draft Decree by Which the Law is Issued (Federal for the Ethical Sovereign and Inclusive Development of the Artificial Intelligence) (Mexico)
fr1_justif: The Federal Bill for the Ethical Sovereign and Inclusive Development of AI (April 2025) establishes a comprehensive redress framework. Art. 5.XVII defines Algorithmic Harm Redress as a legal administrative or technical mechanism for affected persons to obtain restitution compensation or corrective measures against unfair or discriminatory automated decisions. Art. 56 grants affected persons the right to full reparation and access to courts public defenders autonomous bodies and administrative mechanisms. Arts. 131–138 (Ch. XXVII–XXVIII) establish complaint review and appeal procedures including precautionary measures against AI systems causing harm.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: General Law on the Rights of Girls Boys and Adolescents (Mexico)
fr1_justif: Children and adolescents enjoy the right to universal access to Information and Communication Technologies as well as to broadcasting and telecommunications services including broadband and the Internet as established in the Political Constitution and the Federal Telecommunications and Broadcasting Law (Art. 101 BIS). The State must guarantee safe access and use of the Internet by promoting policies for the prevention protection response and punishment of cyberbullying and all forms of ICT-facilitated violence (Art. 101 BIS 3 added DOF 24-12-2024). These provisions apply to AI systems as technologies encompassed within the ICT framework (Arts. 11-12 101 BIS - 101 BIS 3).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: National System for the Comprehensive Protection of the Rights of Children and Adolescents

fr1_title: Initiative with Draft Decree by Which the Law is Issued (Federal for the Ethical Sovereign and Inclusive Development of the Artificial Intelligence) (Mexico)
fr1_justif: The draft law establishes non-discrimination and algorithmic justice as a guiding principle requiring AI systems to avoid direct or indirect discrimination based on ethnic origin gender sexual orientation age social status disability or any other personal condition (Art. 6.VIII). Equity is defined as an AI-specific obligation to eradicate perpetuation of inequalities (Art. 5.VII). Persons affected by discriminatory automated decisions may demand suspension of the system correction of results and compensation (Art. 44.II). The PNAA is mandated to audit datasets for structural biases and differential impact risks (Art. 22.I).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Initiative with Draft Decree by Which the Law is Issued (Federal for the Ethical Sovereign and Inclusive Development of the Artificial Intelligence) (Mexico)
fr1_justif: The draft addresses gender equality mainly as non-discrimination inclusive governance and anti-bias controls. It defines “Equity” to prohibit discrimination by gender and sexual preferences (Art. 5.VII pp. 7–8) and sets “Non-Discrimination & Algorithmic Justice” as a governing principle (Art. 6.VIII pp. 12–13). It requires gender parity in the National AI Council (Arts. 15–16 p. 22). It mandates audits of training data for representativeness and “differential impact” (Art. 22.I p. 26) and gives people the right to challenge discriminatory automated decisions (Art. 44.II p. 36). It also calls for participation of women and LGBTIQ+ in AI decision-making (Art. 103 p. 66).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Initiative with Draft Decree by Which the Law is Issued (Federal for the Ethical Sovereign and Inclusive Development of the Artificial Intelligence) (Mexico)
fr1_justif: The draft law addresses human oversight through several provisions. Article 6(d) grants users the right to request review and appeal of automated decisions affecting their rights. Article 26 requires entities using AI for automated decisions to guarantee that those decisions are explainable and subject to human review. Article 27 establishes that individuals may request review before the OR-IA and appeal before an independent body in case of rejection. Article 38(III) additionally mandates qualified human supervision for AI systems interacting with children. These provisions collectively address accountability obligations human review of automated decisions and individual appeal rights.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Initiative with Draft Decree by Which the Law is Issued (Federal for the Ethical Sovereign and Inclusive Development of the Artificial Intelligence) (Mexico)
fr1_justif: The draft bill defines Algorithmic Impact Assessment (AIA) as the estimation of harm an AI system may cause to the population (Art. 5.VIII). High-risk AI systems — those with significant impact on human rights life health or public safety — are subject to strict design requirements and conformity assessments (Art. 5.XIII). The Risk Traffic Light System classifies all AI systems by their potential impact on fundamental rights health the environment and democratic institutions (Art. 5.XXIII). The Precaution Principle requires that innovation be guided by prior ethical social and environmental impact assessments (Art. 6.XV).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Decree adding various provisions to the Federal Labor Law regarding digital platforms (Mexico)
fr1_justif: Article 291-J regulates assignment algorithms: 'through algorithms or similar mechanisms' must be transparent clear and known to all workers. It requires platforms to develop an 'algorithmic work management policy' explaining: consequences of complying or failing to comply with instructions (waiting times travel etc.); impact of customer ratings; incentives & penalties affecting the intensity frequency & pace of work; existence & rules of categories that positively or negatively affect task assignment; & other criteria affecting access to future tasks bonuses & sanctions with the aim of reducing information asymmetry. Article 291-P requires human oversight for decisions interrupting a worker's connection & linking.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Initiative with Draft Decree to Reform the Articles 153-CY and 153-K of the Federal Labour Law
fr1_justif: This Senate bill filed November 5 2024 proposes two operative reforms to the Federal Labor Law. Article 153-C introduces a new mandatory obligation for employers to provide AI competency training to workers. Article 153-K requires the National Committee on Concertation and Productivity — convened by STPS and the Secretaría de Economía — to incorporate AI skills into its national training and productivity recommendations. Together these provisions establish a binding legal basis for employer-led AI reskilling directly addressing workforce adaptation to AI-driven transitions.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Initiative with Draft Decree by Which the Law is Issued (Federal for the Ethical Sovereign and Inclusive Development of the Artificial Intelligence) (Mexico)
fr1_justif: The draft establishes a comprehensive AI safety and security framework. It defines a Risk Traffic Light System classifying AI into minimal limited high-risk and prohibited categories based on potential harm to rights health environment and democratic institutions (Art. 5.XIII 5.XXIII). High-risk systems are subject to prior algorithmic impact assessments mandatory registration periodic technical audits traceability obligations and meaningful human oversight (Arts 33.III 37).Developers must certify training data integrity and implement incident response protocols (Arts. 46 50). The PNAA is mandated to define and update technical evaluation metrics and audit standards (Art. 21)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Initiative with Draft Decree by Which the Law is Issued (Federal for the Ethical Sovereign and Inclusive Development of the Artificial Intelligence) (Mexico)
fr1_justif: The draft law establishes explainability and algorithmic transparency as a foundational guiding principle requiring AI systems to operate under mechanisms that allow their decisions processes and criteria to be understood for accountability and oversight (Art. 6.VI). An entire chapter (Ch. XVI Arts. 83–90) is dedicated to explainability transparency and algorithmic traceability mandating differentiated explainability for different user profiles technical documentation and prohibiting black-box systems. Developers and operators of high-risk systems must ensure meaningful explanations adapted to citizens authorities and technical auditors (Art. 85).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Education System Digitalization Strategy 2022-2027
fr1_justif: The Education System Digitalization Strategy 2022–2027 developed by Ministry of Education Science Culture and Sport with support from UNICEF Montenegro addresses AI literacy under Strategic Goal 3: Development and Improvement of Digital Skills and Competences.Strategy recognises AI as a key driver of digital transformation and highlights the need to develop digital competences for participation in society and the labour market (page 53). Objective 3.1 focuses on strengthening the digital skills and competences of employees in educational institutions including teachers (pages 53–54). Objective 3.2 states that as AI and digitally enabled automation evolve rapidly students children need to acquire basic understanding of AI (page 54).
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Education Science Culture and Sport (now Ministry of Education Science and Innovation)

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: National Strategy 'Digital Morocco 2030'
fr1_justif: The Digital Morocco 2030 strategy is structured around 2 strategic axes and 3 accelerators to which AI and its inclusive deployment are integral parts_p.9. The AI-related section highlights the need to develop Al skills p.23. The 'Inclusive Digital Use' part promotes further the inclusion of digital education as early as primary school p. 24. Additionally under the 'Digital Talent' accelerator goals have been set to achieve concrete KPIs in relation to initial training upskilling/reskilling and new alien talents. The strategy also outlines the aim to develop a repository of digital skills with stakeholders in the public and private ecosystem and ensure its monitoring p. 19.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Fake news: From disinformation to access to proven and available information (Morocco)
fr1_justif: The Opinion of the Economic Social and Environmental Council discusses new advances in disinformation and offers recommendations to address these challenges. It emphasises that verifying the quality of information remains difficult in the digital environment where the development of sound image and video manipulation techniques (deepfakes) based on AI can create audiovisual documents that appear to be authentic (pp. 10-11). One of the recommendations highlighted is to establish a public digital platform for 'fact-checking' official information in Morocco capitalising on initiatives launched by certain institutions (The High Authority for Audiovisual Communication Maghreb Arab Press Agency (MAP) etc.) (pp. 7-8).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Royal  Decree  No.  1-09-15  of  22  Safar  1430  (February  18  2009)  promulgating  Law  No.  09-08  relating  to  the  protection  of  individuals  with  regard  to  the  processing  of  personal  data
fr1_justif: The law explicitly regulates “automated data processing intended to define the profile” and prohibits decisions producing legal effects from being based solely on such automated processing (Art. 12) thereby addressing profiling practices that are functionally equivalent to AI-driven decision-making and establishes multiple access to remedy mechanisms including the right to object (Art. 11) the right to understand the logic of processing (Art. 9) the right to rectification (Art. 10) and complaint procedures before the CNDP with investigative and corrective powers (Arts. 28–30).
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.
fr1_body_name: National Commission for the Control of Personal Data Protection

fr1_title: Opinion of the Economic Social and Environmental Council: What are the uses and prospects for the artificial intelligence in Morocco?
fr1_justif: Approved by its general assembly in 2024 the Economic Social and Environmental Council published a set of recommendations to guide the development of artificial intelligence in Morocco (p.5). They recommend the sensible use of AI tools in all sectors both public and private by developing a dedicated support plan including training components (p.8)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Strategy 'Digital Morocco 2030'
fr1_justif: The 'Digital Morocco 2030' strategy is structured around 2 strategic axes and 3 accelerators to which AI and inclusivity are integral parts (p 9). Under 'Digital Talents' the strategy aims to ensure the availability and adaptability of talents to market needs with a specific focus on 'upskilling/reskilling' where the aim is to upskill/reskill 50000 trained talents in 2030. A set of measures is planned to achieve this KPI: scaling up bootcamp-type training programs tailoring the training to the needs of the labour market and developing appropriate certification programs across the Kingdom (p 19).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Strategy 'Digital Morocco 2030'
fr1_justif: The strategy is structured around 2 axes and 3 accelerators to which AI and inclusivity are integral parts_p.9. Regarding AI-related goals the strategy's measures include the need to apply evaluation mechanisms to support the responsible use of AI develop AI skills build robust infrastructure for responsible operation and engage in multidisciplinary research to study its impact on society_p.23.  While all the objectives implicitly contribute to the principle of robustness the third one specifically highlights the need for a strong infrastructure to ensure the responsible operation of AI which is essential for its security.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Opinion of the Economic Social and Environmental Council: What are the uses and prospects for the artificial intelligence in Morocco?
fr1_justif: Approved by its general assembly in 2024 the Economic Social and Environmental Council published a set of recommendations to guide the uses and prospects for the development of artificial intelligence in Morocco (p.5). They highlight that reducing bias and developing explainable and transparent AI systems are becoming priorities to ensure the fairness and reliability of systems deployed at scale (p. 16). The document stressed the remaining challenge of results explainability (p. 14) and the need to define a specific legal framework to ensure the ethical and responsible use of artificial intelligence systems (p. 31).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

Page 33 assigns implementation responsibilities to the Ministry of Education and National AI Centre and sets monitoring indicators. Page 37 (10.3) establishes AI Excellence Centres to support AI study research and training.

Page 15 Clause 9.60 delegates the framework to find ways to mitigate and control the harm of  deepfake misinformation and personal bias. The same is reiterated in the  the Policy Implementation Plan on Page 53 S.N 49.

fr1_justif: The document addresses cultural and linguistic diversity by promoting inclusive AI development and use. It emphasizes integrating local languages into AI systems (Sections 9.61 p.16; 50 p.54) and using NLP personalized and adaptive learning to reduce educational inequality (Section 9.30 p.13; 24 p.38). Additionally the policy allows provinces and local levels to adapt AI governance to their cultural and social contexts (Section 11 p.20) supporting culturally responsive implementation.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_justif: In Section 2.3 Foundations of AI (pp.7–8) it states that AI systems should be developed while considering ethical and environmental impacts and should safeguard environmental interests aligned with sustainable development objectives.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence (AI) Policy (2025) (Nepal)
fr1_justif: The document addresses fairness and non-discrimination by promoting inclusive participation and reducing inequalities in AI development and use. Page 11 Clause 9.18 and Page 32 Clause 14 call for skill-based training and awareness programs to ensure equal access and participation of children senior citizens minorities persons with disabilities and other marginalized communities. Page 13 Clause 9.30 and Page 38 Clause 24 aim to reduce learning inequality through AI tools such as NLP and personalized learning. Additionally Page 18 Clause 10.1.1 mandates guidance on fairness and human rights in AI while Page 15 Clause 9.60 and Page 53 Clause 49 address mitigating biases and harms
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

These initiatives are reiterated in the Policy Implementation Plan (p.35 S.N. 19–20) which outlines actions to implement reskilling and upskilling programs to support workforce adaptation to AI-driven technological change.

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence (AI) Policy (2025) (Nepal)
fr1_justif: The document addresses AI safety and security by emphasizing the development of a responsible and reliable AI ecosystem. It highlights the need for proper data management digital infrastructure and governance to ensure the safe and responsible use of AI technologies (pp. 4–5). The policy also sets objectives to build a sustainable and reliable AI ecosystem and strengthen AI governance (Section 6 p. 7). Additionally it proposes benchmarking standardization and certification of AI systems to ensure quality safety and reliability (Section 9.5 p. 10) and the creation of a regulatory sandbox to support the safe development and testing of AI systems (Section 9.10 p. 10).
