fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Bots fake accounts and automated conduct are mechanisms of disinformation campaigns (recital 85) and as such rules are needed to combat them (recital 9). Systemic risk assessments apply to large-scale online platforms and search engines (art. 34) and explicitly include the dissemination or amplification of misleading or fraudulent content and the negative effects on gender-based violence. This includes recommendation systems and other relevant algorithmic systems (art. 34). Mitigation measures include testing and adapting algorithmic and recommendation systems; speeding up the processing of notifications of illegal hate speech and cyber violence; and labeling content generated or manipulated by AI (art.35).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU GDPR
fr1_justif: The law provides a layered redress framework that applies in AI contexts whenever personal data is processed. Data subjects harmed by automated processing based on AI-driven profiling and decision-making hold the right to express their point of view and the right to contest the decision (art. 22); presenting complaints to supervisory authorities (art. 77) resorting to legal measures against gaps in the authorities (art. 78) and bringing proceedings directly against controllers or processors (art. 79). Organizations may be mandated to exercise the rights collectively on behalf of data subjects (art. 80). Supervisory authorities may impose administrative fines because of the violations of core provisions (art. 83).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: This Regulation shall not apply to the processing of personal data by Member States in the context of carrying out activities relating to the Union's common foreign and security policy (recital 16). The processing of personal data by Member States does not apply to activities related to the protection and prevention of threats to public security (Art. 2).
fr1_body_name: The law establishes the European Data Protection Board is comprised of representatives from the supervisory authority of each member states (Arts 68) acting independently (Art 69) providing guidelines recommendations and binding decisions to ensure consistence across Member States (Art 70) The Member State must to establish am independent supervisory authority responsible for monitoring and enforcing the regulation (Art 51)

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Online platform service providers must ensure the privacy security and protection of minors in their services (Art. 28) including prohibiting advertising based on profiling through the automated processing of data about children (Art. 28). Terms and conditions must be understandable to minors (Art. 14) ensuring a high level of privacy and security by default based on good practices (recitals 70 71). Large-scale online platforms and large-scale search engines systematic risk assessment applies periodically evaluating potential negative effects on minors (Art. 34). Mitigation measures are determined: age verification parental control tools abuse reporting tools and support for minors (Art. 35). Voluntary standards apply (Art. 44).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: The Government-wide vision on generative AI of the Netherlands
fr1_justif: It emphasizes supporting open and public language models trained on underrepresented languages such as Frisian Papiamento and sign language to ensure accessibility and equal representation (p.13 Section 3b – Challenges and risks). It also explores participation in the Alliance for Language Technologies (ALT-EDIC) to strengthen European cooperation on linguistic technologies (p.37 Section 5c – Shaping and applying laws and regulations). It highlights opportunities for Generative AI to enhance accessibility and inclusion noting its ability to translate large volumes of text accurately (p.10–11 Sec. 3a) and adjust language levels to make government information more accessible (p.10).
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.
fr1_body_name: Algorithms Coordination Directorate (DCA) and Government AI Validation Team (Rijks Al-validatieteam)

fr1_title: The Government-wide vision on generative AI of the Netherlands
fr1_justif: Sustainability is one of the four guiding principles ensuring that AI development supports both prosperity and climate goals. The document stresses that the development and deployment of generative AI must not negatively affect the climate or ecosystems. It also recognises substantial energy demand and climate change as key environmental risks arising from generative AI’s lifecycle (Sec. 3b p. 12).
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.
fr1_body_name: Algorithms Coordination Directorate (DCA) and Government AI Validation Team (Rijks Al-validatieteam)

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The law focuses on the risk classification system (Art. 6) and the requirements imposed on high-risk AI systems. It requires suppliers of high-risk AI systems to establish implement document and maintain a risk (and damage) management system throughout the system's lifecycle (Art. 9). Training validation and test datasets must conform to data governance in terms of representation error-freeness completeness contextualization and the function the system will be used for handling special personal data for monitoring purposes and detecting and correcting biases. High-risk AI systems must have consistency throughout the lifecycle by requiring appropriate levels of accuracy and robustness and reducing the risk of bias (art.15).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act addresses gender equality through non-discrimination and fundamental rights provisions. Training datasets for high-risk AI must be representative avoinding the discriminatory outcomes and protecting atributes explicitly related gender (Art. 10). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights impact assessments before deploying high-risk AI incluing gender-based discrimination (Art. 27). Equality bodies are granted market surveillance powers enabling them to investigate and sanction AI systems producing discriminatory outcomes (Art. 74).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act establishes requirements to ensure human control over high-risk AI systems requiring providers to design systems that enable natural persons with a minimum level of comprehensibility to monitor outputs detect anomalies and intervene or interrupt operations when necessary (art. 14). Deployers must assign oversight to competent natural persons with authority to act (art. 26). For general-purpose AI with systemic risk adversarial testing and incident reporting reinforce human control (Arts. 55 73). Additional oversight provisions are set in the fundamental rights impact assessment (art. 27) and Annex (IV) 2(e) as part of technical documentation requirements (art. 11).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Law establishes an assessment architecture according to the type and level of risk of the system. High-risk AI suppliers must identify analyze and mitigate risks during the design development and implementation phases within a risk management system (Arti.9) with comprehensive technical documentation (Art.15) and monitoring of operations and anomalies (Art. 26). For AI implementers in the public sector a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment applies before implementing high-risk systems listed in Annex 3 (Art.27). Compliance assessment must include self-assessment or third-party audits depending on the criticality of the system (Art. 43 47). There are ex-post assessments and post-commercialization monitoring (Art.72).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act classifies systems for recruitment promotion termination and worker management as high-risk AI systems (Annex III(4)) (art. 6). Prohibited AI practices include bans on emotion-recognition in the workplace (art. 5). Providers must implement risk-management addressing discrimination (Art. 9) ensure representative datasets and bias-control (art. 10) and maintain technical documentation (Arts. 11). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights assessments before deployment (art. 27). Art. 26 (7-9) establishes safeguards: workers must be informed and may request explanations of AI use. However the Act does not regulate pay conditions or compensation so it mitigates risks without constituting a full labour-rights regime.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Act of 25 October 2021 containing rules on the accessibility of information of public interest (Open Government Act) (Netherlands)
fr1_justif: The Wet open overheid (Woo) (Stb. 2021 499) does not explicitly regulate transparency of government AI or algorithmic systems but provides an implicit legal basis for public access to information through general access-to-information obligations. Art 2.1 broadly defines a “document” as any recorded information which may include documentation related to algorithmic or automated decision-making systems. Art 3.3 and 3.3a establish proactive publication duties for certain categories of information potentially covering research reports or impact assessments underlying algorithms while Art 5.1 limits refusals to narrowly defined exceptions. In practice the Woo has already been used to obtain algorithm-related information about government.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Advisory Board on Transparency and Information Management

fr1_title: The Netherlands' Digitalisation Strategy
fr1_justif: The document addresses skills development primarily in Priority 6 (p. 10) and Priority 3 (p. 7). As part of Priority 3 the government would explore building 'government-wide AI competence centre' (p.7). As part of priority 6  it prioritises 'recruiting and retraining' staff based on technological innovations and scaling 'central pools' of digital experts. Key interventions include implementing the 'Quality Framework IV (KWIV)' to map workforce skills and collaborating with academies like RADIO to teach 'AI literacy' and 'digital leadership' based on specific roles or archetypes. (p.10).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_justif: Reskilling/upskilling is addressed as part of preparing the workforce and society for AI’s impact. It emphasises matching workforce skills to the future labour market and ensuring people have skills to maintain job content amid technological change (see discussion on labour market and skills alignment p. 36). It also includes concrete actions to increase knowledge and skills in education and government (e.g. training civil servants boosting generative AI literacy) under the action lines on increasing knowledge and skills (pp. 41-42).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Safety and security is core to the Act. It establishes a risk-based framework for risk identification and mitigation (Art. 9) oversight measures (Art. 14) and robustness and accuracy requirements (Art. 15). To ensure ongoing safety the Act mandates technical documentation and record-keeping (Arts. 11–12) and in certain high risk situations a conformity assessment procedure prior to market placement (Art. 43). Post-market obligations in Arts. 72–73 require providers and deployers to monitor performance report serious incidents and take corrective action. For GPAI models posing systemic risks Arts. 55–56 impose additional safety and cybersecurity duties including model evaluation and risk mitigation following the Code of Practice.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Articles 11 12 and 13 establish several transparency and documentation obligations for AI developers and deployers. High-risk AI systems must include clear information on their capabilities limitations and intended purpose (Art. 13) and providers must maintain comprehensive technical documentation and automatic logging to ensure traceability (Arts. 11–12). These measures enable deployers to better understand the systems and authorities to audit or investigate when necessary. There are disclosure obligations that require notification when individuals interact with AI systems particularly chatbots and emotion recognition tools (Art. 50). Also for general-purpose AI in terms of model documentation and summary of training data (Art. 53).

fr1_title: Generative AI: Guidance and resources for education professionals on the use of artificial intelligence in schools (New Zealand)
fr1_justif: NZ's Ministry of Education released an initial advisory on generative AI (Ministry of Education 2024) acknowledging AI’s growing role in education and provided high-level principles for schools emphasising student safety data privacy and the primacy of human judgement. Schools have been encouraged to update their digital technology policies to cover AI use and to approach classroom experiments with generative AI in a cautious supervised manner Page numbers are not provided due to the guidance published in a webpage format.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.
fr2_title: AI resource series for educators (Netherlands)
fr2_justif: Ako Aotearoa National Centre for Tertiary Teaching Excellence is a publicly funded organisation. In 2023 the centre released a 6-part beginner's series guide is meant for tertiary educators on using AI and covers many aspects of AI literacy. The series supports educators in using AI in ways that are a) culturally sustaining inclusive and grounded in Aotearoa values (Part 4); b) explore the ethical use of AI in ways that uphold cultural values learner rights and trust in education (Part 5); c) shares practical steps for AI strategy capability and leadership in Aotearoa's tertiary sector. (Part 6).
fr2_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Deepfake Digital Harm and Exploitation Bill (New Zealand)
fr1_justif: This Bill holds accountable those who use digital tools to violate other individuals’ agency. It does this by amending both the Crimes Act 1961 and the Harmful Digital Communications Act 2015 to expand the definition of an 'intimate visual recording' to explicitly include images created synthesised or altered to show a person’s likeness produced without consent (Explanatory Note General Policy Statement Pg 1).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Deepfake Digital Harm and Exploitation Bill (New Zealand)
fr1_justif: This Bill holds accountable those who use digital tools to violate other individuals’ agency. It does this by amending both the Crimes Act 1961 and the Harmful Digital Communications Act 2015 to expand the definition of an 'intimate visual recording' to explicitly include images created synthesised or altered to show a person’s likeness produced without consent (Pg 1).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Responsible AI Guidance for the Public Service: GenAI (New Zealand)
fr1_justif: The Responsible AI Guidance for the Public Service (GenAI) guidance is designed specifically for NZ’s public sector and strongly emphasises on cultural diversity particularly protection for Maori Pacific People and ethnic communities (Pg 31-33). It mandates agencies to assess the impact of GenAI systems on these groups consult with iwi Maori respect Maori data sovereignty and align approaches with Maori-Crown relationship frameworks (Pg 31-33). The document suggests agencies to evaluate the sensitivity of indigenous data foster Maori-led and community-informed GenAI applications and build confidence for culturally safe engagement (Pg 33).
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.
fr2_title: Responsible AI Guidance for Businesses: Investing with confidence (New Zealand)
fr2_justif: This guidance draws on the OECD values-based AI Principles: inclusive growth sustainable development and well-being; human rights and democratic values. It aligns with international equivalent guidance materials while taking into account New Zealand values and interests including respecting te reo Maori (Maori language) Maori imagery tikanga and other matauranga (knowledge) and Maori data (Pg 4). It suggests businesses to preserve and take into account NZ’s diversity including by taking care of New Zealanders’ data exercising good practice engagement with impacted communities. (Pg 4-5)
fr2_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Algorithm Charter for Aotearoa New Zealand
fr1_justif: This Charter demonstrates a commitment to ensuring New Zealanders have confidence in how government agencies use algorithms. This Charter is one of many ways that government is demonstrating transparency and accountability identifying and mitigating biases in AI systems to ensure that privacy ethics and human rights are safeguarded (Privacy Ethics and Human Rights Pg 3). The Charter is a commitment by government to minimise unintended bias better reflect the principles of Te Tiriti o Waitangi and strike the right balance between making sure access the algorithms to ensure deliver better services to New Zealanders whilst still maintaining the trust. (Pg 1).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr2_title: Responsible AI Guidance for Businesses: Investing with confidence (New Zealand)
fr2_justif: The guidance outlines the potential legal implications of AI under the Human Rights Act 1993 which holds individuals and businesses accountable for avoiding discrimination based on sex race and other protected grounds in specified areas and for protecting civil and political rights (See Compliance and legal obligations; Pg 10). Beyond the Human Rights Act the guidance highlights a range of other legal obligations to help businesses understand their responsibilities across all areas of operation.
fr2_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Algorithm Charter for Aotearoa New Zealand
fr1_justif: This Charter demonstrates a commitment to ensuring New Zealanders have confidence in how government agencies use algorithms. This Charter is one of many ways that government is demonstrating commitment to Retain human oversight by: Nominating a point of contact for public inquiries about algorithms providing a channel for challenging or appealing of decisions informed by algorithms clearly explaining the role of humans in decisions informed by algorithms. (Human Oversight Pg 3).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr2_title: Responsible AI Guidance for Businesses: Investing with confidence (New Zealand)
fr2_justif: The Guidance dedicates a section to explain about the 'Human-in-the-loop decision-making' aspects highlighting two scenarios with one of them being if an AI system is helping to make big decisions like those about money health or the law a person should always be involved. The level of human involvement will likely depend on the risk associated with those decisions or the cost of harm of a ‘wrong’ decision. This can range from having a human consistently make the final decision (informed by an AI output) or having human oversight over particularly high-risk or low-confidence AI results. (Human-in-the-loop decision-making Pg 29).
fr2_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Algorithm impact assessment user guide (New Zealand)
fr1_justif: This Algorithm Impact Assessment User Guide forms part of the Algorithm Impact Assessment (AIA) process and documentation prepared by Stats NZ to help government agencies meet their commitments under the Algorithm Charter for Aotearoa New Zealand(the Charter) (p. 4). It is designed to facilitate informed decision-making about the benefits and risks of government use of algorithms and how it affects people (pp 13 20). Adopting a responsible approach to the development and use of algorithms and AI systems is a key contributor to innovation rather than something that stifles or blocks it. The guide takes a risk-based approach intended ensure agencies can use algorithms to provide better services (p. 4).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr2_title: Responsible AI Guidance for Businesses: Investing with confidence (New Zealand)
fr2_justif: The MBIE Responsible AI Guidance for Businesses advises New Zealand organisations to apply structured risk management and impact assessments at all stages of the AI lifecycle (pp. 11-14). It encourages businesses to identify assess and mitigate privacy and stakeholder risks using international standards and tools such as Privacy (p23) and Stakeholder Impact Assessments (p11). The guidance highlights the need for clear documentation recordkeeping (p 13) and ongoing monitoring to ensure transparency and accountability. It promotes a context-driven approach supporting responsible and trustworthy AI use in the private sector.
fr2_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems
fr1_title: Algorithm Charter for Aotearoa New Zealand
fr1_justif: This Charter demonstrates a commitment to ensuring New Zealanders have confidence in how government agencies use algorithms. This Charter is one of many ways that government is demonstrating transparency and accountability in the use of data. However it does not fully address important considerations such as Maori Data Sovereignty as these are complex and require separate consideration (Pg 3).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Responsible AI Guidance for the Public Service: GenAI (New Zealand)
fr1_justif: NZ Responsible AI Guidance covers the Procurement Framework which is flexible and can accommodate the fast-paced innovative nature of generative AI. This flexibility depends on following procurement best practice and incorporating adequate risk assessment and mitigation in the organisation's procurement plan. The guide mentions several domestic and international best practice guides for procuring AI. The guidance shares current NZ Government Procurement website Rules Principles and Charter in line with government policies to help guide the process on any procurement projects in general and also with regards to generative AI. (Pg 15-17).
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: New Zealand’s Strategy for Artificial Intelligence: Investing with confidence
fr1_justif: The Strategy document identified lack of AI skills as a barrier to AI adoption after research and consultation. It cites a 2024 Datacom State of AI Index: AI Attitudes in NZ survey which identified skills shortage as a major barrier for respondents with 43% of non-users specifically citing lack of expertise as their main reason for not adopting AI (Pg 12). To address this in the public sector a few initiatives launched by the Government Chief Digital Officer include AI masterclasses for leaders and foundational courses for public servants reflecting a strong commitment to integrating AI across government operations.(Pg 12).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Responsible AI Guidance for Businesses: Investing with confidence (New Zealand)
fr1_justif: The guidance is for private sector and is applicable to AI systems and dedicates a section to model efficacy outlining that models can be evaluated for safety issues such as prompt stereotyping encoded bias (Pg 25-26). Model safety assessment methods mentioned include penetration testing red teaming threat modelling and audits (Pg 25-26). The guidance advocates for a secure-by-design approach to ensure both physical and digital resilience protecting against attacks and supporting reliability and consistency in operations (Pg 14). By aligning with international best practices agencies are encouraged to rigorously test and secure AI systems to maintain safety & public trust throughout their lifecycle.?
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr2_title: Responsible AI Guidance for the Public Service: GenAI (New Zealand)
fr2_justif: The guidance specifically applies to Gen AI in the public sector discusses “GenAI security (Pp 13-15) and reliability (P 21)” emphasizing that new risks including system vulnerabilities misconfigurations and unreliable outputs can affect public sector organizations. The guidance highlights that high-quality representative data and robust ongoing testing are essential to prevent hallucinations and incorrect information (pg 19-20). It also shares examples of scenarios of security and GenAI along with certifications and guidances on securing AI systems (pg 15).
fr2_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Algorithm Charter for Aotearoa New Zealand
fr1_justif: The document addresses transparency and explainability through explicit commitments requiring government agencies to clearly communicate how algorithms influence decisions. Under the Transparency commitment agencies are expected to provide plain English documentation of algorithms make information about the data and processes used available and publish details on how data are collected secured and stored (p.3). The Charter also reinforces explainability through human oversight mechanisms including identifying a public point of contact for inquiries and providing channels to challenge or appeal algorithm-informed decisions (p.3). In addition the Charter emphasizes the importance of transparency in maintaining public trust.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr2_title: Responsible AI Guidance for Businesses: Investing with confidence (New Zealand)
fr2_justif: The document promotes transparency and explainability by encouraging organizations to clearly communicate when and why AI systems are used and how they influence decisions. It recommends informing users when they interact with AI or AI-generated content and providing information about how systems function including training data sources monitoring practices and performance metrics (pp.18 38). The guidance also suggests labeling AI-generated content documenting risks and mitigation measures and offering channels for feedback complaints or corrections. Additionally it emphasizes that strong governance and oversight should support transparency and explainability to demonstrate responsible and trustworthy AI use (p.9).
fr2_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Law No. 2022-59 (16 December 2022) on the protection of personal data as amended by Law No. 2023-31 (4 July 2023) (Niger)
fr1_justif: The law allows individuals affected by AI or automated processing to seek redress. The data subject (the person impacted) must first raise the issue with the data controller (Art.107). If the issue is unresolved or causes harm the same individual may file a complaint with High Authority for the Protection of Personal Data (HAPDP) which can investigate and order correction or erasure of data (Art.15 p.12; Art.69; Art.108). Decisions based solely on automated processing can be challenged (Art.51–52). The individual may appeal to the Conseil d’État or seek judicial or urgent remedies (Arts.109–110).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: HAPDP (High Authority for the Protection of Personal Data/ Haute Autorit de Protection des Donnes caractre Personnel)

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2025 (Nigeria)
fr1_justif: The NAIS addresses AI literacy mainly under Pillar 3: Accelerating AI Adoption and Sector Transformation (pp. 38-43) and its Guiding Principles (p. 29). It calls for increasing AI literacy among stakeholders (Sec. 3.3.1 p. 41) and empowering citizens with the knowledge and skills to engage with AI (p. 29). It mandates a comprehensive AI skills development programme including teacher training (p. 41). Across the strategy it stresses building national AI capacity urging skill development (p. 35) warning of AI literacy shortages (p. 36) and setting a goal to expand AI professionals within five years (p. 34).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2025 (Nigeria)
fr1_justif: Page 50 of the strategy identifies misinformation as one of the societal risks associated with AI systems and further provided that strategies should be developed to mitigate each risks including misinformation in page 53. But does not specifically address AI- facilitated misinformation and violence
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Nigeria Data Protection Act
fr1_justif: Where the existence of automated decision-making including profiling the significance and envisaged consequences of such processing for the data subject and the right to object to and challenge such processing (section 27). Any data subject whose rights have been violated through automated processing may present a complaint with the National Commission (section 46) seek compliance and enforcement orders (sections 47 48) institute civil proceedings for damages (section 51) and invoke the right to human review of automated decisions (section 37). Data controllers and processors found in violation are subject to administrative fines (section 49).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: This Law does not apply to the processing of personal data carried out by one or more persons exclusively for processing by a competent authority to the extent necessary for national security(section 3).

fr1_title: Nigeria Data Protection Act
fr1_justif: The law refers to the Rights of the Child Act (section 65) where a child is defined as any person under the age of 18 – they do not have the legal capacity to independently consent to the processing of their personal data (section 31). Consent for processing must be obtained from parents or legal guardians. Controllers must adopt consent verification mechanisms checking if it was given by an authorized adult (section 31). Therefore no data subject including children will be subject to a decision based solely on automated processing including profiling (section 37) with applicable and explicit exceptions. A data privacy impact assessment is also required (section 28).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: This Law does not apply to the processing of personal datacarried out by one or more persons exclusively for the purpose of processing by a competent authority to the extent necessary for national security (section 3).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2025 (Nigeria)
fr1_justif: The Strategy promotes a Nigeria-centric approach ensuring local communities provide cultural context and define safety thresholds (p.14). It commits to diversity in AI R&D under the 'Inclusivity and Shared Prosperity' principle (p.28). Art. 4.1.1 establishes a High-Level AI Ethics Expert Group (AIEEG) with diverse stakeholders to guide implementation. Furthermore Art. 4.1.2 mandates the development of National AI Ethical Principles aligned with Nigerian values specifically highlighting decoloniality humanity inclusion and solidarity.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: National Centre for AI and Robotics (NCAIR) and the AI Governance Regulatory Body (Pillar 5 Objective 512 pp 4546) However in practice the National Information Technology Development Agency (NITDA) working through the National Centre for AI and Robotics (NCAIR) is currently coordinating activities aligned with the Strategy (see ecosystem/delivery actors on p 16 and the high-level Roadmap for Implementation on p 59)

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2025 (Nigeria)
fr1_justif: The National Artificial Intelligence Strategy (NAIS) explicitly addresses the environmental impact of AI focusing on Green AI carbon footprint and energy efficiency across multiple sections. Page 5 outlines under Pillar 1 has one of its aims as building AI infrastructure with 'reduced carbon footprint and improved energy efficiency” Pages 31 and 32 detail strategies for deploying energy-efficient AI powering clusters with clean energy (less than 50% grid reliance).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: National Centre for AI and Robotics (NCAIR) and the AI Governance Regulatory Body (Pillar 5 Objective 512 pp 4546) However in practice the National Information Technology Development Agency (NITDA) working through the National Centre for AI and Robotics (NCAIR) is currently coordinating activities aligned with the Strategy (see ecosystem/delivery actors on p 16 and the high-level Roadmap for Implementation on p 59)

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2025 (Nigeria)
fr1_justif: The NAIS 2025 on page 28 provides that companies should develop and deploy AI systems in line with ethical principles such as fairness. Page 29 further emphasises that good data management practices  including fairness  must be upheld in the collection use and sharing of data for AI applications. In addition page 44 calls for the implementation of a comprehensive AI ethics framework that explicitly address fairness and transparency. Finally page 49 encourages companies to adopt the U.S. NIST framework for developing and deploying trusted AI systems which identifies “fairness” as a key characteristic thereby reinforcing the government’s commitment to ethical and responsible AI adoption.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: National Centre for AI and Robotics (NCAIR) and the AI Governance Regulatory Body (Pillar 5 Objective 512 pp 4546) However in practice the National Information Technology Development Agency (NITDA) working through the National Centre for AI and Robotics (NCAIR) is currently coordinating activities aligned with the Strategy (see ecosystem/delivery actors on p 16 and the high-level Roadmap for Implementation on p 59)

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2025 (Nigeria)
fr1_justif: Nigeria’s National Artificial Intelligence Strategy (NAIS) 2025 treats gender equality via its inclusion/ethics stance. On page 16 the National Artificial Intelligence Strategy (2025) commits to advancing gender equality in Nigeria’s AI ecosystem including gender-specific AI learning groups and ensuring innovation is inclusive and accessible to all stakeholders regardless of gender. On page 28 it sets out measures to align AI development with gender-equality objectives reinforcing government commitment to inclusive AI governance.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: National Centre for AI and Robotics (NCAIR) and the AI Governance Regulatory Body (Pillar 5 Objective 512 pp 4546) However in practice the National Information Technology Development Agency (NITDA) working through the National Centre for AI and Robotics (NCAIR) is currently coordinating activities aligned with the Strategy (see ecosystem/delivery actors on p 16 and the high-level Roadmap for Implementation on p 59)

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2025 (Nigeria)
fr1_justif: Nigeria Data Protection Act 2023 sufficiently addresses Human Oversight and Determination and is AI-relevant due to its explicit provisions on automated decision-making (Section 37). In particular it establishes a right to human intervention in such scenarios (Section 37(3)(a)) which directly aligns with the indicator’s requirements.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2025 (Nigeria)
fr1_justif: The NAIS does not expressly mandate “AI Impact Assessments” by name but embeds impact-assessment mechanisms within its ethics risk and governance framework. Under Pillar 4 Objective 4.1 it proposes a “standardised assessment tool” and in Strategy 4.1.3 commits to a comprehensive AI ethics assessment framework to evaluate ethical implications before deployment across the AI lifecycle (p.44). Pillar 5 also calls for a National AI Risk Management Framework to identify assess and mitigate negative impacts (p.47).  It further links AI governance to NDPA safeguards (Sec.1.1.3.5 pp.19–20).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2025 (Nigeria)
fr1_justif: The National Artificial Intelligence Strategy (NAIS) addresses reskilling and upskilling as key risk mitigation measures under Pillar 3: Accelerating AI Adoption and Sector Transformation (pp. 38 - 43) and in the Introduction. It focuses on creating a digitally competent workforce to reduce job displacement (Risk Matrix pp. 53 - 55). Through Objective 3.3: Cultivate an AI-Ready Workforce (p. 41) the strategy mandates launching a comprehensive AI skills development programme including teacher training and leveraging initiatives like the 3 Million Technical Talent (3MTT) programme aiming to equip 70% of youth with AI-related skills (Box: Talent and Youth p. 35).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: National Centre for AI and Robotics (NCAIR) and the AI Governance Regulatory Body (Pillar 5 Objective 512 pp 4546) However in practice the National Information Technology Development Agency (NITDA) working through the National Centre for AI and Robotics (NCAIR) is currently coordinating activities aligned with the Strategy (see ecosystem/delivery actors on p 16 and the high-level Roadmap for Implementation on p 59)

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2025 (Nigeria)
fr1_justif: The NAIS addresses AI safety under Pillar 4 (Ensuring Responsible and Ethical AI Development pp.44 - 45) and Pillar 5 (Developing a Robust AI Governance Framework pp.46–47). It mandates a National AI Risk Management Framework to identify and mitigate safety and security risks (p.47) and proposes an AI Governance Regulatory Body to oversee compliance (p.46). It integrates cybersecurity measures specific to AI systems (p.20) and references the NIST AI Risk Management Framework for trusted AI systems (p.49).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: National Centre for AI and Robotics (NCAIR) and the AI Governance Regulatory Body (Pillar 5 Objective 512 pp 4546) However in practice the National Information Technology Development Agency (NITDA) working through the National Centre for AI and Robotics (NCAIR) is currently coordinating activities aligned with the Strategy (see ecosystem/delivery actors on p 16 and the high-level Roadmap for Implementation on p 59)

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2025 (Nigeria)
fr1_justif: The Strategy links transparency to trust and accountability: it connects the Nigerian Data Protection Act (NDPA) transparency requirements with “transparency and trust” and building public confidence (p.20). It treats transparency as AI explainability noting most AI cannot explain the “how” behind outputs making accountability for harmful decisions harder (pp.26 - 27). It also highlights a policy trade-off-trust via transparency vs “system vulnerability” to attacks (p.26). Transparency is embedded as a guiding principle (p.28) and to be operationalised through a national AI ethics framework/principles and assessment tools (p.44).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: ICT Development Strategy: SMART/MK 2030 (North Macedonia)
fr1_justif: On page 33 the document focuses on priority area on implementing education and training programs for ICT skills in the educational process (of teachers and students) citizens administrative officials and potential ICT professionals. The aim is to minimize the digital divide and support the digital transformation by fostering an entrepreneurial and innovative society with a high level of employment and productivity.In accordance with the objective measures are envisaged for the development of digital competencies aimed at:preschool and school-age childrenhigh school students; students of first second and third cycle study programs according to higher education regulations; educators and researchers; active  active adults pensioners .
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Law on Personal Data Protection (North Macedonia)
fr1_justif: (p.19) Section 3 starting from article 39 mandates that 'Where a type of processing in particular using new technologies (here implies to AI) and taking into account the nature scope context and purposes of the processing is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons the controller shall prior to the processing carry out an assessment of the impact of the envisaged processing operations on the protection of personal data. (a): a systematic and extensive evaluation of personal aspects relating to natural persons which is based on automated processing including profiling and on which decisions are based that produce legal effects concerning the natural person or similarly significantly affect the
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Article 27 (Restrictions p.14) allows for certain restrictions of data subject rights and controller obligations for reasons including national security and defence but does not exclude these sectors from the law’s scope. Instead it permits the applicable law to restrict rights and obligations “where necessary and proportionate”.

fr1_title: ICT Development Strategy: SMART/MK 2030 (North Macedonia)
fr1_justif: The document addresses public sector skills development by strengthening digital competencies among government employees and institutions. Measure M 2.2.4 focuses on improving access to digital skills training for the public administration including the development of training programs promoting gender equality in technology (A 2.2.4.1) and the integration of these trainings into the Ministry of Digital Transformation’s Learning Management System (A 2.2.4.2). In addition Measure M 2.2.5 supports capacity building within the public sector by introducing “Digital Ambassadors” in public institutions to lead digital transformation (A 2.2.5.1).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Operational Plan for Active Employment Programs Measures and Labor Market Services for 2025 (North Macedonia)
fr1_justif: The document addresses reskilling and upskilling by ?ffering a wide range of digital and AI-focused training programs and Ensuring official certification and practical experience. Pages 45-54 specifically mention the AI trainings and courses offered as eligible for government funding.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: SMART/MK: Digital Macedonia 2030
fr1_justif: It is a strategic document with action plans that identifies the weaknesses of missing AI regulation with focus on transparency privacy protection and ethical use. Concretely the Action Plan (Section 4.3.1 pp. 110) has a concrete measure for developing a legal and institutional framework for safe and ethical AI including: Drafting a national AI strategy Developing legal regulation for ethical AI aligned with the EU AI Act Establishing working groups for ethical oversight and risk analysis Creating a national AI ethics code Establishing a public register for AI projects (including legal basis).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The leading body is the National Council for Digital Transformation and Cybersecurity

fr1_title: National Strategy for Artificial Intelligence (Norway)
fr1_justif: Chapter 3.2 addresses AI skills in general and the first part of it points out that AI and digital literacy needs to 'be given more prominence already at primary and lower secondary school level'. programming and technology understanding would be incorporated better in education in Norway to increase AI literacy in population in the long run. It also further addresses opportunities to increase competence in older population already in the job market inviting further education and skill improvement (see Chapter 3.2 'Courses and further education programmes'). P.7 shortly addresses: 'Institutions of higher education ought to evaluate how topics with relevance to artificial intelligence can be integrated into their programmes...'
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Page 8: 'It does not cover the defence sector.'

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Bots fake accounts and automated conduct are mechanisms of disinformation campaigns (recital 85) and as such rules are needed to combat them (recital 9). Systemic risk assessments apply to large-scale online platforms and search engines (art. 34) and explicitly include the dissemination or amplification of misleading or fraudulent content and the negative effects on gender-based violence. This includes recommendation systems and other relevant algorithmic systems (art. 34). Mitigation measures include testing and adapting algorithmic and recommendation systems; speeding up the processing of notifications of illegal hate speech and cyber violence; and labeling content generated or manipulated by AI (art.35).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU GDPR
fr1_justif: The law provides a layered redress framework that applies in AI contexts whenever personal data is processed. Data subjects harmed by automated processing based on AI-driven profiling and decision-making hold the right to express their point of view and the right to contest the decision (art. 22); presenting complaints to supervisory authorities (art. 77) resorting to legal measures against gaps in the authorities (art. 78) and bringing proceedings directly against controllers or processors (art. 79). Organizations may be mandated to exercise the rights collectively on behalf of data subjects (art. 80). Supervisory authorities may impose administrative fines because of the violations of core provisions (art. 83).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: This Regulation shall not apply to the processing of personal data by Member States in the context of carrying out activities relating to the Union's common foreign and security policy (recital 16). The processing of personal data by Member States does not apply to activities related to the protection and prevention of threats to public security (Art. 2).
fr1_body_name: The law establishes the European Data Protection Board is comprised of representatives from the supervisory authority of each member states (Arts 68) acting independently (Art 69) providing guidelines recommendations and binding decisions to ensure consistence across Member States (Art 70) The Member State must to establish am independent supervisory authority responsible for monitoring and enforcing the regulation (Art 51)

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Online platform service providers must ensure the privacy security and protection of minors in their services (Art. 28) including prohibiting advertising based on profiling through the automated processing of data about children (Art. 28). Terms and conditions must be understandable to minors (Art. 14) ensuring a high level of privacy and security by default based on good practices (recitals 70 71). Large-scale online platforms and large-scale search engines systematic risk assessment applies periodically evaluating potential negative effects on minors (Art. 34). Mitigation measures are determined: age verification parental control tools abuse reporting tools and support for minors (Art. 35). Voluntary standards apply (Art. 44).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Strategy for Artificial Intelligence (Norway)
fr1_justif: Introduction brings up language as an important aspect to consider when developing AI with the main point to strengthen the representation of Norwegian Sami and dialects that are used in Norway within language models: 'To enable Norwegian citizens to participate in increasingly advanced services in their own language we need good language resources in both Norwegian language forms and in Sami (p.6). The Government will facilitate the collection of and access to language resources.' Chapter 2.2 is fully dedicated to elaborating on these points and outlining already existing initiatives and collaborations as well as charting further steps that need to be taken by the government or other organizations.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Page 8: 'It does not cover the defence sector.'

fr1_title: The Digital Norway of the Future National Digitalisation Strategy 2024–2030
fr1_justif: Chapter 3.2.1.3 Data centres (page 35) explains how artificial intelligence has a great potential to promote sustainability and contribute to green transition while at the same time this technology and data centers need to be treated as a potential threat to the environment and attention needs to be paid to the efficiency and sustainability of this industry. The Government will emphasise the development and use of the most climate-friendly AI possible and support research into climate-friendly AI models through the Research Council of Norway (p.74).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Digitalisation and Public Governance (DFD)

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The law focuses on the risk classification system (Art. 6) and the requirements imposed on high-risk AI systems. It requires suppliers of high-risk AI systems to establish implement document and maintain a risk (and damage) management system throughout the system's lifecycle (Art. 9). Training validation and test datasets must conform to data governance in terms of representation error-freeness completeness contextualization and the function the system will be used for handling special personal data for monitoring purposes and detecting and correcting biases. High-risk AI systems must have consistency throughout the lifecycle by requiring appropriate levels of accuracy and robustness and reducing the risk of bias (art.15).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act addresses gender equality through non-discrimination and fundamental rights provisions. Training datasets for high-risk AI must be representative avoinding the discriminatory outcomes and protecting atributes explicitly related gender (Art. 10). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights impact assessments before deploying high-risk AI incluing gender-based discrimination (Art. 27). Equality bodies are granted market surveillance powers enabling them to investigate and sanction AI systems producing discriminatory outcomes (Art. 74).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act establishes requirements to ensure human control over high-risk AI systems requiring providers to design systems that enable natural persons with a minimum level of comprehensibility to monitor outputs detect anomalies and intervene or interrupt operations when necessary (art. 14). Deployers must assign oversight to competent natural persons with authority to act (art. 26). For general-purpose AI with systemic risk adversarial testing and incident reporting reinforce human control (Arts. 55 73). Additional oversight provisions are set in the fundamental rights impact assessment (art. 27) and Annex (IV) 2(e) as part of technical documentation requirements (art. 11).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Law establishes an assessment architecture according to the type and level of risk of the system. High-risk AI suppliers must identify analyze and mitigate risks during the design development and implementation phases within a risk management system (Arti.9) with comprehensive technical documentation (Art.15) and monitoring of operations and anomalies (Art. 26). For AI implementers in the public sector a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment applies before implementing high-risk systems listed in Annex 3 (Art.27). Compliance assessment must include self-assessment or third-party audits depending on the criticality of the system (Art. 43 47). There are ex-post assessments and post-commercialization monitoring (Art.72).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act classifies systems for recruitment promotion termination and worker management as high-risk AI systems (Annex III(4)) (art. 6). Prohibited AI practices include bans on emotion-recognition in the workplace (art. 5). Providers must implement risk-management addressing discrimination (Art. 9) ensure representative datasets and bias-control (art. 10) and maintain technical documentation (Arts. 11). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights assessments before deployment (art. 27). Art. 26 (7-9) establishes safeguards: workers must be informed and may request explanations of AI use. However the Act does not regulate pay conditions or compensation so it mitigates risks without constituting a full labour-rights regime.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Strategy for Artificial Intelligence (Norway)
fr1_justif: Chapter 2.3 states that the Law Commission is actively working on enabling the administrative services to use AI solutions efficiently but responsibly with transparency and accountability.They recommend 'that the administrative body must document the legal content of automated decision-making systems. Such information should be made public unless laws regulations or special considerations dictate otherwise.'(p.27).'The algorithm's judgement must be at least as sound and as trustworthy as the human discretion it replaces. To ensure this we need systems that are transparent and explainable[..] the Law Commission on the Archival Act points to the importance ofensuring that AI-driven processes and decisions are documented ..'
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Strategy for Artificial Intelligence (Norway)
fr1_justif: P.8: 'The public sector ought to actively explore opportunities in the market in connection with procurements and innovative public procurements should be used where appropriate. To facilitate innovative solutions the agencies ought to focus on their needs rather than on concrete products or services.' p.54: 'The possibility to share best practice and to cooperate on procurements and training measures may therefore be particularly important for the municipalities. Cooperation on dataalgorithms and competence in AI may also create possibilities to see interrelationships across sectors.' p.55 Subchapter 'Public-private partnerships'. the document underscores public-private collaboration data sharing and green and innovative procurement.

fr1_justif: Chapter3.5 of the document addresses challenges with lacking skills (AI including) outlining the steps that the government need to take to uphold the level of competence - p.57'Government agencies must develop competence related to the opportunities presented by digitalisation and AI.[...]the Government will prepare a separate strategy for digital competence in the public sector'.The same chapter mentions separately AI and digital competence for teachers and those who work in education. Chapter3.1 generally addresses digitalization and adoption of AI in public sector urging for better organization regulation and collaboration which 'play an important role in contributing to the development of skills the sharing of experience [...]'

fr1_justif: Introduction page 7: 'Technological development will lead to changes in the labour market and the pace of change is likely to accelerate. Opportunities for upskilling and reskilling – both in the workplace and in the form of study programmes – will therefore be increasingly important as applications of AI become more widespread in the labour market.' Expanded in Chapter 3.2explaining the necessities mechanisms and strategies in place and further steps/plans. The strategy defines it's both responsibility of the employer and employee (page 44):'Employers must ensure that their employees possess the necessary skills [...] Individual employees must also take responsibility for their own skills development in a labour market [...]'
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Safety and security is core to the Act. It establishes a risk-based framework for risk identification and mitigation (Art. 9) oversight measures (Art. 14) and robustness and accuracy requirements (Art. 15). To ensure ongoing safety the Act mandates technical documentation and record-keeping (Arts. 11–12) and in certain high risk situations a conformity assessment procedure prior to market placement (Art. 43). Post-market obligations in Arts. 72–73 require providers and deployers to monitor performance report serious incidents and take corrective action. For GPAI models posing systemic risks Arts. 55–56 impose additional safety and cybersecurity duties including model evaluation and risk mitigation following the Code of Practice.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Articles 11 12 and 13 establish several transparency and documentation obligations for AI developers and deployers. High-risk AI systems must include clear information on their capabilities limitations and intended purpose (Art. 13) and providers must maintain comprehensive technical documentation and automatic logging to ensure traceability (Arts. 11–12). These measures enable deployers to better understand the systems and authorities to audit or investigate when necessary. There are disclosure obligations that require notification when individuals interact with AI systems particularly chatbots and emotion recognition tools (Art. 50). Also for general-purpose AI in terms of model documentation and summary of training data (Art. 53).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Telecommunications Regulatory Authority: Decision 1152/2/3/2022/2 Issuing the Regulation Governing the Rights of Telecommunication Services Beneficiaries
fr1_justif: Article 38 explicitly requires licensees to impose restrictions on the collection processing storage sale and dissemination of children’s personal data when using information technology or artificial intelligence thereby directly addressing risks of data exploitation and misuse in AI-mediated environments when children interact with AI systems. This provision goes beyond general telecommunications regulation by embedding child-specific safeguards in the context of AI-related data practices ensuring that children’s interactions with AI systems are subject to heightened protection standards.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: The Public Policy for the Safe and Ethical Use of Artificial Intelligence Systems (April 2025) (Oman)
fr1_justif: The policy encourages for human-centered AI systems and to take into account peculiarity of society preservation of human dignity and Islamic principles in the deployment of AI systems emphasising the local cultural and ethical framework of the state. AI itself is defined in page 2 as encompassing fields focused on developing systems that perform cognitive functions such as understanding language placing linguistic performance under the policy's scope.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Ministry of Transport Communications and Information Technology

fr1_title: The Public Policy for the Safe and Ethical Use of Artificial Intelligence Systems (April 2025) (Oman)
fr1_justif: The policy defines fairness inclusivity and AI Ethics separately. Fairness ensures that AI systems are designed and used in a just and objective manner avoiding bias and discrimination in the outputs and decisions resulting from these systems across all user groups. (page 3). The policy recognises Fairness and Inclusivity as one of the ethical principles for developing AI systems which must be designed to include the principle of non-discrimination based on specific protected characteristics including race gender religion or any other personal characteristic. Developers are required to systematically check the algorithms for biases and causal factors that could lead to discrimination (page 7).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Ministry of Transport Communications and Information Technology

fr1_title: The Public Policy for the Safe and Ethical Use of Artificial Intelligence Systems (April 2025) (Oman)
fr1_justif: This policy explicitly includes principles of inclusivity and justice specifying that AI systems must be designed so that no bias occurs based gender or other personal characteristic. It also requires using diverse datasets in training that represent different societal groups (page 7).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Ministry of Transport Communications and Information Technology

fr1_title: The Public Policy for the Safe and Ethical Use of Artificial Intelligence Systems (April 2025) (Oman)
fr1_justif: The principle of humanity is defined in the policy and it aims to ensure that the AI system is designed and used in a way that includes respect for human intervention in decisions that may directly affect their lives or rights (page 3).  Page 4 on control requirements also stipulates providing effective and efficient mechanisms for human intervention in sensitive and influential decisions taken by AI systems ensuring the possibility of interpreting tracking etc
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Ministry of Transport Communications and Information Technology

fr1_title: The Public Policy for the Safe and Ethical Use of Artificial Intelligence Systems (April 2025) (Oman)
fr1_justif: AI system developers must conduct an ethical social and structural impact assessment for AI systems before their deployment and use and retain the results of this assessment in the system's documents (page 5).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Ministry of Transport Communications and Information Technology

fr1_title: Executive Program for Artificial Intelligence and Advanced Technologies
fr1_justif: The document explicitly addresses public procurement as a mechanism for AI adoption. Under Pillar 3 on “Accelerating AI Adoption in the main Sectors” the program states that Oman should support demand for artificial intelligence applications in the local market “through government procurements” (p. 18) which directly aligns with supporting the local AI developers via public procurements of AI solutions.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Program for Artificial Intelligence and Advanced Digital Technologies (Oman)
fr1_justif: The first and second pillars of this programme is promoting and adopting AI in the economic and development sectors and the localisation of AI technologies (page 27). In implementation of the second pillar the programme provides initiatives to be adopted like  re-skilling workers in jobs that will be reduced or eliminated due to the implementation of AI in government entities in addition to qualifying teachers to use new AI educational tools (page 30).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: The Public Policy for the Safe and Ethical Use of Artificial Intelligence Systems (April 2025) (Oman)
fr1_justif: The document addresses AI Safety and Security through mandates for continuous monitoring data protection and adherence to cybersecurity standards (pages 5 and 6). AI Systems Deployers must continuously monitor performance for errors implement strict controls to protect personal data and classify data sensitivity for appropriate protection including multi-layered encryption and compliance with local laws. Developers are required to conduct an ethical social and structural impact assessment before deployment (page 5) ensure systems are safe from breaches and do not cause any physical or psychological harm (page 7) and follow best practices for cybersecurity (page 6).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Ministry of Transport Communications and Information Technology

fr1_title: The Public Policy for the Safe and Ethical Use of Artificial Intelligence Systems (April 2025) (Oman)
fr1_justif: Several provisions require transparency and explainability mechanisms from developers: In section 'Second: Controls and Requirements for the Use of AI Systems' (5) developers are required to document the data used in building their models for verification and compliance purposes. in section 'Third: Controls and Requirements for Developing AI Systems' they are expected to document the data and techniques used in a clear and transparent manner for audit purposes (2) and in (4) transparency in tracking AI generated content; (5) document and publish the logic of decision making of AI systems.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Transport Communications and Information Technology

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy (Pakistan) 2025
fr1_justif: One of the key objectives of the Policy is to integrate AI education into national curricula and collaborate with federal and provincial educational boards to integrate AI curricula into primary secondary and higher education. For this the policy also mention launching of scholarship program on page 9 to offer 3000 scholarships annually in AI and interest-free education financing scheme to support 15000 students annually pursuing high-tech certifications training and degrees in AI. It also on page 12 focuses on ethical use of generative AI in academia.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy (Pakistan) 2025

On page 12 under the heading of '3.7 Generative AI' the AI Directorate and the CoE-AI are tasked to address the challenges of generative AI and clause a says 'a. AI Directorate shall provide regulatory guidelines to address the possible spread of disinformation data privacy breaches and fake news.'

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy (Pakistan) 2025

fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy (Pakistan) 2025
fr1_justif: The policy identifies that there is a gender gap overall and in AI those biases may continue to exist therefore it is important to recognize the problem and take steps to address it. On Page 9 under the section title '2.1.3 Skill Development of Marginalized Women and PWDs' in clause a. The CoE-AI to incorporate gender perspective to ensure that the gender equality is realized and AI systems do not have gender biases. On page 10 in clause f. female participation is encouraged in all stages of an AI system life cycle. Aim is to have a balanced gender participation in AI research in academia gender representation on digital and AI companies' top management and board positions.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy (Pakistan) 2025

There is also a statement about publishing of public sector AI systems in a public register and process of auditing AI decision-making. See page 11 in the section '3.2 Transparency and Human Oversight' clause a. says 'a. Ensure implementation of human oversight mechanisms for critical AI operations particularly in high-risk scenarios.'

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy (Pakistan) 2025

Mandate lifecycle evaluations and impact assessments of high-impact AI systems like advanced models to ensure compliance with evolving standards and address emerging risks promptly.'
