On page 9 there is a section on '2.1.1 Training & Upskilling Workforce' and the on page 10 under the heading '2.2 Public Sector Awareness and Readiness' the CoE-AI is tasked to train 'public servants on AI basics ethical use data governance and personal data protection.'
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy (Pakistan) 2025
fr1_justif: One of the objectives of the strategy is to 'develop specialized training programs to prepare a skilled workforce capable of driving AI innovation' (p.5). P.8: an objective is to 'launch a National AI Skill Development Program in collaboration with NAVTTC Provincial govts Ministry of Education and private sector organizations to train 200000 individuals annually in AI including AI ethics and allied technologies through hybrid learning mechanisms'.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy (Pakistan) 2025
fr1_justif: On page 5 under the heading of “Ethical Objectives” it is mentioned that  “b. To protect personal data privacy and security [...] in AI applications. On page 11 under the heading of “3.1 AI-Based Cybersecurity Solutions” it says “e. Mandate the avoidance addressing prevention and elimination of unwanted harms and vulnerabilities to attack throughout the life cycle of AI systems [...]” and 'Mandate the avoidance addressing prevention and elimination of unwanted harms and vulnerabilities to attack throughout the life cycle of AI systems to ensure human environmental and ecosystem safety and security.”.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy (Pakistan) 2025
fr1_justif: There is strong emphasis on transparency and human oversight in the policy and human oversight mechanisms is mandated to ensure transparency and disclosure of the use of AI systems. There is also a statement about publishing of public sector AI systems in a public register and process of auditing AI decision-making. There is section 3.2 on page 11 on transparency and human oversight which talks about the “implementation of human oversight mechanisms for critical AI operations” “mandates transparency and disclosure of the use of AI systems including through the publishing of public sector AI systems in a public register” and “implementing a framework for auditing AI decision-making as well as transparency and algorithmic accountability.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Bill of Law 413 of September 24 2025 regulates the use of artificial intelligence in the Republic of Panama
fr1_justif: According to Art. 40 the State via SENACYT and AIG shall promote AI development and automation to foster education science and cultural diversity while facilitating paperless processes within state entities. Complementing this Art. 39 mandates that SENACYT promote training campaigns on AI risks for students in public and private institutions.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Bill of Law 413 of September 24 2025 regulates the use of artificial intelligence in the Republic of Panama
fr1_justif: Articles 4 (3) 5 and 9 establishes that people affected by algorithmic decisions have access to adequate complaint and review mechanisms to challenge and obtain redress for possible damages.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Bill of Law 413 of September 24 2025 regulates the use of artificial intelligence in the Republic of Panama
fr1_link: This Bill is numbered prior the legislative process its articles and content are the same as Bill of Law 413 of September 24 2025: https://web.archive.org/web/20260224183425/https://panamabankingnews.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/AdL-339.-Marco-regulatorio-de-la-IA-en-Panama-1.pdf
fr1_justif: Art. 6.3 establishes that AI developers and users must respect and promote diversity actively including individuals regardless of race gender age or disability while honoring diverse beliefs and expressions. Furthermore Art. 40 mandates that the State through SENACYT and AIG promote AI development and automation to foster education science and cultural diversity.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Bill of Law 413 of September 24 2025 regulates the use of artificial intelligence in the Republic of Panama
fr1_justif: The document outlines measures to protect the environment when developing systems based on artificial intelligence technologies. Article 17: In the use and implementation of artificial intelligence systems thepublic or private institutions that develop them will lean toward the fight against climate change as part of their social and environmental responsibility. Article 6.4. Environmental and ecosystem prosperity: The creators and implementers of artificial intelligence will prevent technologies from affecting ecosystems living beings and the environment within the framework of the constitution and the law.They will strive to reduce their carbon footprint minimize climate change and environmental risk factors.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Bill of Law 413 of September 24 2025 regulates the use of artificial intelligence in the Republic of Panama
fr1_justif: This document establishes equality and non-discrimination as core AI principles. Art. 4.2 (Equity) mandates equal treatment and the avoidance of unfair biases while Art. 4.9 (Inclusion) prohibits discrimination against any group and requires a gender and sexual diversity perspective in AI development. Furthermore Art. 5 reinforces human dignity and fundamental rights and Art. 6 highlights values such as respect diversity and inclusion.These provisions create a robust ethical framework for inclusive AI.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Bill of Law 413 of September 24 2025 regulates the use of artificial intelligence in the Republic of Panama
fr1_justif: Art. 4 states the Principle of Equity: 'Artificial intelligence must be developed and used in a manner that promotes equal treatment and opportunities for all people avoiding discrimination and unfair biases'. Art. 6 (3) mentions that the creators and implementers of artificial intelligence shall guarantee respect protection and promotion of diversity and active inclusion of all groups and individuals regardless of their gender among others conditions. Article 35 establish that the use of artificial intelligence shall be subject to rules that guarantee respect for and protection of the human rights which includes a Non-discrimination provition (a).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Bill of Law 413 of September 24 2025 regulates the use of artificial intelligence in the Republic of Panama
fr1_justif: This law focuses on human dignity and rights establishing limits on AI use. Art. 4(5) mandates the prevalence of human intelligence ensuring AI decisions are subject to human review and the ethical responsibility of legal persons. Furthermore Art. 16 clarifies that legal liability lies with the institutions that develop or contract AI systems not the algorithms. Additionally Art. 27 guarantees ARCO rights (access rectification cancellation and opposition) regarding personal data used by AI.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Bill of Law 413 of September 24 2025 regulates the use of artificial intelligence in the Republic of Panama
fr1_justif: The article 10 establishes that the use and implement artificial intelligence systems must publish an assessment of the risks and impact that human and fundamental rights could suffer before deploying such systems. The Art. 20 establish the obligation of 'A prior' impact assessment thatshall analyze possible biases risks of discrimination transparency interpretability and other relevant factors to ensure compliance with the ethical principles established inthe law.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Bill of Law 413 of September 24 2025 regulates the use of artificial intelligence in the Republic of Panama
fr1_justif: The article 14 established that if public or private institutions eliminate jobs due to the implementation of artificial intelligence they must reassign affected employees to a position with equal or better conditions for at least six months after which any further action must follow existing labor laws. Article 35 states that the use of artificial intelligence shall be subject to rules that guarantee respect for and protection of the human rights which includes the Right to Work: 'Respect for labor rights in the context of artificial intelligence shall be promoted. Discrimination shall be prohibited and the protection of workers' rights against artificial intelligence-driven automation shall be guaranteed'.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Bill of Law 413 of September 24 2025 regulates the use of artificial intelligence in the Republic of Panama
fr1_justif: Training or education and awareness-raising on human rights in the context of artificial intelligence shall be promoted (Art. 38 and 40). Also the article 38 establishes that the National Secretariat for Science Technology andInnovation (SENACYT) shall adopt measures to ensure that society is aware of the implications and risks to human and fundamental rights of the development use and implementation of artificial intelligence especially through the training or education of public servants on the subject.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Bill of Law 413 of September 24 2025 regulates the use of artificial intelligence in the Republic of Panama
fr1_justif: According to Article 35 (e) Right to Work: Respect for labor rights in the context of artificial intelligence will be promoted. Discrimination will be prohibited and the protection of workers' rights in the face of automation driven by artificial intelligence will be guaranteed including relocation training and fair compensation.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Bill of Law 413 of September 24 2025 regulates the use of artificial intelligence in the Republic of Panama
fr1_justif: Art. 4. (7) establishes that AI systems must minimize safety risks and ensure information confidentiality. Art. 18 mandates a system registry with the competent authority detailing purposes algorithms and security measures. Furthermore Art. 26 requires specific measures for data privacy including informed consent and anonymity. Lastly Art. 34. (3) links network security to AI particularly for detecting cyber threats.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Bill of Law 413 of September 24 2025 regulates the use of artificial intelligence in the Republic of Panama
fr1_justif: The framework mandates that AI systems be clear traceable and explainable (Art. 4.11). Users must provide informed consent (Art. 11) regarding risks to fundamental rights and data. Art. 12 requires informing subjects when they interact with AI. Furthermore developers must register systems (Art. 18) including algorithms and security measures. A prior impact assessment (Art. 20) is mandatory for high-risk systems to analyze biases and discrimination. Art. 23 & 35(c) establish the right to receive clear explanations about automated decisions affecting citizens ensuring transparency and interpretability in accordance with the Constitution and international treaties.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Law that typifies and punishes the creation dissemination and use of deepfakes and manipulated content
fr1_justif: The draft law addresses AI-facilitated misinformation by criminalising the creation and distribution of deepfakes and digitally manipulated synthetic content. The motive of the law describes risks such as reputational harm deception sexualised non-consensual content and threats to public order caused by AI-generated media. Article 4 defines deepfakes and manipulated content while Articles 5–8 establish penalties for producing sharing or using AI-generated falsified images audio or video to harm or mislead individuals.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Draft Bill 'which Regulates the Development Implementation and Responsible Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Systems' (Paraguay)
fr1_justif: Chapter II establishes enforceable rights for persons affected by AI systems including the right to information and explanation (Arts. 5 7 8) the right to challenge AI decisions with legal/significant effects and correct underlying data (Art. 9) and the right to request human intervention or review (Art. 10). Art. 6 allows enforcement before competent administrative bodies and courts (individual or collective). Chapter V establishes liability and full reparation for AI-caused harm including objective liability for high-risk/excessive-risk systems (Arts. 27–29).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Draft Bill 'which Regulates the Development Implementation and Responsible Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Systems' (Paraguay)
fr1_justif: The Draft Law explicitly includes the right to non-discrimination and the right to the correction of discriminatory biases in AI systems (Art. 5). It also refers to transparency explainability and human oversight ensuring that AI systems operate fairly and ethically. These principles align with the UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of AI (2021) the MERCOSUR Declaration on Human Rights in AI (2024) and the Paraguayan Constitution which guarantees the right to equality and non-discrimination.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Draft Bill 'which Regulates the Development Implementation and Responsible Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Systems' (Paraguay)
fr1_justif: The draft law addresses human oversight and determination through some provisions: In 3(c) it establishes human participation in the AI lifecycle and effective supervision. In 5(d) it recognises the right to human participation and determination in AI-based decisions affecting individuals. In 7 (d) & 8 (e) concern information and explanation rights requiring disclosure of the role of AI and humans in decision-making but do not themselves impose oversight obligations. In 9–11 require human review and final human determination where AI decisions produce significant irreversible or legally relevant effects. Finally arts 20–21 impose concrete oversight documentation and override obligations only for high-risk AI systems.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Draft Bill 'which Regulates the Development Implementation and Responsible Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Systems' (Paraguay)
fr1_justif: The draft introduces algorithmic impact assessments as a mandatory obligation for high-risk and excessive-risk AI systems (art. 13 & 22). It requires developers and deployers to conduct ex-ante assessments identifying potential risks to fundamental rights safety and public interests including risks related to data quality bias discrimination and misuse (Chapter IV). These assessments must document the intended purpose data sources risk mitigation measures and residual risks and be updated through continuous monitoring during deployment. The bill also links impact assessments to transparency auditability and human oversight obligations (Arts. 17–26) establishing a preventive risk-based approach to AI governance.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Draft Bill 'which Regulates the Development Implementation and Responsible Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Systems' (Paraguay)
fr1_justif: Although the draft framework could not be considered as thorough regarding this indicator Article 17 (c) includes among High-Risk AI those systems that could be use within the employment sector to hire recruit select filter and evaluate candidates. It also includes AI-powered systems to manage employees and access to employment. As a consequence of this classification such systems are subject to the mandatory obligations applicable to high-risk AI systems established in Articles 19 onwards.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Draft Bill 'which Regulates the Development Implementation and Responsible Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Systems' (Paraguay)
fr1_justif: The draft addresses AI safety and security through a risk-based regulatory approach. It classifies AI systems into prohibited high-risk & excessive-risk categories & establishes differentiated obligations accordingly (See chapter III; Arts. 14 to 18). For high-risk and excessive-risk AI systems it requires compliance with security accuracy robustness data quality human oversight monitoring & traceability obligations as well as the adoption of mitigation measures to prevent harmful or unsafe outcomes (Chapter IV; Arts. 19 to 26). Certain AI practices are prohibited due to unacceptable risks reinforcing preventive safety safeguards (See art. 14).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Draft Bill 'which Regulates the Development Implementation and Responsible Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Systems' (Paraguay)
fr1_justif: The draft law addresses transparency and explainability through multiple obligations. Article 1 and 2(h&j) includes transparency and explainability as a guiding principles. Articles 5(b) 7(abei) 8(albc)–11 (pp. 12–16) grant rights to prior information clear explanations of automated decisions human review and non-discrimination. Articles 19–21 (pp. 22–24) require transparency in AI deployment disclosure of system logic documentation public consultations and publication of evaluations. Article 35 (p. 29) mandates a public database of high-risk AI systems.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Supreme Decree 115-2025-PCM (Peru)
fr1_justif: he Law defines digital literacy as a continuous process of acquiring and developing essential digital skills for functioning in digital environments including for studying working accessing goods and services socializing and exercising citizenship (Art. 6). It further establishes digital literacy as a guiding principle for AI-based systems emphasizing the need to promote skills connected to the design development implementation and use of AI (Art. 7). Art. 13 also introduces the National Digital Talent Platform (coordinated by the SGTD) which explicitly includes the use of AI to develop student competencies under the National Basic Education Curriculum aiming to prepare students for the digital future by promoting equality and cl
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Yes Article 4 of the law expressly states that the provisions do not apply to the use of Artificial Intelligence-based systems for national defense and security.
fr1_body_name: Secretariat of Government and Digital Transformation (SGTD)

fr1_title: Supreme Decree 115-2025-PCM (Peru)
fr1_justif: The law establishes that there are “Misuses of AI” which are prohibited in all cases. One such misuse is when an AI-based system is used to: 'Deceptively or manipulatively influence people's decision-making especially when: using subliminal or deliberately deceptive techniques or exploiting vulnerabilities with the aim of affecting a person's ability to make conscious and informed decisions; it exploits the cognitive emotional or socioeconomic vulnerabilities of specific individuals or population groups in order to substantially modify their behavior; and it misleads or manipulates information through an AI-based system to affect people's decision-making autonomy.' (Article 23)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Yes Article 4 of the law expressly states that the provisions do not apply to the use of Artificial Intelligence-based systems for national defense and security.
fr1_body_name: Secretariat of Government and Digital Transformation (SGTD)

fr1_title: Supreme Decree 115-2025-PCM (Peru)
fr1_justif: Article 12 (Protection of Vulnerable Groups in the development and use of AI) establishes that all digital services supported by AI-based systems must fully consider the differentiated needs of children and adolescents. Article 24 (Use of high-risk AI-based systems) then establishes that AI-based systems shall be considered “high risk” when these systems perform assessments of children and adolescents in the education sector. It should be noted that the regulation establishes specific obligations for high-risk systems.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Article 4 (Exceptions to the scope of application) explicitly states that the regulation does not apply to the use of AI-based systems for defense and national security.
fr1_body_name: Secretariat of Government and Digital Transformation (SGTD)

fr1_title: Supreme Decree 115-2025-PCM (Peru)
fr1_justif: Article 7 of the law establishes various guiding principles one of which is “Sustainability.” This principle is defined as the obligation that 'in the development implementation and use phases of an AI-based system its human social economic cultural and environmental impacts are comprehensively analyzed and evaluated (...) Likewise the adoption of sustainable practices that minimize energy and resource consumption is promoted fostering environmentally responsible technological solutions as well as their components and committed to sustainable development.' Article 27 also mentions 'c) Consider the social and environmental impact of the development of AI-based systems.'
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Yes Article 4 ('Exceptions to the scope of application') of the law expressly states that the provisions do not apply to the use of Artificial Intelligence-based systems for national defense and security.
fr1_body_name: Secretariat of Government and Digital Transformation (SGTD)

fr1_title: Supreme Decree 115-2025-PCM (Peru)
fr1_justif: The law establishes a series of guiding principles for the development and use of AI-based systems (Article 7). One of these principles is “Non-discrimination” which it defines as the obligation that “measures are implemented to prevent mitigate and correct the generation reinforcement or perpetuation of discriminatory or biased results.”
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Article 4 ('Exceptions to the scope of application') specifically states that the provisions of the law do not apply “to the use of Artificial Intelligence-based systems for national defense and security.”
fr1_body_name: Secretariat of Government and Digital Transformation (SGTD)

fr1_title: Supreme Decree 115-2025-PCM (Peru)
fr1_justif: Article 12 (Protection of Vulnerable Groups in the development and use of AI) establish that all digital services supported by AI-based systems must fully consider the differentiated needs of women and girls. Also in article 14 (Awareness and commitment campaigns for citizens) it is mentioned the possibility that the National Authority on AI support the creation of spaces for citizen reflection to prevent the misuse of AI-based systems including violence against women.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Article 4 (Exceptions to the scope of application) establish that the provisions of this regulation do not apply to the use of AI-based systems for national defense and security.
fr1_body_name: Secretariat of Government and Digital Transformation (SGTD)

fr1_title: Supreme Decree 115-2025-PCM (Peru)
fr1_justif: Article 7 of the law establishes various guiding principles one of which is “Human Oversight.” This principle is defined as the obligation that 'AI-based systems must be subject to human oversight ensuring that their decisions are ethical fair and respectful of fundamental rights as well as guaranteeing the quality of the data used to ensure their accuracy integrity and representativeness. Human intervention is necessary to correct modify or stop the operation of these systems in order to prevent abuse strengthen transparency and accountability and safeguard collective well-being in compliance with current legal ethical and moral principles.'
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Article 4 ('Exceptions to the scope of application') of the law expressly states that the provisions do not apply to the use of Artificial Intelligence-based systems for national defense and security.
fr1_body_name: Secretariat of Government and Digital Transformation (SGTD)

fr1_title: Supreme Decree 115-2025-PCM (Peru)
fr1_justif: The law establishes that in certain cases the development or use of AI-based systems may be considered to constitute the following: “Misuse” and “High-risk use.” In the first case the law prohibits the development or use of this type of AI (Articles 22 and 23). In the second case it creates specific obligations one of which is to conduct a prior “impact assessment” in order to identify potential risks to individuals and mitigate them (Articles 30 and 32).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Yes Article 4 of the law expressly states that the provisions do not apply to the use of artificial intelligence-based systems for national defense and security. Therefore even with regard to high-risk uses impact assessment will not be mandatory.
fr1_body_name: Secretariat of Government and Digital Transformation (SGTD)

fr1_title: Supreme Decree 115-2025-PCM (Peru)
fr1_justif: Article 24 of the law establishes the category of “use of high-risk AI-based systems. These situations are specifically described and entail special obligations such as the need to carry out prior human rights impact assessments. One of the uses considered high risk is when AI is used to “determine selection evaluation hiring and termination processes for workers or job applicants as well as to establish working conditions.” It is also considered high risk when used to infer “the emotions of a natural person in work environments.”
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Yes Article 4 of the law expressly states that the provisions do not apply to the use of Artificial Intelligence-based systems for national defense and security.
fr1_body_name: Secretariat of Government and Digital Transformation (SGTD)

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Supreme Decree 115-2025-PCM (Peru)
fr1_justif: Article 6 of the Law defines certain concepts including 'digital literacy' which it defines as the 'acquisition and development of essential digital skills for interacting in digital environments on a permanent basis necessary for studying working processing goods and services socializing and in general functioning in daily life.' Later Article 13 develops an entire section on capacity building including for public officials.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Yes Article 4 of the law expressly states that the provisions do not apply to the use of Artificial Intelligence-based systems for national defense and security.
fr1_body_name: Secretariat of Government and Digital Transformation (SGTD)

fr1_title: Supreme Decree 115-2025-PCM (Peru)
fr1_justif: The law has a section dedicated to establishing obligations for public entities regarding 'AI knowledge and skills training.' Some of these obligations are relatively generic such as the obligation to 'promote the identification and provision of courses programs workshops and other training modalities to build AI capabilities through the National Digital Talent Platform.' More specific is the obligation for the National Authority to coordinate the implementation of study programs aimed at developing AI competencies especially in STEM-related programs (Article 13).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Yes Article 4 of the law expressly states that the provisions do not apply to the use of Artificial Intelligence-based systems for national defense and security.
fr1_body_name: Secretariat of Government and Digital Transformation (SGTD)

fr1_title: Supreme Decree 115-2025-PCM (Peru)
fr1_justif: Article 7 of the law establishes various guiding principles being two of them: 'Security proportionality and reliability' and 'Risk-based security standards'. These principles are subsequently developed in greater detail including obligations related to security in the development and use of AI-based systems.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Article 4 of the law expressly states that the provisions do not apply to the use of Artificial Intelligence-based systems for national defense and security.
fr1_body_name: Secretariat of Government and Digital Transformation (SGTD)

fr1_title: Supreme Decree 115-2025-PCM (Peru)
fr1_justif: AI-based systems must be clear explainable and accessible ensuring that processes algorithms and decisions are understandable to users implementers and authorities. This includes providing information about their operation the data used decision criteria and potential biases promoting accountability and public trust.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Yes Article 4 of the law ('Exceptions to the scope of application') expressly states that the provisions do not apply to the use of Artificial Intelligence-based systems for national defense and security.
fr1_body_name: Secretariat of Government and Digital Transformation (SGTD)

fr1_title: House Bill No. 1699 or the 'National Digital Skills and AI Readiness Act' (Philippines)
fr1_justif: 'Sec. 5 of HB 1699 states: “Digital Curriculum Reform in Higher Education - The Commission on Higher Education (CHED) shall revise the existing educational curricula in tertiary education by requiring SUCs to offer at least one Al-related subject across non-tech courses including but not limited to Business Education and Agriculture as may be determined by the CHED.” Sec. 6 states: “The Technical Education and Skills Development Authority (TESDA) shall establish Future Skills Centers to offer free training programs in digital coding digital marketing data science cloud computing and AI tools as well as government-subsidized certification programs on global technology platforms...'' (p. 4)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: House Bill No. 3823 or the 'Deepfake Accountability and Transparency Act' (Philippines)
fr1_justif: Sec. 3 of HB 3823 defines (b) Harmful Deepfake as 'a deepfake created or distributed with the intent to deceive defraud or harm an individual or the public.' Sec. 4 makes public disclosure of deepfakes mandatory. Sec 5 states it is 'unlawful to: (a) create or distribute a harmful deepfake without proper disclosure; (b) remove alter or tamper with required disclosures; (c) use deepfake technology to defame deceive commit fraud or incite violence; and (d) obtain consent through coercion deception or misrepresentation for creating a deepfake.' (pp. 3-4)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: House Bill No. 13 or the 'Artificial Intelligence Development and Regulation Act' (Philippines)
fr1_justif: The draft bill directly addresses AI governance and related accountability and redress mechanisms (p.5 Right to remedy): establsihes a right to remedy including the right to opt out of AI systems to obtain timely human review and to appeal and contest AI-driven decisions.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: House Bill No. 807 or the 'Take It Down Act of 2025' (Philippines)
fr1_justif: Sec. 2 of HB 807 provides that it 'is the policy of the State to protect individuals especially women and children from the harms arising from the non-consensual creation possession and dissemination of sexually explicit materials including those produced through artificial intelligence deepfake or any other digital manipulation.' Sec. 3 defines a 'take down request' as a formal verified notification submitted by an affected individual or in the case of minors... seeking removal or disabling of access to content that is alleged to be non-consensual sexually explicit or otherwise prohibited under this Act.'
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: House Bill No. 57 or the 'National Artificial Intelligence Code of the Philippines'
fr1_justif: Sec. 14 of HB 57 promotes Fairness and Non-Discrimination where AI systems shall not result in unjust or disproportionate discrimination based on race... ethnicity religion... (p. 7). Sec. 39 provides support for domestic AI model development and the State's promotion for locally built AI systems and language models particularly those tailored to Philippine languages cultural contexts and institutional needs (p. 14). Sec. 72 tackles the functions of the National Panel of Experts on AI which includes conducting 'emerging risk foresight by identifying analyzing and publicly reporting on speculative or long-term risks associated with AI including potential societal economic and cultural disruptions (p. 20).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Senate Bill No. 25 – Artificial Intelligence Regulation Act (AIRA) (Philippines)
fr1_justif: Sec. 3 (h) of SB No. 25 provides as one of its objectives  'Ensure that policies of both the national and local governments on AI are driven by futures thinking strategic foresight and proactive risk management and are governed by principles of transparency accountability and sustainability.' (p. 2) Sec. 9 (j) states that the National AI Commission shall 'prioritize AI systems that promote the use of AI in agriculture health 31 education disaster risk reduction governance environmental sustainability renewable energy agriculture and biodiversity and other sectors. (p. 6); (k) Issue advisory opinions on AI systems that pose unacceptable risks to human rights... or environmental sustainability. (p. 7)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Privacy Commission Advisory No. 2024-04 or the Guidelines on the Application of Republic Act No. 10173 or the Data Privacy Act of 2012 (DPA) Its Implementing Rules and Regulations and the Issuances of the Commission to Artificial Intelligence Systems Processing Personal Data
fr1_justif: Section 2 provides that Personal Information Controllers (PICs) ensure that personal data is processed in a manner that is neither manipulative nor unduly oppressive to data subjects... PICs shall implement mechanisms to identify and monitor biases in the AI systems and to limit such biases and their impact on the data subjects. PICs must consider systemic bias human bias and statistical bias when assessing or auditing their AI systems... PICs shall not utilize AI Washing... PICs must maintain the accuracy of personal data to ensure the fairness of the output of the AI systems. PICs shall implement appropriate measures to ensure that personal data utilized in the AI systems is correct and kept up to date. (p. 3)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Updated Gender Equality and Women’s Empowerment (GEWE) Plan 2019-2025 (Philippines)
fr1_justif: The Plan talks about how due to 'restricted access to education and training structural barriers and social norms' 'low-skilled and routine manufacturing jobs are predominantly held by women.' (p. 34) The plan discusses how to 'proactively prepare to address the potential job losses” caused by AI and other emerging technologies.” (p. 24) The Plan thinks it is 'imperative that policy responses and investments are appropriately targeted both accelerating technological trends as well as deeply-rooted management practices in global value chains to avoid further deepening of the existing inequalities between men and women in the production sectors of the economy.' (p. 34)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr2_title: House Bill 02323 or the 'Artificial Intelligence Act' of 2025 (Philipines)
fr2_justif: The bill provides that it shall be the policy of the State to 'incorporate a gender-sensitive pro-children and pro-poor perspective' in all AI efforts plans and programs.' (p. 5) Sec. 12 provides for the 'identification of differential impact of AI on local sectors and distinct populations including the labor sector women...' (p. 12) Sec. 13 mandates government institutions 'related to the welfare of women children... to examine current biases and discriminatory practices in AI technologies and work the council to create troubleshooting strategies to address these discriminatory practices.' (p. 14)
fr2_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: House Bill No. 57 or the 'National Artificial Intelligence Code of the Philippines'
fr1_justif: Sec. 4.j. 'Human oversight' means the presence of operational procedures and technical mechanisms allowing humans to understand intervene in suspend or override an AI system's behavior where necessary for safety legality or accountability. (p. 2). Sec. 5.a. says 'every individual shall have the right to meaningful human oversight in decisions that affect their rights freedoms opportunities or safety. No AI system shall have final authority over such decisions without a mechanism for human review correction or override. (p. 4) Sec. 8 The Bureau of Artificial Intelligence Systems shall... (b) The Division on Ethical Risk and Human Oversight responsible for enforcing ethical safeguards and human fallback mechanisms... (p. 6).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: House Bill No. 13 or the 'Artificial Intelligence Development and Regulation Act' (Philippines)
fr1_justif: Sec 5 of HB 13 provides that the development application and use of AI systems shall be guided by certain rights of every Filipino including the Right Against Algorithmic Discrimination. To protect equal rights it is required to institute 'proactive equity assessments as part of the system design... pre-deployment and ongoing disparity testing and mitigation and clear organizational oversight.' 'Independent evaluation and plain language reporting in the form of an algorithmic impact assessment including disparity testing results and mitigation information shall be performed and made public whenever possible to confirm these protections' (p.  4)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: House Bill No. 9448 or the “Protection of Labor Against AI Automation Act”
fr1_justif: Sec. 5 (a) prohibits employers and recruitment entities from using AI as their sole or primary basis in the hiring and termination of employees. Human evaluation discretion and judgment shall always be a significant factor or part in the process. Any use of Al shall be directly supervised and complemented by human oversight. Sec. 5(b) prohibits the use of AI to replace human workers resulting in displacement loss of security of tenure diminution of existing salaries or benefits or unemployment' 'unless an equivalent alternative employment opportunity for affected human workers are available.” Sec. 6 allows the use of AI... in case of 'valid retrenchment' subject to Department of Labor approval.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: DBM-DICT-DEPDev Joint Resolution No. 2025-05 Prescribing the Implementing Rules and Guidelines on the Adoption of Standards-based Approach to Information System Strategic Plan Compliance (Philippines)
fr1_justif: Sec. 5 provides the Policy for Responsible and Acceptable Use of AI and Machine Learning (AIML) in Government which shall: (1) 'Establish clear guidelines and safeguards for the development training procurement deployment and evaluation of AIML systems in public sector operations; and (2) Uphold the principles of Fairness Ethics Accountability and Transparency (FEAT) throughout the AIML lifecycle' (p. 3) Annex E says the document 'provides a foundational framework for the responsible development procurement and management of Al systems and services by government agencies.' (p. 34)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Department of Information and Communications Technology (DICT)

fr1_title: Senate Bill No. 79 or the 'Artificial Intelligence (AI) Training for Government Workforce Act' (Philippines)
fr1_justif: Sec. 3 states that the bill aims to 'harness the potential of AI as a positive and innovative approach to help the government workforce in accomplishing tasks... (pp. 1-2) Sec. 5 provides for the establishment of an AI Training Program.” (p. 2) Sec. 7 outline the topics which include: (a) the science underlying AI; (b) introductory concepts on AI systems; (c) the ways in which AI can benefit the national government; (d) the risks posed by AI including discrimination and risks to privacy; (e) ways to mitigate the risks described in subparagraph (D) including efforts to create and identify AI that is reliable safe and trustworthy; and (f) future trends in AI including trends for national security and innovation.” (p. 3)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: House Bill No. 1699 or the 'National Digital Skills and AI Readiness Act' (Philippines)
fr1_justif: Sec. 3 states that the bill's objectives include to 'integrate digital skills and AI education across all levels of formal government' 'establish a future skills centers to provide free training and certification' and 'facilitate public-private partnerships in upskilling programs' (p. 3) Sec. 6 requires the Technical Education and Skills Development Authority (TESDA) to establish regional future skills centers to offer free training programs in digital coding digital marketing data science cloud computing and AI tools and government-subsidized certification programs. (p. 4)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Draft Department of Information and Communications Technology and Civil Service Commission Joint Memorandum Circular Series of 2024 'Principles and Guidelines for an Ethical and Trustworthy Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) In the Government' (Philippines)
fr1_justif: Sec. 5 (b) on Cyber Safety and Security requires protection of AI systems from cybersecurity threats and risks that cause physical and digital harm. It requires all AI systems to undergo pre-deployment testing risk identification and mitigation and monitoring and evaluation to demonstrate the safety and effectiveness based on the intended use mitigation of unsafe outcomes and adherence to internationally established standards. (pp. 4-5)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy Roadmap (NAISR) 2.0 (Philippines)
fr1_justif: The National AI Strategy Roadmap 2.0 (NAISR 2.0) promotes transparency as a key AI governance principle. It states that responsible AI deployment should include regulatory frameworks that promote “transparency and accountability” (p. 26). The roadmap also highlights stakeholder engagement including civil society advocating transparency and accountability (p. 28). Under the “AI Governance and Ethics” dimension transparency is listed among core principles for responsible AI (p. 30). Additionally the proposed National AI Governance Framework identifies “transparency of policies rules and regulations governing AI systems” as indispensable (p. 39).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Policy for Artificial Intelligence Development in Poland from 2020
fr1_justif: It addresses AI literacy through a multi-tiered approach targeting the general public the formal education system the existing workforce and specialized professionals. Through mass media campaigns to promote awareness of AI's societal impact and combat misinformation regarding the risks and opportunities of automation (p.26 point 3a) a key short-term objective is to educate consumers (users) of AI systems so they can understand how these systems operate (p.26 point 3e) introducing basic AI tools in primary education for developing 'computational thinking' algorithmic skills and creative problem-solving (p.52 point 1c).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_body_name: Council of Ministers and Committee for Digital Affairs

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Bots fake accounts and automated conduct are mechanisms of disinformation campaigns (recital 85) and as such rules are needed to combat them (recital 9). Systemic risk assessments apply to large-scale online platforms and search engines (art. 34) and explicitly include the dissemination or amplification of misleading or fraudulent content and the negative effects on gender-based violence. This includes recommendation systems and other relevant algorithmic systems (art. 34). Mitigation measures include testing and adapting algorithmic and recommendation systems; speeding up the processing of notifications of illegal hate speech and cyber violence; and labeling content generated or manipulated by AI (art.35).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU GDPR
fr1_justif: The law provides a layered redress framework that applies in AI contexts whenever personal data is processed. Data subjects harmed by automated processing based on AI-driven profiling and decision-making hold the right to express their point of view and the right to contest the decision (art. 22); presenting complaints to supervisory authorities (art. 77) resorting to legal measures against gaps in the authorities (art. 78) and bringing proceedings directly against controllers or processors (art. 79). Organizations may be mandated to exercise the rights collectively on behalf of data subjects (art. 80). Supervisory authorities may impose administrative fines because of the violations of core provisions (art. 83).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: This Regulation shall not apply to the processing of personal data by Member States in the context of carrying out activities relating to the Union's common foreign and security policy (recital 16). The processing of personal data by Member States does not apply to activities related to the protection and prevention of threats to public security (Art. 2).
fr1_body_name: The law establishes the European Data Protection Board is comprised of representatives from the supervisory authority of each member states (Arts 68) acting independently (Art 69) providing guidelines recommendations and binding decisions to ensure consistence across Member States (Art 70) The Member State must to establish am independent supervisory authority responsible for monitoring and enforcing the regulation (Art 51)

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Online platform service providers must ensure the privacy security and protection of minors in their services (Art. 28) including prohibiting advertising based on profiling through the automated processing of data about children (Art. 28). Terms and conditions must be understandable to minors (Art. 14) ensuring a high level of privacy and security by default based on good practices (recitals 70 71). Large-scale online platforms and large-scale search engines systematic risk assessment applies periodically evaluating potential negative effects on minors (Art. 34). Mitigation measures are determined: age verification parental control tools abuse reporting tools and support for minors (Art. 35). Voluntary standards apply (Art. 44).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Policy for Artificial Intelligence Development in Poland from 2020
fr1_justif: The Policy for the Development of Artificial Intelligence in Poland (2020) promotes AI projects tailored to Polish linguistic needs including machine processing of the Polish language and translation (p.39). It supports grants for Polish-language NLP and removing legal barriers to exploring Polish text corpora (p.39). The Policy also promotes open cultural data and digital repositories (p.27 p.32) and highlights AI’s role in access to cultural heritage (p.13). However it does not establish specific obligations regarding minority languages or multicultural representation.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Page 2 This AI Policy does not cover state activities in the area of ??national security and defense.
fr1_body_name: Council of Ministers and Committee for Digital Affairs

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The law focuses on the risk classification system (Art. 6) and the requirements imposed on high-risk AI systems. It requires suppliers of high-risk AI systems to establish implement document and maintain a risk (and damage) management system throughout the system's lifecycle (Art. 9). Training validation and test datasets must conform to data governance in terms of representation error-freeness completeness contextualization and the function the system will be used for handling special personal data for monitoring purposes and detecting and correcting biases. High-risk AI systems must have consistency throughout the lifecycle by requiring appropriate levels of accuracy and robustness and reducing the risk of bias (art.15).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act addresses gender equality through non-discrimination and fundamental rights provisions. Training datasets for high-risk AI must be representative avoinding the discriminatory outcomes and protecting atributes explicitly related gender (Art. 10). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights impact assessments before deploying high-risk AI incluing gender-based discrimination (Art. 27). Equality bodies are granted market surveillance powers enabling them to investigate and sanction AI systems producing discriminatory outcomes (Art. 74).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act establishes requirements to ensure human control over high-risk AI systems requiring providers to design systems that enable natural persons with a minimum level of comprehensibility to monitor outputs detect anomalies and intervene or interrupt operations when necessary (art. 14). Deployers must assign oversight to competent natural persons with authority to act (art. 26). For general-purpose AI with systemic risk adversarial testing and incident reporting reinforce human control (Arts. 55 73). Additional oversight provisions are set in the fundamental rights impact assessment (art. 27) and Annex (IV) 2(e) as part of technical documentation requirements (art. 11).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Law establishes an assessment architecture according to the type and level of risk of the system. High-risk AI suppliers must identify analyze and mitigate risks during the design development and implementation phases within a risk management system (Arti.9) with comprehensive technical documentation (Art.15) and monitoring of operations and anomalies (Art. 26). For AI implementers in the public sector a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment applies before implementing high-risk systems listed in Annex 3 (Art.27). Compliance assessment must include self-assessment or third-party audits depending on the criticality of the system (Art. 43 47). There are ex-post assessments and post-commercialization monitoring (Art.72).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act classifies systems for recruitment promotion termination and worker management as high-risk AI systems (Annex III(4)) (art. 6). Prohibited AI practices include bans on emotion-recognition in the workplace (art. 5). Providers must implement risk-management addressing discrimination (Art. 9) ensure representative datasets and bias-control (art. 10) and maintain technical documentation (Arts. 11). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights assessments before deployment (art. 27). Art. 26 (7-9) establishes safeguards: workers must be informed and may request explanations of AI use. However the Act does not regulate pay conditions or compensation so it mitigates risks without constituting a full labour-rights regime.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Policy for the Development of Artificial Intelligence in Poland
fr1_justif: The document contains a dedicated provision on “increasing the number of AI procurements in the public sector” explicitly targeting central and local government as well as state-owned entities (p. 71). It further outlines concrete measures to operationalize AI procurement including promoting innovative procurement modes beyond traditional tenders training public officials in procurement procedures for AI solutions developing best practice catalogues for contracting authorities conducting workshops on public procurement of AI-based systems and facilitating private sector participation in AI procurement markets (p. 71).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: 'This AI Policy does not cover state activities in the areaof national security and defence; however it assumesthe cooperation of the civilian sector with the militarysector in all areas considered useful for the needs ofnational defence in accordance with the priorities setout in the National Security Strategy of the Republic ofPoland approved by the order of the President of theRepublic of Poland from 12 May 2020 (Monitor Polskiitem 413).' (Introduction)
fr1_body_name: minister in charge of computerisation AI Policy Task Force

fr1_title: Policy for Artificial Intelligence Development in Poland from 2020
fr1_justif: In the medium term the policy aims to boost the competences and digital skills of public sector staffers. This explicitly includes promoting modern agile working methodologies and tools to modernize how the administration operates - p.72 point 1C. The policy recommends increasing the number of AI procurements in the public sector including central and local government as well as state-owned companies - p.71 point 8C.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_body_name: Council of Ministers and Committee for Digital Affairs

fr1_title: Policy for Artificial Intelligence Development in Poland from 2020
fr1_justif: The document addresses AI-related reskilling and upskilling by emphasising the need to prepare workers and society for labour market changes caused by automation. It calls for ensuring conditions for the upskilling of the general public to prevent technological unemployment (p.9) and for creating opportunities for people at risk of job loss due to AI to acquire new market-relevant skills (AI and Society section p.20). The policy also proposes education training and retraining programmes for workers in threatened professions and encourages developing modern technology skills through the Integrated Qualification System (p.28). Long-term goals promote lifelong learning and rapid reskilling (p.28).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Safety and security is core to the Act. It establishes a risk-based framework for risk identification and mitigation (Art. 9) oversight measures (Art. 14) and robustness and accuracy requirements (Art. 15). To ensure ongoing safety the Act mandates technical documentation and record-keeping (Arts. 11–12) and in certain high risk situations a conformity assessment procedure prior to market placement (Art. 43). Post-market obligations in Arts. 72–73 require providers and deployers to monitor performance report serious incidents and take corrective action. For GPAI models posing systemic risks Arts. 55–56 impose additional safety and cybersecurity duties including model evaluation and risk mitigation following the Code of Practice.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Articles 11 12 and 13 establish several transparency and documentation obligations for AI developers and deployers. High-risk AI systems must include clear information on their capabilities limitations and intended purpose (Art. 13) and providers must maintain comprehensive technical documentation and automatic logging to ensure traceability (Arts. 11–12). These measures enable deployers to better understand the systems and authorities to audit or investigate when necessary. There are disclosure obligations that require notification when individuals interact with AI systems particularly chatbots and emotion recognition tools (Art. 50). Also for general-purpose AI in terms of model documentation and summary of training data (Art. 53).

fr1_title: Portugal’s National Digital Strategy
fr1_justif: Portugal’s National Digital Strategy integrates AI literacy into education through explicit curriculum reforms and skill-building measures (Annex I 2.1 p. 6). Page 6 highlights that the Strategy aims to “increase the digital literacy of the population so that citizens can participate fully in the digital age” supported by an action that seeks to strengthen the population’s digital skills and promote a more inclusive society prepared for the challenges and opportunities of the digital era. To achieve this strategic actions will be developed that encompass digital inclusion basic literacy education professional reskilling and advanced training (p. 8).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU GDPR
fr1_justif: The law provides a layered redress framework that applies in AI contexts whenever personal data is processed. Data subjects harmed by automated processing based on AI-driven profiling and decision-making hold the right to express their point of view and the right to contest the decision (art. 22); presenting complaints to supervisory authorities (art. 77) resorting to legal measures against gaps in the authorities (art. 78) and bringing proceedings directly against controllers or processors (art. 79). Organizations may be mandated to exercise the rights collectively on behalf of data subjects (art. 80). Supervisory authorities may impose administrative fines because of the violations of core provisions (art. 83).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: This Regulation shall not apply to the processing of personal data by Member States in the context of carrying out activities relating to the Union's common foreign and security policy (recital 16). The processing of personal data by Member States does not apply to activities related to the protection and prevention of threats to public security (Art. 2).
fr1_body_name: The law establishes the European Data Protection Board is comprised of representatives from the supervisory authority of each member states (Arts 68) acting independently (Art 69) providing guidelines recommendations and binding decisions to ensure consistence across Member States (Art 70) The Member State must to establish am independent supervisory authority responsible for monitoring and enforcing the regulation (Art 51)

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Online platform service providers must ensure the privacy security and protection of minors in their services (Art. 28) including prohibiting advertising based on profiling through the automated processing of data about children (Art. 28). Terms and conditions must be understandable to minors (Art. 14) ensuring a high level of privacy and security by default based on good practices (recitals 70 71). Large-scale online platforms and large-scale search engines systematic risk assessment applies periodically evaluating potential negative effects on minors (Art. 34). Mitigation measures are determined: age verification parental control tools abuse reporting tools and support for minors (Art. 35). Voluntary standards apply (Art. 44).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Council of Ministers Resolution No. 201/2024 (Portugal)
fr1_justif: The Resolution No. 201/2024 addresses Cultural and Linguistic Diversity by establishing the national LLM AMÁLIA to preserve the Portuguese language and its varieties (Whereas) promote cultural historical and literary content and ensure linguistic and cultural representativity in AI systems (Annex 1 article 1 (b)).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The law focuses on the risk classification system (Art. 6) and the requirements imposed on high-risk AI systems. It requires suppliers of high-risk AI systems to establish implement document and maintain a risk (and damage) management system throughout the system's lifecycle (Art. 9). Training validation and test datasets must conform to data governance in terms of representation error-freeness completeness contextualization and the function the system will be used for handling special personal data for monitoring purposes and detecting and correcting biases. High-risk AI systems must have consistency throughout the lifecycle by requiring appropriate levels of accuracy and robustness and reducing the risk of bias (art.15).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act addresses gender equality through non-discrimination and fundamental rights provisions. Training datasets for high-risk AI must be representative avoinding the discriminatory outcomes and protecting atributes explicitly related gender (Art. 10). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights impact assessments before deploying high-risk AI incluing gender-based discrimination (Art. 27). Equality bodies are granted market surveillance powers enabling them to investigate and sanction AI systems producing discriminatory outcomes (Art. 74).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act establishes requirements to ensure human control over high-risk AI systems requiring providers to design systems that enable natural persons with a minimum level of comprehensibility to monitor outputs detect anomalies and intervene or interrupt operations when necessary (art. 14). Deployers must assign oversight to competent natural persons with authority to act (art. 26). For general-purpose AI with systemic risk adversarial testing and incident reporting reinforce human control (Arts. 55 73). Additional oversight provisions are set in the fundamental rights impact assessment (art. 27) and Annex (IV) 2(e) as part of technical documentation requirements (art. 11).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Law establishes an assessment architecture according to the type and level of risk of the system. High-risk AI suppliers must identify analyze and mitigate risks during the design development and implementation phases within a risk management system (Arti.9) with comprehensive technical documentation (Art.15) and monitoring of operations and anomalies (Art. 26). For AI implementers in the public sector a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment applies before implementing high-risk systems listed in Annex 3 (Art.27). Compliance assessment must include self-assessment or third-party audits depending on the criticality of the system (Art. 43 47). There are ex-post assessments and post-commercialization monitoring (Art.72).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act classifies systems for recruitment promotion termination and worker management as high-risk AI systems (Annex III(4)) (art. 6). Prohibited AI practices include bans on emotion-recognition in the workplace (art. 5). Providers must implement risk-management addressing discrimination (Art. 9) ensure representative datasets and bias-control (art. 10) and maintain technical documentation (Arts. 11). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights assessments before deployment (art. 27). Art. 26 (7-9) establishes safeguards: workers must be informed and may request explanations of AI use. However the Act does not regulate pay conditions or compensation so it mitigates risks without constituting a full labour-rights regime.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: INA Training Program for 2025 (Portugal)
fr1_justif: The Strategic plan (p.2) according advances Public Sector Skills Development by expanding digital skills training (p.3) and structuring the Digital Boost for Public Administration pathway (p.4). It emphasises building Digital Skills for civil servants (p.19) aiming to strengthen workers’ digital capabilities to improve productivity (p.20) support digital transformation plans (p.20) and enhance access to citizen-focused digital services (p.20). It also develops AI-related competencies through strategies to leverage AI (p.21) and specialised courses on Artificial Intelligence for Directors and Managers (p.25) and for Executives and Technical Teams including AI and Machine Learning fundamentals (p.27).P.S: P. of the pdf.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr2_title: Plan activities 2024/2025' (Center for Judicial Studies) (Portugal)
fr2_justif: The Activity Plan 2024/2025 of the Center for Judicial Studies addresses Public Sector Skills Development by providing specialized judicial training on emerging technologies. On p.30 it introduces a course on artificial intelligence and machine learning in the exercise of the judicial function covering AI concepts terminology applications and regulatory frameworks. On p.37 it includes modules on digitalization and AI in criminal justice. On p.38 it offers training on AI tools and participation in EU programs to strengthen judges’ and prosecutors’ digital and AI competencies.
fr2_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Recovery and Resilience Plan (PRR) (Portugal)
fr1_justif: The document addresses AI-related reskilling/upskilling by combining training rights with safeguards on AI use. It guarantees access to training and retraining (Art. 24(b) p.38) and a right to continuous training (Art. 106(1)(p) p.209). It also explicitly covers decision-making based on AI systems ensuring non-discrimination (Art. 24(3) p.38) and requiring transparency on algorithmic parameters affecting employment (Arts. 106(1)(s) 424 and 466 p.209).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Ministry of Labour Solidarity and Social Security

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Safety and security is core to the Act. It establishes a risk-based framework for risk identification and mitigation (Art. 9) oversight measures (Art. 14) and robustness and accuracy requirements (Art. 15). To ensure ongoing safety the Act mandates technical documentation and record-keeping (Arts. 11–12) and in certain high risk situations a conformity assessment procedure prior to market placement (Art. 43). Post-market obligations in Arts. 72–73 require providers and deployers to monitor performance report serious incidents and take corrective action. For GPAI models posing systemic risks Arts. 55–56 impose additional safety and cybersecurity duties including model evaluation and risk mitigation following the Code of Practice.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.
