fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Articles 11 12 and 13 establish several transparency and documentation obligations for AI developers and deployers. High-risk AI systems must include clear information on their capabilities limitations and intended purpose (Art. 13) and providers must maintain comprehensive technical documentation and automatic logging to ensure traceability (Arts. 11–12). These measures enable deployers to better understand the systems and authorities to audit or investigate when necessary. There are disclosure obligations that require notification when individuals interact with AI systems particularly chatbots and emotion recognition tools (Art. 50). Also for general-purpose AI in terms of model documentation and summary of training data (Art. 53).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy for Qatar 2019
fr1_justif: The Strategy addresses AI literacy primarily under Pillar 1: “Race for Talent in the AI+X Era” (pp. 6–7). It calls for integrating AI education at all levels including AI curricula AI+X programmes AI courses and boot camps and recommends preparing students teachers and workers to understand and work with AI technologies. It also promotes developing AI education pathways and strengthening universities' capacity to deliver AI-focused programmes supporting national AI literacy.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Principles and Guidelines for Ethical Development and Deployment of Artificial Intelligence (Qatar)
fr1_justif: The document addresses AI-facilitated misinformation by explicitly defining deepfakes and hallucinations (incorrect or misleading outputs) (Key definitions p. 5). It requires risk assessments that consider malicious use and specifically test for risks such as hallucinations data poisoning and adversarial attacks. It also calls for governance/feedback mechanisms and tools for reporting incidents so developers can identify gaps and fix issues (Principle 1 “Do no harm” – Guidelines p. 8).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Principles and Guidelines for Ethical Development and Deployment of Artificial Intelligence (Qatar)
fr1_justif: The framework addresses access to redress and remedy in AI by requiring channels for users to contest and correct AI enabled decisions. Principle 3 mandates feedback mechanisms for users to report instances of perceived bias (p.12). Principle 5 requires enabling users to flag issues related to privacy or data protection (p.15). Principle 7 calls for feedback mechanisms to receive input from the public/users on AI systems (p.17). Principle 8 requires human intervention external audits and gives users the ability to appeal override and question significant automated decisions (p18).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Principles and Guidelines for Ethical Development and Deployment of Artificial Intelligence (Qatar)
fr1_justif: The document embeds cultural & linguistic diversity throughout:Introduction (p.3): AI must align with local values and respect cultural norms. Principle 3 – Avoid bias (pp.7): Calls for representative datasets and audits citing Qatar’s multicultural society and a constitutional ban on discrimination by gender race language or religion. (Rationale) . Principle 7 – Human-centered (p.11): Requires adherence to Qatari cultural/religious values and to consider users cultural linguistic and demographic diversity stresses accessibility. (Guidelines) .... Examples warn against content offensive to ethnic/religious groups. (pp.711)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Principles and Guidelines for Ethical Development and Deployment of Artificial Intelligence (Qatar)
fr1_justif: The MCIT Developers & Deployers Guideline addresses AI’s environmental impact via Principle 4 :Protect the environment: organizations should understand their AI systems’ sustainability impacts set thresholds/metrics measure energy use training/inference carbon emissions and cloud related impacts prioritize energy efficient algorithms/models prefer recycled/repurposed GPUs & proceed only where the net positive outweighs the negative (p.13). It also ties these duties to QNV 2030’s environmental pillar (pp.6 8)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Principles and Guidelines for Ethical Development and Deployment of Artificial Intelligence (Qatar)
fr1_justif: Principle 3 “Reduce bias and avoid discrimination” (p 12 - 13) explicitly addresses AI fairness. Guidelines on p.12 require that AI systems in Qatar are “fair and non-discriminatory” use datasets representative of the Qatari population identify under-represented groups and biased information conduct discrimination impact assessments with ethical and legal experts and perform regular audits monitoring and user feedback for perceived bias. The rationale on p.13 links these duties to Qatar’s multicultural society the constitutional prohibition of discrimination and a commitment to equal opportunities under Qatar National Vision 2030.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Principles and Guidelines for Ethical Development and Deployment of Artificial Intelligence (Qatar)
fr1_justif: The Guidelines address Gender Equality chiefly under Principle 6 – “Minimise bias and promote fairness” (p.15). It warns against discrimination or unfair treatment based on gender and instructs users to: pick tools with bias-minimisation features seek transparency in algorithms/decision-making evaluate training data request validation/bias assessments and ensure human supervision for high-risk uses. Fairness and equity are also highlighted as cross-cutting aims in the framework overview (p.7).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Guidelines for Secure Adoption and Usage of Artificial Intelligence (Qatar)
fr1_justif: The Guideline embeds human oversight by requiring organizations to clearly define implement and monitor the role and intervention of humans across the AI lifecycle (Sec. 5.2.1 Risk Management p.21). It mandates user-facing safeguards: plain language disclosure of AI use reversibility opt-out options and clearly marked decision-review/feedback channels (Sec. 5.2.2.1 Stakeholder interaction p.23). Accountability is reinforced via documentation for explainability/auditability (Sec. 5.2.2.2 p.23) and logging of decision-making (Sec. 5.2.2.3 pp.22–23). It also notes human in the loop aspects in risk criteria. Finally it explicitly notes under 5.1.5 Human oversight in AI decision making different human-in-the-loop models of oversight.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Guidelines for Secure Adoption and Usage of Artificial Intelligence (Qatar)
fr1_justif: The guideline addresses Impact Assessments through a comprehensive AI risk-management approach. Section 5.2.1 Risk management (p. 21–23) outlines the need to identify assess prioritize and continuously re-evaluate AI-related risks including data risks societal impact explainability regulatory compliance and human oversight. It further integrates risk evaluation across the AI lifecycle (p.18) and requires documentation auditability monitoring and testing to mitigate potential harms.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Qatar Digital Government Strategy
fr1_justif: The document addresses public sector skills development through initiatives aimed at strengthening digital capabilities within government institutions. Under “Strategic Actions to Achieve This Objective – Elevating Digital Maturity” it includes digital upskilling as a key assessment domain specifically referencing ICT training and digital literacy for government officials (p. 15). In addition the establishment of a central digital advisory hub will support entities by onboarding digital experts and providing guidance to build in-house capabilities and capacity for transformation initiatives (p. 15). These measures aim to enhance government skills needed for digital transformation and emerging technologies.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Smart Government and Digital Excellence Committee Robust governance model

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy for Qatar 2019
fr1_justif: The Strategy addresses reskilling/upskilling mainly under Pillar 1: “Race for Talent in the AI+X Era” and Pillar 3: “The Changing Landscape of Employment” (p 6–9). Pillar 1 mandates AI education at all levels and “AI courses or boot camps” for those already in the workforce plus management/executive training to work in AI-related jobs with recommendations to build AI+X curricula and degree programmes (p 6–7). Pillar 3 analyses AI-driven job automation in Qatar and recommends educating/training citizens to manage build and invest in AI solutions (p. 9–10).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Guidelines for Secure Adoption and Usage of Artificial Intelligence (Qatar)
fr1_justif: The Guidelines address AI safety and security by requiring organizations to ensure the accuracy reliability and robustness of AI systems across the full AI lifecycle. They mandate risk assessments secure model development adversarial-attack protection data integrity controls and continuous monitoring. The document also specifies safeguards fallback mechanisms and human-oversight requirements to prevent system failure or harm. Sec 3-5 ( P.11- 17)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Principles and Guidelines for Ethical Development and Deployment of Artificial Intelligence (Qatar)
fr1_justif: The document mandates transparency and explainability as core user responsibilities. Principle 4 (p.13) obligates users to 'demand transparency' from AI providers. Principle 6 (p.15) requires users to 'seek transparent AI solutions' that explain decision-making processes and to leverage 'usage-related monitoring & reporting mechanisms.' It further commands users to 'understand the AI scope' and acknowledge 'algorithmic limitations' (Principle 5 p.14) ensuring a foundational comprehension of system logic for effective oversight and informed use.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: White Book on Artificial Intelligence and Data Governance (Moldova)
fr1_justif: The White Book addresses AI literacy through its focus on “Education and Skills Development” as a general objective and recommendation (p.1449). It calls for strengthening education research and training systems specific to AI and data governance promoting public awareness and workforce preparation for an AI-based economy (p.12). The document also highlights ethical and inclusive AI education aligned with EU and UNESCO standards ensuring citizens understand AI technologies their impacts and responsible use within society.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Sub-council for AI and Data Governance (SCIA) and the National Council on Digital Transformation

fr1_title: Draft law on digital public services (Moldova)
fr1_justif: The draft law addresses AI-facilitated misinformation and violence by mandating rapid removal of illegal content (Art.16) and establishing trusted notifiers for harmful material (Art.22). Article 35 requires testing and adapting algorithmic systems and content moderation to detect and mitigate risks of hate speech and online violence. Together with transparency obligations (Art.15–17) these provisions support proactive control of AI-amplified misinformation and harm.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: White Book on Artificial Intelligence and Data Governance (Moldova)
fr1_justif: The White Book embeds Fairness & Non-discrimination as core governance aims. In Guiding Principles (p.16) it explicitly upholds non-discrimination equality inclusivity and diversity plus transparency and accountability to steer design development use and deployment. Inclusivity & Accessibility requires inclusive design diverse teams and measuring impacts on under-represented groups noting AI can perpetuate bias (p.10). Ethical & Socio-Economic Considerations commit to addressing algorithmic bias so AI does not exacerbate inequalities ensuring fair and rights-respecting adoption (p.12–13).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Sub-council for AI and Data Governance (SCIA) and the National Council on Digital Transformation

fr1_title: White Book on Artificial Intelligence and Data Governance (Moldova)
fr1_justif: One of the guiding principles of the White Book is inclusivity and diversity which means promoting diversity equality of opportunity and gender equality in AI-based products and services. (p.16). Another guiding principle is diversity equal opportunities and gender equality requiring AI systems to be user-centric and designed so that anyone can use AI products or services regardless of age gender abilities or other characteristics (p.18).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Sub-council for AI and Data Governance (SCIA) and the National Council on Digital Transformation

fr1_title: White Book on Artificial Intelligence and Data Governance (Moldova)
fr1_justif: The White Book builds Human Oversight into governance. Guiding Principles commit to human-centered AI and accountability (p.16). The lifecycle framework requires mechanisms for human intervention when AI produces unwanted/erroneous decisions and encourages human validation of outputs with user instructions/limitations and access to performance to enable oversight (p.38–39). For higher-risk uses it envisages a High-Risk Management System (HRMS) with traceability (logs) technical documentation and post-market monitoring to support ongoing human supervision (p.38–39).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Sub-council for AI and Data Governance (SCIA) and the National Council on Digital Transformation

fr1_title: White Book on Artificial Intelligence and Data Governance (Moldova)
fr1_justif: The White Book addresses impact assessments implicitly through its discussion of risk-based assessments and AI lifecycle governance. On p.36–37 within the section Framework for an Effective AI and Data Governance it outlines an Assessment phase requiring companies to identify societal risks classify potential harms and establish a High-Risk Management System (HRMS). This lifecycle approach includes post-market monitoring traceability and ethical compliance evaluations functioning as de facto AI impact assessments to ensure transparency accountability and safety.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Sub-council for AI and Data Governance (SCIA) and the National Council on Digital Transformation

fr1_title: White Book on Artificial Intelligence and Data Governance (Moldova)
fr1_justif: The White Book addresses labour protections primarily on p.13 where it highlights the need for fair working conditions skills development and safeguards to ensure an equitable transition amid AI-driven changes. It also references the obligation to respect internationally recognized labour rights (p.18). While p.16 outlines general AI principles such as transparency accountability human oversight and non-discrimination these apply broadly to AI governance rather than labour protections specifically.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Sub-council for AI and Data Governance (SCIA) and the National Council on Digital Transformation

fr1_title: Professional Development Program for Civil Servants for the years 2025-2027 (Moldova)
fr1_justif: The Professional Development Programme for Civil Servants 2025–2027 approved by Government Decision No. 166/2025 establishes a national framework for continuous upskilling of public servants. It introduces the development and implementation of a program on advanced digital skills and the use of AI in work processes for civil servants via Objective 2.4 activities 2.4.1 and 2.4.2 (p.29) to be implemented by the Cyber Security Agency by training at least 150 civil servants by Q4 of 2027 (p.29). The Programme mandates specialized modules e-learning resources and partnerships with institutions to strengthen civil servants’ capacity to apply AI and digital tools in governance.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: White Book on Artificial Intelligence and Data Governance (Moldova)
fr1_justif: The White Book highlights the government’s commitment to ensuring an equitable shift to an AI-driven economy through education and training programs that prepare the workforce for new technological realities (p.12). It emphasizes education and skills development and capacity-building initiatives promoting lifelong learning professional development and talent cultivation to enhance competitiveness and adaptability in the AI sector (p.7 14 16 49).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Sub-council for AI and Data Governance (SCIA) and the National Council on Digital Transformation

fr1_title: White Book on Artificial Intelligence and Data Governance (Moldova)
fr1_justif: The White Book addresses safety and security as a core requirement for trustworthy AI (p.16). It mandates that AI systems operate securely be protected against cyber threats and misuse and ensure robustness and reliability. The document promotes a risk-based approach through HRMS regulatory sandboxes testing validation and post-market monitoring (p.36–37). It specifies that the HRMS must include procedures to collect and preserve technical documentation maintain traceability (logs and records of verification teams) and enforce data governance policies for model training validation and testing. It calls for performance tracking (accuracy robustness) and post-market monitoring to ensure continued compliance safety and resilience.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Sub-council for AI and Data Governance (SCIA) and the National Council on Digital Transformation

fr1_title: White Book on Artificial Intelligence and Data Governance (Moldova)
fr1_justif: The White Book outlines Transparency & Explainability as a key requirement for AI as AI systems must explain their decisions and actions in human-understandable terms (p.7). In Guiding Principles it elevates transparency and trust across the AI lifecycle (p.16). In the AI lifecycle governance framework it requires information & transparency for low-risk systems mandates technical documentation traceability and data-governance for training/validation/testing and sets post-market monitoring instructions limitations and performance access for end-users (p.36–37). It also states users should understand how the system works and why it makes decisions (p.38).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Sub-council for AI and Data Governance (SCIA) and the National Council on Digital Transformation

fr1_title: National Strategy in the Field of Artificial Intelligence 2024-2027 (Romania)
fr1_justif: The National AI Strategy promotes AI literacy through education at secondary and tertiary levels. It identifies education as a priority sector for AI use (p.9) and outlines objectives to raise the population’s understanding of AI benefits use and regulation (p.10). Actions include creating AI undergraduate and postgraduate programmes integrating interdisciplinary modules beyond ICT (p.76) embedding AI fundamentals in high school curricula of mandatory study disciplines and optional disciplines at middle school and high school levels that address general issues of AI (p.77). Further measures encourage partnerships with international universities and support for doctoral and postdoctoral mobility (p.76).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Interministerial Commission for the Coordination of the Implementation of the National AI Strategy

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Bots fake accounts and automated conduct are mechanisms of disinformation campaigns (recital 85) and as such rules are needed to combat them (recital 9). Systemic risk assessments apply to large-scale online platforms and search engines (art. 34) and explicitly include the dissemination or amplification of misleading or fraudulent content and the negative effects on gender-based violence. This includes recommendation systems and other relevant algorithmic systems (art. 34). Mitigation measures include testing and adapting algorithmic and recommendation systems; speeding up the processing of notifications of illegal hate speech and cyber violence; and labeling content generated or manipulated by AI (art.35).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU GDPR
fr1_justif: The law provides a layered redress framework that applies in AI contexts whenever personal data is processed. Data subjects harmed by automated processing based on AI-driven profiling and decision-making hold the right to express their point of view and the right to contest the decision (art. 22); presenting complaints to supervisory authorities (art. 77) resorting to legal measures against gaps in the authorities (art. 78) and bringing proceedings directly against controllers or processors (art. 79). Organizations may be mandated to exercise the rights collectively on behalf of data subjects (art. 80). Supervisory authorities may impose administrative fines because of the violations of core provisions (art. 83).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: This Regulation shall not apply to the processing of personal data by Member States in the context of carrying out activities relating to the Union's common foreign and security policy (recital 16). The processing of personal data by Member States does not apply to activities related to the protection and prevention of threats to public security (Art. 2).
fr1_body_name: The law establishes the European Data Protection Board is comprised of representatives from the supervisory authority of each member states (Arts 68) acting independently (Art 69) providing guidelines recommendations and binding decisions to ensure consistence across Member States (Art 70) The Member State must to establish am independent supervisory authority responsible for monitoring and enforcing the regulation (Art 51)

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Online platform service providers must ensure the privacy security and protection of minors in their services (Art. 28) including prohibiting advertising based on profiling through the automated processing of data about children (Art. 28). Terms and conditions must be understandable to minors (Art. 14) ensuring a high level of privacy and security by default based on good practices (recitals 70 71). Large-scale online platforms and large-scale search engines systematic risk assessment applies periodically evaluating potential negative effects on minors (Art. 34). Mitigation measures are determined: age verification parental control tools abuse reporting tools and support for minors (Art. 35). Voluntary standards apply (Art. 44).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The law focuses on the risk classification system (Art. 6) and the requirements imposed on high-risk AI systems. It requires suppliers of high-risk AI systems to establish implement document and maintain a risk (and damage) management system throughout the system's lifecycle (Art. 9). Training validation and test datasets must conform to data governance in terms of representation error-freeness completeness contextualization and the function the system will be used for handling special personal data for monitoring purposes and detecting and correcting biases. High-risk AI systems must have consistency throughout the lifecycle by requiring appropriate levels of accuracy and robustness and reducing the risk of bias (art.15).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act addresses gender equality through non-discrimination and fundamental rights provisions. Training datasets for high-risk AI must be representative avoinding the discriminatory outcomes and protecting atributes explicitly related gender (Art. 10). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights impact assessments before deploying high-risk AI incluing gender-based discrimination (Art. 27). Equality bodies are granted market surveillance powers enabling them to investigate and sanction AI systems producing discriminatory outcomes (Art. 74).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act establishes requirements to ensure human control over high-risk AI systems requiring providers to design systems that enable natural persons with a minimum level of comprehensibility to monitor outputs detect anomalies and intervene or interrupt operations when necessary (art. 14). Deployers must assign oversight to competent natural persons with authority to act (art. 26). For general-purpose AI with systemic risk adversarial testing and incident reporting reinforce human control (Arts. 55 73). Additional oversight provisions are set in the fundamental rights impact assessment (art. 27) and Annex (IV) 2(e) as part of technical documentation requirements (art. 11).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Law establishes an assessment architecture according to the type and level of risk of the system. High-risk AI suppliers must identify analyze and mitigate risks during the design development and implementation phases within a risk management system (Arti.9) with comprehensive technical documentation (Art.15) and monitoring of operations and anomalies (Art. 26). For AI implementers in the public sector a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment applies before implementing high-risk systems listed in Annex 3 (Art.27). Compliance assessment must include self-assessment or third-party audits depending on the criticality of the system (Art. 43 47). There are ex-post assessments and post-commercialization monitoring (Art.72).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act classifies systems for recruitment promotion termination and worker management as high-risk AI systems (Annex III(4)) (art. 6). Prohibited AI practices include bans on emotion-recognition in the workplace (art. 5). Providers must implement risk-management addressing discrimination (Art. 9) ensure representative datasets and bias-control (art. 10) and maintain technical documentation (Arts. 11). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights assessments before deployment (art. 27). Art. 26 (7-9) establishes safeguards: workers must be informed and may request explanations of AI use. However the Act does not regulate pay conditions or compensation so it mitigates risks without constituting a full labour-rights regime.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Strategy in the Field of Artificial Intelligence 2024-2027 (Romania)
fr1_justif: The National AI Strategy prioritises developing public sector skills to enable effective AI adoption. It identifies key challenges including underfunded education low digital skills of the population and uneven access to digital education among public administration personnel (p.9). Limited resources and resistance to change further hinder progress (pp.63 68–69). To address these the strategy introduces measures such as AI training for public employees (M 1.2.1 p.77) awareness initiatives and annual conferences that put AI developers in connection with public sector employees (M 5.1.1 p.83) creating an AI curriculum for administration (M 5.1.4 p.83) and training procurement staff in advanced technologies (M 5.1.9 p.83).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Interministerial Commission for the Coordination of the Implementation of the National AI Strategy

fr1_title: National Strategy in the Field of Artificial Intelligence 2024-2027 (Romania)
fr1_justif: The National AI Strategy promotes reskilling and upskilling through one of its strategic objectives which stresses training AI specialists and enhancing public understanding of AI use and regulation (p.10). Measures include collaboration between academia industry and technology clusters for AI certifications and retraining (p.77) partnerships for employee digital skills (p.82) and clinical trial AI training (p.84). Continuous learning is deemed vital for employability and a future-ready workforce (p.91).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Interministerial Commission for the Coordination of the Implementation of the National AI Strategy

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Safety and security is core to the Act. It establishes a risk-based framework for risk identification and mitigation (Art. 9) oversight measures (Art. 14) and robustness and accuracy requirements (Art. 15). To ensure ongoing safety the Act mandates technical documentation and record-keeping (Arts. 11–12) and in certain high risk situations a conformity assessment procedure prior to market placement (Art. 43). Post-market obligations in Arts. 72–73 require providers and deployers to monitor performance report serious incidents and take corrective action. For GPAI models posing systemic risks Arts. 55–56 impose additional safety and cybersecurity duties including model evaluation and risk mitigation following the Code of Practice.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Articles 11 12 and 13 establish several transparency and documentation obligations for AI developers and deployers. High-risk AI systems must include clear information on their capabilities limitations and intended purpose (Art. 13) and providers must maintain comprehensive technical documentation and automatic logging to ensure traceability (Arts. 11–12). These measures enable deployers to better understand the systems and authorities to audit or investigate when necessary. There are disclosure obligations that require notification when individuals interact with AI systems particularly chatbots and emotion recognition tools (Art. 50). Also for general-purpose AI in terms of model documentation and summary of training data (Art. 53).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
