fr1_title: The National AI Policy (Rwanda)
fr1_justif: Building 21st-century skills and AI literacy is one of the six priority areas in the national AI policy. The policy also includes an implementation plan that lists several outputs and indicators. Enabling a highly skilled workforce with 21st-century skills and AI literacy is one of them. The indicator to be tracked is: the number of professionals trained in data and AI-related skills. Other outputs under the 'Building 21st-century skills and AI literacy' priority area include: (1) a solid foundation in AI university education and applied research driving the national AI skills base (2) the general public in Rwanda has a general understanding of AI etc (page 3 page 7).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Responsible AI Office at the Ministry of ICT and Innovation

fr1_title: Guidelines on the Ethical Development and Implementation of Artificial Intelligence (Rwanda)
fr1_justif: The AI development ethical guidelines set requirements for ethical AI systems including fairness and non-discrimination. The guidelines call for equality and non-discrimination requirements to ensure that AI systems do not treat individuals or groups unfairly. Fairness and non-discrimination are discussed in the principles section and the ethical AI requirements (pages 6 8). AI developers implementers and users need to ensure that individuals minority groups and protected classes in Rwanda maintain freedom from bias stigmatization and discrimination (p. 6).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Guidelines on the Ethical Development and Implementation of Artificial Intelligence (Rwanda)
fr1_justif: The national guidelines encourage the development of AI systems that promote gender equality and eliminate gender bias and discrimination. Also one of the key requirements of ethical systems under the Guidelines - Equality and Non-discrimination requires that AI systems do not treat individuals or groups of individuals unfairly based on their characteristics such as ethnicity gender sexual orientation or age whether intentionally or unintentionally (page 8).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Guidelines on the Ethical Development and Implementation of Artificial Intelligence (Rwanda)
fr1_justif: Rwanda's AI Development Ethical Guidelines include 'human oversight and autonomy' as one of the main requirements to attain ethical AI. This indicator is addressed under the Principle of Autonomy (page 5) and the requirement of Human Oversight and Autonomy as one of the principles of the guidelines (page 8).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: The National AI Policy (Rwanda)
fr1_justif: The national AI policy states that the public sector is the driver of AI development in Rwanda. One of the main areas is engaging local AI solution providers through innovation-friendly procurement processes. The government is encouraged to develop procurement guidelines that incentivise AI development nationwide. The Policy also recommends: Revision of ICT procurement guidelines and contract management practices for Cloud services and products development of procurement guidelines incentivising data sharing & access for public projects including ICT/data/Al components development of procurement guidelines of Al solutions in the public sector organisation of trainings for public procurement (page 4 tables on pages 11 13 and 15).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Responsible AI Office at the Ministry of ICT and Innovation

fr1_title: The National AI Policy (Rwanda)
fr1_justif: The Rwanda national AI policy captures policy recommendations among which reskilling the workforce with 21st-century AI is number one. The policy recommends improving public service delivery using AI. It also suggests that the government will invest in raising awareness of the benefits by piloting and demonstrating and building capacity to implement and manage AI projects delivering improved public services. The Government plans to build the public sector's capacity to raise awareness of AI's potential to improve public sector service delivery (page 4). Activities 49 to 52 (p.15) of the action plan target the development of skills in the public sector.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Responsible AI Office at the Ministry of ICT and Innovation

fr1_title: The National AI Policy (Rwanda)
fr1_justif: Rwanda's national AI policy has six priority areas among which building 21st-century skills and high AI literacy are listed as critical enablers. The national AI policy recommends reskilling the workforce with 21st-century AI and data skills. The government plans to implement a Tax Relief and Grants programme to help employers upskill and reskill their employees. This aligns with the development of a National Skills Building Programme that prioritises AI and data skills together with a Young Professionals/Apprenticeship Programme to develop AI talent and career opportunities in the knowledge economy (pages 3 & 7).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Responsible AI Office at the Ministry of ICT and Innovation

fr1_title: Guidelines on the Ethical Development and Implementation of Artificial Intelligence (Rwanda)
fr1_justif: The ethical guidelines address safety and security through the ethical principle of non-maleficence: 'do no harm' (page 5) and the ethical requirement of safety (page 9). Furthermore Rwanda's AI Development Ethical Guidelines feature critical aspects for ethical AI development in Rwanda. These aspects include 'safety' which concerns ensuring that AI systems operate without harming the mental and physical well-being of humans the well-being of our society or the environment (page 9).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Guidelines on the Ethical Development and Implementation of Artificial Intelligence (Rwanda)
fr1_justif: Rwanda's national AI development ethical guidelines include the Principle of Explicability or 'Operate Transparently' which requires that AI systems be transparent understandable and explainable to users and affected parties. AI systems should be designed and implemented so that users and stakeholders can understand how they work how they make decisions and what results they produce (page 6). Also the guidelines expect developers and implementers to provide relevant information to facilitate understanding of the system make impacted humans aware of the AI interaction and enable them to understand and challenge outcomes when necessary (page 9).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: National Framework for Artificial Intelligence in Digital Learning (Saudi Arabia)
fr1_justif: The document addresses AI Literacy mainly in Section 7.1: AI Curriculum for Learners (p.23) which outlines teaching AI concepts hands-on AI activities critical thinking about AI and ethical awareness. It also appears in Section 8.1: AI Skills Training for Educators (p.25) ensuring teachers can teach AI effectively. Foundational AI literacy terms and definitions are provided in the Glossary (p.4–5) supporting understanding of key AI concepts used in learning. The explicit goal is to Advanced integration of AI with entire educational systems facilitating independent learning and potential shifts in educational paradigms (p. 7).
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Guidelines: Generative Artificial Intelligence for Public (2025) (Saudi Arabia)
fr1_justif: The Generative Artificial Intelligence Guidelines for Public address AI-facilitated misinformation and violence in Section 5: “Generative AI Risks and Potential Mitigations” (pp. 13–15). It warns that deepfakes and misrepresentation can enable scams fraud and identity theft and that malicious GenAI manipulation can pose safety threats to public security. The document proposes safeguards such as watermarking content moderation dataset filtering output verification and fact-checking to detect and prevent harmful biased or deceptive content promoting transparency and user vigilance to mitigate misinformation and related harms.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: AI Ethics Principles (September 2023) (Version 1.0) (Saudi Arabia)
fr1_justif: The document address Access to Redress and Remedy primarily through ethical guidance rather than binding obligations. In the AI Ethics Checklist – Deploy and Monitor phase (page 46) entities are asked to ensure: “mechanisms that allow for redress in case of harm or adverse impact” and to “provide information to end-users/third parties about opportunities for redress.” These provisions promote transparency and accountability by requiring organizations to establish internal accessible grievance mechanisms for AI-related harms.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: AI Ethics Principles (September 2023) (Version 1.0) (Saudi Arabia)
fr1_justif: The document addresses Environmental Impact under Principle 4 – Social & Environmental Benefits (pp. 19–21). It requires AI systems to prevent harm to humans and the environment promote sustainability and measure resource and energy consumption. It directs developers to ensure AI supports ecological responsibility and continuous assessment of environmental effects in line with sustainable development goals.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: AI Ethics Principles (September 2023) (Version 1.0) (Saudi Arabia)
fr1_justif: The document addresses Fairness and Non-discrimination through Principle 1 – Fairness which requires AI systems to eliminate bias discrimination and stigmatization across the entire AI System Lifecycle (pp. 12–13). It mandates fairness-aware design identification of affected and vulnerable groups and selection of fairness assessment metrics during the Plan and Design phase (p. 13). The framework further requires training models on representative bias-cleansed data careful handling of sensitive attributes and continuous monitoring of fairness thresholds during Build Validate and Deploy stages (pp. 13–15). These measures aim to ensure AI outcomes are inclusive objective and non-discriminatory.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: AI Ethics Principles (September 2023) (Version 1.0) (Saudi Arabia)
fr1_justif: The policy emphasizes Human Oversight and Determination as part of Principle 7 – Accountability & Responsibility (pp. 25–27) and in the AI Ethics Checklist (p. 41). The framework requires AI systems to be human-controlled ensuring decisions are monitored governed and open to intervention by authorized users. It mandates clear assignment of accountability across the AI lifecycle defining oversight processes KPIs and fallback mechanisms to prevent overreliance on automation and preserve human judgment in decision-making.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Guidelines: Generative Artificial Intelligence for Public (2025) (Saudi Arabia)
fr1_justif: The document incorporates multiple elements of AI Impact Assessments tailored specifically to generative AI risks. It requires structured risk evaluation and harm analysis under Privacy & Security (p.11) including “privacy impact assessment” and “risk management assessment” for GenAI models. It mandates reviewing data quality bias and discriminatory outputs within Fairness (p.8) reflecting known GenAI vulnerabilities. Reliability & Safety (p.9) addresses safety controls hallucination prevention and system robustness. Risks & Mitigations (pp.13–15) require output verification misinformation detection and ongoing monitoring—all core risk areas.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Generative Artificial Intelligence Guidelines for Government (Version 1) (2024) (Saudi Arabia)
fr1_justif: The document supports Public Sector Skills Development by requiring continuous training for government employees on GenAI use risks and data-handling practices. Appendix 2 (p. 23–24) mandates training courses to build awareness technical skills and the ability to review AI outputs. Section 4.3 (p. 12) stresses educational initiatives to enhance staff understanding of GenAI while Appendix 1 (p. 12–17) shows practical skill-building in content creation analysis translation and project planning.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: National Strategy for Data & AI (Saudi Arabia)
fr1_justif: The Strategy links AI to reskilling / upskilling by prioritizing development of Data & AI skills specialists and experts to meet future labor-market needs. It highlights AI literacy machine-learning training programs STEM and AI bootcamps and the creation of Data & AI educational tracks and certification programs to build a domestic talent pipeline. These appear in Section 3.0 (p. 10) and Section 4.1 – Objective 2 (p. 21).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: AI Ethics Principles (September 2023) (Version 1.0) (Saudi Arabia)
fr1_justif: The policy address Safety and Security through Principle 2 – Privacy & Security (pp. 15–17) and Principle 5 – Reliability & Safety (pp. 21–23). These sections require AI systems to be designed with security-by-design data protection and risk-management mechanisms to prevent cyberattacks data breaches and system failures. They also mandate continuous monitoring safety self-assessments and fallback plans to ensure reliability robustness and resilience. Collectively these measures protect individuals and society from potential harms caused by unsafe or insecure AI systems.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: AI Ethics Principles (September 2023) (Version 1.0) (Saudi Arabia)
fr1_justif: The Principles of AI Ethics address Transparency and Explainability in Principle 6 (pp. 23–24) emphasizing clarity traceability and justification of AI decisions. It requires AI systems to document data algorithms and decision logic ensuring outcomes are understandable and auditable. Entities must provide stakeholders access to explanations maintain logs of failures and enable complaint mechanisms. Annex A (p. 38) adds “AI Methods Explanation Report” and “Algorithm Auditing” tools to enhance interpretability and oversight.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Senegal's $206 Million Strategy to Digitize Education
fr1_justif: Senegal’s Stratégie du Numérique pour l’Éducation (Digital Strategy for Education) 2025–2029 is a major government framework that explicitly includes digital and AI literacy as key components of educational reform. It aims to modernize education by integrating information and communication technologies (ICTs) and AI tools into teaching and learning helping students and teachers build relevant 21st-century skills.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Development Strategy (Senegal)
fr1_justif: Section 1.2 from pages 14 to 16 discusses various impact that AI would be expected to have upon Senegalese society. The impact assessment discusses use of AI from an economic perspective and also discusses about AI and data sovereignty. However the impact assessment is not application specific but in the implementation plan the oversight mechanism infer performing some audits.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Communication Telecommunications and Digital Economy (MCTEN)

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: National Strategy and Roadmap of Senegal on Artificial Intelligence up to 2028
fr1_justif: Action 2.16 establishes a structured procurement mechanism by mandating the creation and implementation of a program to accelerate public procurement of AI including consultations to identify public-sector challenges followed by calls for expressions of interest directed at AI stakeholders to propose solutions (p. 9). This is reinforced by Action 2.17 which provides for the establishment of a dedicated organization within the State to facilitate procurement with an AI liaison in each ministry as well as Action 2.18 which promotes recurring procurement mechanisms such as annual calls and incentives and Action 2.19 which introduces legal and procedural tools such as regulatory sandboxes and simplified procurement processes.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Development Strategy (Senegal)
fr1_justif: This strategy is the central government framework for AI and includes explicit goals related to skills development: Human capital and training: A dedicated pillar focuses on training and building AI talent targeting the development of AI and data science skills among Senegalese citizens including potential public-sector professionals. Vision for public sector transformation: The strategy situates AI as a tool for public sector performance economic growth and digital sovereignty implying that government officials and civil servants need new competencies in AI and digital governance.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Communication Telecommunications and Digital Economy (MCTEN)

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Development Strategy (Senegal)
fr1_justif: Senegal’s National AI Strategy launched in 2023 and coordinated by the Ministry of Communication Telecommunications and the Digital Economy (MCTEN) explicitly incorporates human capital development as a core pillar:It sets ambitious targets to train tens of thousands of people in data science and AI by 2028 (about 90000 individuals) to build national capacity and prepare the workforce for AI-related opportunities. The strategy outlines actions to develop training programs for trainers create national and regional AI training institutes and establish an observatory for skills needs and training programs to ensure continuous alignment with labour needs.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Communication Telecommunications and Digital Economy (MCTEN)

fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Development Strategy (Senegal)
fr1_justif: On page 61 the values/principles of transparency and explainability are clearly stated as very important for growth of AI in Senegal. Action 4 on page 60 the strategy states that: Senegal also intends that AI providers starting with social networks vectors of disinformation online hate and cyber malice of all kinds respect national law but also the more flexible regulatory provisions that would be put inplace'. Furthermore Transparency and explainability is one of the guiding principles to be taken into account in the future legal framework for AI (p.61).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Communication Telecommunications and Digital Economy (MCTEN)

fr1_title: Strategy for the Development of Artificial Intelligence in the Republic of Serbia for the Period 2025–2030
fr1_justif: The Strategy for the Development of Artificial Intelligence in the Republic of Serbia 2025-2030 addresses AI literacy through Measure 3.2 which mandates integration of AI into primary and secondary curricula interdisciplinary AI projects and AI-enabled tools in classrooms. It sets targets for teacher training and certification and literacy monitoring. The education chapter (Section 3) also emphasizes ethical AI use personalized learning and continuous professional development for teachers. Under the implementation plan it also measures the amount of universities currently having AI Curricula in their program and sets a target to increase the number by 2030 (p. 49).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Ethical Guidelines for Development Implementation and Use of Robust and Accountable Artificial Intelligence (Serbia)
fr1_justif: The Ethical Guidelines for Development Implementation and Use of Robust and Accountable AI address children’s rights by emphasizing the protection of vulnerable groups including children (p.12-13 Section 3.3). AI systems must be safe reliable and secure prevent harm and avoid malicious use. Any damage must be repaired promptly with legal compensation. The guidelines integrate civil criminal and ethical obligations to ensure AI does not cause physical mental or property harm highlighting the special care required for children in AI deployment and use.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Strategy for the Development of Artificial Intelligence in the Republic of Serbia for the Period 2025–2030
fr1_justif: The strategy addresses Cultural and Linguistic Diversity primarily through the national program for Serbian language technologies (Sections 4.3 6.4). It supports AI development for natural language processing language models annotated/unannotated corpora speech recognition and generative AI ensuring Serbian is represented in digital services education and public administration. Measures include digital infrastructure archives pilot projects and training programs (Sections 4.3 6.4) preserving linguistic heritage and improving accessibility while enabling AI-mediated interaction in the local language.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Strategy for the Development of Artificial Intelligence in the Republic of Serbia for the Period 2025–2030
fr1_justif: It notes that AI development requires a “large amount of electrical energy” and stresses that sustainability cannot rely only on renewable energy production but also on “energy-efficient solutions” and efficient use of AI technologies. Environmental protection is embedded as a core ethical constraint stating that AI “must not negatively affect the environment” (Section 3.3) and reaffirmed in Section 6.7 which requires continuous impact assessments monitoring of AI systems and prevention of harmful effects including environmental ones.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Ethical Guidelines for Development Implementation and Use of Robust and Accountable Artificial Intelligence (Serbia)
fr1_justif: Section 3.4 Fairness defines fairness as the protection of rights and integrity against discrimination and emphasizes that AI systems must prevent discrimination (including against vulnerable groups such as persons with disabilities). It describes both substantive fairness (equal opportunities no unfair prejudice) and procedural fairness (ability to challenge AI decisions transparently). Section 4.5 Diversity non-discrimination and equality incorporates the principle into broader ethical requirements highlighting equal treatment and protection from bias as essential for responsible AI.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Strategy for the Development of Artificial Intelligence in the Republic of Serbia for the Period 2025–2030
fr1_justif: The Strategy for the Development of Artificial Intelligence in the Republic of Serbia 2025-2030 addresses gender equality by mandating that AI development deployment and education provide no preferential treatment based on gender. 'Legal and Regulatory Challenges emphasize' equal rights and opportunities for all genders in AI-related activities ensuring that women and men have the same access to education professional development and participation in AI programs and initiatives (pp. 32-33).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Strategy for the Development of Artificial Intelligence in the Republic of Serbia for the Period 2025–2030
fr1_justif: Section 3.3 highlights the need for monitoring intervention and control procedures throughout the AI lifecycle especially in education and employment acknowledging risks that require human intervention. Sections 6.7 commit to establishing a legal and institutional framework continuous monitoring of all AI systems impact assessments before deployment and oversight by competent authorities with humans 'as the measure” guiding AI use.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Strategy for the Development of Artificial Intelligence in the Republic of Serbia for the Period 2025–2030
fr1_justif: Measure 6.1 explicitly requires that during the implementation of AI incentives in the public sector impact assessments are considered for systems that may be classified as high-risk linking these assessments to safety security and reliability requirements. Section 3.3 outlines key AI risks including algorithmic bias discrimination privacy violations lack of transparency and insufficient lifecycle control. Section 6.7 further outlines combating challenges in data protection potential negative impacts on lifestyles and the safety of citizens and the state by requiring continuous impact assessments and reevaluation of how potential AI solutions are used before deployment.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: ETHICAL GUIDELINES FOR DEVELOPMENT IMPLEMENTATION AND USE OF ROBUST AND ACCOUNTABLE ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
fr1_justif: The document identifies AI related labour harms by classifying employment related systems such as recruitment hiring evaluation monitoring promotion dismissal and labour management as high risk AI systems (p. 8) recognizing their impact on workers’ rights and conditions. It further defines high risk systems as those affecting human integrity and rights requiring analysis and impact assessment (p. 7) linking employment related AI use to heightened oversight. These concerns are operationalized through stakeholder engagement mechanisms requiring that employees are informed of AI impacts trained accordingly and that employees and trade unions can access information on relevant high risk systems (pp. 33–34).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Strategy for the Development of Artificial Intelligence in the Republic of Serbia for the Period 2025–2030
fr1_justif: The strategy explicitly requires that public procurement contracts include mandatory obligations for private providers to transfer data to state authorities including specifications on structure format and timelines (p. 21) and further proposes the use of procurement law to impose enforceable obligations on contractors in cases of public-interest services (p. 44). In addition it promotes the representation of domestic AI solutions in public procurement as a strategic tool for strengthening national AI capacity (p. 37)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Information System for Planning Implementation Monitoring Public Policy Coordination and Reporting

fr1_title: Strategy for the Development of Artificial Intelligence in the Republic of Serbia for the Period 2025–2030
fr1_justif: Measure 5.1 focuses on capacity building in data management and data engineering including structured training programs for public servants as a prerequisite for AI use. Measure 5.3 supports skills development through workshops trainings and expert consultations on data standardization and AI-compatible formats.  Measure 6.2 directly targets AI skills by organizing trainings and pilot projects for public employees with quantified targets for trained staff. Section 6.1 (Effects of measures) highlights institutional learning coordination and the role of the AI Council in strengthening long-term skills.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Strategy for the Development of Artificial Intelligence in the Republic of Serbia for the Period 2025–2030
fr1_justif: in Section “Labor Market and Education Implications” it recognises AI-driven automation and calls for timely reskilling and continuous employee training. Measure 3.4 establishes informal AI training requalification programs certification and labour market monitoring (2025–2030). Measure 6.4 ) links AI adoption incentives with targets for upskilled workers in AI-enabled environments (5000 by 2030) and under Measure f) raise the number of participants in AI educational campaigns and workshops (also 5000 by 2030) to increase society's overall AI capabilities. Section 6.2 also notes support for professional development and AI training programs will enable employees to acquire new skills and knowledge thereby increasing productivity.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Ethical Guidelines for Development Implementation and Use of Robust and Accountable Artificial Intelligence (Serbia)
fr1_justif: Section 3.3 Prohibition to cause damage notes that an artificial intelligence system must meet safety standards and have adequate mechanisms to prevent damage to persons and their property. Section 4.2 Technical Reliability and Security calls for risk assessment resilience against threats and validation/testing to ensure accuracy and reliable operation. Finally there is also a questionnaire checklist covering Security Accuracy and reliability (pp. 19-20).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Ethical Guidelines for Development Implementation and Use of Robust and Accountable Artificial Intelligence (Serbia)
fr1_justif: Ethical Guidelines address Transparency and Explainability in Sections 3.1 and 4.4 (pp. 10-11 14 26-31). Explainability ensures AI processes purposes and decisions are clear to users and stakeholders with “black box” models requiring traceability external evaluation and transparent communication. Transparency involves disclosing system parameters and decision rationale. The Questionnaire for Assessment of AI Systems operationalizes these principles promoting user understanding accountability stakeholder dialogue and context-sensitive explanations for high-impact AI applications.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Elections (Integrity of Online Advertising) (Amendment) Bill 2024 (Bill No. 19 of 2024) (Singapore)
fr1_justif: The Act prohibits the publication sharing boosting and reposting of digitally generated or manipulated online election advertising depicting candidates (Sections 2 and 3 pp. 1-4). It targets content created by 'generative artificial intelligence' or 'digital means' that produces realistic audiovisual representations which a reasonable person would believe depicts a candidate (Sections 2(1C)-(1D) 3(5A)-(5B) pp. 2-6). Criminal penalties apply: individuals face fines up to $1000 and/or 12 months' imprisonment; platforms face fines up to $1 million (Sections 2(1E) 3(5C) pp. 2-6). Exemptions include satire parody and commentary if clearly indicated (Section 2(1F) p. 3).
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Advisory Guidelines on the Personal Data Protection Act (PDPA) for Children’s Personal Data in the Digital Environment (Singapore)
fr1_justif: The Guidelines address children’s rights around AI-enabled data practices by requiring age-appropriate notices consent and withdrawal mechanisms for children including higher protection for those under 13 (Notification; Consent for collection use or disclosure of personal data p.4–6). They regulate AI-related practices such as age assurance profiling behavioural/telemetric data and geolocation requiring data minimisation safeguards against harmful or inappropriate content and higher security for children’s personal data (Reasonable purposes; Ascertainment of age; Geolocation data; Protection pp.6–10; Annex A DPIA pp.12–13).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National AI Strategy - ADVANCING OUR SMART NATION JOURNEY
fr1_justif: The document explicitly links AI development to cultural context and societal diversity through its Human-Centric AI R&D Programme. It emphasises that AI systems should understand “culture and social norms (especially Asian cultures and norms)” and calls for the integration of social sciences such as psychology and sociology to account for socio-cultural differences in design and deployment (p. 19). This demonstrates a clear effort to embed cultural sensitivity into AI systems. While a newer strategy (2.0) has been introduced it shifts focus toward AI for the public good and does not formally supersede the original strategy which remains valid within its original scope.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: National AI Office Digital Government Office

fr1_title: Driving a Greener Digital Future: Singapore's Green Data Centre Roadmap
fr1_justif: The Green Data Centre Roadmap links AI growth to environmental impacts by noting that generative AI and other digital technologies increase demand for compute and data-centre capacity (pp. 4–5). As data centres are major users of energy and water the roadmap proposes measures to reduce their environmental footprint including energy-efficient infrastructure and cooling solutions for high-density AI workloads such as liquid and immersion cooling (pp. 8–9). It also promotes green computing and carbon-efficient software design to optimise workloads and reduce emissions (pp. 11–12) alongside the use of low-carbon energy sources and improved water efficiency (pp. 13–15).
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Model AI Governance Framework (2nd Edition IMDA/PDPC) (Singapore)
fr1_justif: The Model AI Governance Framework addresses Fairness and Non-discrimination by requiring organizations to ensure AI decisions are explainable transparent and fair (pp. 15–16) and by promoting human-centric risk-based oversight (pp. 28–34). It focuses on minimizing bias in data (pp. 38–39) mitigation of discriminatory decisions regular model auditing traceability and inclusive stakeholder engagement (pp. 35–43). These measures help prevent unjust impacts and ensure equitable AI outcomes.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Second Edition of Model AI Governance Framework
fr1_justif: The document contains concrete and operational mechanisms addressing gender bias within AI systems. In particular it provides detailed methodological approaches to fairness testing including the use of demographic datasets to evaluate bias across groups such as gender the application of bias ratios and the “four-fifths rule” to ensure equitable outcomes in hiring contexts and proactive de-biasing of models to prevent disadvantage based on demographic characteristics (pp. 42–43). These measures are complemented by counterfactual fairness testing which requires that AI decisions remain unchanged when sensitive attributes such as gender are altered (p. 46) thereby directly targeting gender-based discrimination at the model level.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Model AI Governance Framework (2nd Edition IMDA/PDPC) (Singapore)
fr1_justif: The framework details three models: (1) human-in-the-loop (2) human-on-the-loop and (3) human-out-of-the-loop explaining when and how humans should intervene review and override AI outputs especially in high-risk contexts (pp. 28–34). It recommends meaningful human control responsible staff and the ability to appeal or override decisions made by AI (pp. 22–24 30–31 57–58). A risk-based approach determines the level of oversight (p. 30).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Second Edition of Model AI Governance Framework
fr1_justif: The document contains clear and structured provisions requiring impact assessments of AI systems framed through a risk-based governance approach that explicitly considers impacts on individuals. It requires organisations to assess and manage risks associated with AI deployment including potential adverse effects arising from automated decision-making (p. 22) and operationalises this through a risk assessment methodology based on the likelihood and severity of harm (pp. 28–29). These harms are not limited to technical failures but include effects on individuals such as unfair or discriminatory outcomes which the framework explicitly highlights as a key risk to be mitigated (p. 14).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Platform Workers Act 2024 (No.30 of 2024) (Singapore)
fr1_justif: The Platform Workers Act 2024 addresses labour protections in contexts of automated platform management. Section 6(1)–(2) (pp.11–12) defines “management control” as the use of data to determine worker eligibility allocate tasks or determine payment without human intervention on a regular or routine basis which indicate automated decision processes in platform work. The Act extends protections to such workers including mandatory records and earnings slips (ss.13–14 pp.15–16) financial protections such as priority of earnings and injury compensation (ss.16–17 pp.18–21) and collective representation through platform work associations (page 141 Part 3; Sixth Schedule).
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Manpower Commissioner for Platform Work

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Singapore National AI Strategy 2.0 (2023)
fr1_justif: The document addresses public sector skills development by strengthening AI capabilities within the public service. It highlights the growing use of AI in public services such as education healthcare immigration clearance and scam detection demonstrating the need for a skilled public workforce (p. 8). It promotes mass awareness and adoption of AI tools among public officers and the use of government-developed AI systems such as the Pair LLM suite (pp. 24–25). Crucially the government commits to uplifting AI literacy and proficiency of all public officers by rolling out targeted AI training courses for different workforce segments including senior management policy and operational roles (p. 26).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Skills Framework for Infocomm Technology (ICT) (Skills in Generative Artificial Intelligence- Gen AI) (Singapore)
fr1_justif: The document addresses reskilling/upskilling by defining structured skill pathways for workforce adaptation to AI. It organizes competencies for two worker archetypes: AI Users (Chapter 1 pages 3) and AI Practitioners (Chapter 2 page 6 ) enabling workers to identify relevant upskilling paths. For AI Users it covers GenAI Principles and Applications (TSC #1) and Prompt Design (TSC #2) to help workers 'enhance productivity drive innovation' and adapt to AI-enabled work environments (pages 4-5). For AI Practitioners it provides seven advanced technical skills (TSCs #3-7) including Prompt Engineering Model Selection Evaluation Application Development and Model Fine-Tuning.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Model AI Governance Framework for Generative AI (2024) (Singapore)
fr1_justif: Page 21 to 22 of the guideline speaks about security in the context of AI particularly asking for the adoption of security by design with refinements based on unique characteristics of AI as well as development of new security safeguards with new tools such as input filters to detect unsafe prompts and digital forensics tools for generative AI to investigate and analyse digital data (e.g. file contents) to reconstruct a cybersecurity incident. Page 12 to 15 discusses trusted development and development including using baseline safety practices like fine-tuning transparency via labels.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Model AI Governance Framework (2nd Edition IMDA/PDPC) (Singapore)
fr1_justif: The framework treats transparency and explainability as core governance principles (p.15). Section 3.26–3.29 (p.44–45) defines explainability and recommends documenting model training selection design decisions and using supplementary explanation tools. It then links explainability with transparency through guidance on product descriptions and technical specifications and complements this with related guidance on transparency and consumer interactions later in the document (pp.53–56).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: 2030 Digital Transformation Strategy for Slovakia
fr1_justif: The strategy acknowledges that based on experience of Slovak IT companies the content of education at various types of schools (primarysecondary college) already contain the matter of IoT and Slovak pupils/students have excellent results in IoT and robotics at competitions for secondary school and college students and in international contests (p. 18). However it also notes that Slovakiai mmediately needs a comprehensive systemic change of education which will ensure that primary and secondary schools and universities offer more subjects and majors from the viewpoint of quantity and quality where pupils and students will acquire advanced digital and technological skills and high quality education. (p. 19).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Office of the Deputy Prime Minister of the Slovak Republic for Investments and Informatization

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Bots fake accounts and automated conduct are mechanisms of disinformation campaigns (recital 85) and as such rules are needed to combat them (recital 9). Systemic risk assessments apply to large-scale online platforms and search engines (art. 34) and explicitly include the dissemination or amplification of misleading or fraudulent content and the negative effects on gender-based violence. This includes recommendation systems and other relevant algorithmic systems (art. 34). Mitigation measures include testing and adapting algorithmic and recommendation systems; speeding up the processing of notifications of illegal hate speech and cyber violence; and labeling content generated or manipulated by AI (art.35).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU GDPR
fr1_justif: The law provides a layered redress framework that applies in AI contexts whenever personal data is processed. Data subjects harmed by automated processing based on AI-driven profiling and decision-making hold the right to express their point of view and the right to contest the decision (art. 22); presenting complaints to supervisory authorities (art. 77) resorting to legal measures against gaps in the authorities (art. 78) and bringing proceedings directly against controllers or processors (art. 79). Organizations may be mandated to exercise the rights collectively on behalf of data subjects (art. 80). Supervisory authorities may impose administrative fines because of the violations of core provisions (art. 83).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: This Regulation shall not apply to the processing of personal data by Member States in the context of carrying out activities relating to the Union's common foreign and security policy (recital 16). The processing of personal data by Member States does not apply to activities related to the protection and prevention of threats to public security (Art. 2).
fr1_body_name: The law establishes the European Data Protection Board is comprised of representatives from the supervisory authority of each member states (Arts 68) acting independently (Art 69) providing guidelines recommendations and binding decisions to ensure consistence across Member States (Art 70) The Member State must to establish am independent supervisory authority responsible for monitoring and enforcing the regulation (Art 51)

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Online platform service providers must ensure the privacy security and protection of minors in their services (Art. 28) including prohibiting advertising based on profiling through the automated processing of data about children (Art. 28). Terms and conditions must be understandable to minors (Art. 14) ensuring a high level of privacy and security by default based on good practices (recitals 70 71). Large-scale online platforms and large-scale search engines systematic risk assessment applies periodically evaluating potential negative effects on minors (Art. 34). Mitigation measures are determined: age verification parental control tools abuse reporting tools and support for minors (Art. 35). Voluntary standards apply (Art. 44).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The law focuses on the risk classification system (Art. 6) and the requirements imposed on high-risk AI systems. It requires suppliers of high-risk AI systems to establish implement document and maintain a risk (and damage) management system throughout the system's lifecycle (Art. 9). Training validation and test datasets must conform to data governance in terms of representation error-freeness completeness contextualization and the function the system will be used for handling special personal data for monitoring purposes and detecting and correcting biases. High-risk AI systems must have consistency throughout the lifecycle by requiring appropriate levels of accuracy and robustness and reducing the risk of bias (art.15).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.
fr1_body_name: AI office coordinates EU implementation (art64) European AI Board composed of one representative per Member State aims to issue guidance support consistent application and coordinate national authorities (art65 66) the national competent authorities (art70) once each country must designate at least one market surveillance authority and a notifying authority The Advisory Forum (Art 67) provides technical expertise advising the commission and council The Scientific Panel of Independent Experts (Art 68) provides support with specialized scientific and technical knowledge However neither directly participates in implementation

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act addresses gender equality through non-discrimination and fundamental rights provisions. Training datasets for high-risk AI must be representative avoinding the discriminatory outcomes and protecting atributes explicitly related gender (Art. 10). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights impact assessments before deploying high-risk AI incluing gender-based discrimination (Art. 27). Equality bodies are granted market surveillance powers enabling them to investigate and sanction AI systems producing discriminatory outcomes (Art. 74).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: Draft law on the organization of state administration in the field of artificial intelligence (Slovakia)
fr1_justif: This draft law includes in its article 6 (4) mention of obligation of public authorities to inform when dealing with high risk AI systems: “Under the conditions established by a special regulation ) the deploying entity ) which is a public authority is obliged to provide information in the individual legal act that it issued based on the output from the high-risk AI system on the extent and in relation to which part of the individual legal act and the procedure that preceded its issuance the output from the high-risk AI system was used.”
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Articles 11 12 and 13 establish several transparency and documentation obligations for AI developers and deployers. High-risk AI systems must include clear information on their capabilities limitations and intended purpose (Art. 13) and providers must maintain comprehensive technical documentation and automatic logging to ensure traceability (Arts. 11–12). These measures enable deployers to better understand the systems and authorities to audit or investigate when necessary. There are disclosure obligations that require notification when individuals interact with AI systems particularly chatbots and emotion recognition tools (Art. 50). Also for general-purpose AI in terms of model documentation and summary of training data (Art. 53).

fr1_title: National Programme to Promote the Development and Use of Artificial Intelligence in the Republic of Slovenia by 2025 (NpAI)
fr1_justif: The National Programme (NpUI) addresses AI literacy through Strategic Objective 2: 'Education and strengthening of human resources' (Section 6.2 page 30) which mandates a comprehensive update of educational curricula across all levels. Specifically Measure 2.3 (page 31) requires the introduction of 'computational thinking' and 'core content of computer and information science' at the primary and secondary levels to build foundational technical skills. For higher education Measures 2.1 and 2.2 (page 31) focus on integrating AI content into diverse study programmes to develop 'advanced professional digital skills.' Furthermore the strategy extends AI literacy to the general public through Measure 2.9 (page 32) and Measure 7.2 (page 50).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Public Administration and the NpAI Steering Committee

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Bots fake accounts and automated conduct are mechanisms of disinformation campaigns (recital 85) and as such rules are needed to combat them (recital 9). Systemic risk assessments apply to large-scale online platforms and search engines (art. 34) and explicitly include the dissemination or amplification of misleading or fraudulent content and the negative effects on gender-based violence. This includes recommendation systems and other relevant algorithmic systems (art. 34). Mitigation measures include testing and adapting algorithmic and recommendation systems; speeding up the processing of notifications of illegal hate speech and cyber violence; and labeling content generated or manipulated by AI (art.35).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU GDPR
fr1_justif: The law provides a layered redress framework that applies in AI contexts whenever personal data is processed. Data subjects harmed by automated processing based on AI-driven profiling and decision-making hold the right to express their point of view and the right to contest the decision (art. 22); presenting complaints to supervisory authorities (art. 77) resorting to legal measures against gaps in the authorities (art. 78) and bringing proceedings directly against controllers or processors (art. 79). Organizations may be mandated to exercise the rights collectively on behalf of data subjects (art. 80). Supervisory authorities may impose administrative fines because of the violations of core provisions (art. 83).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: This Regulation shall not apply to the processing of personal data by Member States in the context of carrying out activities relating to the Union's common foreign and security policy (recital 16). The processing of personal data by Member States does not apply to activities related to the protection and prevention of threats to public security (Art. 2).
fr1_body_name: The law establishes the European Data Protection Board is comprised of representatives from the supervisory authority of each member states (Arts 68) acting independently (Art 69) providing guidelines recommendations and binding decisions to ensure consistence across Member States (Art 70) The Member State must to establish am independent supervisory authority responsible for monitoring and enforcing the regulation (Art 51)

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Online platform service providers must ensure the privacy security and protection of minors in their services (Art. 28) including prohibiting advertising based on profiling through the automated processing of data about children (Art. 28). Terms and conditions must be understandable to minors (Art. 14) ensuring a high level of privacy and security by default based on good practices (recitals 70 71). Large-scale online platforms and large-scale search engines systematic risk assessment applies periodically evaluating potential negative effects on minors (Art. 34). Mitigation measures are determined: age verification parental control tools abuse reporting tools and support for minors (Art. 35). Voluntary standards apply (Art. 44).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Programme to Promote the Development and Use of Artificial Intelligence in the Republic of Slovenia by 2025 (NpAI)
fr1_justif: NpAI explicitly links AI to “language technologies cultural identity and the art of research” and warns that digitalisation and English-language dominance can erode cultural differences and national languages. It states that AI can help ensure the effective use of the Slovene language in the digital environment and thus preserve cultural identity. It also states that AI can support preservation of cultural heritage and archival material through metadata creation indexing recognition and geospatial analysis (section 6.4 pp. 37–38). The framework aligns with the strategic direction of the National Development Strategy 2030 which includes 'Objective 4: Culture and language as fundamental factors of national identity' (p. 16).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Public Administration and the NpAI Steering Committee

fr1_title: Green Public Procurement Decree (Slovenia)
fr1_justif: The Decree regulates the environmental footprint of ICT infrastructure essential for AI. Article 6 (page 4) mandates environmental requirements for servers and data centers. Specifically items 31 and 32 define strict energy efficiency standards for the hardware used in AI deployment. For data centers it requires the calculation of Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE) according to ISO/IEC 30134 and mandates the recapture of waste heat for facilities with a total rated power exceeding 1MW. These provisions directly mitigate the carbon footprint and energy consumption associated with the high-performance computational power required for AI development.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Article 5 point 4 provides exclusions for armed forces and military procurement.
fr1_body_name: Ministrstvo pristojno za javna narocila ministrstvo pristojno za okolje resorna ministrstva in vladne slube (Ministry responsible for public procurement Ministry responsible for the environment sectoral ministries and government services) It is phrased like this because the responsibilities between ministries very often shift when governments change in Slovenia

fr1_justif: The law focuses on the risk classification system (Art. 6) and the requirements imposed on high-risk AI systems. It requires suppliers of high-risk AI systems to establish implement document and maintain a risk (and damage) management system throughout the system's lifecycle (Art. 9). Training validation and test datasets must conform to data governance in terms of representation error-freeness completeness contextualization and the function the system will be used for handling special personal data for monitoring purposes and detecting and correcting biases. High-risk AI systems must have consistency throughout the lifecycle by requiring appropriate levels of accuracy and robustness and reducing the risk of bias (art.15).

Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: National Programme to Promote the Development and Use of Artificial Intelligence in the Republic of Slovenia by 2025 (NpAI)
fr1_justif: The National Programme addresses public procurement through Strategic Objective 4 specifically Section 6.4 (Page 35) which focuses on deploying AI reference implementations in the public sector. The document explicitly states that standard procurement procedures are often too rigid for AI and therefore promotes 'innovation public procurement' (Measure 4.2 Page 38) to foster an agile environment. To implement this the programme utilizes specific instruments detailed in Table 4 (Page 62) and Appendix 3 (Page 90) including Pre-commercial procurement (PCP) for R&D services and Public Procurement of Innovative Solutions (PPI) for deploying mature technologies.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Public Administration and the NpAI Steering Committee

fr1_title: National Programme to Promote the Development and Use of Artificial Intelligence in the Republic of Slovenia by 2025 (NpAI)
fr1_justif: The framework addresses public sector skills under Strategic Objective 4 (p. 35). SO4 focuses on providing quality public services and increasing administrative efficiency with Point 4 specifically detailing 'Digital services of public administration' (p. 38). Implementation is driven by measures on p. 41. Measure 4.1 mandates professional training programmes (seminars courses) for development teams from the public sector. Measure 4.8 ensures ethical capacity by supporting education and awareness of the 'legal and ethical aspects of AI development and deployment' for public sector employees (p. 41).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Public Administration and the NpAI Steering Committee

fr1_title: National Programme to Promote the Development and Use of Artificial Intelligence in the Republic of Slovenia by 2025 (NpAI)
fr1_justif: The NpAI explicitly addresses workforce adaptation under Strategic Objective 2 (SO2): 'Education and strengthening of human resources.' It outlines measures to support 'lifelong learning' and 'reskilling' for employees to acquire AI-related skills (Measure 2.9). It aims to 'prevent the digital divide' by funding training programs for those whose jobs are threatened by automation. Specifically Measure 2.10 focuses on 'Forecasting future skills needs' to align training with market demands. The strategy also supports 'Digital Innovation Hubs' (DIHs) to act as intermediaries for SME workforce training.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Public Administration and the NpAI Steering Committee

fr1_title: Law No. 5 of 2023: Data Protection Act (Somalia)
fr1_justif: The Act includes the 'Right Not to Be Subject to Automated Decisions' (cited as one of the rights of a data subject in the articles. 20-23). This right mandates that individuals can request human intervention and an explanation if a decision significantly affecting them is based solely on automated processing (AI/algorithms). The second point formal complaint (Remedy Mechanism): The Act through Article 35 grants the 'Right to Lodge a Complaint' with the established Data Protection Authority (DPA). The DPA is mandated to investigate and enforce compliance providing the non-judicial remedy path for individuals whose rights including the right to challenge an automated decision have been violated.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Article 5 (3) (Exemptions) page 8 states: ' the law does not apply to processing of personal data' when carried out by competent authorities as 'necessary for national security' or for 'the prevention and investigation of crimes by public authorities national security and the prevention and defense of a legal claim. The Data Protection Authority (DPA) is explicitly mandated to comply with directions from the President and Cabinet in the interests of 'national security public order and the defence of the Federal Republic of Somalia' (Article 6 (4)(b)).

fr1_title: Law No. 5 of 2023: Data Protection Act (Somalia)
fr1_justif: The Act establishes the Right to object (Art. 22) and the Right not to be subject  to a decision based solely on automated decisions (Art. 23) including profiling when these produce legal or significant effects. Art. 22(1) allows data subjects to object to processing that causes substantial unwarranted damage or distress or violates Art. 23. Per Art. 22(2) controllers must cease processing unless public interest or legitimate grounds outweigh the damage. Additionally Art. 2 defines 'Data processing' as any collection storage or disclosure of personal data by electronic means. These provisions directly address human oversight and determination and provide a clear mechanism for redress.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 5(3) the law does not apply to data processing by competent authorities for national security or the prevention investigation and prosecution of criminal offences.

fr1_title: Law No. 5 of 2023: Data Protection Act (Somalia)
fr1_justif: The act addresses AI through Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) in Article 29 (p. 24) mentioning controllers must provide a systematic description of envisaged processing and evaluate necessity and proportionality. Article 23 (p. 20) regulates automated processing and profiling. Article 30 (p. 26) mandates prior consultation with the Data Protection Authority before deployment to ensure algorithmic accountability.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Article 5 (3) (Exemptions) page 8 states: ' the law does not apply to processing of personal data' when carried out by competent authorities as 'necessary for national security' or for 'the prevention and investigation of crimes by public authorities national security and the prevention and defense of a legal claim. The Data Protection Authority (DPA) is explicitly mandated to comply with directions from the President and Cabinet in the interests of 'national security public order and the defence of the Federal Republic of Somalia' (Article 6 (4)(b)).
