fr1_title: South Africa National Artificial Intelligence Policy Framework
fr1_justif: In the background section of the Framework it emphazises that it  'addresses the need for capacity building and economic development by promoting AI education and training programmes.' (p.1). The first strategic pillar of the framework is entitled 'Talent Development / Capacity Development' and is aimed at ensuring that South Africa has a robust AI talent pool which includes 'incorporat[ing] AI into the educational curricula from basic education to tertiary levels.' (p. 9 pillar 1).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Protection of Personal Information Act (No. 4 of 2013) (South Africa)
fr1_justif: Chapter 3 provides conditions for the lawful processing of personal information and ‘processing' is defined as 'any operation or activity or any set of operations whether or not by automatic means concerning personal information.' (Section 1). Section 74 read with Section 73(1) provides that a person can submit a claim to the Information Regulator for non-compliance with certain provisions of the Act including Chapter 3 which will be investigated by the Regulator. A settlement may be reached according to Section 80. But if an investigation conducted by the Regulator concludes that there has been non-compliance then individuals may institute a civil action for damages in terms of Section 99.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Article 6(1) of the Act specifically provides that: 'This Act does not apply to the processing of personal information—a)by or on behalf of a public body—i)which involves national security including activities that are aimed at assisting in the identification of the financing of terrorist and related activities defence or public safety...'

fr1_title: South Africa National Artificial Intelligence Policy Framework
fr1_justif: South Africa’s National AI Policy Framework explicitly includes a strategic pillar called “Promotion of Cultural and Human Values' (section 6.12.) Strategic pillars of this AI Policy are the fundamental components or key areas of focus that support and drive the implementation of the policy’s goals and objectives. They serve as the core framework around which the policy is structured providing clear guidance and direction for its development (pg. 9). Related is the pillar on Fairness and Mitigating Bias (section 6.9)  which provides -'Inclusive Data Sets: Ensure AI systems are trained on diverse data sets representing all demographics.'
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: South Africa National Artificial Intelligence Policy Framework
fr1_justif: The AI Policy framework integrates guidelines for ethical AI development including to ensure that AI systems are 'designed to promote fairness while mitigating biases'(p. 1) as well as 'discrimination' [p. 12] This is emphasized in two of the strategic pillars (fundamental components/key focus areas that support and drive the implementation of the policy's goals and objectives). Pillar 5 on 'Ethical AI Guidelines Development' provides for ethical development and deployment 'addressing issues which include bias [and] fairness' (p. 10). Pillar 9 is dedicated to Fairness and Mitigation Bias to 'ensure equitable AI development.' This includes developing methods to identify and mitigate biases in AI systems and inclusive datasets (p. 11).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: South Africa National Artificial Intelligence Policy Framework
fr1_justif: The AI Policy framework 'emphasizes the importance of human-centered AI ensuring that AI applications augment human decision-making rather than replace it'. (p. 1). This is especially captured in Pillar 10 - pillars are fundamental components/key focus areas that support and drive the implementation of the policy's goals and objectives - on 'Human Control of Technology (Human Centered Approach in AI Systems)  and requiring 'Human-in-the-Loop Systems: Ensure critical AI decisions involve human oversight (especially inGenerative AI)' and developing 'frameworks for AI decision-making that prioritize human judgment.' (p. 11)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: South Africa National Artificial Intelligence Policy Framework
fr1_justif: While the document does not specifically mention 'public sector skills development' it applies to both the private and public sectors. One of the framework's strategic pillars is Talent Development / Capacity Development (Pillar 1 pg. 9) which provides for 'Training Programmes: Develop specialized training and continuous learning programmes in AI.'
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: South Africa National Artificial Intelligence Policy Framework
fr1_justif: The first strategic pillar for the South Africa AI Policy is entitled Talent Development / Capacity Development aimed at 'ensur[ing] that South Africa has a robust AI talent pool'. It includes 'Training Programs: Develop specialized training and continuous learning programs in AI' and 'Industry Collaboration: Foster partnerships between academia and industry for real-world AI application training.' (p. 9).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: South Africa National Artificial Intelligence Policy Framework
fr1_justif: Pillar 7 (pillars are fundamental components/key focus areas that support and drive the implementation of the policy's goals and objectives) of the framework specifically provides for Safety and Security 'To protect citizens and infrastructure' (p.10). It has two components: 1) Cybersecurity Measures: Implement robust cybersecurity protocols to safeguard AI systems; 2) Risk Management: Develop frameworks to identify and mitigate risks associated with AI. (p. 10)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: South Africa National Artificial Intelligence Policy Framework
fr1_justif: The AI Policy framework is aimed at establishing robust data governance frameworks that sets standards for AI transparency and explainability (p. 1). This is emphasized in a strategic pillar for Transparency and Explainability (Pillar 8):  'Explainable AI: Promote the development of AI systems that provide clear understandable outputs (The ability to understand and interpret how AI systems arrive at their decisions or conclusions)' which includes principles of trust and acceptance accountability and bias detection and mitigation and insight and improvement; ' Transparency: The clear and open operation of AI systems ensuring that their processes decisionmaking criteria and outcomes are understandable and accessible...' (p.11).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Basic Act on the Development of Artificial Intelligence and Establishment of Trust (South Korea)
fr1_justif: Article 3(4) requires that national and local governments create policies that help all people “adapt stably” to social economic cultural and daily-life changes brought about by AI implicitly requiring public AI education. Article 13(2)(6) authorizes the government to conduct education and awareness-raising projects to improve public understanding of safe AI development safe use and proper usage practices. Article 29(4) requires polices for the promotion of education and public campaigns for a secure and trustworthy AI society. Article 26(3) assigns the Korea AI Promotion Association to conduct “education and promotion of the development and use of safe and reliable AI” making AI literacy an explicit national goal.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Article 4(2) states that “This Act shall not apply to AI developed and used solely for national defense or national security purposes as prescribed by Presidential Decree.” This means that while the Act applies horizontally across all civilian sectors including industry economy and government it carves out an exception for military and security-related AI systems.
fr1_body_name: National AI Committee AI Safety Institute Korea AI Promotion Association and Ministry of Science and ICT (MSIT)

fr1_title: Basic Act on the Development of Artificial Intelligence and Establishment of Trust (South Korea)
fr1_justif: The core discussion regarding deepfakes is found in Article 31 (Obligation to Secure Transparency) page 20. Paragraph 3 specifically targets outputs that are 'virtually indistinguishable from reality.' Providers must clearly notify or label such content so users are not deceived. The law balances this by allowing 'flexible' labeling for artistic or creative works to avoid ruining the viewer's experience provided the user is still made aware. While the Basic AI Act sets high-level rules the specific technical methods for how to label these deepfakes (e.g. visible watermarks vs. metadata) are detailed in Article 22 of the Enforcement Decree (which is not available in English).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Article 4(2) states that “This Act shall not apply to AI developed and used solely for national defense or national security purposes as prescribed by Presidential Decree.” This means that while the Act applies horizontally across all civilian sectors including industry economy and government it carves out an exception for military and security-related AI systems.
fr1_body_name: National AI Committee AI Safety Institute Korea AI Promotion Association and Ministry of Science and ICT (MSIT)

fr1_title: FRAMEWORK ACT ON INTELLIGENT INFORMATIZATION
fr1_justif: The Act meets the minimum threshold as it explicitly covers AI through its definition of “intelligent information technology” including technologies that perform learning reasoning and decision making (Art. 2(4)(a)). Article 63(1) requires rapid and fair remedial measures for user complaints and damage as well as systems for compensation and protection of rights. These apply to “intelligent information services” thereby covering AI enabled systems. Article 19(3) further provides avenues for petitions and mediation. While no AI specific procedural rights are set the Act establishes a general framework for complaints remedies and dispute resolution applicable to AI satisfying the indicator at a baseline level.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: heads of central administrative agencies and the heads of local governments

fr1_title: Guidelines for the protection of children's and adolescents' privacy
fr1_justif: The guidelines explicitly regulate AI-enabled services including conversational systems such as AI speakers chatbots and similar devices noting that they do not unfairly collect children’s personal information and that inappropriate content is not provided to children (p. 40). It also suggests limiting profiling-based services and requirements to implement protective measures where automated processing may negatively affect children (p. 41) and restrict the use of behavioral data for personalized advertising targeting children under 14 noting the need for prior consent and in many cases complete avoidance (p. 35) and discourage the use of manipulative “nudge” techniques that could exploit children’s decision-making (p. 36–37).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Basic Act on the Development of Artificial Intelligence and Establishment of Trust (South Korea)
fr1_justif: The Basic AI Act does not explicitly regulate fairness or non-discrimination it references these principles indirectly. Article 1 (p. 1) frames the Act around protecting “human rights and dignity” and Article 3(2) (p. 4) requires providing “meaningful explanations” to affected persons. Article 27(1) (p. 17) establishes ethical principles such as safety reliability and accessibility yet omits fairness or equity. It mentions safety and risk-management obligations in Articles 32–34 (pp. 20–21) and the impact-assessment requirement in Article 35(1) (p. 22) but it does not mention bias and non-discrimination. Chapter 2 Article 6 (5): 'Measures for establishing a foundation of trust ensuring the fairness transparency accountability ..
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Article 4 (Scope of Application) states: 'This Act shall not apply to AI developed and used solely for national defense or national security purposes as prescribed by Presidential Decree.'
fr1_body_name: National AI Committee AI Safety Institute Korea AI Promotion Association and Ministry of Science and ICT (MSIT)

fr1_title: Basic Act on the Development of Artificial Intelligence and Establishment of Trust (South Korea)
fr1_justif: Article 34 (p. 21) of the AI Basic Act (“Responsibilities of High-Impact AI Business Operators”) mandates human management and supervision of high-impact AI systems (#4) effectively establishing a requirement for human oversight. However this obligation applies only to the ten categories of high-impact AI specifically defined in Article 2 (p. 2).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: The AI Basic Act of South Korea excludes AI systems developed and used exclusively for national defense or national security purposes from its scope. This exemption is explicitly stated in Article 4(2) (p. 4) of the Act which specifies that the Act does not apply to such AI systems.
fr1_body_name: National AI Committee AI Safety Institute Korea AI Promotion Association and Ministry of Science and ICT (MSIT)

fr1_title: Basic Act on the Development of Artificial Intelligence and Establishment of Trust (South Korea)
fr1_justif: The Act addresses impact assessments primarily through Article 35 (Impact Assessment of High-Impact AI). This provision requires business operators who develop or use designated high-impact AI systems to conduct AI impact assessments to evaluate potential risks including those related to safety reliability and social or ethical implications.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Article 4 (Scope of Application) states that the Act shall not apply to AI developed and used solely for national defense or national security purposes. That means that the defense and national security uses of AI are functionally outside the scope of the Basic Act excluding them also from the impact assessment provisions in Article 35.
fr1_body_name: National AI Committee AI Safety Institute Korea AI Promotion Association and Ministry of Science and ICT (MSIT)

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems
fr1_title: Basic Act on the Development of Artificial Intelligence and Establishment of Trust (South Korea)
fr1_justif: The Act applies to government agencies when they develop operate or use AI systems in an operational or service-delivery capacity. Article 2(7) defines an “AI business operator” to include government agencies engaged in business related to the AI industry (p. 3). Accordingly Articles 30 and 31 require transparency and disclosure when AI systems are used including informing users when they interact with AI and clearly labeling AI-generated content (pp. 19–20). In addition where government systems qualify as high-impact AI Articles 33–35 require prior review and impact assessment to ensure safety rights protection and accountability (pp. 20–22).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Article 4(2) states that “This Act shall not apply to AI developed and used solely for national defense or national security purposes as prescribed by Presidential Decree.” This means that while the Act applies horizontally across all civilian sectors including industry economy and government it carves out an exception for military and security-related AI systems.
fr1_body_name: National AI Committee AI Safety Institute Korea AI Promotion Association and Ministry of Science and ICT (MSIT)

fr1_title: Basic Act on the Development of Artificial Intelligence and Establishment of Trust (South Korea)
fr1_justif: The Basic Act explicitly addresses human-resource development in Article 6(2)(2) and Article 21. Article 6 requires that the Basic Plan include measures for “training professional talent” and establishing a foundation for developing and utilizing AI. Article 21 mandates that the Minister of Science and ICT “train and support AI and AI technology expert talents” to advance AI technology and the AI industry and allows the government to recruit and support overseas AI experts. Together these provisions form the legal basis for Korea’s AI-related reskilling upskilling and talent-development policies.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Article 4(2) states that “This Act shall not apply to AI developed and used solely for national defense or national security purposes as prescribed by Presidential Decree.” This means that while the Act applies horizontally across all civilian sectors including industry economy and government it carves out an exception for military and security-related AI systems.
fr1_body_name: National AI Committee AI Safety Institute Korea AI Promotion Association and Ministry of Science and ICT (MSIT)

fr1_title: Basic Act on the Development of Artificial Intelligence and Establishment of Trust (South Korea)
fr1_justif: The Basic AI Act addresses safety and security through a set of obligations designed to ensure the responsible development and use of AI technologies. Article 32 establishes the duty of AI operators to secure the safety and reliability of AI systems. Article 34 outlines specific responsibilities for high-impact AI operators requiring human oversight and risk management to prevent harm. The Act also authorizes the Ministry of Science and ICT (MSIT) to investigate violations and enforce corrective actions reinforcing accountability (Article 40 page 24). Overall the Act embeds safety and security as core principles to maintain public trust and prevent risks arising from AI deployment.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Article 4 (2) states the following: 'This Act shall not apply to AI developed and used solely for national defense or national security purposes as prescribed by Presidential Decree.'
fr1_body_name: National AI Committee AI Safety Institute Korea AI Promotion Association and Ministry of Science and ICT (MSIT)

fr1_title: Basic Act on the Development of Artificial Intelligence and Establishment of Trust (South Korea)
fr1_justif: The Act embeds transparency and explainability across multiple provisions. Art. 3(2) (p.4) grants affected persons a right to a “clear and meaningful explanation” of key criteria and principles behind AI outcomes. Art. 31 requires prior notice when services use High-Impact AI or GenAI and mandates clear labeling of AI-generated content. Art. 34 (p.21) obliges High-Impact AI operators to implement explainability plans disclose key factors and training data summaries. Art. 6(2)(5) includes transparency fairness and accountability in the AI Basic Plan.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Article 4(2) states: “This Act shall not apply to AI developed and used solely for national defense or national security purposes as prescribed by Presidential Decree.” This means that while the Act applies horizontally across all civilian sectors including industry economy and government it carves out an exception for military and national security-related AI systems.
fr1_body_name: National AI Committee AI Safety Institute Korea AI Promotion Association and Ministry of Science and ICT (MSIT)

fr1_title: 2024 Artificial Intelligence Strategy (Spain)
fr1_justif: The 2024 Spanish National AI Strategy addresses AI literacy through capacity-building and formal education (Initiative 4.2 pp. 34–35). It provides “specific training in AI” under Investment 4 (Component 19) funds AI research talent programmes and supports high-TRL AI R&D projects (p. 34). It embeds AI in education via curricular development of computational thinking and digital skills teacher training and Código Escuela 4.0 (2023–2026) (p. 35). It also expands AI university degrees vocational AI modules and includes AI ethics in programmes (p. 35).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Secretary of State for Digitalisation and AI

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Bots fake accounts and automated conduct are mechanisms of disinformation campaigns (recital 85) and as such rules are needed to combat them (recital 9). Systemic risk assessments apply to large-scale online platforms and search engines (art. 34) and explicitly include the dissemination or amplification of misleading or fraudulent content and the negative effects on gender-based violence. This includes recommendation systems and other relevant algorithmic systems (art. 34). Mitigation measures include testing and adapting algorithmic and recommendation systems; speeding up the processing of notifications of illegal hate speech and cyber violence; and labeling content generated or manipulated by AI (art.35).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU GDPR
fr1_justif: The law provides a layered redress framework that applies in AI contexts whenever personal data is processed. Data subjects harmed by automated processing based on AI-driven profiling and decision-making hold the right to express their point of view and the right to contest the decision (art. 22); presenting complaints to supervisory authorities (art. 77) resorting to legal measures against gaps in the authorities (art. 78) and bringing proceedings directly against controllers or processors (art. 79). Organizations may be mandated to exercise the rights collectively on behalf of data subjects (art. 80). Supervisory authorities may impose administrative fines because of the violations of core provisions (art. 83).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: This Regulation shall not apply to the processing of personal data by Member States in the context of carrying out activities relating to the Union's common foreign and security policy (recital 16). The processing of personal data by Member States does not apply to activities related to the protection and prevention of threats to public security (Art. 2).
fr1_body_name: The law establishes the European Data Protection Board is comprised of representatives from the supervisory authority of each member states (Arts 68) acting independently (Art 69) providing guidelines recommendations and binding decisions to ensure consistence across Member States (Art 70) The Member State must to establish am independent supervisory authority responsible for monitoring and enforcing the regulation (Art 51)

fr1_title: EU Digital Services Act
fr1_justif: Online platform service providers must ensure the privacy security and protection of minors in their services (Art. 28) including prohibiting advertising based on profiling through the automated processing of data about children (Art. 28). Terms and conditions must be understandable to minors (Art. 14) ensuring a high level of privacy and security by default based on good practices (recitals 70 71). Large-scale online platforms and large-scale search engines systematic risk assessment applies periodically evaluating potential negative effects on minors (Art. 34). Mitigation measures are determined: age verification parental control tools abuse reporting tools and support for minors (Art. 35). Voluntary standards apply (Art. 44).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: 2024 Artificial Intelligence Strategy (Spain)
fr1_justif: Most actions relate to fundamental models and language. Initiative 3.2 aims at developing fundamental models in Spanish and co-official languages creating a family of “Iberian” models focused exclusively on Spanish Portuguese and co-official languages. Sectoral applications are also planned (p. 28). Initiative 3.3 aims to generate specialized or task-specific language models discriminative models i.e. models specialized in a particular domain  that consider inclusion and equal opportunities in the technological ecosystem (p. 2829).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Secretary of State for Digitalisation and AI

fr1_title: 2024 Artificial Intelligence Strategy (Spain)
fr1_justif: The Strategy addresses environmental impact by integrating sustainability requirements into AI infrastructure and model development notably through measures to reduce the energy water and carbon footprint of data centres and AI training and inference (Lever 2 p. 19–21). It promotes “sustainable AI” and “Green AI” via standards voluntary certification schemes and assessment frameworks that explicitly include environmental impact as an evaluation criteria for AI systems (p. 21 and 31). The document also highlights AI applications for environmental and climate-related goals such as energy efficiency renewable energy optimisation water resource management agriculture and biodiversity protection (p. 21–22).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Secretary of State for Digitalisation and AI

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The law focuses on the risk classification system (Art. 6) and the requirements imposed on high-risk AI systems. It requires suppliers of high-risk AI systems to establish implement document and maintain a risk (and damage) management system throughout the system's lifecycle (Art. 9). Training validation and test datasets must conform to data governance in terms of representation error-freeness completeness contextualization and the function the system will be used for handling special personal data for monitoring purposes and detecting and correcting biases. High-risk AI systems must have consistency throughout the lifecycle by requiring appropriate levels of accuracy and robustness and reducing the risk of bias (art.15).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act addresses gender equality through non-discrimination and fundamental rights provisions. Training datasets for high-risk AI must be representative avoinding the discriminatory outcomes and protecting atributes explicitly related gender (Art. 10). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights impact assessments before deploying high-risk AI incluing gender-based discrimination (Art. 27). Equality bodies are granted market surveillance powers enabling them to investigate and sanction AI systems producing discriminatory outcomes (Art. 74).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

Recital 24 explains that this exclusion is due to Art. 4(2) of TEU which notes that national security remains the sole responsibility of Member States but that if a defense or security system is used outside this scope then it temporary would fall within the regulation.

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act establishes requirements to ensure human control over high-risk AI systems requiring providers to design systems that enable natural persons with a minimum level of comprehensibility to monitor outputs detect anomalies and intervene or interrupt operations when necessary (art. 14). Deployers must assign oversight to competent natural persons with authority to act (art. 26). For general-purpose AI with systemic risk adversarial testing and incident reporting reinforce human control (Arts. 55 73). Additional oversight provisions are set in the fundamental rights impact assessment (art. 27) and Annex (IV) 2(e) as part of technical documentation requirements (art. 11).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Art. 2(3) states that: 'does not apply to AI systems where and in so far they are placed on the market put into service or used with or without modification exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' and 'does not apply to AI systems which are not placed on the market or put into service in the Union where the output is used in the Union exclusively for military defence or national security purposes...' fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Law establishes an assessment architecture according to the type and level of risk of the system. High-risk AI suppliers must identify analyze and mitigate risks during the design development and implementation phases within a risk management system (Arti.9) with comprehensive technical documentation (Art.15) and monitoring of operations and anomalies (Art. 26). For AI implementers in the public sector a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment applies before implementing high-risk systems listed in Annex 3 (Art.27). Compliance assessment must include self-assessment or third-party audits depending on the criticality of the system (Art. 43 47). There are ex-post assessments and post-commercialization monitoring (Art.72).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: The Act classifies systems for recruitment promotion termination and worker management as high-risk AI systems (Annex III(4)) (art. 6). Prohibited AI practices include bans on emotion-recognition in the workplace (art. 5). Providers must implement risk-management addressing discrimination (Art. 9) ensure representative datasets and bias-control (art. 10) and maintain technical documentation (Arts. 11). Deployers must conduct fundamental rights assessments before deployment (art. 27). Art. 26 (7-9) establishes safeguards: workers must be informed and may request explanations of AI use. However the Act does not regulate pay conditions or compensation so it mitigates risks without constituting a full labour-rights regime.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: 2024 Artificial Intelligence Strategy (Spain)
fr1_justif: The AI Strategy barely meets the indicator threshold. It signals the need to upskill public administration staff and a more capable public workforce for AI adoption but it does not operationalise this into a concrete public AI training framework. The document links AI use in the public sector to workforce modernisation and talent retention but these references are embedded within broader narratives on responsible AI deployment and public service improvement (pp. 31–36 38). No curricula target groups or implementation timelines are specified. Overall the approach reflects recognition of skills needs but not a defined AI capacity-building framework for the public administration.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Secretary of State for Digitalisation and AI

fr1_title: 2024 Artificial Intelligence Strategy (Spain)
fr1_justif: The 2024 AI strategy includes section 4 “fostering talent in AI” which includes a plan and investment for “promoting AI Skills”. To further develop the fostering of AI talent the AI National strategy includes a 30M€ budget for the training and upskilling in AI of digital professionals (page 34). The strategy objectives on talent development and digital skills are operationalised through different actions including AI training programmes and sub-initiatives such as Generación D and Red.es courses. In addition The Strategy’s fostering talent in AI section (page 34) references and provides extra funding for the preexisting “component 19” which is a separate investment government led initiative focused on building digital capabilities across
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Secretary of State for Digitalisation and AI

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Safety and security is core to the Act. It establishes a risk-based framework for risk identification and mitigation (Art. 9) oversight measures (Art. 14) and robustness and accuracy requirements (Art. 15). To ensure ongoing safety the Act mandates technical documentation and record-keeping (Arts. 11–12) and in certain high risk situations a conformity assessment procedure prior to market placement (Art. 43). Post-market obligations in Arts. 72–73 require providers and deployers to monitor performance report serious incidents and take corrective action. For GPAI models posing systemic risks Arts. 55–56 impose additional safety and cybersecurity duties including model evaluation and risk mitigation following the Code of Practice.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: EU AI Act
fr1_justif: Articles 11 12 and 13 establish several transparency and documentation obligations for AI developers and deployers. High-risk AI systems must include clear information on their capabilities limitations and intended purpose (Art. 13) and providers must maintain comprehensive technical documentation and automatic logging to ensure traceability (Arts. 11–12). These measures enable deployers to better understand the systems and authorities to audit or investigate when necessary. There are disclosure obligations that require notification when individuals interact with AI systems particularly chatbots and emotion recognition tools (Art. 50). Also for general-purpose AI in terms of model documentation and summary of training data (Art. 53).

fr1_title: Annexes: Sri Lanka's National Strategy on AI (July 2024 - Draft Strategy for Public Consultation)
fr1_justif: The evidence addresses this indicator under section 1.2 of Annex 1 which outlines a set of educational initiatives aimed at embedding AI literacy within Sri Lankan society (pp. 7–9). Initiative 1.2.7 notes that the common digital skills framework should be updated “as part of the implementation of the Digital Strategy 2030 to include Artificial Intelligence as well as to establish a formal measurable definition of ‘AI literacy’ that can characterize different levels of AI skills” (p. 7). This update would be undertaken in collaboration with the Ministry of Education the National Centre for Artificial Intelligence (NCAI) the Data Transformation Agency (DTA) and the Sri Lanka Association for Software and Services Companies (SLASSCOM).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Personal Data Protection Act (PDPA) No. 9 of 2022 (Sri Lanka)
fr1_justif: While the law doesn’t mention AI it directly references automate processing and automated decision-making. It provides general mechanisms for remedy relating to personal data processing including accountability measures for controllers in processing personal data (Section 12 pp. 6-7); rights of data subjects (Part II pp. 7-13); personal data breach notifications (Section 23 p. 17); and authority review and orders for compensation (Section 35 pp. 31-32). Section 18 further provides that every data subject has the right to request a review of decisions based solely on automated processing (p. 11). Additionally Section 19 establishes an appeal and judicial review mechanism for data subjects to appeal to the Authority (pp. 12-13).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Data Protection Authority of Sri Lanka

fr1_title: Annexes: Sri Lanka's National Strategy on AI (July 2024 - Draft Strategy for Public Consultation)
fr1_justif: Annex 11 the “White Paper on Enabling Inclusive AI through Local Language Models” outlines the national initiative to develop LLMs in Sinhala and Tamil enabling all Sri Lankans to ‘access digital services support educational and governmental initiatives and preserve cultural heritage embedded in the nation’s languages’ (Executive Summary p. 195). Three key proposals include developing local language LLMs data curation for AI training and building infrastructure and partnerships (p. 195). The initiative aims to enhance educational access improve public service accessibility drive economic growth through local AI integration and safeguard cultural and linguistic diversity (Benefit Overview pp.195-196).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Annexes: Sri Lanka's National Strategy on AI (July 2024 - Draft Strategy for Public Consultation)
fr1_justif: On page 86 subsection 1.2 of Annex 4 outlines sustainability as a primary objective emphasizing that sustainable models for AI implementation should be developed to adapt to “evolving landscapes and changing citizen needs ensuring long term benefits”. Further subsection 2.1 underscores the importance of establishing sustainable and future-ready AI noting that AI initiatives must consider the environmental impact of AI systems including “energy consumption of data centres and leveraging AI to address climate change and environmental challenges” (p. 89).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Annexes: Sri Lanka's National Strategy on AI (July 2024 - Draft Strategy for Public Consultation)
fr1_justif: On page 90 under Section 2.3 of Annex 4 the document promotes ethics by design emphasizing the use of algorithms and data sources that minimize bias. It mandates bias detection and mitigation processes including regular audits of training data algorithms and outputs. These measures extend explicitly to gender ethnicity age or other protected characteristics.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Annexes: Sri Lanka's National Strategy on AI (July 2024 - Draft Strategy for Public Consultation)
fr1_justif: On page 90 under Section 2.3 of Annex 4 the document briefly mentions measures for mitigating potential biases including “biases related to gender” in ethical AI development and deployment. In addition the proposed strategy explicitly identifies gender inclusivity as a core element of the human capital component (p. 222). One proposed approach as outlined on page 223 under “Table 2: Coverage of Sub-Indicators of the Technology Pillar” is to promote STEM education among females. Importantly on page 8 Action 1.2.11 of Annex 1 highlights the need to conduct AI literacy programs for underrepresented groups including girls to ensure inclusive participation within the AI ecosystem.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Annexes: Sri Lanka's National Strategy on AI (July 2024 - Draft Strategy for Public Consultation)
fr1_justif: The Act addresses human oversight primarily through ex post review and appeal mechanisms. Section 18 (pp. 11–12) grants data subjects the right to request a review of decisions based solely on automated processing implying human intervention. Section 19 (pp. 12–13) establishes a right to appeal such decisions to the Authority which can investigate and determine their lawfulness with further appeal to courts. Schedule V (pp. 58–59) requires transparency about automated decision-making. Additionally Section 33 (pp. 29–31) empowers the Authority to regulate automated decision-making and ensure compliance.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.
fr1_body_name: Data Protection Authority of Sri Lanka

fr1_title: Personal Data Protection Act (PDPA) No. 9 of 2022 (Sri Lanka)
fr1_justif: The Act addresses impact assessments through a privacy-centric framework applicable to AI systems that process personal data. Section 24(1) requires controllers to conduct Personal Data Protection Impact Assessments prior to high-risk processing including profiling and systematic monitoring. These assessments must identify risks of harm to data subjects and specify mitigation measures and safeguards and must be updated where there are changes in technology or methodology (Sections 24(2) and 24(4) p. 18). Section 25 obliges controllers to mitigate identified risks before processing and where risks remain to consult the Authority which is empowered to require additional safeguards or the cessation of processing.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Data Protection Authority of Sri Lanka

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Annexes: Sri Lanka's National Strategy on AI (July 2024 - Draft Strategy for Public Consultation)
fr1_justif: Initiative 2.2 of Annex 1 underscores that when developing the AI Playbook for the public sector to drive government AI adoption it must include “procurement guidelines that favour open-source and open-standards technology while also limiting vendor lock-in” (p. 16). Additionally the finance pillar of the operational model of the National Center for Artificial Intelligence (NCAI) is responsible for “managing contracts and procurement processes to support AI project implementation” (Appendix B10 p. 84).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Annexes: Sri Lanka's National Strategy on AI (July 2024 - Draft Strategy for Public Consultation)
fr1_justif: The evidence addresses public sector skills development under Section 1.2 of Annex 1 which sets out a series of preliminary initiatives aimed at public sector training and competency development (pp. 6–7). For instance it outlines concrete actions to build AI capacity among civil servants including AI awareness courses and AI project design and management training for senior officials (Actions 1.2.1–1.2.2 p. 6). It further proposes updating the Digital Government Competency Framework (DGCF) and defining AI and data competencies for public sector roles (Actions 1.2.3–1.2.4 p. 7). The annex also includes training for the judiciary and relevant regulatory agencies on responsible AI regulation and governance (Action 1.2.6 p. 8).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Annexes: Sri Lanka's National Strategy on AI (July 2024 - Draft Strategy for Public Consultation)
fr1_justif: On page 54 of Annex 3 as part of the immediate setup and functions of the National Center for Artificial Intelligence (NCAI) capacity building and talent development are identified as a key pillar outlining efforts to “establish AI learning pathways” with a focus on up-skilling the current workforce and integrating AI modules into higher education. Initiatives 1.2.16 and 1.2.17 mention the launch of a “Data and AI Skills Development Fund” to support upskilling and reskilling initiatives alongside data- and AI-focused vocational training for professionals affected by job displacement (Annex 1 p. 9). In addition postgraduate AI programs aim to upskill existing professionals with advanced technical knowledge (Annex 7 p. 158).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Annexes: Sri Lanka's National Strategy on AI (July 2024 - Draft Strategy for Public Consultation)
fr1_justif: The trust and safety pillar of the National Center for Artificial Intelligence (NCAI) seeks to ensure the responsible development and use of AI through legal and ethical oversight (Appendix B9 p. 83). Its mandate includes developing AI governance frameworks monitoring compliance and managing risks associated with AI deployment. Safety and security are further operationalised through performance metrics with KPIs on accuracy reliability system uptime and stability (Annex 6 pp. 118–119; Annex 7 pp. 142–143). Annex 7 additionally introduces a system-specific AI development lifecycle requiring rigorous testing to ensure functionality and accuracy prior to deployment (p. 136).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Annexes: Sri Lanka's National Strategy on AI (July 2024 - Draft Strategy for Public Consultation)
fr1_justif: On page 19 Action 3.4 of Annex 1 states that an AI Governance Framework must be developed in line with Sri Lanka’s AI governance approach addressing harm disclosure impact assessments transparency risk mitigation human oversight and redress mechanisms. Annex 4 further notes that government agencies are to use AI to enhance efficiency transparency and accessibility for improved public service delivery (p. 86). It emphasises that trustworthy and transparent AI is “fundamental to building public trust” and requires robust ethical standards privacy safeguards and security protocols aligned with international frameworks (p. 88). Subsection 9.2 further outlines criteria to ensure transparency in government AI decision-making (p. 102).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy in the State of Palestine
fr1_justif: The framework addresses the indicator by including a project on “artificial intelligence in culture protecting Palestinian content and natural language processing” (p. 10) thereby linking AI development to cultural context and local content preservation. It also promotes the use of Arabic language processing technologies to enhance accessibility and usability of AI systems in the local context (p. 10). In addition the strategy recognises the role of AI in fostering inclusion noting its potential to “integrate marginalized groups into initiatives that promote human development and self-improvement” (p. 7).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Ministry of Communications and Information Technology the Ministry of Higher Education and Scientific Research the Ministry of Entrepreneurship and Empowerment

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy in the State of Palestine
fr1_justif: While the strategy includes broad capacity-building measures across public and private sectors it also contains specific provisions targeting the public sector bringing it within the scope of the indicator. In particular the guidance media and implementation plans explicitly provide for building and developing governmental capabilities and skills in artificial intelligence and supporting exchange programs (pp. 8 11–12 18). In addition the strategy mandates raising awareness of artificial intelligence across all government agencies (p. 9) and emphasizes the necessity of digital transformation within the government sector (p. 5).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Ministry of Communications and Information Technology the Ministry of Higher Education and Scientific Research the Ministry of Entrepreneurship and Empowerment

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Policy
fr1_justif: The framework explicitly identifies the need to “build capacity and adapt to the rapid changes in skills and requirements of the intelligence-based labor market” (p. 3) directly linking AI deployment to workforce transformation. It supports this through measures such as training and education across sectors and levels from foundational skills to lifelong learning (p. 4) and integrating AI and data competencies into the education system including training for teachers and administrators (p. 8). It further calls for reskilling and upskilling in response to labor market changes (p. 10) alongside workshops awareness programs and investment in local talent through scholarships and training (pp. 8 10).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Federal Act on Data Protection (Switzerland)
fr1_justif: The law serves as a pillar to combat manipulation disinformation or violence when it involves the processing of personal data by AI systems: profiling high-risk profiling (Art. 5) high-risk profiling carried out by private entities or any profiling by federal agencies (Art. 6); protection against manipulation and automated decisions (Art. 21); impact assessment (Art. 22); and sanctions (Art. 60). The communication from the Federal Commissioner clarifies that the law is directly applicable to data processing by AI (https://www.edoeb.admin.ch/en/update-current-legislation-directly-applicable-ai). It states that 'the use of programs that allow the falsification of faces images or voice messages of identifiable people must be indicated.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Federal Data Protection and Information Commissioner (FDPIC)

fr1_title: Federal Act on Data Protection (Switzerland)
fr1_justif: The data subject may request that the automated individual decision be reviewed by a natural person. (Art. 21) The data subject may request that incorrect personal data be corrected; an individual can request that a specific data processing activity be prohibited and that the personal data be deleted or destroyed. (Art. 32) A person can view automated decisions they have the right to stop the unlawful processing of the personal data concerned redress the consequences of unlawful processing declare the processing to be unlawful. (Art. 41) While protection in the context of automated decisions is clearly stated a specific connection to redress and remedy in the context of AI is missing. However such connection can be implicitly assumed.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Federal Data Protection and Information Commissioner (FDPIC)

fr1_title: Guidelines on Artificial Intelligence for the Confederation (Switzerland)
fr1_justif: Guideline 1: Putting people first (p. 3) states that the use of AI should help improve people's quality of life and sustainably develop our society. Guideline 2: Regulatory conditions for the development and application of AI: AI should help ensure high-quality living standards based on the principles of sustainable development (p. 3). AI offers the potential for economic growth prosperity security and employment as well as for reducing the ecological footprint and energy consumption and this potential should be explored in the best possible way (p. 4).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Guidelines on Artificial Intelligence for the Confederation (Switzerland)
fr1_justif: Individuals groups and genders must be protected from discrimination and stigmatisation (page 3). The Confederation must ensure well-balanced high-quality data are used or that 'suitable compensatory safeguards' are put in place to counter inherent bias (page 3). The use of AI should actively create equal opportunities for people and 'encourage and facilitate their access to education goods services and technology (page 3). AI applications likely to affect fundamental rights should be accompanied by an 'Equality Impact Assessment' (EIA) as a frame of reference in addition to continuous monitoring and safeguards (page 3).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Guidelines on Artificial Intelligence for the Confederation (Switzerland)
fr1_justif: Preservation of Human Agency: The guiding principle is to prioritize people mandating that Self-determination should be preserved and that individuals must be able to participate in life in an autonomous manner. The role of AI is to complement and enhance human activities and skills. (page 3) Non-Delegation of Responsibility: The framework strictly limits AI's authority under Guideline 4 (Accountability) which explicitly states: 'It must not be possible to delegate responsibility to machines' (page 5) Legal Mandate for Human Review: The Federal Act on Data Protection (FADP) provides the mandatory 'human-in-the-loop' mechanism: Individuals have the right to review automated decision(page 10)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Guidelines on Artificial Intelligence for the Confederation (Switzerland)
fr1_justif: The document's core purpose for mandating Impact Assessment is to achieve proactive risk management and prevent harm across multiple dimensions. The guidelines mandate proactive assessments by requiring that AI applications be designed with 'ethics by design'. This ensures safeguards are integrated from the planning stage to eliminate or mitigate risks early preventing reactive crisis management. Assessments are explicitly required where AI is likely to affect fundamental rights necessitating the upfront evaluation of potential societal harm including discrimination and bias. They demand the tracking and assessing of impact across a broad scope: individuals society the economy and the environment.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Federal Act on Data Protection (Switzerland)
fr1_justif: The document addresses specifically profiling which means any form of automated processing of personal data consisting of the use of personal data to evaluate certain personal aspects relating to a natural person in particular to analyse or predict aspects concerning that natural person's performance at work. (Art. 5)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Federal Data Protection and Information Commissioner (FDPIC)

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems
fr1_title: Strategy Use of AI systems in the Federal Administration (Switzerland)
fr1_justif: The strategy sets parameters for the Federal Administration's digital transformation using AI. It emphasizes that the administration must lead by example and use AI systems in accordance with shared principles including legal/ethical principles ensuring public interest and placing high value on trustworthiness including explainability transparency data protection accuracy. (page 3) The main goal for public disclosure is found under field of action 2 'Earning trust': The expected benefit is that internal and external stakeholders from the general public will 'gain transparency about which Al systems are used in the Federal Administration and how their use is monitored'. The use of AI systems 'must remain traceable'. (page 6)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Strategy Use of AI systems in the Federal Administration (Switzerland)
fr1_justif: The document addresses public procurement of AI primarily by linking it to the overarching core principles for AI use. It includes an unprioritized strategic goal explicitly stating that procurement legislation and organizational principles must 'enable and promote procurement of Al systems in accordance with the principles for the use of Al systems in the Federal Administration' (Appendix page 10). The goal integrates specific requirements into the procurement process namely the core principles (page 3) which mandate that procured systems must be: trustworthy (explainability transparency data protection accuracy); governed by clearly defined responsibilities and accountability across the entire Al system's life cycle. (pages 3 10)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Strategy Use of AI systems in the Federal Administration (Switzerland)
fr1_justif: The document addresses public sector skills development as its dedicated field of action 1: Developing skills (page 4). The strategic objective is to ensure the Federal Administration has the necessary skills and knowledge to competently design and use AI systems (page 5). The primary goal is that employees have access to training and further education programmes on the use of AI that are appropriate to their level and role and that they are prepared for changes resulting from AI use (page 5). New skills are explicitly required in fields such as AI data science law and business management (page 4).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Guidelines on Artificial Intelligence for the Confederation (Switzerland)
fr1_justif: The document addresses specifically safety in the context of AI namely Guideline 5 discusses the safety of AI systems and that they have to be designed to be safe robust and resilient. (page 5) Importantly it does not include security.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Guidelines on Artificial Intelligence for the Confederation (Switzerland)
fr1_justif: To ensure trustworthiness the guidelines impose core requirements for transparency traceability and explainability and confirm that the existing federal legal framework such as the Data Protection Act (FADP) fully applies to all AI applications. (page 4)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_justif: The document Literacy in AI usage among its key objectives - section 3 subsection 2 provision #60; It also outlines establishing of AI courses and department in education institutions section 4 subsection 2 provision #84

fr1_title: Strategy for the Development of Artificial Intelligence in theRepublic of Tajikistan for the Period up to 2040
fr1_justif: The strategy explicitly includes public sector capacity-building in the context of artificial intelligence. It provides for the creation of institutional structures within government bodies and addresses personnel shortages (p. 7) while mandating training increasing AI literacy and integrating AI into public administration (p. 8). It further introduces concrete measures to improve the qualifications of civil servants and retrain personnel within government structures (pp. 16–17) particularly in relation to digital governance and AI-based systems.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: The Strategy on Development of Artificial Intelligence in the Republic of Tajikistan until 2040
fr1_justif: The strategy states as one of the principles that AI should be human and human security-centered  —simple understandable and beneficial and that it also should ensure information security protection of human dignity Section 3 sub-section 2 provisions # 59-60 page 9
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: The Strategy on Development of Artificial Intelligence in the Republic of Tajikistan until 2040
fr1_justif: The strategy states that AI should be used based on principle of transparency. This is outline among key objectives of the strategy section 3 paragraph 2 point #60. It also contains a provision that states that AI should be simple understandable and useful - section 3 subsection 2 point #59
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Action Plan for the Development of Thailand (2022–2027)
fr1_justif: In the NAIS Strategy 3: Enhancing Human Resource Capacity and Advancing AI Education aims to promote AI literacy among the public by equipping people with the knowledge understanding and skills needed to use artificial intelligence responsibly and beneficially in ways that are appropriate and aligned with their level of education and age group. (pp.147-148)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Minister of Education and Ministry of Higher Education Science Research and Innovation (p149)

fr1_title: AI Ethics Guideline (Thailand)
fr1_justif: AI systems should be designed developed and deployed to generate benefits for humans society and the environment throughout their lifecycle (p.17). The document requires accountability mechanisms including internal and external audits of data algorithms deployment and outcomes to identify and mitigate environmental and societal risks (p.19). Designers and developers must ensure data integrity ethical compliance and environmental protection (p.20). AI should be developed sustainably adapt to different environments and include built-in risk mitigation security recovery mechanisms and continuous testing under real-world conditions to avoid harm to human life and the environment (pp.23–33).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: AI Ethics Guideline (Thailand)
fr1_justif: The Guideline embeds fairness and non-discrimination across the entire lifecycle of AI systems. It requires AI design and development to recognise social diversity and actively reduce exclusion and bias with the aim of maximising benefits for society particularly for disadvantaged and vulnerable groups. It also mandates that key decisions in AI research design deployment and use be demonstrably fair rather than assumed to be neutral. To achieve this the Guideline emphasises diverse research teams representative datasets and technical and procedural mechanisms to assess and verify fairness in practice (p. 13-14).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: AI Ethics Guideline (Thailand)
fr1_justif: Under the topic of Laws Ethics and International Standards the AI Ethics guideline clearly states that artificial intelligence should be designed based on a human-centred approach with humans as the final decision-makers.(p.10)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: AI Ethics Guideline (Thailand)
fr1_justif: Under the topic of Laws Ethics and International Standards the AI Ethics Guideline states that public procurement should require AI developers and service providers to comply with transparency principles conduct impact assessments on how data processing affects human rights and privacy and assume responsibility for any negative consequences arising from the use of artificial intelligence (p.18).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: AI Ethics Guideline (Thailand)
fr1_justif: Under the topic of Laws Ethics and International Standards the AI Ethics Guideline states that public procurement should require AI developers and service providers to comply with transparency principles conduct impact assessments on how data processing affects human rights and privacy and assume responsibility for any negative consequences arising from the use of artificial intelligence. (p.18)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Action Plan for the Development of Thailand (2022–2027)
fr1_justif: In the NAIS Strategy 3: Enhancing Human Resource Capacity and Advancing AI Education aims to develop and strengthen AI-related capabilities among personnel in the government sector by providing training that meets national needs and enhances their ability to work effectively with artificial intelligence systems. (p.148)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Ministry of Higher Education Science Research and Innovation (Office of the Science Research and Innovation Promotion Commission) and the Digital Government Development Agency

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Action Plan for the Development of Thailand (2022–2027)
fr1_justif: In the NAIS Strategy 3: Enhancing Human Resource Capacity and Advancing AI Education aims to promote education and develop specialised skills at all levels of learning (Upskill / Reskill / Newskill). It requires a targeted plan for AI workforce development and encourages the public to gain knowledge of artificial intelligence cultivating AI literacy an understanding of and ability to use AI technologies effectively appropriately and responsibly in accordance with one’s level of education and age group. (p.147)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Minister of Education and Ministry of Higher Education Science Research and Innovation (p149)

fr1_title: AI Ethics Guideline (Thailand)
fr1_justif: Under the topic of Security and Privacy the AI Ethics guideline states that AI should be created to serve but must not be used to deceive oppose or threaten human beings. AI should be designed with risk-prevention principles to protect against attacks and other threats ensuring the security of data and systems. This includes safeguarding personal data ethics and the safety of human life and the external environment throughout the system’s entire life cycle. It should also have the capability to detect report and respond in order to avoid or mitigate negative impacts. (p.12)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: AI Ethics Guideline (Thailand)
fr1_justif: Under the topic of Transparency and Explainability the AI Ethics guideline clearly states that AI should be researched designed developed provided and used in a transparent manner with explainable and predictable outcomes and with the ability to audit and review past activities. Also AI should have the capacity for traceability to monitor and detect anomalies and to diagnose system failures.(p.11)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Information technology — Artificial intelligence — Management system (PTTS/ISO/IEC 42001:20XX) (Trinidad and Tobago)
fr1_justif: It does not create dedicated rules for deepfakes election interference or gender-based or child-focused harms. Instead it addresses these risks through its general AI governance and risk-management framework. Clause 4.2 requires organisations to assess impacts on affected stakeholders while Clause 6.1 mandates identification and mitigation of risks from foreseeable misuse. Annex C lists social psychological and reputational harm and B.5.5 explicitly refers to misinformation for political gain and deepfakes. However no sector-specific prohibitions safeguards or victim-redress mechanisms are established.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Digital Transformation Strategy 2024-2027 (Trinidad and Togo)
fr1_justif: While the NDTS 2024-2027 is a broader digital strategy it contains explicit provisions addressing AI fairness and non-discrimination. Page 69 commits to removing bias from systems to ensure they are representative of and relevant to the diverse needs of society directly addressing algorithmic fairness in AI systems. Page 59 establishes that the e-ID system must focus on preventing discriminatory practices providing a concrete non-discrimination requirement in an AI-relevant context. Together these provisions demonstrate a demonstrable and explicit link to the Fairness and Non-Discrimination indicator
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Public Administration and AI (MPAAI)

fr1_title: Information technology — Artificial intelligence — Management system (PTTS/ISO/IEC 42001:20XX) (Trinidad and Tobago)
fr1_justif: The standard establishes a dedicated AI impact assessment process through Annex B.5 requiring evaluation of effects on individuals groups and society. Annex B.5.4 (p. 31) addresses impacts on individuals/groups; Annex B.5.5 (p. 32) covers broader societal and health/safety impacts. Annex A (p.20) summarises these controls. Supporting clauses include: Clause 6 (risk-based planning) Clause 8 (operational controls) Clause 9 (performance monitoring) and Clause 10 (corrective action). Together these establish a lifecycle-wide framework for both ex-ante & ex-post AI impact assessment.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: National Digital Transformation Strategy 2024-2027 (Trinidad and Togo)
fr1_justif: Page 66 refers explicitly to ' Future-proof skills by continuously ensuring curriculum relevance by matching Emerging Technologies with growing opportunities;' (ai is defined as emerging tech) and page 77 states that: High engagement in emerging technology training equipping citizens with the skills needed to participate in and shape the future of the digital landscape. The strategy commits to reskilling and upskilling the workforce to support 'emerging technologies' including AI (p. 18). The Strategy mandates continuous reskilling of public officers to manage automated and data-driven digital systems (p. 27). It promotes lifelong learning and future-ready competencies aligned with evolving labour market needs (p. 20).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Public Administration and AI (MPAAI)

fr1_title: Information technology — Artificial intelligence — Management system (PTTS/ISO/IEC 42001:20XX) (Trinidad and Tobago)
fr1_justif: The Draft TTS/ISO/IEC 42001:2023 embeds safety & security throughout its AI management system architecture such as safety policy update requirements (B.2.3); assignment of roles & responsibilities (B.3.2); inclusion of diverse trustworthiness experts including safety specialists (B.4.6); assessment of societal impacts (B.5.5); AI system verification against safety requirements including acceptable error rates (Clause 6.2.4); structured risk assessments (Clause 6.2); impact assessment (Annex B.5.4); incident communication plans for safety problems (B.8.4); and safety explicitly listed as a risk source in Annex C (p.50). Lifecycle-wide monitoring & corrective a action mechanisms (Clauses 9-10) further ensure ongoing safety compliance.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Information technology — Artificial intelligence — Management system (PTTS/ISO/IEC 42001:20XX) (Trinidad and Tobago)
fr1_justif: Transparency is identified as a core principle within the standard and is to be incorporated into the design of organisational processes information systems and control mechanisms (p. x). Clause B.5.4 specifies that AI impact assessments must take account of transparency and explainability considerations. Clause 7.2 further requires that data-management practices address transparency and explainability including how data are used to determine system outputs. Clause 9.3 lists transparency among the objectives guiding the responsible use of AI. In addition Annex C.3 highlights a lack of transparency as a key AI-related risk that organisations should assess and mitigate.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems
Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Data Protection and Privacy Act 2019 (Uganda)
fr1_justif: The Data Protection and Privacy Act 2019 provides several specific sections that relate to access to redress and remedy including in contexts relevant to automated/AI-driven processing. Section 27 gives a data subject the right to challenge a decision significantly affecting them if that decision is solely based on automated processing a scenario common in AI-driven decision-making It mandates notification to the data subject gives a right to require reconsideration and allows a complaint to the regulator. Section 32 mandates the regulator to compel remedy/restoration of rights an important enforcement/redress tool. Section 33 provides judicial redress (monetary compensation) for harms caused by non-compliance.
