fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: According to Section 7 subsections 2 (ii) and (iii) personal data collected for purposes of national security or the prevention detection investigation and prosecution of offenses are exempt from the standard consent requirements. Subsection (3) further clarifies that while data subjects generally have the right to object to data collection this right does not apply to data processed under the aforementioned legal exceptions in subsection (2)

fr1_title: Data Protection and Privacy Act 2019 (Uganda)
fr1_justif: In Section 27 (Rights in relation to automated decision-taking p. 11-12) a data subject may require a data controller to ensure any decision that significantly affects them is not based solely on automated processing of personal data. If a significant decision is based solely on automated processing the controller must notify the data subject as soon as reasonably practicable and the data subject may require the controller to reconsider the decision within 21 days. In response the controller must inform the data subject in writing of steps taken. Also the data subject may complain to the Personal Data Protection Office (PDPO) if unsatisfied.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: According to Section 7 subsections 2 (ii) and (iii) personal data collected for purposes of national security or the prevention detection investigation and prosecution of offenses are exempt from the standard consent requirements. Subsection (3) further clarifies that while data subjects generally have the right to object to data collection this right does not apply to data processed under the aforementioned legal exceptions in subsection (2)

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Concept of AI development in Ukraine
fr1_justif: As one of the strategic areas in the field of general secondary education it proposes: organizing courses for teachers on working with data and the basics of artificial intelligence; and spreading digital literacy among schoolchildren (using digital tools to solve applied problems searching for information on the Internet protecting personal data media literacy digital hygiene etc.).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Recommendations for the responsible use of artificial intelligence in the media sector (Ukraine)
fr1_justif: (p.4) In cases where media outlets resort to AI-generated content in particular to illustrate cultural materials model situations and illustrate examples and theories they are recommended to: check whether the content generated by the AI ??system clearly and accurately reflects reality; assess in each specific case whether the use of AI-generated content is contextually appropriate and whether the use of such content will not mislead readers/viewers/listeners even if labeled;refrain from using or distributing news and news-analytical content generated by AI that contain hate speech propaganda for war or any other kind of unlawful content.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Law of Ukraine on Personal Data Protection
fr1_justif: Article 8 addresses automated decision-making by granting individuals the right to be protected from automated decisions that have legal consequences for them (Art. 8(13)). It also establishes avenues for redress by allowing data subjects to lodge complaints with the Commissioner or courts and to use legal remedies in case of violations of personal data protection legislation (Art. 8(8)–(9)).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Article 25 of the Law explicitly provides for restrictions on the application of Articles 6 7 and 8 in the interests of national security. This constitutes an explicit exclusion of defence and security domains from the core obligations of the framework.
fr1_body_name: Ukrainian Parliamentary Commissioner for Human Rights

fr1_title: Instructive Methodological Recommendations on the introduction and use of artificial intelligence technologies in secondary education institutions (Ukraine)
fr1_justif: The document provides for best interests of the child and rights of children being by-default considered when applying any AI-driven technology throughout the educational process within secondary education. It specifically refers to the need of ensuring inclusivity safeguard personal data protection of confidentiality of children (see pp.8 17)
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Recommendations on Responsible Use of AI in the Sphere of Advertising and Marketing Communications (Ukraine)
fr1_justif: When the document elaborates on how AI systems for marketing purposes should be developed it focuses on diversity as one of the key requirements. Quote: This means that AI systems must be developed and used in a way that involves diverse participants and promotes equal access gender equality and cultural diversity while avoiding discriminatory influences and unfair biases that are prohibited by law. - p.23
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Recommendations on Responsible Use of AI in the Sphere of Advertising and Marketing Communications (Ukraine)
fr1_justif: Developers should promote inclusion and avoid discriminatory advertising which is any advertising that contains or uses statements or images that are discriminatory on the basis of race skin color political religious and other beliefs sex age disability ethnic or social origin citizenship family or property status place of residence language or other characteristics in relation to a person or group of persons. (p.22)
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Recommendations on responsible use of AI for legal professionals (Ukraine)
fr1_justif: It is necessary to critically evaluate and adjust the results obtained from AI to ensure that they reflect and protect the interests of the client. Even if a consultant or lawyer uses auxiliary AI tools he or she bears full responsibility to the client for the accuracy and correctness of the recommendations and conclusions provided. It is imperative to conduct a final check of the results generated by the system. Key aspects for lawyers AI cannot replace professional judgment based on legal knowledge experience and ethical standard. (p.39)
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Recommendations on responsible development of systems using AI technologies (Ukraine)
fr1_justif: The document (pp.18-33 44-45) proposes to approach development of AI systems applying HUDERIA methodology for human rights impact assessment as well as follow the AI Act approach after harmonisation of Ukrainian regulation with the EU standards.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: (p.4) These recommendations do not cover issues related to the national security and defense sector.

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Advisory on responsible use of artificial intelligence by public servants (Ukraine)
fr1_justif: (p.2) These tips are one of the components of the roadmap for regulating artificial intelligence (hereinafter referred to as AI) in Ukraine. The text outlines a systematic approach to the implementation and responsible use of artificial intelligence in professional activities by public servants. - Basically the document's aim is to improve the civil servants' skills and capacities to engage with AI responsibly and ethically.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Concept  for  the  Development  of  Artificial  Intelligence  in  Ukraine
fr1_justif: The Concept explicitly addresses workforce reskilling and upskilling in response to AI-driven labour market transformation. It directly acknowledges the risk of job displacement due to automation (p. 3) and provides targeted workforce measures most notably the development of a roadmap for retraining individuals whose work may be automated in the next five to ten years (p. 5). In addition the framework includes provisions for advanced training and professional retraining of personnel including adult education programs in artificial intelligence and support mechanisms such as scholarships for individuals undergoing such retraining (p. 4).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Recommendations on responsible development of systems using AI technologies (Ukraine)
fr1_justif: (p.4)The objectives of these recommendations are:To guide AI system developers in adhering to ethical principles in the development and use of AI including risk-based security and reliability privacy and data protection fairness and justice transparency and accountability human oversight and monitoring leadership and awareness-raising and protection of intellectual property rights.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Scope limitations — contextually specific challenges in the development and use of AI systems such as industry differences data access restrictions privacy etc. that cannot be fully covered by general recommendations. These recommendations do not cover issues related to the national security and defense sector. (p.4)

fr1_title: White Paper on Artificial Intelligence Regulation in Ukraine: Vision of the Ministry of Digital Transformation of Ukraine
fr1_justif: The document provides for a list of measures to be introduced within the governance and regulatory framework. One of such measures is Voluntary Labeling of AI Systems. Quote: Labeling of AI systems is the provision of clear and structured information about the design functions algorithms and other aspects of artificial intelligence systems. This process is aimed at ensuring transparency and openness regarding the way intelligent systems operate. Following the disclosure of information about the system by the developer such a system receives the appropriate labeling tags. - p.20
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Defence Sector is Not Subject to Regulation. While implementing our approach during the full-scale invasion of the Russian Federation were main aware of the importance of developing innovative solutions to repel aggression and in noway intend to propose regulation of AI systems in the defence sector. This is due to both the national interests of the state and the real state of affairs in the security sector – unilateral regulation (restriction) of the use of AI in the defence sector at the level of national legislation will only put our country in a less favorable position compared to the aggressor which will not implement such regulation. - p.10

fr1_title: Federal Decree by Law No. (45) of 2021 Concerning the Protection of Personal Data (United Arab Emirates)
fr1_justif: The Data Subject has the fundamental right to object to any decisions resulting from automated processing including profiling that adversely affect them(18). The Controller shall include the human element in reviewing automated processing decisions upon request providing immediate remedy against algorithmic error or bias. Data Subjects have the right to submit complaints to the UAE Data Bureau if a violation occurs(24). The Bureau examines complaints and can impose administrative penalties against the Controller/Processor if the violation is proven serving as a formal redress action. Individuals may communicate directly with the DPO regarding their rights and submit requests and complaints related to their personal data processing.(12)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Provisions of the Decree by Law shall not apply to Personal Data held by the security and judicial authorities. Provisions also shall not apply to Government Data which often encompasses defence and security-related information. However the framework includes a caveat regarding international data transfer stating that the cross-border transfer of data requires the Data Subject's consent provided that such transfer shall not contradict the public or security interest of the State

fr1_title: ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE ETHICS AI ETHICS PRINCIPLES & GUIDELINES
fr1_justif: The framework explicitly addresses the protection of children in AI systems particularly under the Human-Centered AI (Principle 6) which require that AI systems capable of influencing behavior must be designed to distinguish between adults and children and implement additional safeguards for vulnerable populations such as children “when informed consent cannot be obtained or when it may not be a sufficient safeguard” (p. 35).
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: UAE National Strategy for Artificial Intelligence 2031
fr1_justif: The policy addresses the UAE's diversified culture (over 200 nationalities) as its greatest advantage for AI development (p40). This diversity provides unique 'impeccable' data sets essential for machine learning and Artificial Intelligence applications specifically citing diagnostics for diseases like Tuberculosis (p40). The document also seeks to contextualize global AI debates for young Arab countries and leverage the strong regional talent pool in MENA (p7). For Linguistic Diversity the AI Office will establish an open-access digital library of research and papers in both English and Arabic (p39). This initiative aims to boost the research sector and close the research gap in the region (p39).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: AI Ethics: Principles and Guidelines (United Arab Emirates)
fr1_justif: The document addresses fairness and non-discrimination under 'Fairness (Principle 1)'. It mandates that AI systems 'should not discriminate against people or groups' and must be 'provably fair' especially for 'significant and critical decisions'.To ensure this organizations must define their context-specific meaning of fairness and undertake 'Discrimination Impact Assessments'. Guidelines require mitigating and disclosing 'biases inherent in datasets' and ensuring data is 'accurate and representative of the affected population'. The framework emphasizes 'inclusiveness equality human benefit and rights' and requires considering whether subjects receive 'equal treatment' in the same circumstances
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: DIFC Regulation 10 – Accreditation and Certification Framework For Autonomous and Semi-Autonomous Systems Processing Personal Data (“Framework”) (United Arab Emirates)
fr1_justif: DIFC Regulation 10 addresses gender equality indirectly through its mandatory principle of Fairness. It requires high-risk AI systems to be certified and designed to 'treat all individuals equally and fairly regardless of race gender or other specifically subjective factors'. The framework also mandates that systems must be designed to avoid or mitigate 'unjust bias' and 'discrimination'. This creates an enforceable auditable mechanism to challenge gender-based harm from AI within the DIFC's jurisdiction.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Federal Decree by Law No. (45) of 2021 Concerning the Protection of Personal Data (United Arab Emirates)
fr1_justif: The framework ensures human oversight in AI through explicit rights and obligations on automated processing (Art18 c.4). Under Article 18(p21) individuals may object to decisions made via automation or profiling that have legal or adverse effects (c.1). When an objection is raised the Controller must include human review(c.4) ensuring a person reassesses the outcome. For high-risk automation a Personal Data Protection Impact Assessment (PDPIA) is required to evaluate risks and safeguards (Art 21 c.1) p23. Together these provisions guarantee that AI-driven decisions remain subject to meaningful human intervention.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: The Federal Decree-Law No. 45 of 2021 on Personal Data Protection explicitly excludes defence security judicial authorities and government entities from its scope (art 2 c.2). This carve-out preserves state sovereignty and public order as such data is governed by specialised security and intelligence laws. The exemption ensures national security operations aren’t constrained by data-subject rights like erasure or objection(art17 c.2 & art18c2.b). The law further allows processing and transfers tied to judicial or security procedures confirming that critical state functions operate under separate higher-level legal regimes (art 4 c3 & art 23 c. 1(ce)).

fr1_title: AI Ethics: Principles and Guidelines (United Arab Emirates)
fr1_justif: The guidelines addresses Impact Assessments (IA) through specific non-mandatory guidelines (p5 1.1). It recommends Discrimination Impact Assessments (DIAs) for fairness which should be conducted both pre-release and regularly after as an ongoing evaluation (p17 3.1.2).For Privacy Preserving AI algorithmic systems require adequate Privacy Impact Assessments (PIAs) that must also include societal and ethical considerations (p42 3.8.2). Operators should consider internal risk assessments and human rights impact assessments (p37 3.6.2.4). The document also mandates that AI applications be assessed through critical examination of resource usage and environmental impact throughout the AI lifecycle (p.38 3.7.1).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: UAE Strategy for Artificial Intelligence 2031
fr1_justif: The strategy contains explicit targeted measures for public sector AI skill development most notably under Objective 5 which introduces dedicated “Government Training” programs designed to equip civil servants with AI competencies (p. 33). These include advanced courses for government employees mandatory engagement projects tied to their institutional roles and the stated goal that “100% of senior leadership in government… are trained and versed in AI” (p. 33). The document further recognizes the urgency of reskilling public sector workers due to automation risks explicitly identifying government employment as a key area requiring intervention (p. 32).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: UAE Strategy for Artificial Intelligence 2031
fr1_justif: The strategy explicitly addresses workforce reskilling and upskilling in response to AI-driven labour market transformation particularly under Objective 5. It recognises that a significant share of jobs may be affected by automation and identifies retraining as “particularly critical” (p. 32). It outlines concrete pathways such as professional upskilling through specialised AI training secondments and study opportunities (pp. 34–35) as well as employment transition support including career guidance and training for workers at risk of displacement (p. 35). It further presents a structured multi-tiered approach encompassing apprenticeships short courses scholarships and project-based learning (p. 34).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: The UAE Charter for the Development and Use of Artificial Intelligence (July 2024)
fr1_justif: Under Safety the UAE places great importance on ensuring that all AI systems comply with the highest safety standards (p3 s2). The country also actively encourages modifying or removing systems that pose risks (p3 s2). Regarding security the Charter emphasizes that the development of AI must ensure peaceful coexistence with the technology (p5 s10). This peaceful coexistence is crucial to ensure technology enhances well-being without compromising human security or fundamental rights(p5 s10).Furthermore the principles of Human Oversight Governance and Accountability and Commitment to Treaties and Applicable Laws collectively enforce a robust and responsible environment that ensures safety and protects the rights of the UAE community (p5.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Federal Decree by Law No. (45) of 2021 Concerning the Protection of Personal Data (United Arab Emirates)
fr1_justif: The document addresses AI transparency and explainability through specific controls on 'Automated Processing' and 'Profiling'. Processing must be transparent. Data Subjects have the right to information regarding 'Decisions made based on automated processing including profiling' and the right to object to adverse decisions resulting from such processes. Crucially the Controller must include the human element in reviewing automated processing decisions upon the Data Subject's request. Furthermore automated processing involving systematic assessment or high risk requires a Personal Data Protection Impact Assessment and a Data Protection Officer.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: The Scope of Application (Article 2) mandates that the provisions of the Federal Decree by Law No. (45) of 2021 shall not apply to personal data held by the security and judicial authorities. This clause ensures that data processing activities handled by authorities responsible for security functions are exempted from the law's obligations.

country: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
fr1_title: National AI Strategy (2021) (United Kingdom)
fr1_justif: The strategy addresses AI literacy under Pillar 2: Skills and Talent (pp. 40–47). It promotes access to AI data and digital skills via computing education in schools Skills Bootcamps and lifelong learning. It backs teacher training through the National Centre for Computing Education and calls for inclusive pathways into AI careers. The strategy emphasizes AI literacy as vital for public understanding equity and preparing the workforce for the AI-driven economy.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

country: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
fr1_title: Online Safety Act 2023 (United Kingdom)
fr1_justif: While the Online Safety Act 2023 framework is relevant as it regulates the algorithmic systems through which such content spreads. Section 152 (p. 158) establishes an advisory committee on disinformation and misinformation to guide OFCOM. Sections 9(5)(b) and 11(6)(b) (pp. 8 11) require providers to assess risks considering 'algorithms used by the service and how easily quickly and widely content may be disseminated.' Section 231(10) (p. 229) defines 'proactive technology' utilising 'artificial intelligence or machine learning' for content identification and moderation. Thus the Act addresses the infrastructure enabling harmful content dissem
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Defence and security are explicitly excluded from the Act's scope. Schedule 1 paragraph 9(3) (p. 248) states that the provisions do not apply to services provided by the Crown which includes government departments and agencies such as the Ministry of Defence and intelligence bodies. The Act's duties apply only to commercial online services not to state-run defence or security systems.

country: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
fr1_title: Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 (United Kingdom)
fr1_justif: The Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 addresses Access to Redress and Remedy in the AI context primarily through safeguards for automated decision-making. Articles 22A–22C (pp. 95–96) define significant decisions based solely on automated processing and require protections for individuals affected. These safeguards include providing information about the decision enabling individuals to make representations obtain human intervention and contest the decision ensuring meaningful avenues for redress. Further provisions on meaningful human involvement and reconsideration of decisions are set out in Article 22D and related sections (pp. 96–98) reinforcing the right to challenge and remedy AI-driven outcomes.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: In the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 defence intelligence and national security are treated as explicit exclusions from the general framework through dedicated exemptions and carve-outs for intelligence services and national security processing (pp. 105–108). These sections remove such activities from the ordinary application of safeguards and rights meaning defence and security are effectively excluded from the framework’s scope for the purposes of this assessment.

country: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
fr1_title: Online Safety Act 2023 (United Kingdom)
fr1_justif: The Act protects children from AI-driven harms by requiring regulated services to assess and mitigate algorithmic risks (ss. 9–13 pp. 9–15). It obliges providers to consider how algorithms automated decision-making and design features affect children’s exposure to harmful content (s. 11 (6)(b) p. 11). Services must use proportionate age-verification or estimation technologies (s. 12 (4–8) pp. 12–13) and apply safety-by-design principles. Ofcom enforces these duties ensuring AI systems used online uphold children’s safety and wellbeing.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Defence and security are explicitly excluded from the Act's scope. Schedule 1 paragraph 9(3) (p. 248) states that the provisions do not apply to services provided by the Crown which includes government departments and agencies such as the Ministry of Defence and intelligence bodies. The Act's duties apply only to commercial online services not to state-run defence or security systems.
fr2_title: Data (Use and Access) Act 2025
fr2_justif: The Act strengthens children’s privacy in AI contexts by amending UK GDPR data-protection-by-design duties to include “children’s higher protection matters” (Bill cl.81) and by requiring ICO codes of practice on AI/ADM and EdTech. It also revises ADM rules with safeguards (info challenge human intervention). DSIT’s guidance highlights a dedicated section on Children’s Data Protection and ADM safeguards.
fr2_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

country: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
fr1_title: National AI Strategy (2021) (United Kingdom)
fr1_justif: The diversity focus is limited to workforce representation (gender race disability) on pages 16-17 (Reflecting and protecting society section) and page 23 (conversion courses diversity statistics). Page 31 addresses algorithmic bias through representative datasets and mentions bias detection using sensitive data. The Commission on Race and Ethnic Disparities is referenced on page 51 regarding algorithmic bias concerns not cultural/linguistic preservation.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

country: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
fr1_title: AI Opportunities Action Plan (United Kingdom)
fr1_justif: The document addresses Environmental Impact minimally primarily in Section 1.1 'Building sufficient secure and sustainable AI infrastructure' (pages 7-9). Recommendation 5 (page 9) calls on government to 'mitigate the sustainability and security risks of AI infrastructure' and support companies demonstrating 'sustainability or security advancements.' Recommendation 4 (pages 8-9) references accelerating 'clean power' provisioning for AI Growth Zones.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The UK Sovereign AI and Digital Centre of Government

country: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
fr1_title: Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 (United Kingdom)
fr1_justif: The Act addresses fairness and non-discrimination through Section 80 on Automated Decision-Making. Article 22B restricts significant decisions based solely on automated processing when involving special categories of personal data (e.g. race ethnicity health political opinions) under Article 9(1). Article 22C mandates safeguards including: (a) informing data subjects about automated decisions; (b) enabling representations; (c) providing human intervention; and (d) enabling individuals to contest decisions. Section 50B-50C extends similar protections for law enforcement contexts. These provisions protect against discriminatory AI outcomes by limiting automated decisions on protected characteristics and ensuring redress mechanisms.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: In the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 defence intelligence and national security are treated as explicit exclusions from the general framework through dedicated exemptions and carve-outs for intelligence services and national security processing (pp. 105–108). These sections remove such activities from the ordinary application of safeguards and rights meaning defence and security are effectively excluded from the framework’s scope for the purposes of this assessment.

country: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
fr1_title: AI Opportunities Action Plan (United Kingdom)
fr1_justif: The Plan promotes gender equality by committing to “increase the diversity of the talent pool” in AI noting that Only 22% of people working in AI and datascience are women and that the government needs to focus on increasing the representation by targeted education and initiatives (pp. 11-12).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

country: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
fr1_title: Data Protection Act 2018 (United Kingdom)
fr1_justif: The Act hardwires human oversight into AI decisions. Part 2 s.14 (p.14) supplementing UK GDPR Art.22 requires that when a 'qualifying significant decision' is made solely by automated processing the controller must notify the person and if asked within one month reconsider or take a new non-automated decision. For law enforcement (Part 3) ss.49–50 (pp.50–51) and ss.50A–50D (pp.52–54) enable individuals to obtain human intervention make representations and contest decisions; reconsideration must involve 'meaningful human involvement.' For intelligence services (Part 4) ss.96–97 (pp.93–94) provide similar rights.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: The Act contains broad national security exemptions (e.g. Section 26) and a separate regime for intelligence services (Part 4) which allow the government to disapply many GDPR-style protections including Article 22–type rights. As a result human oversight is not legally guaranteed for individuals in security and defence contexts although institutional oversight mechanisms (e.g. commissioners and parliamentary committees) still exist.

country: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
fr1_title: Data Protection Act 2018 (United Kingdom)
fr1_justif: It addresses impact assessments primarily through Section 64 which mandates Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) when processing is 'likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of individuals.' Controllers must conduct DPIAs prior to processing including a description of operations risk assessment mitigation measures and safeguards. Section 65 requires prior consultation with the Information Commissioner when a DPIA indicates high risk. These requirements apply directly to AI systems processing personal data particularly those involving automated decision-making or profiling. Court-case proving application of the law to AI: https://www.judiciary.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/08/R-Bridges-v-CC-South-Wales-ors-Judgment
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: The Act contains broad national security exemptions (e.g. Section 26) and a separate regime for intelligence services (Part 4) which allow the government to disapply many GDPR-style protections including Article 22–type rights. As a result human oversight is not legally guaranteed for individuals in security and defence contexts although institutional oversight mechanisms (e.g. commissioners and parliamentary committees) still exist.

country: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
fr1_title: Data Protection Act 2018 (United Kingdom)
fr1_justif: Article 22(1) provides workers the right not to be subject to decisions based solely on automated processing including profiling which produce legal effects or similarly significantly affect them. Recital 71 explicitly references employment contexts including 'e-recruiting practices without any human intervention' and decisions evaluating 'performance at work.' Article 22(3) mandates safeguards including the right to obtain human intervention express one's point of view and contest automated decisions. Articles 13(2)(f) and 14(2)(g) require employers to provide meaningful information about the logic involved in automated decision-making and its significance and consequences.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: The Act contains broad national security exemptions (e.g. Section 26) and a separate regime for intelligence services (Part 4) which allow the government to disapply many GDPR-style protections including Article 22–type rights. As a result human oversight is not legally guaranteed for individuals in security and defence contexts although institutional oversight mechanisms (e.g. commissioners and parliamentary committees) still exist.

country: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems
fr1_title: Algorithmic Transparency Recording Standard (ATRS): Mandatory Scope and Exemptions Policy (United Kingdom)
fr1_justif: The ATRS establishes mandatory public disclosure of government algorithmic systems. Section 1 (Summary) mandates publication of ATRS records on GOV.UK. Section 3 (Organisations in scope) defines covered entities: ministerial departments non-ministerial departments and arm's-length bodies providing public services. Section 4 (Algorithmic tools in scope) requires disclosure for tools with 'significant influence on decision-making with public effect' or that 'directly interact with the public.' The framework requires proactive transparency through a standardised template capturing purpose data risks and human oversight.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: The ATRS Mandatory Scope and Exemptions Policy explicitly provides for national security exemptions stating that 'situations where no information can be safely published are expected to be unusual (e.g. in cases where even the existence of a tool cannot be made public for security reasons).' The policy also notes that national security was a key consideration in finalising the detailed scope of the mandatory requirement.

country: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
fr1_title: Guidelines for AI procurement (United Kingdom)
fr1_justif: AI-specific guidelines beyond IT requiring bias/risk checks vendor transparency (avoid ‘black box’) and lifecycle accountability. ‘Top 10 Considerations’ covers data assessment iterative AI impact assessment SME/VCSE-friendly market engagement governance/info assurance and avoiding ‘Black Box’ algorithms/vendor lock-in. In ‘AI-specific considerations’: Publication asks bidders to address data quality/bias and disclose algorithms/models (incl. possible independent audits); Selection/Evaluation lists robust practices (accountability non-discrimination reproducibility testing); Contract Implementation/Ongoing Management requires lifecycle monitoring and end-to-end auditability.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

country: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
fr1_title: Artificial Intelligence Playbook for the UK Government
fr1_justif: The document addresses public sector skills development through capacity building initiatives and training programs for civil servants. Principle 9 (p.14) establishes the competency requirement that civil servants gain skills to 'use design build and maintain AI solutions.' The Acquiring Skills and Talent section (pp.30-31) operationalises this through five learning pathways targeting different civil service roles from beginners to senior leaders. Training programs include free AI courses on Civil Service Learning covering AI fundamentals generative AI machine learning deep learning NLP and computer vision. The Digital Excellence Programme provides specialised AI training for senior civil servants while sandboxes enable practical e
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

country: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
fr1_title: AI Opportunities Action Plan (United Kingdom)
fr1_justif: In Section 1.3 (“Training retaining and attracting the next generation of AI scientists and founders” pp.10–13) the Action Plan sets an AI skills agenda: it proposes training “tens of thousands” of AI professionals by 2030 (p.11) and expanding pathways into AI careers via further education apprenticeships and employer- or self-led upskilling (Recommendation 17 p.12). It also states the lifelong skills programme must be “ready for AI” ensuring opportunities for workers to reskill into AI and AI-enabled jobs (Recommendation 19 p.12).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

country: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
fr1_title: Code of Practice for the Cyber Security of AI (United Kingdom)
fr1_justif: The Code establishes 13 principles across 5 AI lifecycle phases to address cyber security risks specific to AI systems (Introduction). It mandates security obligations including: threat modelling and risk management for AI-specific attacks like data poisoning and model inversion (Principle 3); pre-deployment testing and security assessments (Principle 9); safeguards requiring systems to withstand adversarial attacks and failures (Principle 2); incident management and recovery plans (Principle 6); and monitoring for anomalies and security breaches (Principle 12). The Code applies to all AI supply chain stakeholders including developers system operators and data custodians (Audience section).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

country: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
fr1_title: Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 (United Kingdom)
fr1_justif: The Act hard-codes transparency and explainability for automated decision-making. It defines when a decision is 'solely automated' and 'significant' (Art. 22A p. 95) and mandates safeguards (Art. 22C pp. 95–96) that must: provide information about the decision let people make representations obtain human intervention and contest outcomes. It also amends UK GDPR Articles 13–15 to require information about automated decision-making in notices and access rights (Sch. 6 paras 3–5 p. 198) and ties penalties to breaches of 22B/22C (Art. 83(5) p. 199). In addition restrictions for sensitive data apply (Art. 22B p. 95).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: In the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 defence intelligence and national security are treated as explicit exclusions from the general framework through dedicated exemptions and carve-outs for intelligence services and national security processing (pp. 105–108). These sections remove such activities from the ordinary application of safeguards and rights meaning defence and security are effectively excluded from the framework’s scope for the purposes of this assessment.

fr1_title: Guidelines for the Secure and Ethical Use of Artificial Intelligence in Tanzania (Draft Version 2.0)
fr1_justif: The document directly addresses AI literacy as a core objective. On page 9 Objective (i) explicitly commits to 'Elevate AI literacy and specialized skills' across the workforce. This is to be achieved through inclusive national education and training initiatives including specific skills-building programs focused on AI and data literacy complemented by apprenticeship programs to nurture local talent. This establishes a clear strategic focus on building foundational public understanding and technical capacity in AI.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: The Personal Data Protection Act (Tanzania)
fr1_justif: 1. The Act classifies “data related to children” as sensitive personal data (Sec. 3) and prohibits processing without prior written consent (Sec. 30). 2. Since minors cannot consent parents or guardians must provide it (Sec. 30(4)) processing of children’s data. 4. Consent can be withdrawn anytime without cost (Sec. 30(2)). 5. Guardians may also require that decisions affecting the child are not based solely on automated processing (Sec. 36(1)).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Guidelines for the Secure and Ethical Use of Artificial Intelligence in Tanzania (Draft Version 2.0)
fr1_justif: The document proposes measures to enhance cultural and linguistic diversity. The Diversity and Inclusion guideline (Page 12) suggests AI training data be 'representative of different genders races ethnicities and other diverse factors.'
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Guidelines for the Secure and Ethical Use of Artificial Intelligence in Tanzania (Draft Version 2.0)
fr1_justif: The document proposes that fairness and non-discrimination are core principles in AI. Principle 3: Managed Bias (Page 10) acknowledges bias risks mandates proactive bias minimization and emphasizes human oversight. Guidelines 2 and 6 (Pages 12-13) call for diverse inclusive training data and ongoing vigilance against bias. Proposal 5 (Pages 20-21) and Recommendation 5 outline measures such as real-time analysis pre-emptive data checks bias-free design user feedback diverse teams objective mechanisms decommissioning biased systems and early testing. Fairness is linked to Transparency (Pages 10-11 19) Accountability (Page 11) and Safety (Page 10).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Guidelines for the Secure and Ethical Use of Artificial Intelligence in Tanzania (Draft Version 2.0)
fr1_justif: The document addresses Human Oversight and Determination through Proposal 1: Human  Agency and Control which emphasizes that AI systems must remain under constant human monitoring and control supported by proper documentation (Page 13). It further details steps for implementation (Page 14) including providing technical documentation designing systems for effective monitoring and enabling human choice between AI outputs. Finally it assigns responsibility to system designers requires training of designated personnel and establishes clear criteria for system use and intervention (Page 15).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Guidelines for the Secure and Ethical Use of Artificial Intelligence in Tanzania (Draft Version 2.0)
fr1_justif: The guidelines require developers and deployers to proactively identify and mitigate risks before and during AI deployment. Guideline 9 mandates anticipating risks from data algorithms and system use. Proposal 1 requires early detection of harmful impacts on equality and human rights and Proposal 6 defines risk management as timely identification assessment documentation and minimization of potential harms. Impacts cover societal ethical environmental and economic consequences. Guideline 10 and Proposals 3–5 mandate ethical reviews bias detection data quality checks rigorous testing and thorough documentation with accountability assigned to all responsible parties.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems

fr1_title: Guidelines for the Secure and Ethical Use of Artificial Intelligence in Tanzania (Draft Version 2.0)
fr1_justif: The guidelines prioritize reskilling and upskilling to build local AI capacity. A core objective is to 'Develop Human Capital' by elevating AI literacy through national skills-building programs and apprenticeships to nurture local talent (Pg 9). It also mandates educating employees on ethical AI use (Pg 12-13) and promotes ongoing team education on emerging threats (Pg 17-18) ensuring a skilled workforce for Tanzania's digital economy.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Guidelines for the Secure and Ethical Use of Artificial Intelligence in Tanzania (Draft Version 2.0)
fr1_justif: The Guideline provide for guide and principles in deployment and use of AI in the country. Among the principles is 'safe' i.e. AI deployment and usage must conform to safety expectations for the environment in which the AI is used - not to cause or exacerbate harm.(p. 10).It require the conduct of risk assessment (p. 21) guideline to ensure privacy and security concerns (p.12) and requires routine monitoring of AI systems to anticipate risks identify ethical use and potential bias (p.12-13)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Guidelines for the Secure and Ethical Use of Artificial Intelligence in Tanzania (Draft Version 2.0)
fr1_justif: The document proposes the following AI principles and guidelines. Principle 5: Transparency (Pages 10-11) encourages users to know when AI is used understand its functions and receive clear explanations. Principle 7: Explainable and Interpretable (Page 11) emphasizes technical traceability and the right to explanation. Guideline 5 (Pages 12-13) proposes disclosure of data algorithms and decision processes. Proposal 4 (Pages 19-20) offers recommended measures for implementing these guidelines detailing traceability explainability and communication at data algorithm and process levels.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Advancing Artificial Intelligence Education for American Youth
fr1_justif: The document addresses AI literacy primarily in Section 1 (Background.) noting to equip students with essential AI skills andcompetencies it frames AI literacy as essential for preparing youth to “understand use and help develop AI technologies.” Section 2 (Policy) establishes AI literacy and proficiency as national priorities emphasizing early exposure critical thinking and ethical understanding of AI systems. Section 4 (Developing an AI-Ready Task Force) and Section 5-7 (Supporting Educators) direct the Department of Education to expand K-12 AI literacy resources integrate AI concepts across subjects and prioritize teacher training and professional development using federal grants.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.
fr1_body_name: White House Task Force on AI Education

fr1_title: Public Law 119-21 'Take it down Act' (USA)
fr1_justif: The TAKE IT DOWN Act targets AI-enabled harms mainly via “digital forgeries” (AI/ML-generated intimate images) and treats them as a driver of abuse/harassment and coercive threats. It (1) defines “digital forgery” as content created using “machine learning” or “artificial intelligence” (p.1 Sec.2(h)(1)(B); (2) criminalizes knowing publication of such forgeries intended to cause or causing harm and for minors where intent is to “abuse humiliate harass or degrade” (pp.3–4 Sec.2(h)(3); (3) covers threats made to intimidate/coerce/extort or cause “mental distress” (p.5 Sec.2(h)(6); (4) requires platform removal within 48 hours and efforts to remove.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.

fr1_title: Accelerating Federal Use of AI through Innovation Governance and Public Trust (USA)
fr1_justif: On p. 17 it states that under 'Offer Consistent Remedies or Appeals' that Agencies must ensure that individuals affected byAI-enabled decisions have access to a timely human review and a chance to appeal any negative impacts when appropriate. If an agency already has an appeals or human review process in place such as appeals of adverse actions it may extend or adapt that process to cover decisions made with AI consistent with applicable law. Any remedy process should be designed to avoid placing unnecessary burdens on the individual and should follow established guidance for minimizing administrative burdens.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Appendix §1(c) “Applicability to National Security Systems” (p.5) states: “This memorandum does not cover AI when it is being used as a component of a National Security System” (as defined in 44 U.S.C. §3552(b)(6)). Additionally Appendix §1(a) (p.4) says it applies to “agencies” per 44 U.S.C. §3502(1) which excludes certain national defense GOCO facilities.

fr1_title: Children´s Online Privacy Protection Rule Amendments (USA)
fr1_justif: The document addresses children's rights in the context of AI by expanding the definition of 'Personal information' (p.7) to include biometric identifiers (such as facial templates voiceprints and gait patterns) that can be used for automated or semi-automated recognition (pp.33-35). This change is a direct response to the rapid advancement of technologies like facial recognition and machine learning intended to strengthen the protection of this sensitive immutable data (p.7). The Commission noted concerns about AI/ML tools being used for mass profiling targeted advertising and engagement-maximizing techniques (pp.8 17).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Algorithmic Accountability Act 2025 (USA)
fr1_justif: The bill operationalizes fairness/non-discrimination by mandating impact assessments for “covered algorithms” used in “critical decisions.” It requires testing for differential performance by protected and other characteristics (Sec. 4(a)(4)(E) pp. 18–19) and identifying needed resources to improve “fairness including bias and non-discrimination” (Sec. 4(a)(11)(B) pp. 24-25). It also mandates consultation with civil-rights and impacted communities (Sec. 3(b)(1)(G) pp. 9–11) and mitigation of material harms (Sec. 3(b)(1)(H) p. 11). FTC must publish aggregated findings and a public repository (Sec. 6 pp. 31–36).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Executive Order 14110 Safe Secure and Trustworthy Development and Use of Artificial Intelligene
fr1_justif: Gender Equality is addressed through its equity/civil rights mandate: AI must not “disadvantage” groups denied equal opportunity (Policy & Principles Sec.2(d) pp.2-3/36). It directs action against algorithmic discrimination and unlawful discrimination in hiring and housing/tenant screening (Sec.7.1–7.3 pp.21–24/36). In health it requires “equity principles” using disaggregated data and monitoring bias across population groups (Sec.8(b)(i)(C) p.25/36). It also requires agencies to assess/mitigate disparate impacts and provide human remedies (Sec.10.1(b)(iv) p.29/36). Under Sec10.2(a) the Director of the Gender Policy Council among others is also tasked with identifying priority mission areas for increasedFederal Government AI talent.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: The Attorney GeneralAssistant Attorney General in charge of the Civil Rights DivisionHeads of Federal civil rights officesDepartment of Justices Civil Rights Division

fr1_title: Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights MAKING AUTOMATED SYSTEMS WORK FOR THE AMERICAN PEOPLE
fr1_justif: The framework explicitly operationalizes human oversight as a core principle of AI governance through its “Human Alternatives Consideration and Fallback” section which establishes that individuals must have access to human review (p. 8) and the ability to contest automated decisions (p. 7). It further requires that automated systems especially in sensitive domains such as employment healthcare and criminal justice incorporate human oversight mechanisms and ensure “human consideration before any high-risk decision” (p. 50–51)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: 'These principles are not intended to and do not prohibit or limit any lawful activity of a government agency including law enforcement national security or intelligence activities.' p. 2

fr1_title: Advancing Governance Innovation and Risk Managment for Agency Use of AI (USA)
fr1_justif: M-24-10 makes AI Impact Assessments a pre-use gate for “safety-impacting” and “rights-impacting” AI: agencies must complete and periodically update an “AI impact assessment” documenting purpose/expected benefit (metrics or qualitative analysis) risks/mitigations stakeholders & failure modes and data quality/provenance/representativeness (§5(a)(iv)(A) pp.17–18). It pairs AI with real-world performance testing (§5(a)(iv)(B) pp.18–19) and independent evaluation using the IA + test results integrated into release/ATO processes (§5(a)(iv)(C) p.19). For rights-impacting AI agencies must record equity/fairness issues and algorithmic discrimination in the AI (§5(a)(v)(A)(1) p.21).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: The memo explicitly excludes national-security/defense use: “Applicability to National Security Systems. This memorandum does not cover AI when it is being used as a component of a National Security System” (§2(c) p.4). It also carves out defense/security actors from specific requirements—for example agencies must publish annual AI use-case inventories “except for the Department of Defense and the Intelligence Community” (§3(c)(iv) p.5) and some requirements “do not apply to elements of the Intelligence Community” (§2(a) p.3).
fr1_body_name: Chief AI Officer (CAIO) with defined governance/risk-management duties

fr1_title: Bipartisan House Task Force Report on Artificial Intelligence (USA)
fr1_justif: According to the document labour protections are addressed in “Education and Workforce” under “Labor Laws for Modern Needs”: employers using AI must comply with anti-discrimination and labor laws and Congress should consider workplace AI transparency to protect workers’ rights (p.104). It states workplace AI must respect NLRA rights (noting NLRB work on emerging tech and protected activity) and calls for OSHA to monitor/update standards as AI enters workplaces (p.104). It also recommends: “Monitor the interaction of labor laws and worker protections with AI adoption” (p.110).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems
fr1_title: Accelerating Federal Use of AI through Innovation Governance and Public Trust (USA)
fr1_justif: M-25-21 advances public disclosure mainly through proactive publication requirements: Each agency (except for the Department of Defense and the Intelligence Community) must inventory its AI use cases at least annually submit the inventory to OMB and post a public version on the agency's website. Agencies are encouraged to update the public versions of their inventories on an ongoing basis to reflect their current use of AI. OMB will issue detailed instructions to agencies regarding the inventory and its scope (pp. 12-13).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: The memo draws a hard boundary around defense/security uses that qualify as National Security Systems (NSS): in the Appendix Section 1 “SCOPE” subsection (c) “Applicability to National Security Systems” (p. 4) it states the memo “does not cover AI when it is being used as a component of a National Security System.” It also clarifies NSS AI is governed “through other policy” (footnote 8) reinforcing that this framework’s transparency/governance duties don’t apply to NSS contexts.
fr1_body_name: Chief AI Officer (CAIO) agency AI Governance Board Chief AI Officer Council

fr1_title: Driving Efficient Acquisition of Artificial Intelligence in Government (USA)
fr1_justif: M-25-22 is a federal AI acquisition/procurement framework: it sets scope/applicability to contracts for “AI systems or services” (§2 pp.2–4) mandates agency-level procurement controls (privacy terms IP/lock-in mitigation limits on use of government data for training and documentation/transparency needs) (§3(d)–(f) pp.5–6) and gives AI acquisition lifecycle guidance—cross-functional teams market research product demos/tests performance-based acquisition and ongoing performance/risk management—so agencies buy AI that is fit-for-purpose and preserves public trust (§4(a)–(c) pp.6–8).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: The memo excludes national-security/defense acquisition by stating it “does not apply to AI acquired for use as a component of a National Security System” (§2(d) Applicability to National Security Systems p.4). It also notes it does not apply to elements of the Intelligence Community (§2(a) Covered Agencies p.2) reinforcing the security carve-out.

fr1_title: Accelerating Federal Use of AI through Innovation Governance and Public Trust (USA)
fr1_justif: M-25-21 embeds public-sector skills development in three places: (1) “Agency AI Strategies” requires agencies to plan to “recruit hire train retain… an AI-ready workforce” and achieve “AI literacy for non-practitioners” (p. 6). (2) “Enabling an AI-Ready Federal Workforce” links training/upskilling to responsible AI adoption and prioritizes developing and retaining AI talent (p. 9). (3) Minimum practice “Ensure Adequate Human Training and Assessment” requires periodic system-specific training for AI operators (p. 17).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: Chief AI Officer (CAIO) Agency AI Governance Board

fr1_title: America´s AI Action Plan
fr1_justif: In “Empower American Workers in the Age of AI” (pp. 6–7) the Plan calls for a “serious workforce response” including DOL funding for rapid retraining for AI-related displacement and guidance so states can use Rapid Response funds to proactively upskill workers at risk; it also proposes pilots under WIOA to address AI-driven workforce challenges (p.7). In “Train a Skilled Workforce for AI Infrastructure” (pp. 17–18) it recommends skills frameworks/competency models industry-driven training CTE and Registered Apprenticeships and partnerships with community/technical colleges to transition the existing workforce into critical AI roles (pp.17–18).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_body_name: AI Workforce Research Hub (under the US Department of Labor DOL)

fr1_title: Accelerating Federal Use of AI through Innovation Governance and Public Trust (USA)
fr1_justif: M-25-21 links AI Safety/Security to “high-impact AI” and requires “minimum risk management practices” prioritizing AI that is “safe secure and resilient” and mandates a plan to discontinue non-performing high-impact AI or cease use if risk mitigation isn’t possible (Main memo pp.2-3). In the Appendix it requires processes to measure monitor and evaluate ongoing performance (p.10) and mandates termination of non-compliant AI (Sec. 4(a)/4(b) pp.13–14) plus pre-deployment testing and risk mitigation plans (Sec. 4(b)(i) pp.14–15).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: M-25-21 explicitly states: “Applicability to National Security Systems. This memorandum does not cover AI when it is being used as a component of a National Security System” (Appendix Section 1 ‘SCOPE’ subsection (c) p. 4).

fr1_title: Accelerating Federal Use of AI through Innovation Governance and Public Trust (USA)
fr1_justif: OMB Memorandum M-25-21 embeds Transparency and Explainability as core governance requirements for federal AI use.Transparency is mandated through public disclosure mechanisms including publicly released AI Strategies (Section 2(a) pp. 5–6) AI Use Case Inventories (Section 3(b)(v) pp. 12–13) and public reporting of high-impact AI determinations and waivers (Section 4(a)(iv) p. 15).Explainability is operationalized via documentation and traceability duties requiring agencies to document AI capabilities limitations intended use data provenance and model performance (Section 2(d)(ii) p. 9; Section 4(b)(ii) pp. 16–17). Agencies are also encouraged to favor models that are “inherently more explainable” (Section 3(b)(iii) p. 12).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Defence and security are explicitly excluded from the scope of OMB Memorandum M-25-21. The Appendix states that the memorandum “does not cover AI when it is being used as a component of a National Security System” (Appendix §1(c) p. 5). It further clarifies that elements of the Intelligence Community are exempt from multiple obligations including minimum risk management practices and public reporting (Appendix §1(a) p. 4; Sections 3(b)(v) and 4 pp. 12–15). Additionally agencies are directed to rely on separate defense- and intelligence-specific AI policies for such systems confirming a clear exclusion from this framework’s scope
fr1_body_name: Chief AI Officer (CAIO)Agency AI Governance BoardChief AI Officer Council (interagency chaired by OMB)Office of Management and Budget (OMB) (oversight coordination reporting authority)

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy of Uruguay 2024-2030
fr1_justif: The National AI Strategy 2024-2030 addresses AI-facilitated misinformation through Line 1.2 Action F (p. 38) which explicitly commits to addressing the impacts of AI on disinformation and the integrity of democratic processes in a manner consistent with freedom of expression through a holistic and systemic approach. Line 1.2 (p. 37) more broadly commits to developing risk-based regulatory frameworks that protect human rights democracy and the rule of law throughout the AI lifecycle. The Strategy does not address gender-based AI-facilitated violence hate speech deepfakes platform content moderation obligations or redress mechanisms for victims of AI-facilitated harm.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Law No. 18331: Personal Data Protection Law (Uruguay)
fr1_justif: Article 16 of Law 18.331 protects individuals from decisions made solely on the basis of automated decision-making that signi?cantly affect them granting the right to demand human intervention review and explanation. It is mentioned in the National Artificial Intelligence Strategy 2024-2030 as part of the legal basis for regulating the automated processing of personal data. The Strategy explains that the Personal Data Regulatory and Control Unit (URCDP) is empowered to “establish the criteria and procedures that must be observed by those responsible and in charge in the automated processing of personal data indicated in Article 16 of the aforementioned Law.”
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).
fr1_defence_and_security_justif: Article 3 lit. b) of Law 18.331 explicitly excludes from the law's scope databases whose purpose is public security national defence State security and activities related to criminal investigation and repression of crime. Accordingly the rights established under the law — including the automated decision-making right under Art. 16 and the Habeas Data remedy under Art. 37 — don't apply to databases operated by armed forces police or intelligence bodies for these operational purposes. Note that administrative data held by these bodies & personal background data provided to judicial or administrative authorities remain subject to the law under Art. 25.
fr1_body_name: Personal Data Regulatory and Control Unit (URCDP)

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy of Uruguay 2024-2030
fr1_justif: The National AI Strategy 2024-2030 addresses cultural diversity in AI through three provisions. Principle 4 (p. 29-30) requires AI actors to prevent ethnic-racial and national-origin biases in AI systems mandates diversity in AI development teams and calls on the public sector to address structural underrepresentation of diverse social groups in training data. Line 2.2 Action a (p. 43) explicitly requires generating training datasets reflecting Uruguay's culture and social diversity. In p. 15 cite as a milestone the Montevideo Declaration which establishes cultural and geographical diversity as a guiding principle for responsible AI. Linguistic diversity is not addressed.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy of Uruguay 2024-2030
fr1_justif: Principle 4 of the Strategy addresses'Equity and non Discrimination' setting the standard for this issue. (Page 29). It requires AI systems to respect equality and explicitly address both algorithmic bias and automation bias recognizing risks of reproducing gender racial social and disability-based inequalities (pp. 29–30). The Strategy promotes proactive measures such as improving training data quality fostering diversity in development teams and ensuring equitable access to AI benefits (p. 30). 'This principle covers both discrimination based on algorithmic bias and discrimination based on automation bias—the human tendency to rely excessively on the support of algorithms even when they make mistakes.' (p.30)
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy of Uruguay 2024-2030
fr1_justif: Principle 4 'Equity and Non-Discrimination' (pp. 29-30) mandates public & private sector actors to adopt proactive measures to address AI's impact on gender equality & other structural inequalities throughout the entire AI system lifecycle preventing discriminatory stereotypes & biases based on gender ethnic-racial identity social status & national origin from being reproduced in AI systems. It further requires all AI actors to reduce undesired biases in training data & models & obliges the public sector to promote AI models using quality data to overcome the structural underrepresentation of diverse social groups. Line 2.4 Action d (p. 45) promotes women's enrolment in technology & STEM careers to strengthen their participation in AI.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy of Uruguay 2024-2030
fr1_justif: Action 3 of the Strategy focuses on “Respect for Dignity and Human Rights” requiring AI systems to be developed and used in accordance with ethical principles human dignity and internationally protected human rights including autonomy privacy equality and non-discrimination (p. 27). The Strategy warns that the use of AI in decision-making poses significant risks to these rights if decisions are not fair transparent and accountable. As emphasized on page 29 meaningful human oversight and human decision-making are essential particularly in high-risk applications through controls over data and systems monitoring outcomes and ensuring the reliability of AI outputs before decisions are made.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy of Uruguay 2024-2030
fr1_justif: Under the guiding principle of 'Respect for Human Dignity and Human Rights' the State is required to maintain or adopt frameworks and procedures to identify assess prevent and mitigate adverse impacts on human rights according to the risk and intended use of AI systems (p. 28). In employment the document mandates studies and projections on labor impacts monitoring mechanisms and retraining policies (p. 51). Línea 3.2 includes a specific action to investigate the social economic and environmental impact of AI and develop measurement instruments protocols and good practice guides (p. 48). The Strategy also includes indicators to evaluate economic impact and public services (Línea 3.4 action e p. 50).
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: Law 20.396/2025 on Minimum Protections for Workers Performing Tasks through Digital Platforms
fr1_justif: Law 20.396/2025 establishes minimum protections for digital platform workers. Article 4 requires companies to respect equality and non-discrimination in algorithm implementation and mandates disclosure of automated monitoring and decision-making systems. Article 5 requires disclosure of key parameters used in automated decisions. Article 7 grants workers the right to a written explanation within 48 hours of any automated decision affecting their working conditions including account suspension or termination. Article 8 guarantees digital reputation portability and data access. Article 10 requires risk assessment of automated systems. Article 19 upholds collective bargaining rights for autonomous workers.
fr1_scope: Vertical approach (the framework targets specific sectors specific types of data or  AI technologies.
fr1_body_name: Ministry of Labour and Social Security - General Labour and Social Security Inspectorate

indicator: Public Disclosure of Government Algorithmic Systems
fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy of Uruguay 2024-2030
fr1_justif: The National AI Strategy 2024-2030 (p. 31) requires public sector entities that use AI whether in service delivery or decision-making support to guarantee public access to information about those systems in accordance with Law 18381/2018 (Uy's Access to Information law). It further mandates public sector compliance with Law 19179/2013 (regulated by Decree 44/2015) on open software & the use of open AI models as mechanisms to ensure transparency of AI systems developed used & acquired by the State. These provisions establish a government-specific disclosure obligation grounded in existing access to information law directly addressing public accountability for government algorithmic systems rather than general AI transparency principles.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy of Uruguay 2024-2030
fr1_justif: Uruguay's National AI Strategy 2024-2030 (p. 38 Axis 1 Action G) calls for the establishment of a public procurement policy specifically covering the development use acquisition and implementation of AI solutions incorporating principles of ethical responsible and safe use. However this remains a prospective commitment — no AI-specific procurement guidelines vendor requirements or accountability mechanisms are yet in force.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy of Uruguay 2024-2030
fr1_justif: Uruguay's National AI Strategy 2024-2030 (p. 49 Section 3.4 'Public Management') explicitly addresses AI capacity-building for public servants. Action d mandates the design and implementation of a comprehensive training plan in collaboration with AI experts targeting technical specialised and operational roles across all levels of government including the legislative body justice system and regulatory agencies. Action e introduces monitoring mechanisms to assess the effectiveness of these training initiatives. Source: National AI Strategy 2024-2030 p. 49.
fr1_scope: Horizontal approach (the framework applies across multiple sectors or applications and is not limited to a particular domain or  AI technology type).

fr1_title: National Artificial Intelligence Strategy of Uruguay 2024-2030
fr1_justif: The National AI Strategy 2024-2030 addresses reskilling and upskilling through two dedicated lines of action. Line 2.4 (Talent Management pp. 44-45) commits to incorporating AI training across all educational levels and mandates that all labour training plans include content on AI's impact on work strengthening occupational reconversion.
